an abnormally increased activity of the: An abnormally increased activity of the renin-angiotensin system, causing hypertension by a combination of volume excess and vasoconstrictor mechanisms
an increased concentration of insulin in: An increased concentration of insulin in the blood
excessive production of parathyroid horm: Excessive production of parathyroid hormone (PTH) by the parathyroid glands
acromegaly is a condition resulting from: Acromegaly is a condition resulting from overproduction of growth hormone by the pituitary gland in persons with closed epiphyses, and consists chiefly in the enlargement of the distal parts of the body. The circumference of the skull increases, the nose becomes broad, the tongue becomes enlarged, the facial features become coarsened, the mandible grows excessively, and the teeth become separated. The fingers and toes grow chiefly in thickness
insufficient production of steroid hormo: Insufficient production of steroid hormones (primarily cortisol) by the adrenal glands
an abnormality of the renin-angiotensin: An abnormality of the renin-angiotensin system
an increased level of renin (pro:0000138: An increased level of renin (PRO:000013883) in the blood
a type of hypothyroidism with congenital: A type of hypothyroidism with congenital onset
a condition characterized by resistance: A condition characterized by resistance to the action of parathyroid hormone, in which there is hypocalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, and (appropriately) high levels of parathyroid hormone
an enlargement of the thyroid gland: An enlargement of the thyroid gland
the presence of a adenoma of the thyroid: The presence of a adenoma of the thyroid gland
increased resistance towards insulin, th: Increased resistance towards insulin, that is, diminished effectiveness of insulin in reducing blood glucose levels
overproduction of the mineralocorticoid: Overproduction of the mineralocorticoid aldosterone by the adrenal cortex
developmental hypoplasia of the parathyr: Developmental hypoplasia of the parathyroid gland
a form of diabetes insipidus related to: A form of diabetes insipidus related to a failure of vasopressin (AVP) release from the hypothalamus
secondary hyperparathyroidism refers to: Secondary hyperparathyroidism refers to the production of higher than normal levels of parathyroid hormone in the presence of hypocalcemia
the presence of abnormally increased lev: The presence of abnormally increased levels of prolactin in the blood. Prolactin is a peptide hormone produced by the anterior pituitary gland that plays a role in breast development and lactation during pregnancy
a pituitary functional deficit affecting: A pituitary functional deficit affecting all the anterior pituitary hormones (growth hormone, thyroid-stimulating hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, adrenocorticotropic hormone, and prolactin)
a chronic, autoimmune type of thyroiditi: A chronic, autoimmune type of thyroiditis associated with hypothyroidism
a state of excessive water intake and hy: A state of excessive water intake and hypotonic (dilute) polyuria. Diabetes insipidus may be due to failure of vasopressin (AVP) release (central or neurogenic diabetes insipidus) or to a failure of the kidney to respond to AVP (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus)
infrequent menses (less than 6 per year: Infrequent menses (less than 6 per year or more than 35 days between cycles)
presence of only 11 pairs of ribs: Presence of only 11 pairs of ribs
decreased inferosuperior length of the s: Decreased inferosuperior length of the sternum
a horizontal (flat) conformation of the: A horizontal (flat) conformation of the ribs, the long curved bones that form the rib cage and normally progressively oblique (slanted) from ribs 1 through 9, then less slanted through rib 12
any abnormality of the clavicles (collar: Any abnormality of the clavicles (collar bones)
a bifid rib refers to cleavage of the st: A bifid rib refers to cleavage of the sternal end of a rib, usually unilateral. Bifid ribs are usually asymptomatic, and are often discovered incidentally by chest x-ray
abnormal outward curving (protuberance): Abnormal outward curving (protuberance) of the junction of ribs and costal cartilage
an excessive upward convexity of the lat: An excessive upward convexity of the lateral clavicle
multiple circumscribed bony excrescences: Multiple circumscribed bony excrescences located in the ribs
a row of beadlike prominences at the jun: A row of beadlike prominences at the junction of a rib and its cartilage, resembling a rosary
complete or partial merging of adjacent: Complete or partial merging of adjacent ribs
the presence of wide, concave anterior r: The presence of wide, concave anterior rib ends
progressive bone resorption in the dista: Progressive bone resorption in the distal part of the clavicle
abnormally flat configuration of the gle: Abnormally flat configuration of the glenoid fossa, also known as the glenoid cavity, which is the articular surface of the scapula that articulates with the head of the humerus
anomalie squelettique congénitale avec é: A congenital skeletal deformity characterized by the elevation of one scapula (thus, one scapula is located superior to the other)
complete or partial merging of the poste: Complete or partial merging of the posterior part of adjacent ribs
pectus excavatum (defect of the chest wa: Pectus excavatum (defect of the chest wall characterized by depression of the sternum) affecting primarily the inferior region of the sternum
increased width (cross-sectional diamete: Increased width (cross-sectional diameter) of the clavicles
pectus carinatum affecting primarily the: Pectus carinatum affecting primarily the superior part of the sternum
the presence of multiple exostoses on th: The presence of multiple exostoses on the scapula. An exostosis is a benign growth the projects outward from the bone surface. It is capped by cartilage
any anomaly of the costochondral junctio: Any anomaly of the costochondral junction. The costochondral junctions are located between the distal part of the ribs and the costal cartilages, which are bars of hyaline cartilage that connect the ribs to the sternum
abnormally increased size of the costoch: Abnormally increased size of the costochondral junctions, which are located between the distal part of the ribs and the costal cartilages, which are bars of hyaline cartilage that connect the ribs to the sternum
a developmental anomaly with absence of: A developmental anomaly with absence of one or more ribs
the presence of a row of multiple rounde: The presence of a row of multiple rounded expansions (beadlike prominences) at the junction of a rib and its cartilage
an abnormality of the skeletal system: An abnormality of the skeletal system
any abnormality of the vertebral column: Any abnormality of the vertebral column
a flattened vertebral body shape with re: A flattened vertebral body shape with reduced distance between the vertebral endplates
the bones of the skeleton undergo a seri: The bones of the skeleton undergo a series of characteristic changes in size, shape, and calcification from fetal life until puberty. An abnormality of this process can include delayed or accelerated skeletal maturation, or deviation of some, but not all bones from the expected patterns of maturation
an abnormality of the skull, the bony fr: An abnormality of the skull, the bony framework of the head which is comprised of eight cranial and fourteen facial bones
an abnormality of the fossa cranii poste: An abnormality of the fossa cranii posterior (the posterior fossa), which is made up primarily of the occipital bone and which surrounds to the foramen magnum
radiographic evidence of articular calci: Radiographic evidence of articular calcification that represent calcium pyrophosphate depositions in soft tissue surrounding joints and at the insertions of tendons near joints (Entheses/Sharpey fibers)
abnormal thickening of the cortex of lon: Abnormal thickening of the cortex of long bones
osteopenia is a term to define bone dens: Osteopenia is a term to define bone density that is not normal but also not as low as osteoporosis. By definition from the World Health Organization osteopenia is defined by bone densitometry as a T score -1 to -2.5
osteoporosis is a systemic skeletal dise: Osteoporosis is a systemic skeletal disease characterized by low bone density and microarchitectural deterioration of bone tissue with a consequent increase in bone fragility. According to the WHO criteria, osteoporosis is defined as a BMD that lies 2.5 standard deviations or more below the average value for young healthy adults (a T-score below -2.5 SD)
an abnormality of the structure or form: An abnormality of the structure or form of the diaphysis, i.e., of the main or mid-section (shaft) of a long bone
an abnormality of one or more metaphysis: An abnormality of one or more metaphysis, i.e., of the somewhat wider portion of a long bone that is adjacent to the epiphyseal growth plate and grows during childhood
an abnormal appearance of the long bones: An abnormal appearance of the long bones with resemblance to a dumbbell, a short bar with a weight at each end. That is, the long bone is shortened and displays flaring (widening) of the metaphyses
yellow pigmentation of the skin due to b: Yellow pigmentation of the skin due to bilirubin, which in turn is the result of increased bilirubin concentration in the bloodstream
a darkening of the skin related to an in: A darkening of the skin related to an increase in melanin production and deposition
the distal and proximal transverse palma: The distal and proximal transverse palmar creases are merged into a single transverse palmar crease
a dermatosis characterized by thickened,: A dermatosis characterized by thickened, hyperpigmented plaques, typically on the intertriginous surfaces and neck
cafe-au-lait spots are hyperpigmented le: Cafe-au-lait spots are hyperpigmented lesions that can vary in color from light brown to dark brown with smooth borders and having a size of 1.5 cm or more in adults and 0.5 cm or more in children
skin characterized by the lack of natura: Skin characterized by the lack of natural or normal moisture
a subtype of skin dimples presenting as: A subtype of skin dimples presenting as an indentation in the skin of the intergluteal cleft
bluish discoloration of the skin and muc: Bluish discoloration of the skin and mucosa due to poor circulation or inadequate oxygenation of arterial or capillary blood
hyperkeratosis is thickening of the oute: Hyperkeratosis is thickening of the outer layer of the skin, the stratum corneum, which is composed of large, polyhedral, plate-like envelopes filled with keratin which are the dead cells that have migrated up from the stratum granulosum
reduction in thickness of the skin, gene: Reduction in thickness of the skin, generally associated with a loss of suppleness and elasticity of the skin
eczema is a form of dermatitis. the term: Eczema is a form of dermatitis. The term eczema is broadly applied to a range of persistent skin conditions and can be related to a number of underlying conditions. Manifestations of eczema can include dryness and recurring skin rashes with redness, skin edema, itching and dryness, crusting, flaking, blistering, cracking, oozing, or bleeding
a reticular discoloration of the skin wi: A reticular discoloration of the skin with cyanotic (reddish-blue appearing) areas surrounding pale central areas due to dilation of capillary blood vessels and stagnation of blood within the vessels. Cutis marmorata, also called livedo reticularis, generally occurs on the legs, arms and trunk and is often more severe in cold weather
petechiae are pinpoint-sized reddish/pur: Petechiae are pinpoint-sized reddish/purple spots, resembling a rash, that appear just under the skin or a mucous membrane when capillaries have ruptured and some superficial bleeding into the skin has happened. This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to developing petechiae
ectodermal dysplasia is a group of condi: Ectodermal dysplasia is a group of conditions in which there is abnormal development of the skin, hair, nails, teeth, or sweat glands
an abnormal accumulation of fluid beneat: An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin, or in one or more cavities of the body
any structural abnormality of the sweat: Any structural abnormality of the sweat gland
hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the: Hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the hand and the sole of the foot
wrinkled, redundant, inelastic and saggi: Wrinkled, redundant, inelastic and sagging skin
a condition in which the skin can be str: A condition in which the skin can be stretched beyond normal, and then returns to its initial position
abnormal excessive perspiration (sweatin: Abnormal excessive perspiration (sweating) despite the lack of appropriate stimuli like hot and humid weather
subjective impression of increased softn: Subjective impression of increased softness upon palpitation of the skin
an ecchymosis (bruise) refers to the ski: An ecchymosis (bruise) refers to the skin discoloration caused by the escape of blood into the tissues from ruptured blood vessels. This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to bruising. The corresponding phenotypic abnormality is generally elicited on medical history as a report of frequent ecchymoses or bruising without adequate trauma
purpura (from latin: purpura, meaning \": Purpura (from Latin: purpura, meaning "purple") is the appearance of red or purple discolorations on the skin that do not blanch on applying pressure. They are caused by bleeding underneath the skin. This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to developing purpura. Purpura are larger than petechiae
abnormal thickening of the skin of the p: Abnormal thickening of the skin of the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet
a red eruption of the skin: A red eruption of the skin
pruritus is an itch or a sensation that: Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased disposition to experience pruritus
the presence of multiple xanthomas (xant: The presence of multiple xanthomas (xanthomata) in the skin. Xanthomas are yellowish, firm, lipid-laden nodules in the skin
an increased sensitivity of the skin to: An increased sensitivity of the skin to light. Photosensitivity may result in a rash upon exposure to the sun (which is known as photodermatosis). Photosensitivity can be diagnosed by phototests in which light is shone on small areas of skin
bluish-grey, spongy nodules associated w: Bluish-grey, spongy nodules associated with scars over pressure points and easily traumatized areas like the elbows and knees
a oval and round, colored (usually mediu: A oval and round, colored (usually medium-to dark brown, reddish brown, or flesh colored) lesion. Typically, a melanocytic nevus is less than 6 mm in diameter, but may be much smaller or larger
hemangioma, a benign tumor of the vascul: Hemangioma, a benign tumor of the vascular endothelial cells with small endothelial spaces, occurring in the face
the presence in the axillary region (arm: The presence in the axillary region (armpit) of an increased number of freckles, small circular spots on the skin that are darker than the surrounding skin because of deposits of melanin
hypertrichosis is increased hair growth: Hypertrichosis is increased hair growth that is abnormal in quantity or location
any manifestation of a skin disease asso: Any manifestation of a skin disease associated with the production of pus
an abnormality of the pigmentation of th: An abnormality of the pigmentation of the skin
presence of an unusually high number of: Presence of an unusually high number of lentigines (singular: lentigo), which are flat, tan to brown oval spots
localized fluid retention and tissue swe: Localized fluid retention and tissue swelling caused by a compromised lymphatic system
abnormally increased hair growth referri: Abnormally increased hair growth referring to a male pattern of body hair (androgenic hair)
telangiectasias refer to small dilated b: Telangiectasias refer to small dilated blood vessels located near the surface of the skin or mucous membranes, measuring between 0.5 and 1 millimeter in diameter. Telangiectasia are located especially on the tongue, lips, palate, fingers, face, conjunctiva, trunk, nail beds, and fingertips
a reduction of skin color related to a d: A reduction of skin color related to a decrease in melanin production and deposition
the presence of multiple lipomas (a type: The presence of multiple lipomas (a type of benign tissue made of fatty tissue)
eruptive xanthomas are yellow-orange-to-: Eruptive xanthomas are yellow-orange-to-red-brown papules that are often surrounded by an erythematous halo. They appear in crops on the buttocks, extensor surfaces of the extremities, and flexural creases. Acutely, variable amounts of pruritus and pain occur
a vascular lesion defined histologically: A vascular lesion defined histologically as one or more dilated blood vessels lying directly subepidermal and showing an epidermal proliferative reaction. Clinically, angiokeratoma presents as a small, raised, dark-red spot
a condition in which superficial veins (: A condition in which superficial veins (i.e., veins just under the skin) are more conspicuous or noticable than normal
a type of pallor that is secondary to th: A type of pallor that is secondary to the presence of anemia
an abnormality of the dermatoglyphs, i.e: An abnormality of the dermatoglyphs, i.e., an abnormality of the patterns of ridges of the skin of palm of hand
an inflammatory exfoliative dermatosis i: An inflammatory exfoliative dermatosis involving nearly all of the surface of the skin. Erythroderma develops suddenly. A patchy erythema may generalize and spread to affect most of the skin. Scaling may appear in 2-6 days and be accompanied by hot, red, dry skin, malaise, and fever
an abnormal reduction in the amount of p: An abnormal reduction in the amount of pigmentation (reduced or absent) of skin, hair and eye (iris and retina)
raised, well-circumscribed areas of eryt: Raised, well-circumscribed areas of erythema and edema involving the dermis and epidermis. Urticaria is intensely pruritic, and blanches completely with pressure
a skin texture that is unusually soft (a: A skin texture that is unusually soft (and may feel silky), and has a malleable consistency resembling that of dough
a hemangioma is a benign tumor character: A hemangioma is a benign tumor characterized by blood-filled spaces lined by benign endothelial cells. A hemangioma characterized by large endothelial spaces (caverns) is called a cavernous hemangioma (in contrast to a hemangioma with small endothelial spaces, which is called capillary hemangioma)
poikiloderma refers to a patch of skin w: Poikiloderma refers to a patch of skin with (1) reticulated hypopigmentation and hyperpigmentation, (2) wrinkling secondary to epidermal atrophy, and (3) telangiectasias
skin that splits easily with minimal inj: Skin that splits easily with minimal injury
absence of the distal interphalangeal fl: Absence of the distal interphalangeal flexion creases of the fingers
a hyperpigmented circumscribed area of c: A hyperpigmented circumscribed area of change in normal skin color without elevation or depression of any size
abnormal formation of the keratinocytes: Abnormal formation of the keratinocytes of the epidermis characterized by persistence of nuclei, incomplete formation of keratin, and moistness and swelling of the keratinocytes
redness of the skin of the face, caused: Redness of the skin of the face, caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin
jaundice that is sometimes present, some: Jaundice that is sometimes present, sometimes not
atopic dermatitis (ad) or atopic eczema: Atopic dermatitis (AD) or atopic eczema is an itchy, inflammatory skin condition with a predilection for the skin flexures. It is characterized by poorly defined erythema with edema, vesicles, and weeping in the acute stage and skin thickening (lichenification) in the chronic stage
The presence of a cavernous hemangioma. A hemangioma characterized by large endothelial spaces (caverns) is called a cavernous hemangioma
seborrheic dermatitis is a form of eczem: Seborrheic dermatitis is a form of eczema which is closely related to dandruff. It causes dry or greasy peeling of the scalp, eyebrows, and face, and sometimes trunk
a congenital vascular malformation consi: A congenital vascular malformation consisting of superficial and deep dilated capillaries in the skin which produce a reddish to purplish discolouration of the skin
increased susceptibility to erysipelas,: Increased susceptibility to erysipelas, as manifested by a medical history of repeated episodes of erysipelas, which is a superficial infection of the skin, typically involving the lymphatic system
presence of multiple small cysts contain: Presence of multiple small cysts containing keratin (skin protein) and presenting as tiny pearly-white bumps just under the surface of the skin
a developmental defect resulting in the: A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin in multiple or solitary non-inflammatory, well-demarcated, oval or circular ulcers with a diameter of about 1 to 2 cm. Aplasia cutis congenita most commonly occurs on the scalp, but may present in the face, trunk, or limbs
a reduced ability to heal cutaneous woun: A reduced ability to heal cutaneous wounds
pterygia are 'winglike' triangular membr: Pterygia are 'winglike' triangular membranes occurring in the neck, eyes, knees, elbows, ankles or digits
presence of a cutaneous membrane (flap): Presence of a cutaneous membrane (flap) in the armpit
a skin condition in which there is an in: A skin condition in which there is an increase in sebum secretion by the pilosebaceous apparatus associated with open comedones (blackheads), closed comedones (whiteheads), and pustular nodules (papules, pustules, and cysts)
a large pigmented lesion measuring 5-15: A large pigmented lesion measuring 5-15 mm in diameter with irregular, notched, and ill defined border and with color that may range from tan to dark brown to pink
thinned, erythematous, depressed bands o: Thinned, erythematous, depressed bands of atrophic skin. Initially, striae appear as flattened and thinned, pinkish linear regions of the skin. Striae tend to enlarge in length and become reddish or purplish. Later, striae tend to appear as white, depressed bands that are parallel to the lines of skin tension. Striae distensae occur most often in areas that have been subject to distension such as the lower back, buttocks, thighs, breast, abdomen, and shoulders
the presence of multiple cutaneous neuro: The presence of multiple cutaneous neurofibromas
intermittent episodes of abnormally incr: Intermittent episodes of abnormally increased perspiration
scars that form a depression compared to: Scars that form a depression compared to the level of the surrounding skin because of damage to the collagen, fat or other tissues below the skin
an abnormality of the biliary tree: An abnormality of the biliary tree
hard, pebble-like deposits that form wit: Hard, pebble-like deposits that form within the gallbladder
the presence of inflammatory changes in: The presence of inflammatory changes in the gallbladder
dislocation or malposition of the crysta: Dislocation or malposition of the crystalline lens of the eye. A partial displacement (or dislocation) of the lens is described as a subluxation of the lens, while a complete displacement is termed luxation of the lens. A complete displacement occurs if the lens is completely outside the patellar fossa of the lens, either in the anterior chamber, in the vitreous, or directly on the retina. If the lens is partially displaced but still contained within the lens space, then it is termed subluxation
a hazy, grayish-white ring about 2 mm in: A hazy, grayish-white ring about 2 mm in width located close to but separated from the limbus (the corneoscleral junction). Corneal arcus generally occurs bilaterally, and is related to lipid deposition in the cornea. Corneal arcus can occur in elderly persons as a part of the aging process but may be associated with hypercholesterolemia in people under the age of 50 years
papilledema refers to edema (swelling) o: Papilledema refers to edema (swelling) of the optic disc secondary to any factor which increases cerebral spinal fluid pressure
glaucoma which forms during the early ye: Glaucoma which forms during the early years of a child's life is called developmental or congenital glaucoma
the presence of whitish spots in a ring-: The presence of whitish spots in a ring-like arrangement at the periphery of the iris
diffusely large eye (with megalocornea): Diffusely large eye (with megalocornea) without glaucoma
no identifiable superior and/or inferior: No identifiable superior and/or inferior lacrimal punctum
the presence of developmental dysplasia: The presence of developmental dysplasia of the optic nerve
a type of anterior uveitis, in which the: A type of anterior uveitis, in which there is Inflammation of the iris and the ciliary body
inflammation of the cornea and conjuncti: Inflammation of the cornea and conjunctiva
dryness of the eye related to deficiency: Dryness of the eye related to deficiency of the tear film components (aqueous, mucin, or lipid), lid surface abnormalities, or epithelial abnormalities. Keratoconjunctivitis sicca often results in a scratchy or sandy sensation (foreign body sensation) in the eyes, and may also be associated with itching, inability to produce tears, photosensitivity, redness, pain, and difficulty in moving the eyelids
any structural abnormality of the fundus: Any structural abnormality of the fundus of the eye
heterochromia iridis is a difference in: Heterochromia iridis is a difference in the color of the iris in the two eyes
irregular lines in the deep retina that: Irregular lines in the deep retina that are typically configured in a radiating fashion and emanate from the optic disc. Angioid streaks are crack-like dehiscences in abnormally thickened and calcified Bruch's membrane, resulting in atrophy of the overlying retinal pigment epithelium. They may be associated with a number of endocrine, metabolic, and connective tissue abnormalities but are frequently idiopathic
a structural abnormality of the macula l: A structural abnormality of the macula lutea, which is an oval-shaped highly pigmented yellow spot near the center of the retina
well-demarcated area(s) of partial or co: Well-demarcated area(s) of partial or complete depigmentation in the fundus, reflecting atrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium with associated retinal photoreceptor loss
increased pigmentation of the skin in th: Increased pigmentation of the skin in the region surrounding the orbit of the eye
an abnormal reduction in the amount of p: An abnormal reduction in the amount of pigmentation (reduced or absent) of the iris and retina
degeneration of retinal ganglion cells a: Degeneration of retinal ganglion cells and their axons
the presence of xanthomata in the skin o: The presence of xanthomata in the skin of the eyelid
a polar cataract that affects the poster: A polar cataract that affects the posterior pole of the lens
a congenital defect of the macula distin: A congenital defect of the macula distinct from coloboma associated with optic fissure closure defects. Macular coloboma is characterized by a sharply defined, rather large defect in the central area of the fundus that is oval or round, and coarsely pigmented
severe loss of visual acuity within hour: Severe loss of visual acuity within hours or days. This is characteristic of Leber hereditary optic neuropathy
a type of cataract that is not apparent: A type of cataract that is not apparent at birth but that arises in childhood or adolescence
limbus-to-limbus corneal thinning, often: Limbus-to-limbus corneal thinning, often greatest in the periphery, with globular protrusion of the cornea
any abnormality of the size or morpholog: Any abnormality of the size or morphology of the cornea
transient blurring of vision associated: Transient blurring of vision associated with the aura phase of migraine
cryptophthalmos is a condition of total: Cryptophthalmos is a condition of total absence of eyelids and the skin of forehead is continuous with that of cheek, in which the eyeball is completely concealed by the skin, which is stretched over the orbital cavity
inversion and rubbing of the eyelashes a: Inversion and rubbing of the eyelashes against the globe of the eye
an abnormality of the cornea that is cha: An abnormality of the cornea that is characterized by opacity of one or parts of the cornea
partial dislocation of the lens of the e: Partial dislocation of the lens of the eye
an absolute or relative decrease in reti: An absolute or relative decrease in retinal sensitivity extending from edge (periphery) of the visual field in a concentric pattern. The visual field is the area that is perceived simultaneously by a fixating eye
a polar cataract that affects the anteri: A polar cataract that affects the anterior pole of the lens
the presence of an increased number of t: The presence of an increased number of twists and turns of the retinal arterioles
esotropia in which either eye may be use: Esotropia in which either eye may be used for fixation
a benign tumor typically found at the ju: A benign tumor typically found at the junction of the cornea and sclera (limbal epibullar dermoid)
severe reduction of the ability to see d: Severe reduction of the ability to see defined as visual acuity less than 6/60 (20/200 in US notation; 0.1 in decimal notation) but at least 3/60 (20/400 in US notation; 0.05 in decimal notation)
a conical projection of the anterior or: A conical projection of the anterior or posterior surface of the lens, occurring as a developmental anomaly
presence of a cyst in the region of the: Presence of a cyst in the region of the periorbital tissues. Orbital cysts can be derived from epithelial or glandular tissue within or surrounding the orbit (lacrimal glands, salivary glands, conjunctival, oral, nasal, or sinus epithelium)
fluid which has escaped from retinal blo: Fluid which has escaped from retinal blood vessels with a high concentration of lipid, protein, and cellular debris with a typically bright, reflective, white or cream colored appearance on the surface of the retina
the presence of fine, branching linear o: The presence of fine, branching linear opacities in Bowman's layer in the central area that may spread to the periphery in the clinical course. The deep corneal stroma may be involved but the process does not reach Descemet's membrane. Recurrent corneal erosion may occur. Histologic examination reveals amyloid deposits in the collagen fibers of the cornea
an abnormality of ocular smooth pursuit: An abnormality of ocular smooth pursuit characterized by an impairment of the ability to track horizontally moving objects
an abnormality of tracking eye movements: An abnormality of tracking eye movements in which smooth pursuit is interrupted by an abnormally high number of saccadic movements
the presence of a vaginal septum, thereb: The presence of a vaginal septum, thereby creating a vaginal duplication. The septum is longitudinal in the majority of cases
an abnormality affecting one or both han: An abnormality affecting one or both hands
digits that appear disproportionately sh: Digits that appear disproportionately short compared to the hand/foot. The word brachydactyly is used here to describe a series distinct patterns of shortened digits (brachydactyly types A-E). This is the sense used here
webbing or fusion of the fingers or toes: Webbing or fusion of the fingers or toes, involving soft parts only or including bone structure. Bony fusions are referred to as "bony" syndactyly if the fusion occurs in a radio-ulnar axis. Fusions of bones of the fingers or toes in a proximo-distal axis are referred to as "symphalangism"
a kind of polydactyly characterized by t: A kind of polydactyly characterized by the presence of a supernumerary finger or fingers
supernumerary digits located at the ulna: Supernumerary digits located at the ulnar side of the hand (that is, on the side with the fifth finger)
an abnormality of the metacarpal bones: An abnormality of the metacarpal bones
abnormally long and slender fingers (\"s: Abnormally long and slender fingers ("spider fingers")
for children from birth to 4 years of ag: For children from birth to 4 years of age the palm width is more than 2 SD above the mean; for children from 4 to 16 years of age the palm width is above the 95th centile; or, the width of the palm appears disproportionately wide for the length
a condition in which middle parts of the: A condition in which middle parts of the hand (fingers and metacarpals) are missing giving a cleft appearance. The severity is very variable ranging from slightly hypoplastic middle fingers over absent middel fingers as far as oligo- or monodactyl hands
an abnormal structure of the first digit: An abnormal structure of the first digit of the hand
supernumerary digits located at the radi: Supernumerary digits located at the radial side of the hand. Polydactyly (supernumerary digits) involving the thumb occurs in many distinct forms of high variability and severity. Ranging from fleshy nubbins over varying degrees of partial duplication/splitting to completely duplicated or even triplicated thumbs or preaxial (on the radial side of the hand) supernumerary digits
an abnormal hand position characterized: An abnormal hand position characterized by hyperextension of the fourth and fifth fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints of the same fingers such that they are curled towards the palm
a developmental defect resulting in the: A developmental defect resulting in the presence of fewer than the normal number of fingers
in the resting position, the tip of the: In the resting position, the tip of the thumb is on, or near, the palm, close to the base of the fourth or fifth finger
the gradual reduction in girth of the fi: The gradual reduction in girth of the finger from proximal to distal
the ability of the finger joints to move: The ability of the finger joints to move beyond their normal range of motion
an abnormal hand posture in which the ha: An abnormal hand posture in which the hands are clenched to fists. All digits held completely flexed at the metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints
an abnormality affecting the carpal bone: An abnormality affecting the carpal bones of the wrist (scaphoid, lunate, triquetral, pisiform, trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate)
an abnormality of the placenta (the orga: An abnormality of the placenta (the organ that connects the developing fetus to the uterine wall) or of the umbilical cord (the cord that connects the fetus to the placenta)
single umbilical artery (sua) is the abs: Single umbilical artery (SUA) is the absence of one of the two umbilical arteries surrounding the fetal bladder and in the fetal umbilical cord
decreased length of the umbilical cord: Decreased length of the umbilical cord
an abnormality of the fetus or the birth: An abnormality of the fetus or the birth of the fetus, excluding structural abnormalities
a thumb with three phalanges in a single: A thumb with three phalanges in a single, proximo-distal axis. Thus, this term applies if the thumb has an accessory phalanx, leading to a digit like appearance of the thumb
the term distal symphalangism refers to: The term distal symphalangism refers to a bony fusion of the distal and middle phalanges of the digits of the hand, in other words the distal interphalangeal joint (DIJ) is missing which can be seen either on x-rays or as an absence of the distal interphalangeal finger creases
an abnormal structure of the tip (end) o: An abnormal structure of the tip (end) of a finger
a soft tissue prominence of the ventral: A soft tissue prominence of the ventral aspects of the fingertips. The term "persistent fetal fingertip pads" is often used as a synonym, but should better not be used because it implies knowledge of history of the patient which often does not exist
the distal interphalangeal joint and/or: The distal interphalangeal joint and/or the proximal interphalangeal joint of the second to fifth fingers cannot be extended to 180 degrees by either active or passive extension
ossification of carpal bones occurs late: Ossification of carpal bones occurs later than age-adjusted norms
broadening of the soft tissues (non-edem: Broadening of the soft tissues (non-edematous swelling of soft tissues) of the digital tips in all dimensions associated with an increased longitudinal and lateral curvature of the nails
spontaneous detachment (amputation) of a: Spontaneous detachment (amputation) of an appendage from the body
chronic loss of joint motion in an inter: Chronic loss of joint motion in an interphalangeal joint of a finger due to structural changes in non-bony tissue
all of the metacarpal bones of the hand: All of the metacarpal bones of the hand have a pointed proximal appearance
an abnormality of the thenar eminence, i: An abnormality of the thenar eminence, i.e., of the muscle on the palm of the human hand just beneath the thumb
telangiectases in the area of the nails: Telangiectases in the area of the nails
syndactyly with fusion of fingers two an: Syndactyly with fusion of fingers two and three
with the hand relaxed and the thumb in t: With the hand relaxed and the thumb in the plane of the palm, the axis of the thumb forms an angle of at least 90 degrees with the long axis of the hand
fingers that are disproportionately narr: Fingers that are disproportionately narrow (reduced girth) for the hand/foot size or build of the individual
a chronic loss of wrist joint motion due: A chronic loss of wrist joint motion due to structural changes in muscle, tendons, ligaments, or skin that prevent normal movement of the joints of the wrist
underdevelopment of the thenar eminence: Underdevelopment of the thenar eminence with reduced palmar soft tissue mass surrounding the base of the thumb
decreased length of the tubular bones of: Decreased length of the tubular bones of the hand, that is, the phalanges and metacarpals
subnormal intellectual functioning which: Subnormal intellectual functioning which originates during the developmental period. Intellectual disability, previously referred to as mental retardation, has been defined as an IQ score below 70
seizures are an intermittent abnormality: Seizures are an intermittent abnormality of the central nervous system due to a sudden, excessive, disorderly discharge of cerebral neurons and characterized clinically by some combination of disturbance of sensation, loss of consciousness, impairment of psychic function, or convulsive movements. The term epilepsy is used to describe chronic, recurrent seizures
cerebellar ataxia refers to ataxia due t: Cerebellar ataxia refers to ataxia due to dysfunction of the cerebellum. This causes a variety of elementary neurological deficits including asynergy (lack of coordination between muscles, limbs and joints), dysmetria (lack of ability to judge distances that can lead to under- oder overshoot in grasping movements), and dysdiadochokinesia (inability to perform rapid movements requiring antagonizing muscle groups to be switched on and off repeatedly)
muscular hypotonia is an abnormally low: Muscular hypotonia is an abnormally low muscle tone (the amount of tension or resistance to movement in a muscle), often involving reduced muscle strength. Hypotonia is characterized by a diminished resistance to passive stretching
a state of disinterestedness, listlessne: A state of disinterestedness, listlessness, and indifference, resulting in difficulty performing simple tasks or concentrating
mild intellectual disability is defined: Mild intellectual disability is defined as an intelligence quotient (IQ) in the range of 50-69
a motor disorder characterized by a velo: A motor disorder characterized by a velocity-dependent increase in tonic stretch reflexes with increased muscle tone, exaggerated (hyperexcitable) tendon reflexes
spasticity and weakness of the leg and h: Spasticity and weakness of the leg and hip muscles
complete absence of wakefulness and cont: Complete absence of wakefulness and content of conscience, which manifests itself as a lack of response to any kind of external stimuli
dysarthric speech is a general descripti: Dysarthric speech is a general description referring to a neurological speech disorder characterized by poor articulation. Depending on the involved neurological structures, dysarthria may be further classified as spastic, flaccid, ataxic, hyperkinetic and hypokinetic, or mixed
a state of abnormally strong desire for: A state of abnormally strong desire for sleep during the daytime
a delay in the achievement of motor or m: A delay in the achievement of motor or mental milestones in the domains of development of a child, including motor skills, speech and language, cognitive skills, and social and emotional skills. This term should only be used to describe children younger than five years of age
spasticity (neuromuscular hypertonia) pr: Spasticity (neuromuscular hypertonia) primarily in the muscles of the legs, hips, and pelvis
reduction of neurologic reflexes such as: Reduction of neurologic reflexes such as the knee-jerk reaction
involuntary movements characterized by b: Involuntary movements characterized by both athetosis (inability to sustain muscles in a fixed position) and chorea (widespread jerky arrhythmic movements)
loss of previously present mental abilit: Loss of previously present mental abilities, generally in adults
loss of strength in the arm, leg, and so: Loss of strength in the arm, leg, and sometimes face on one side of the body. Hemiplegia refers to a complete loss of strength, whereas hemiparesis refers to an incomplete loss of strength
a type of developmental delay characteri: A type of Developmental delay characterized by a delay in acquiring motor skills
a generalized disorder of peripheral ner: A generalized disorder of peripheral nerves
absence of the corpus callosum as a resu: Absence of the corpus callosum as a result of the failure of the corpus callosum to develop, which can be the result of a failure in any one of the multiple steps of callosal development including cellular proliferation and migration, axonal growth or glial patterning at the midline
a condition in which there is increased: A condition in which there is increased muscle tone so that arms or legs, for example, are stiff and difficult to move
a form of hypotension characterized by a: A form of hypotension characterized by a sudden fall in blood pressure that occurs when a person assumes a standing position
syncope refers to a generalized weakness: Syncope refers to a generalized weakness of muscles with loss of postural tone, inability to stand upright, and loss of consciousness. Once the patient is in a horizontal position, blood flow to the brain is no longer hindered by gravitation and consciousness is regained. Unconsciousness usually lasts for seconds to minutes. Headache and drowsiness (which usually follow seizures) do not follow a syncopal attack. Syncope results from a sudden impairment of brain metabolism usually due to a reduction in cerebral blood flow
a condition characterized by intermitten: A condition characterized by intermittent involuntary contraction of muscles (spasms) related to hypocalcemia or occasionally magneisum deficiency
bulbar weakness (or bulbar palsy) refers: Bulbar weakness (or bulbar palsy) refers to bilateral impairment of function of the lower cranial nerves IX, X, XI and XII, which occurs due to lower motor neuron lesion either at nuclear or fascicular level in the medulla or from bilateral lesions of the lower cranial nerves outside the brain-stem. Bulbar weakness is often associated with difficulty in chewing, weakness of the facial muscles, dysarthria, palatal weakness and regurgitation of fluids, dysphagia, and dysphonia
absence of neurologic reflexes such as t: Absence of neurologic reflexes such as the knee-jerk reaction
spastic weakness affecting all four limb: Spastic weakness affecting all four limbs
the term gait disturbance can refer to a: The term gait disturbance can refer to any disruption of the ability to walk. In general, this can refer to neurological diseases but also fractures or other sources of pain that is triggered upon walking. However, in the current context gait disturbance refers to difficulty walking on the basis of a neurological or muscular disease
lack of clarity and coherence of thought: Lack of clarity and coherence of thought, perception, understanding, or action
generalized muscular hypotonia (abnormal: Generalized muscular hypotonia (abnormally low muscle tone)
structural abnormality affecting one or: Structural abnormality affecting one or more of the cranial nerves, which emerge directly from the brain stem
sudden impairment of blood flow to a par: Sudden impairment of blood flow to a part of the brain due to occlusion or rupture of an artery to the brain
encephalopathy is a term that means brai: Encephalopathy is a term that means brain disease, damage, or malfunction. In general, encephalopathy is manifested by an altered mental state
characteristic neurologic anomaly result: Characteristic neurologic anomaly resulting form degeneration of dopamine-generating cells in the substantia nigra, a region of the midbrain, characterized clinically by shaking, rigidity, slowness of movement and difficulty with walking and gait
a congenital abnormality of the cerebral: A congenital abnormality of the cerebral hemisphere chacterized by unusually thick gyrations (convolutions) of the cerebral cortex
sustained involuntary muscle contraction: Sustained involuntary muscle contractions that produce twisting and repetitive movements of the body
a congenital brain malformation typicall: A congenital brain malformation typically characterized by incomplete formation of the cerebellar vermis, dilation of the fourth ventricle, and enlargement of the posterior fossa. In layman's terms, Dandy Walker malformation is a cyst in the cerebellum (typically symmetrical) that is involved with the fourth ventricle. This may interfere with the ability to drain cerebrospinal fluid from the brain, resulting in hydrocephalus. Dandy Walker cysts are formed during early embryonic development, while the brain forms. The cyst in the cerebellum typically has several blood vessels running through it connecting to the brain, thereby prohibiting surgical removal
fasciculations or fibrillation affecting: Fasciculations or fibrillation affecting the tongue muscle
a type of ataxia characterized by the in: A type of ataxia characterized by the inability to carry out movements with the correct range and motion across the plane of more than one joint related to incorrect estimation of the distances required for targeted movements
an abnormality of the function of the el: An abnormality of the function of the electrical signals with which nerve cells communicate with each other or with muscles as measured by electrophysiological investigations
an abnormal enlargement (i.e. increase i: An abnormal enlargement (i.e. increase in measured voltage) of somatosensory evoked potentials
diminution of tendon reflexes, which is: Diminution of tendon reflexes, which is an invariable sign of peripheral nerve disease
any structural abnormality of the cerebe: Any structural abnormality of the cerebellum
muscular hypotonia (abnormally low muscl: Muscular hypotonia (abnormally low muscle tone) manifesting in the neonatal period
underdevelopment of the vermis of cerebe: Underdevelopment of the vermis of cerebellum
eeg shows spikes (<80 ms) and waves, whi: EEG shows spikes (<80 ms) and waves, which are recorded over the entire scalp and do not have a specific frequency
impairment of certain skills such as rea: Impairment of certain skills such as reading or writing, coordination, self-control, or attention that interfere with the ability to learn. The impairment is not related to a global deficiency of intelligence
an abnormally increased muscular tone th: An abnormally increased muscular tone that causes fixed abnormal postures. There is a slow, intermittent twisting motion that leads to exaggerated turning and posture of the extremities and trunk
a form of hydrocephalus in which there i: A form of hydrocephalus in which there is no visible obstruction to the flow of the cerebrospinal fluid between the ventricles and subarachnoid space
very brief, involuntary random muscular: Very brief, involuntary random muscular contractions occurring at rest, in response to sensory stimuli, or accompanying voluntary movements
an unintentional, oscillating to-and-fro: An unintentional, oscillating to-and-fro muscle movement about a joint axis
a partial failure of the development of: A partial failure of the development of the corpus callosum
lissencephaly (lis, which subsumes the t: Lissencephaly (LIS, which subsumes the terms agyria and pachygyria), together with subcortical-band heterotopia (SBH) comprises a spectrum of malformations of cortical development caused by insufficient neuronal migration. The key features of LIS are an abnormally thick cortex with reduced or absent formation of the cerebral convolutions, while SBH consists of abnormal bands of neurons beneath a normal cortex, although the cerebral gyri may be separated by unusually shallow sulci
increase in amplitude of a long-loop res: Increase in amplitude of a long-loop response upon somatosensory evoked potential testing, representing an electrically evoked myoclonic response
hemorrhage into the parenchyma of the br: Hemorrhage into the parenchyma of the brain
damage to cerebral nuclei caused in infa: Damage to cerebral nuclei caused in infants by highly increased levels of unconjugated bilirubin. The basal ganglia and brainstem nuclei could be shown to have a yellow staining historically in infants who died of kernicterus, that is, kernicterus is strictly speaking a pathological diagnosis. The presence of kernicterus may be inferred in infants with characteristic acute or chronic bilirubin-induced neurological dysfunction
complete lack of development of speech a: Complete lack of development of speech and language abilities
hyperreflexia is the presence of hyperac: Hyperreflexia is the presence of hyperactive stretch reflexes of the muscles
tendon reflexes that are noticably more: Tendon reflexes that are noticably more active than usual (conventionally denoted 3+ on clinical examnation). Brisk reflexes may or may not indicate a neurological lesion. They are distinguished from hyperreflexia by the fact that hyerreflexia is characterized by hyperactive repeating (clonic) reflexes, which are considered to be always abnormal
facial diplegia refers to bilateral faci: Facial diplegia refers to bilateral facial palsy (bilateral facial palsy is much rarer than unilateral facial palsy)
abnormal coordination of muscles involve: Abnormal coordination of muscles involved in speech
jerk-locked averaging (jla) is used to r: Jerk-locked averaging (JLA) is used to record the timing and distribution of brain activity preceding brisk involuntary movements such as those observed in patients with myoclonus. JLA is capable of revealing a premyoclonus spike in the absence of paroxysmal activity in the routine EEG
enlargement of all or parts of the cereb: Enlargement of all or parts of the cerebral hemispheres
asymmetric head shape, which is usually: Asymmetric head shape, which is usually a combination of unilateral occipital flattening with ipsilateral frontal prominence, leading to rhomboid cranial shape
holoprosencephaly is a structural anomal: Holoprosencephaly is a structural anomaly of the brain in which the developing forebrain fails to divide into two separate hemispheres and ventricles
head movements associated with nystagmus: Head movements associated with nystagmus, that may represent an attempt to compensate for the involuntary eye movements and to improve vision
a localized defect in the bone of the sk: A localized defect in the bone of the skull resulting from abnormal embryological development. The defect is covered by normal skin. In some cases, skull x-rays have shown underlying lytic bone lesions which have closed before the age of one year
craniosynostosis refers to the premature: Craniosynostosis refers to the premature closure of the cranial sutures. Primary craniosynostosis refers to the closure of one or more sutures due to abnormalities in skull development, and secondary craniosynostosis results from failure of brain growth
an abnormal structure or form of the joi: An abnormal structure or form of the joints, i.e., one or more of the articulations where two bones join
inflammatory changes in the synovial mem: Inflammatory changes in the synovial membranes and articular structures with widespread fibrinoid degeneration of the collagen fibers in mesenchymal tissues, as well as atrophy and rarefaction of bony structures
a flexion contracture is a bent (flexed): A flexion contracture is a bent (flexed) joint that cannot be straightened actively or passively. It is thus a chronic loss of joint motion due to structural changes in muscle, tendons, ligaments, or skin that prevents normal movement of joints
a reduction in the freedom of movement o: A reduction in the freedom of movement of one or more joints
limited ability to straighten the arm at: Limited ability to straighten the arm at the elbow joint
the ability of a joint to move beyond it: The ability of a joint to move beyond its normal range of motion
an abnormality of the hip joint: An abnormality of the hip joint
the presence of developmental dysplasia: The presence of developmental dysplasia of the hip
joint stiffness is a perceived sensation: Joint stiffness is a perceived sensation of tightness in a joint or joints when attempting to move them after a period of inactivity. Joint stiffness typically subsides over time
lack of stability of a joint: Lack of stability of a joint
a chronic disorder of the liver in which: A chronic disorder of the liver in which liver tissue becomes scarred and is partially replaced by regenerative nodules and fibrotic tissue resulting in loss of liver function
the presence of excessive fibrous connec: The presence of excessive fibrous connective tissue in the liver. Fibrosis is a reparative or reactive process
impairment of bile flow due to obstructi: Impairment of bile flow due to obstruction in bile ducts
the presence of steatosis in the liver: The presence of steatosis in the liver
a kind of neoplasm of the liver that ori: A kind of neoplasm of the liver that originates in hepatocytes and presents macroscopically as a soft and hemorrhagic tan mass in the liver
a form of hepatic steatosis characterize: A form of hepatic steatosis characterized by the presence of large, lipid-laden vesicles in the affected hepatocytes
the presence of fibrosis affecting the i: The presence of fibrosis affecting the interlobular stroma of liver
impairment of bile flow due to obstructi: Impairment of bile flow due to obstruction in the small bile ducts within the liver
proliferative changes of the bile ducts: Proliferative changes of the bile ducts
increased pressure in the portal vein: Increased pressure in the portal vein
reduced ability of the liver to perform: Reduced ability of the liver to perform its functions
inflammation of the liver due to infecti: Inflammation of the liver due to infection with enterovirus
a type of cirrhosis characterized by the: A type of cirrhosis characterized by the presence of small regenerative nodules
a form of hepatic steatosis characterize: A form of hepatic steatosis characterized by the presence of small, lipid-laden vesicles in the affected hepatocytes
a mode of inheritance that is observed f: A mode of inheritance that is observed for traits related to a gene encoded on the X chromosome

A mode of inheritance that is observed for recessive traits related to a gene encoded on the X chromosome. In the context of medical genetics, X-linked recessive disorders manifest in males (who have one copy of the X chromosome and are thus hemizygotes), but generally not in female heterozygotes who have one mutant and one normal allele

A mode of inheritance that is observed for dominant traits related to a gene encoded on the X chromosome. In the context of medical genetics, X-linked dominant disorders tend to manifest very severely in affected males. The severity of manifestation in females may depend on the degree of skewed X inactivation
a mode of inheritance that depends on a: A mode of inheritance that depends on a mixture of major and minor genetic determinants possibly together with environmental factors. Diseases inherited in this manner are termed complex diseases
a mode of inheritance that is observed f: A mode of inheritance that is observed for traits related to a gene encoded on the mitochondrial genome. Because the mitochondrial genome is essentially always maternally inherited, a mitochondrial condition can only be transmitted by females, although the condition can affect both sexes. The proportion of mutant mitochondria can vary (heteroplasmy)
a mode of inheritance in which a trait o: A mode of inheritance in which a trait or disorder results from a de novo mutation occurring after conception, rather than being inherited from a preceding generation
simultaneous enlargement of the liver an: Simultaneous enlargement of the liver and spleen
an anomaly of the musculature of foot: An anomaly of the musculature of foot
a structural abnormality of the abdomen: A structural abnormality of the abdomen ('belly'), that is, the part of the body between the pelvis and the thorax
the presence of genetically distinct pop: The presence of genetically distinct populations of somatic cells in a given organism caused by DNA mutations, epigenetic alterations of DNA, chromosomal abnormalities or the spontaneous reversion of inherited mutations
being related to a de novo variant that: Being related to a de novo variant that occurs in a single cell in developing somatic tissue. The cell is the progenitor of a population of identical mutant cells, all of which have descended from the cell that mutated. Clinical manifestations depend on the identity and proportion of affected cells in the body
a mode of inheritance that is observed f: A mode of inheritance that is observed for traits related to a gene encoded on the Y chromosome
syndactyly with fusion of toes one to th: Syndactyly with fusion of toes one to three
absence or underdevelopment of the muscu: Absence or underdevelopment of the musculature
absence or underdevelopment of the muscl: Absence or underdevelopment of the muscles of the shoulder
absence or underdevelopment of the muscu: Absence or underdevelopment of the musculature of the upper limbs
absence or underdevelopment of the muscl: Absence or underdevelopment of the muscles of the upper arm
increased density of the bony tissue of: Increased density of the bony tissue of the scapula
a delay in closure (ossification) of the: A delay in closure (ossification) of the anterior fontanelle, which generally undergoes closure around the 18th month of life
a tendency to hold the chin elevated by: A tendency to hold the chin elevated by about 20 to 30 degrees to compensate for a limitation of eye movement
the presence of an increased number of f: The presence of an increased number of freckles, small circular spots on the skin that are darker than the surrounding skin because of deposits of melanin
slightly elevated lesions on or in the s: Slightly elevated lesions on or in the skin with a diameter of over 5 mm
repetitive pressing, poking, and/or rubb: Repetitive pressing, poking, and/or rubbing in the eyes
separation of the vitreous humor from th: Separation of the vitreous humor from the retina
congenital non-progressive ophthalmopleg: Congenital non-progressive ophthalmoplegia with multiple extraocular muscle restrictions. Typically, there is ptosis and variable degrees of restriction of horizontal and vertical eye movements
axenfeld's anomaly is a bilateral disord: Axenfeld's anomaly is a bilateral disorder characterized by a prominent, anteriorly displaced Schwalbe's line (posterior embryotoxon) and peripheral iris strands which span the anterior chamber angle to attach to Schwalbe's line
an area of the retina that is buckled so: An area of the retina that is buckled so that a sector-shaped sheet of retina lies in front of the normal retina. This feature is of congenital onset
underdevelopment of one or more carpal b: Underdevelopment of one or more carpal bones
increased width of a non-thumb digit of: Increased width of a non-thumb digit of the hand
failure to thrive (ftt) refers to a chil: Failure to thrive (FTT) refers to a child whose physical growth is substantially below the norm
a deficiency or slowing down of growth p: A deficiency or slowing down of growth pre- and postnatally
an abnormal restriction of fetal growth: An abnormal restriction of fetal growth with fetal weight below the tenth percentile for gestational age
accumulation of substantial excess body: Accumulation of substantial excess body fat
smaller than normal size according to se: Smaller than normal size according to sex and gestational age related norms, defined as a weight below the 10th percentile for the gestational age
a tall and slim body build with increase: A tall and slim body build with increased arm span to height ratio (>1.05) and a reduced upper-to-lower segment ratio (<0.85), i.e., unusually long arms and legs. The extremities as well as the hands and feet are unusually slim
the term large for gestational age appli: The term large for gestational age applies to babies whose birth weight lies above the 90th percentile for that gestational age
death within the first 24 months of life: Death within the first 24 months of life
overgrowth of only one side of the body: Overgrowth of only one side of the body
a mild degree of slow or limited growth: A mild degree of slow or limited growth after birth, being between two and three standard deviations below age- and sex-related norms
asthenic habitus refers to a slender bui: Asthenic habitus refers to a slender build with long limbs, an angular profile, and prominent muscles or bones
protrusion of abdominal contents through: Protrusion of abdominal contents through a defect in the abdominal wall musculature around the umbilicus. Skin and subcutaneous tissue overlie the defect
a thrusting or bulging out of the abdome: A thrusting or bulging out of the abdomen
a midline anterior incomplete closure of: A midline anterior incomplete closure of the abdominal wall in which there is herniation of the abdominal viscera into the base of the abdominal cord
a separation of the rectus abdominis mus: A separation of the rectus abdominis muscle into right and left halves (which are normally joined at the midline at the linea alba)
accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal: Accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity
a type of congenital ventral incomplete: A type of congenital ventral incomplete closure of the abdominal wall in which the intestines and sometimes other organs extend freely into the amniotic fluid space through a small opening in the abdomen, usually to the right of the umbilicus
a morphological anomaly of the rib cage: A morphological anomaly of the rib cage
excessive postnatal growth which may com: Excessive postnatal growth which may comprise increased weight, increased length, and/or increased head circumference
an abnormality of the structure or appea: An abnormality of the structure or appearance of the umbilicus
a rounded, bulging chest that resembles: A rounded, bulging chest that resembles the shape of a barrel. That is, there is an increased anteroposterior diameter and usually some degree of kyphosis
lack of symmetry between the left and ri: Lack of symmetry between the left and right halves of the thorax
an abnormal reduction in quantity or str: An abnormal reduction in quantity or strength of fetal movements
abnormality of the amniotic fluid, which: Abnormality of the amniotic fluid, which is the fluid contained in the amniotic sac surrounding the developing fetus
the presence of excess amniotic fluid in: The presence of excess amniotic fluid in the uterus during pregnancy
diminished amniotic fluid volume in preg: Diminished amniotic fluid volume in pregnancy
abnormally increased production of urine: Abnormally increased production of urine by the fetus resulting in polyhydramnios
increased distance between the maxillary: Increased distance between the maxillary central permanent incisor tooth
the presence of multiple impacted teeth: The presence of multiple impacted teeth
increased size of the teeth, which can b: Increased size of the teeth, which can be defined as a mesiodistal tooth diameter (width) more than 2 SD above mean for age. Alternatively, an apparently increased maximum width of the tooth
an abnormality of the integument, which: An abnormality of the integument, which consists of the skin and the superficial fascia
hypercortisolemia associated with a prim: Hypercortisolemia associated with a primary defect of the adrenal gland leading to overproduction of cortisol
infections of the skin that happen multi: Infections of the skin that happen multiple times
loose and sagging skin often associated: Loose and sagging skin often associated with loss of skin elasticity
a form of nystagmus in which the eyeball: A form of nystagmus in which the eyeball makes rotary motions around the axis
the presence of a fistula connecting the: The presence of a fistula connecting the urinary bladder to the vagina
the rib cage has the shape of a wide mou: The rib cage has the shape of a wide mouthed bell. That is, the superior portion of the rib cage is constricted, follwed by a convex region, and the inferior portion of the rib cage expands again to have a large diameter
agenesis specifically affecting one of t: Agenesis specifically affecting one of the classes incisor, premolar, or molar
decreased size of the maxillary permanen: Decreased size of the maxillary permanent incisor
loss of hair from the head or body: Loss of hair from the head or body
the natural longitudinal (posterodistal): The natural longitudinal (posterodistal) convex arch is not present or is inverted
laryngomalacia is a congenital abnormali: Laryngomalacia is a congenital abnormality of the laryngeal cartilage in which the cartilage is floppy and prolapses over the larynx during inspiration
stricture or narrowing of the larynx tha: Stricture or narrowing of the larynx that may be associated with symptoms of respiratory difficulty depending on the degree of laryngeal narrowing
decreased strength of the vocal folds: Decreased strength of the vocal folds
a loss of the ability to move the vocal: A loss of the ability to move the vocal folds
hoarseness refers to a change in the pit: Hoarseness refers to a change in the pitch or quality of the voice, with the voice sounding weak, very breathy, scratchy, or husky
a type of speech characterized by the pr: A type of speech characterized by the presence of an abnormally increased nasal airflow during speech
an impairment in the ability to produce: An impairment in the ability to produce voice sounds
an abnormal increase in the pitch (frequ: An abnormal increase in the pitch (frequency) of the voice
the birth of a baby of less than 37 week: The birth of a baby of less than 37 weeks of gestational age
a position of the fetus at delivery in w: A position of the fetus at delivery in which the fetus enters the birth canal with the buttocks or feet first
any abnormality of the cardiovascular sy: Any abnormality of the cardiovascular system
any structural anomaly of the heart: Any structural anomaly of the heart
a hole between the two bottom chambers (: A hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart. The defect is centered around the most superior aspect of the ventricular septum
atrial septal defect (asd) is a congenit: Atrial septal defect (ASD) is a congenital abnormality of the interatrial septum that enables blood flow between the left and right atria via the interatrial septum
any structural anomaly of the mitral val: Any structural anomaly of the mitral valve
one or both of the leaflets (cusps) of t: One or both of the leaflets (cusps) of the mitral valve bulges back into the left atrium upon contraction of the left ventricle
the presence of an abnormality of cardia: The presence of an abnormality of cardiac function that is responsible for the failure of the heart to pump blood at a rate that is commensurate with the needs of the tissues or a state in which abnormally elevated filling pressures are required for the heart to do so. Heart failure is frequently related to a defect in myocardial contraction
a congenital cardiac malformation compri: A congenital cardiac malformation comprising pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, ventricular septum defect, and right ventricular hypertrophy. The diagnosis of TOF is made if at least three of the four above mentioned features are present
a structural anomaly of the muscle layer: A structural anomaly of the muscle layer of the heart wall
a myocardial disorder in which the heart: A myocardial disorder in which the heart muscle is structurally and functionally abnormal, in the absence of coronary artery disease, hypertension, valvular disease and congenital heart disease sufficient to cause the observed myocardial abnormality
hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (hcm) is def: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is defined by the presence of increased ventricular wall thickness or mass in the absence of loading conditions (hypertension, valve disease) sufficient to cause the observed abnormality
any structural abnormality of the pulmon: Any structural abnormality of the pulmonary valve
a narrowing of the right ventricular out: A narrowing of the right ventricular outflow tract that can occur at the pulmonary valve (valvular stenosis) or just below the pulmonary valve (infundibular stenosis)
in utero, the ductus arteriosus (da) ser: In utero, the ductus arteriosus (DA) serves to divert ventricular output away from the lungs and toward the placenta by connecting the main pulmonary artery to the descending aorta. A patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) in the first 3 days of life is a physiologic shunt in healthy term and preterm newborn infants, and normally is substantially closed within about 24 hours after bith and completely closed after about three weeks. Failure of physiologcal closure is referred to a persistent or patent ductus arteriosus (PDA). Depending on the degree of left-to-right shunting, PDA can have clinical consequences
dilated cardiomyopathy (dcm) is defined: Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) is defined by the presence of left ventricular dilatation and left ventricular systolic dysfunction in the absence of abnormal loading conditions (hypertension, valve disease) or coronary artery disease sufficient to cause global systolic impairment. Right ventricular dilation and dysfunction may be present but are not necessary for the diagnosis
the heart suddenly and unexpectedly stop: The heart suddenly and unexpectedly stops beating resulting in death within a short time period (generally within 1 h of symptom onset)
any abnormality of the aortic valve: Any abnormality of the aortic valve
the presence of an aortic valve with two: The presence of an aortic valve with two instead of the normal three cusps (flaps). Bicuspid aortic valvue is a malformation of a commissure (small space between the attachment of each cusp to the aortic wall) and the adjacent parts of the two corresponding cusps forming a raphe (the fused area of the two underdeveloped cusps turning into a malformed commissure between both cusps; the raphe is a fibrous ridge that extends from the commissure to the free edge of the two underdeveloped, conjoint cusps)
right-sided heart failure resulting from: Right-sided heart failure resulting from chronic hypertension in the pulmonary arteries and right ventricle
a rapid heartrate that exceeds the range: A rapid heartrate that exceeds the range of the normal resting heartrate for age
the presence of a stenosis (narrowing) o: The presence of a stenosis (narrowing) of the aortic valve
the heart is located in the right hand s: The heart is located in the right hand sided hemithorax. That is, there is a left-right reversal (or "mirror reflection") of the anatomical location of the heart in which the heart is locate on the right side instead of the left
an abnormality of the mitral valve chara: An abnormality of the mitral valve characterized by insufficiency or incompetence of the mitral valve resulting in retrograde leaking of blood through the mitral valve upon ventricular contraction
any structural abnormality of a cardiac: Any structural abnormality of a cardiac valve
failure of the foramen ovale to seal pos: Failure of the foramen ovale to seal postnatally, leaving a potential conduit between the left and right cardiac atria
increased time between the start of the: Increased time between the start of the Q wave and the end of the T wave as measured by the electrocardiogram (EKG)
necrosis of the myocardium caused by an: Necrosis of the myocardium caused by an obstruction of the blood supply to the heart and often associated with chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, and anxiety as well as characteristic EKG findings and elevation of serum markers including creatine kinase-MB fraction and troponin
an insufficiency of the aortic valve, le: An insufficiency of the aortic valve, leading to regurgitation (backward flow) of blood from the aorta into the left ventricle
a single arterial trunk arises from the: A single arterial trunk arises from the cardiac mass. The pulmonary arteries, aorta and coronary arteries arise from this single trunk with no evidence of another outflow tract
a slower than normal heart rate (in adul: A slower than normal heart rate (in adults, slower than 60 beats per minute)
uncontrolled contractions of muscles fib: Uncontrolled contractions of muscles fibers in the left ventricle not producing contraction of the left ventricle. Ventricular fibrillation usually begins with a ventricular premature contraction and a short run of rapid ventricular tachycardia degenerating into uncoordinating ventricular fibrillations
a type of ventricular tachycardia charac: A type of ventricular tachycardia characterized by polymorphioc QRS complexes that change in amplitue and cycle length, and thus have the appearance of oscillating around the baseline in the EKG
in this case the right ventricle is more: In this case the right ventricle is more muscular than normal, causing a characteristic boot-shaped (coeur-en-sabot) appearance as seen on anterior- posterior chest x-rays. Right ventricular hypertrophy is commonly associated with any form of right ventricular outflow obstruction or pulmonary hypertension, which may in turn owe its origin to left-sided disease. The echocardiographic signs are thickening of the anterior right ventricular wall and the septum. Cavity size is usually normal, or slightly enlarged. In many cases there is associated volume overload present due to tricuspid regurgitation, in the absence of this, septal motion is normal
a complex congenital heart defect in whi: A complex congenital heart defect in which the aorta arises from the morphologic right ventricle and the pulmonary artery arises from the morphologic left ventricle
hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with an asym: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with an asymmetrical pattern of hypertrophy, with a predilection for the interventricular septum and myocyte disarray
an anomaly of the intra-atrial or intrav: An anomaly of the intra-atrial or intraventricular septum
a congenital heart defect characteizred: A congenital heart defect characteizred by a specific combination of heart defects with a common atrioventricular valve, primum atrial septal defect and inlet ventricular septal defect
reduction of the diameter of the coronar: Reduction of the diameter of the coronary arteries as the result of an accumulation of atheromatous plaques within the walls of the coronary arteries, which increases the risk of myocardial ischemia
delayed or lack of conduction of atrial: Delayed or lack of conduction of atrial depolarizations through the atrioventricular node to the ventricles
coarctation of the aorta is a narrowing: Coarctation of the aorta is a narrowing or constriction of a segment of the aorta
paroxysmal chest pain that occurs with e: Paroxysmal chest pain that occurs with exertion or stress and is related to myocardial ischemia
a fixed form of obstruction to blood flo: A fixed form of obstruction to blood flow across the left-ventricular outflow tract related to stenosis (narrowing) below the level of the aortic valve
congenital malformation of the ventral w: Congenital malformation of the ventral wall with partial or total evisceration of the heart outside the thoracic cavity and through the defect in the ventral wall
a kind of atrial septum defect arising f: A kind of atrial septum defect arising from an enlarged foramen ovale, inadequate growth of the septum secundum, or excessive absorption of the septum primum
myocardial fibrosis is characterized by: Myocardial fibrosis is characterized by dysregulated collagen turnover (increased synthesis predominates over unchanged or decreased degradation) and excessive diffuse collagen accumulation in the interstitial and perivascular spaces as well as by phenotypically transformed fibroblasts, termed myofibroblasts
bradycardia related to a mean resting si: Bradycardia related to a mean resting sinus rate of less than 50 beats per minute
a type of subvalvular aortic stenosis re: A type of subvalvular aortic stenosis resulting from thickening of the musculature of the interventricular septum, which results in obstruction to blood flow through the left-ventricular outflow tract
a type of supraventricular tachycardia i: A type of supraventricular tachycardia in which the atria are the principal site of electrophysiologic disturbance
pattern of blood flow in the heart that: Pattern of blood flow in the heart that deviates from the normal circuit of the circulatory system
Pattern of blood flow in the heart that deviates from the normal circuit of the circulatory system from the right side of the heart to the left
an abrupt loss of heart function: An abrupt loss of heart function
a left-right reversal (or \"mirror refle: A left-right reversal (or "mirror reflection") of the anatomical location of the major thoracic and abdominal organs
an abnormality of the pericardium, i.e.,: An abnormality of the pericardium, i.e., of the fluid filled sac that surrounds the heart and the proximal ends of the aorta, vena cava, and the pulmonary artery
accumulation of fluid within the pericar: Accumulation of fluid within the pericardium
irreversible damage to heart tissue (myo: Irreversible damage to heart tissue (myocardium) due to lack of oxygen after a heart attack (myocardial infarction)
