inflammation of the sac-like covering ar: Inflammation of the sac-like covering around the heart (pericardium)
any structural anomaly of the tricuspid: Any structural anomaly of the tricuspid valve
one or more of the leaflets (cusps) of t: One or more of the leaflets (cusps) of the tricuspid valve bulges back into the right atrium upon contraction of the right ventricle
an obstruction to the forward flow of bl: An obstruction to the forward flow of blood in the outflow tract of the right ventricle
reduced ability of the right ventricle t: Reduced ability of the right ventricle to perform its function (to receive blood from the right atrium and to eject blood into the pulmonary artery), often leading top pitting peripheral edema, ascites, and hepatomegaly
third-degree atrioventricular (av) block: Third-degree atrioventricular (AV) block (also referred to as complete heart block) is the complete dissociation of the atria and the ventricles. Third-degree AV block exists when more P waves than QRS complexes exist and no relationship (no conduction) exists between them
a congenital malformation of the outflow: A congenital malformation of the outflow tract of the heart. Conotruncal defects are thought to result from a disturbance of the outflow tract of the embryonic heart, and comprise truncus arteriosus, tetralogy of Fallot, interrupted aortic arch, transposition of the great arteries, and double outlet right ventricle
any structural abnormality of the left v: Any structural abnormality of the left ventricle of the heart
enlargement or increased size of the hea: Enlargement or increased size of the heart left ventricle
an abnormality of a cardiac ventricle: An abnormality of a cardiac ventricle
enlargement of the cardiac ventricular m: Enlargement of the cardiac ventricular muscle tissue with increase in the width of the wall of the ventricle and loss of elasticity. Ventricular hypertrophy is clinically differentiated into left and right ventricular hypertrophy
a disorder of the cardiac conduction sys: A disorder of the cardiac conduction system of the heart characterized by ventricular preexcitation due to the presence of an abnormal accessory atrioventricular electrical conduction pathway
an accumulation of calcium and phosphate: An accumulation of calcium and phosphate in arteries with mineral deposits in the intimal or medial layer of the vessel wall in a coronary artery
an abnormal narrowing of the orifice of: An abnormal narrowing of the orifice of the mitral valve
double outlet right ventricle (dorv) is: Double outlet right ventricle (DORV) is a type of ventriculoarterial connection in which both great vessels arise entirely or predominantly from the right ventricle
a form of heart failure characterized by: A form of heart failure characterized by elevated cardiac output. This may be seen in patients with heart failure and hyperthyroidism, anemia, pregnancy, arteriovenous fistulae, and others
restrictive left ventricular physiology: Restrictive left ventricular physiology is characterized by a pattern of ventricular filling in which increased stiffness of the myocardium causes ventricular pressure to rise precipitously with only small increases in volume, defined as restrictive ventricular physiology in the presence of normal or reduced diastolic volumes (of one or both ventricles), normal or reduced systolic volumes, and normal ventricular wall thickness
a cerebrovascular accident (stroke) that: A cerebrovascular accident (stroke) that occurs because of thromboembolism
a progressive form of hearing impairment: A progressive form of hearing impairment
the presence of inflammation in the panc: The presence of inflammation in the pancreas
a congenital anomaly in which the pancre: A congenital anomaly in which the pancreas completely (or sometimes incompletely) encircles the second portion of duodenum and occasionally obstructs the more proximal duodenum
a cyst of the pancreas that possess a li: A cyst of the pancreas that possess a lining of mucous epithelium
impaired function of the exocrine pancre: Impaired function of the exocrine pancreas associated with a reduced ability to digest foods because of lack of digestive enzymes
reduced ability to pass air through the: Reduced ability to pass air through the nasal cavity often leading to mouth breathing
abnormal increased size of the spleen: Abnormal increased size of the spleen
an accessory spleen is a round, iso-echo: An accessory spleen is a round, iso-echogenic, homogenic and smooth structure and is seen as a normal variant mostly on the medial contour of the spleen, near the hilus or around the lower pole. This has no pathogenic relevance
polysplenia is a congenital disease mani: Polysplenia is a congenital disease manifested by multiple small accessory spleens
the presence of only one working lower c: The presence of only one working lower chamber in the heart, usually with a virtual absence of the ventricular septum and usually present in conjunction with double inlet left or right ventricle
an abnormality of the functioning of the: An abnormality of the functioning of the vestibular apparatus
reduced functioning of the vestibular ap: Reduced functioning of the vestibular apparatus
a form of sensorineural hearing impairme: A form of sensorineural hearing impairment that affects primarily the higher frequencies
an abnormality of the skeleton of foot: An abnormality of the skeleton of foot
the presence of an unusually high planta: The presence of an unusually high plantar arch. Also called high instep, pes cavus refers to a distinctly hollow form of the sole of the foot when it is bearing weight
talipes equinovarus (also called clubfoo: Talipes equinovarus (also called clubfoot) typically has four main components: inversion and adduction of the forefoot; inversion of the heel and hindfoot; equinus (limitation of extension) of the ankle and subtalar joint; and internal rotation of the leg
a foot where the longitudinal arch of th: A foot where the longitudinal arch of the foot is in contact with the ground or floor when the individual is standing; or, in a patient lying supine, a foot where the arch is in contact with the surface of a flat board pressed against the sole of the foot by the examiner with a pressure similar to that expected from weight bearing; or, the height of the arch is reduced
hyperextension of the metatarsal-phalang: Hyperextension of the metatarsal-phalangeal joint with hyperflexion of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint
a foot for which the measured width is a: A foot for which the measured width is above the 95th centile for age; or, a foot that appears disproportionately wide for its length
webbing or fusion of the toes, involving: Webbing or fusion of the toes, involving soft parts only or including bone structure. Bony fusions are referred to as "bony" Syndactyly if the fusion occurs in a radio-ulnar axis. Fusions of bones of the toes in a proximo-distal axis are referred to as "Symphalangism"
a contracture of the achilles tendon: A contracture of the Achilles tendon
a deformity of foot and ankle in which t: A deformity of foot and ankle in which the foot is bent down and outwards
a measured foot length that is more than: A measured foot length that is more than 2 SD below the mean for a newborn of 27 - 41 weeks gestation, or foot that is less than the 3rd centile for individuals from birth to 16 years of age (objective). Alternatively, a foot that appears disproportionately short (subjective)
an abnormality of the morphology of the: An abnormality of the morphology of the toes, such that the tips of the toes are prominent and bulbous
increased side-to-side width of a metata: Increased side-to-side width of a metatarsal bone
a foot for which the measured width is b: A foot for which the measured width is below the 5th centile for age; or, a foot that appears disproportionately narrow for its length
an abnormality of the birth process: An abnormality of the birth process
premature rupture of membranes (prom) is: Premature rupture of membranes (PROM) is a condition which occurs in pregnancy when the amniotic sac ruptures more than an hour before the onset of labor
the abnormal accumulation of fluid in tw: The abnormal accumulation of fluid in two or more fetal compartments, including ascites, pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, and skin edema
a type of hydrops fetalis in which there: A type of hydrops fetalis in which there is no identifiable circulating antibody to red blood cell antigens
accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal: Accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity during the fetal period
a nail that is diminished in length and: A nail that is diminished in length and width, i.e., underdeveloped nail
when viewed on end (with the digit tip p: When viewed on end (with the digit tip pointing toward the examiner's eye) the curve of the nail forms a tighter curve of convexity
small (typically about 1 mm or less in s: Small (typically about 1 mm or less in size) depressions on the dorsal nail surface
nail that appears thick when viewed on e: Nail that appears thick when viewed on end
detachment of the nail from the nail bed: Detachment of the nail from the nail bed
longitudinal, linear prominences in the: Longitudinal, linear prominences in the nail plate
a nail plate that has a longitudinal sep: A nail plate that has a longitudinal separation and the two sections of the nail share the same lateral radius of curvature
toenail changes apart from changes of th: Toenail changes apart from changes of the color of the toenail (nail dyschromia) that involve partial or complete disruption of the various keratinous layers of the nail plate
when viewed on end (with the finger tip: When viewed on end (with the finger tip pointing toward the examiner's eye) the curve of the fingernail forms a tighter curve of convexity
nail that appears thin when viewed on en: Nail that appears thin when viewed on end
the nail disease paronychia is an often-: The nail disease paronychia is an often-tender bacterial or fungal hand infection or foot infection where the nail and skin meet at the side or the base of a finger or toenail. The infection can start suddenly (acute paronychia) or gradually (chronic paronychia)
Lateral deviation of the great toe (i.e., in the direction of the little toe)
congenital occlusion of a tube in the ge: Congenital occlusion of a tube in the genital tract
a kind of polydactyly characterized by t: A kind of polydactyly characterized by the presence of a supernumerary toe or toes
polydactyly of the foot most commonly re: Polydactyly of the foot most commonly refers to the presence of six toes on one foot. Postaxial polydactyly affects the lateral ray and the duplication may range from a well-formed articulated digit to a rudimentary digit
a toe that appears disproportionately sh: A toe that appears disproportionately short compared to the foot
abnormalities of the metatarsal bones (i: Abnormalities of the metatarsal bones (i.e. of five tubular bones located between the tarsal bones of the hind- and mid-foot and the phalanges of the toes)
increased back to front length of the fo: Increased back to front length of the foot
camptodactyly is a painless flexion cont: Camptodactyly is a painless flexion contracture of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint that is usually gradually progressive. This term refers to camptodactyly of one or more toes
visible increase in width of the non-hal: Visible increase in width of the non-hallux digit without an increase in the dorso-ventral dimension
the presence of both a prominent heel an: The presence of both a prominent heel and a convex contour of the sole
a condition in which middle parts of the: A condition in which middle parts of the foot (toes and metatarsals) are missing giving a cleft appearance. The severity is very variable ranging from slightly hypoplastic 3rd toe over absent 2nd or 3rd toes as far as oligo- or monodactyl feet
the metatarsals are deviated medially (t: The metatarsals are deviated medially (tibially), that is, the bones in the front half of the foot bend or turn in toward the body
duplication of all or part of the first: Duplication of all or part of the first ray
this term applies for all abnormalities: This term applies for all abnormalities of the big toe, also called hallux
describes a foot digit resting on the do: Describes a foot digit resting on the dorsal surface of an adjacent digit when the foot is at rest
increased length of the big toe: Increased length of the big toe
this is a postural deformity in which th: This is a postural deformity in which the foot is positioned up against the tibia. The heel (calcaneus) is positioned downward (that is, the ankle is flexed upward), and the heel is turned outward (valgus)
a developmental defect resulting in the: A developmental defect resulting in the presence of fewer than the normal number of toes
an abnormality of the tarsus are the clu: An abnormality of the tarsus are the cluster of seven bones in the foot between the tibia and fibula and the metatarsus, including the calcaneus (heel) bone and the talus (ankle) bone
a widely spaced gap between the first to: A widely spaced gap between the first toe (the great toe) and the second toe
gout affecting the metatarsophalangeal j: Gout affecting the Metatarsophalangeal joint of big toe
short distance from the end of the toe t: Short distance from the end of the toe to the most distal interphalangeal crease or distal interphalangeal joint flexion point, i.e., abnormally short distal phalanx of toe
bending or curvature of a toe in the tib: Bending or curvature of a toe in the tibial direction (i.e., towards the big toe)
bending or curvature of a fifth toe in t: Bending or curvature of a fifth toe in the tibial direction (i.e., towards the big toe)
spontaneous detachment of a foot from th: Spontaneous detachment of a foot from the body
the presence of unusually deep creases (: The presence of unusually deep creases (ridges/wrinkles) on the skin of sole of foot
an abnormality of the hematopoietic syst: An abnormality of the hematopoietic system
a reduction in the number of circulating: A reduction in the number of circulating thrombocytes
an abnormally low number of neutrophils: An abnormally low number of neutrophils in the peripheral blood
an abnormal reduction in numbers of all: An abnormal reduction in numbers of all blood cell types (red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets)
any structural abnormality of erythrocyt: Any structural abnormality of erythrocytes (red-blood cells)
a type of anemia caused by premature des: A type of anemia caused by premature destruction of red blood cells (hemolysis)
increased count of eosinophils in the bl: Increased count of eosinophils in the blood
an abnormal decreased number of leukocyt: An abnormal decreased number of leukocytes in the blood
a deformity of foot and ankle that has d: A deformity of foot and ankle that has different subtypes that are talipes equinovarus, talipes equinovalgus, talipes calcaneovarus and talipes calcaneovalgus
talipes calcaneovalgus is a flexible foo: Talipes calcaneovalgus is a flexible foot deformity (as opposed to a rigid congenital vertical talus foot deformity) that can either present as a positional or structural foot deformity depending on severity and/or causality. The axis of calcaneovalgus deformity is in the tibiotalar joint, where the foot is positioned in extreme hyperextension. On inspection, the foot has an "up and out" appearance, with the dorsal forefoot practically touching the anterior aspect of the ankle and lower leg
an infection of bone of the foot: An infection of bone of the foot
a reduced number of lymphocytes in the b: A reduced number of lymphocytes in the blood
anemia characterized by the presence of: Anemia characterized by the presence of erythroblasts that are larger than normal (megaloblasts)
an autoimmune form of hemolytic anemia: An autoimmune form of hemolytic anemia
an abnormal susceptibility to bleeding,: An abnormal susceptibility to bleeding, often referred to as a bleeding diathesis. A bleeding diathesis may be related to vascular, platelet and coagulation defects
increased numbers of platelets in the pe: Increased numbers of platelets in the peripheral blood
a reduced number of reticulocytes in the: A reduced number of reticulocytes in the peripheral blood
a kind of anemia in which the volume of: A kind of anemia in which the volume of the red blood cells is normal
the presence of an increased mass of red: The presence of an increased mass of red blood cells in the circulation
an elevation above the normal ratio of t: An elevation above the normal ratio of the volume of red blood cells to the total volume of blood
polycythemia is diagnosed if the red blo: Polycythemia is diagnosed if the red blood cell count, the hemoglobin level, and the red blood cell volume all exceed the upper limits of normal
giant platelets are larger than 7 microm: Giant platelets are larger than 7 micrometers and usually 10 to 20 micrometers. The term giant platelet is used when the platelet is larger than the size of the average red cell in the field. (Description adapted from College of American Pathologists, Hematology Manual, 1998)
a reduction in erythrocytes volume or he: A reduction in erythrocytes volume or hemoglobin concentration
a type of neutropenia that is observed i: A type of neutropenia that is observed in the presence of granulocyte-specific antibodies
the formation of a blood clot inside a b: The formation of a blood clot inside a blood vessel that subsequently travels through the blood stream from the site where it formed to another location in the body, generally leading to vascular occlusion at the distant site
anemia with varying degrees of erythrocy: Anemia with varying degrees of erythrocytic hypoplasia without leukopenia or thrombocytopenia
a cancer of the blood and bone marrow ch: A cancer of the blood and bone marrow characterized by an abnormal proliferation of leukocytes
any structural abnormality or abnormal c: Any structural abnormality or abnormal count of granulocytes
Any structural abnormality or abnormal count of basophils
an abnormally reduced number of granuloc: An abnormally reduced number of granulocytes in the blood
aplastic anemia is defined as pancytopen: Aplastic anemia is defined as pancytopenia with a hypocellular marrow
a form of amyloidosis that affects the k: A form of amyloidosis that affects the kidney
sudden loss of renal function, as manife: Sudden loss of renal function, as manifested by decreased urine production, and a rise in serum creatinine or blood urea nitrogen concentration (azotemia)
the presence of stenosis of the renal ar: The presence of stenosis of the renal artery
the presence of clear, sharply defined v: The presence of clear, sharply defined vacuoles in the lymphocyte cytoplasm
an elevation in the number of reticulocy: An elevation in the number of reticulocytes (immature erythrocytes) in the peripheral blood circulation
sideroblastic anemia results from a defe: Sideroblastic anemia results from a defect in the incorporation of iron into the heme molecule. A sideroblast is an erythroblast that has stainable deposits of iron in cytoplasm (this can be demonstrated by Prussian blue staining)
acanthocytosis is a type of poikilocytos: Acanthocytosis is a type of poikilocytosis characterized by the presence of spikes on the cell surface. The cells have an irregular shape resembling many-pointed stars
an abnormality of the process of blood c: An abnormality of the process of blood coagulation. That is, altered ability or inability of the blood to clot
decreased activity of coagulation factor: Decreased activity of coagulation factor XI. Factor XI, also known as plasma thromboplastin antecedent, is a serine proteinase that activates factor IX
a type of anemia characterized by an abn: A type of anemia characterized by an abnormally low concentration of hemoglobin in the erythrocytes
this term refers to an abnormally increa: This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to bruising (purpura, petechiae, or ecchymoses)
a kind of anemia in which the volume of: A kind of anemia in which the volume of the red blood cells is reduced
abnormal acid accumulation or depletion: Abnormal acid accumulation or depletion of base
acid accumulation or depletion of base i: Acid accumulation or depletion of base in the body due to buildup of metabolic acids
a decreased concentration of glucose in: A decreased concentration of glucose in the blood
elevated body temperature due to failed: Elevated body temperature due to failed thermoregulation
presence of elevated levels of ketone bo: Presence of elevated levels of ketone bodies in the body
acidosis owing to malfunction of the kid: Acidosis owing to malfunction of the kidney tubules with accumulation of metabolic acids and hyperchloremia, potentially leading to complications including hypokalemia, hypercalcinuria, nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis
depletion of acid or accumulation base i: Depletion of acid or accumulation base in the body fluids
alkalosis due to excess loss of carbon d: Alkalosis due to excess loss of carbon dioxide from the body
glucose intolerance (gi) can be defined: Glucose intolerance (GI) can be defined as dysglycemia that comprises both prediabetes and diabetes. It includes the conditions of impaired fasting glucose (IFG) and impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) and diabetes mellitus (DM)
a type of diabetic metabolic abnormality: A type of diabetic metabolic abnormality with an accumulation of ketone bodies
periodic (episodic or recurrent) bouts o: Periodic (episodic or recurrent) bouts of fever
episodes of fever for which no infectiou: Episodes of fever for which no infectious cause can be identified
obesity located preferentially in the tr: Obesity located preferentially in the trunk of the body as opposed to the extremities
excessive thirst manifested by excessive: Excessive thirst manifested by excessive fluid intake
a sensation that the heart is pounding o: A sensation that the heart is pounding or racing, which is a non-specific sign but may be a manifestation of arrhythmia
a type of hearing impairment prominently: A type of hearing impairment prominently characterized by a difficulty in understanding speech, rather than an inability to hear speech. Poor speech discrimination is a very common symptom of high frequency hearing loss
absence or underdevelopment of the metat: Absence or underdevelopment of the metatarsal bones
any anomaly of the scalp, the skin an su: Any anomaly of the scalp, the skin an subcutaneous tissue of the head on which head hair grows
an abnormality of the mesangium, i.e., o: An abnormality of the mesangium, i.e., of the central part of the renal glomerulus between capillaries
diffuse sclerosis of the mesangium, as m: Diffuse sclerosis of the mesangium, as manifestated by diffuse mesangial matrix expansion
an abnormality that involves the tubules: An abnormality that involves the tubules and interstitial tissue of the kidney
a form of inflammation of the kidney aff: A form of inflammation of the kidney affecting the interstitium of the kidneys surrounding the tubules
a malfunctioning of the spleen in which: A malfunctioning of the spleen in which it prematurely destroys red blood cells
a type of anemia characterized by increa: A type of anemia characterized by increased size of erythrocytes with increased mean corpuscular volume (MCV) and increased mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH)
the presence of thrombocytopenia in comb: The presence of thrombocytopenia in combination with detection of antiplatelet antibodies
an abnormal increase in the number of le: An abnormal increase in the number of leukocytes in the blood
decreased cell membrane concentration of: Decreased cell membrane concentration of glycoprotein IIb-IIIa
an abnormality of coagulation related to: An abnormality of coagulation related to a decreased concentration of antithrombin-III
venous or arterial thrombosis (formation: Venous or arterial thrombosis (formation of blood clots) of spontaneous nature and which cannot be fully explained by acquired risk (e.g. atherosclerosis)
the process of hematopoiesis occurring o: The process of hematopoiesis occurring outside of the bone marrow (in the liver, thymus, and spleen) in the postnatal organisms
abnormal increased number of megaloblast: Abnormal increased number of megaloblasts in the bone marrow
the presence of an abnormal number of fr: The presence of an abnormal number of fragmented red blood cells (schistocytes) in the blood
an abnormality of histiocytes, in which: An abnormality of histiocytes, in which the cells take on a sea blue appearance due to abnormally increased lipid content. Histiocytes are a type of macrophage. Sea-blue histiocytes are typically large macrophages from 20 to 60 micrometers in diameter with a single eccentric nucleus whose cytoplasm if packed with sea-blue or blue-green granules when stained with Wright-Giemsa
a reduction in the expression of cd43 on: A reduction in the expression of CD43 on the cell surface of lymphocytes
a decreased concentration of glucose in: A decreased concentration of glucose in the blood associated with a reduced concentration of ketone bodies
an increased concentration of ammonia in: An increased concentration of ammonia in the blood
recurrent episodes of decreased concentr: Recurrent episodes of decreased concentration of glucose in the blood
decreased fetal activity associated with: Decreased fetal activity associated with multiple joint contractures, facial anomalies and pulmonary hypoplasia. Ultrasound examination may reveal polyhydramnios, ankylosis, scalp edema, and decreased chest movements (reflecting pulmonary hypoplasia)
excretion of non-amino organic acids in: Excretion of non-amino organic acids in urine
acidosis resulting from accumulation of: Acidosis resulting from accumulation of ketone bodies
an inability of the tubules in the kidne: An inability of the tubules in the kidney to reabsorb small molecules, causing increased urinary loss of electrolytes (sodium, potassium, bicarbonate), minerals, glucose, amino acids, and water
recurrent attacks of acute inflammatory: Recurrent attacks of acute inflammatory arthritis of a joint or set of joints caused by elevated levels of uric acid in the blood which crystallize and are deposited in joints, tendons, and surrounding tissues
an abnormal morphology (form) of the fac: An abnormal morphology (form) of the face or its components
reduced distance from the anterior borde: Reduced distance from the anterior border of the naris to the subnasale
accentuated, prominent philtral ridges g: Accentuated, prominent philtral ridges giving rise to an exaggerated groove in the midline between the nasal base and upper vermillion border
a congenital malformation with a cleft (: A congenital malformation with a cleft (gap or opening) in the face
bilateral bulging of the lateral frontal: Bilateral bulging of the lateral frontal bone prominences with relative sparing of the midline
a facial appearance characteristic of a: A facial appearance characteristic of a fetus or neonate due to oligohydramnios experienced in the womb, comprising ocular hypertelorism, low-set ears, receding chin, and flattening of the nose
a structural abnormality of the central: A structural abnormality of the central nervous system
an abnormality of the viscera of the abd: An abnormality of the viscera of the abdomen
forceful ejection of the contents of the: Forceful ejection of the contents of the stomach through the mouth by means of a series of involuntary spasmic contractions
abnormally increased frequency of loose: Abnormally increased frequency of loose or watery bowel movements
a sensation of unease in the stomach tog: A sensation of unease in the stomach together with an urge to vomit
infrequent or difficult evacuation of fe: Infrequent or difficult evacuation of feces
a condition in which the stomach content: A condition in which the stomach contents leak backwards from the stomach into the esophagus through the lower esophageal sphincter
an abnormal narrowing of the pylorus: An abnormal narrowing of the pylorus
congenital absence of the anus, i.e., th: Congenital absence of the anus, i.e., the opening at the bottom end of the intestinal tract
impaired ability to absorb one or more n: Impaired ability to absorb one or more nutrients from the intestine
abnormal narrowing of the anal opening: Abnormal narrowing of the anal opening
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) and perceived to originate in the abdomen
the presence of chronic diarrhea, which: The presence of chronic diarrhea, which is usually taken to mean diarrhea that has persisted for over 4 weeks
a structural abnormality of the esophagu: A structural abnormality of the esophagus
a developmental defect resulting in comp: A developmental defect resulting in complete obliteration of the lumen of the esophagus such that the esophagus ends in a blind pouch rather than connecting to the stomach
an inadequate sucking reflex, resulting: An inadequate sucking reflex, resulting in the difficult of newborns to be breast-fed
an abnormaltiy of the rectum, the final: An abnormaltiy of the rectum, the final segment of the large intestine that stores solid waste until it passes through the anus
protrusion of the rectal mucous membrane: Protrusion of the rectal mucous membrane through the anus
the presence of a hernia in which the up: The presence of a hernia in which the upper part of the stomach, i.e., mainly the gastric cardia protrudes through the diaphragmatic esophageal hiatus
inflammation, or an inflammatory state i: Inflammation, or an inflammatory state in the large intestine
a lack or loss of appetite for food (as: A lack or loss of appetite for food (as a medical condition)
extreme dilation of the submucusoal vein: Extreme dilation of the submucusoal veins in the lower portion of the esophagus
a pathological narrowing of the esophagu: A pathological narrowing of the esophagus that is caused by the development of a ring of scar tissue that constricts the esophageal lumen
a condition in which there is increased: A condition in which there is increased production of gastrin by a gastrin-secreting tumor (usually located in the pancreas, duodenum, or abdominal lymph nodes) that stimulates the gastric mucosa to maximal activity, with consequent gastrointestinal mucosal ulceration
reduced body temperature due to failed t: Reduced body temperature due to failed thermoregulation
the inability to maintain a comfortably: The inability to maintain a comfortably body temperature in warm or hot weather
malignant hyperthermia is characterized: Malignant hyperthermia is characterized by a rapid increase in temperature to 39-42 degrees C in response to inhalational anesthetics such as halothane or to muscle relaxants such as succinylcholine
atrophy of the cortex of the kidney: Atrophy of the cortex of the kidney
a type of renal tubular acidosis charact: A type of renal tubular acidosis characterized by a failure of the proximal tubular cells to reabsorb bicarbonate, leading to urinary bicarbonate wasting and subsequent acidemia
a facial appearance characteristic of my: A facial appearance characteristic of myopathic conditions. The face appears expressionless with sunken cheeks, bilateral ptosis, and inability to elevate the corners of the mouth, due to muscle weakness
atrophy (wasting, decrease in size of ce: Atrophy (wasting, decrease in size of cells or tissue) affecting the cerebrum
an abnormality of the telencephalon, whi: An abnormality of the telencephalon, which is also known as the cerebrum
spasticity (velocity-dependent increase: Spasticity (velocity-dependent increase in tonic stretch reflexes with increased muscle tone and hyperexcitable tendon reflexes) in the muscles of the lower limbs, hips, and pelvis
any structural abnormality of the pyrami: Any structural abnormality of the pyramidal tract, whose chief element, the corticospinal tract, is the only direct connection between the brain and the spinal cord. In addition to the corticospinal tract, the pyramidal system includes the corticobulbar, corticomesencephalic, and corticopontine tracts
continuous involuntary sustained muscle: Continuous involuntary sustained muscle contraction. When an affected muscle is passively stretched, the degree of resistance remains constant regardless of the rate at which the muscle is stretched. This feature helps to distinguish rigidity from muscle spasticity
spasticity is manifested by increased st: Spasticity is manifested by increased stretch reflex which is intensified with movement velocity. This results in excessive and inappropriate muscle activation which can contribute to muscle hypertonia. Spastic gait is characterized by manifestations such as muscle hypertonia, stiff knee, and circumduction of the leg
a type of ataxia characterized by the im: A type of ataxia characterized by the impairment of the ability to coordinate the movements required for normal walking. Gait ataxia is characteirzed by a wide-based staggering gait with a tendency to fall
bradykinesia literally means slow moveme: Bradykinesia literally means slow movement, and is used clinically to denote a slowness in the execution of movement (in contrast to hypokinesia, which is used to refer to slowness in the initiation of movement)
generalized tonic-clonic seizures are ge: Generalized tonic-clonic seizures are generalized seizures with bilateral symmetrical tonic contraction then bilateral clonic contractions of somatic muscles usually associated with autonomic phenomena
a kind of ataxia that affects movements: A kind of ataxia that affects movements of the extremities
a neurological condition related to lesi: A neurological condition related to lesions of the basal ganglia leading to typical abnormalities including akinesia (inability to initiate changes in activity and perform volitional movements rapidly and easily), muscular rigidity (continuous contraction of muscles with constant resistance to passive movement), chorea (widespread arrhythmic movements of a forcible, rapid, jerky, and restless nature), athetosis (inability to sustain the muscles of the fingers, toes, or other group of muscles in a fixed position), and akathisia (inability to remain motionless)
chorea (greek for 'dance') refers to wid: Chorea (Greek for 'dance') refers to widespread arrhythmic involuntary movements of a forcible, jerky and restless fashion. It is a random-appearing sequence of one or more discrete involuntary movements or movement fragments. Movements appear random because of variability in timing, duration or location. Each movement may have a distinct start and end. However, movements may be strung together and thus may appear to flow randomly from one muscle group to another. Chorea can involve the trunk, neck, face, tongue, and extremities
lipofuscin, a generic term applied to au: Lipofuscin, a generic term applied to autofluorescent lipopigment, is a mixture of protein and lipid that accumulates in most aging cells, particularly those involved in high lipid turnover (e.g., the adrenal medulla) or phagocytosis of other cell types (e g., the retinal pigment epithelium or RPE; macrophage). This term pertains if there is an increase in the neuronal accumulation of lipofuscin (also known as autofluorescent lipoprotein) more than expected for the age of the patient
a type of ataxia characterized by the im: A type of ataxia characterized by the impairment of the ability to perform rapidly alternating movements, such as pronating and supinating his or her hand on the dorsum of the other hand as rapidly as possible
migraine is a chronic neurological disor: Migraine is a chronic neurological disorder characterized by episodic attacks of headache and associated symptoms
a type of migraine in which there is an: A type of migraine in which there is an aura characterized by focal neurological phenomena that usually proceed, but may accompany or occur in the absence of, the headache. The symptoms of an aura may include fully reversible visual, sensory, and speech symptoms but not motor weakness. Visual symptoms may include flickering lights, spots and lines and/or loss of vision and/or unilateral sensory symptoms such as paresthesias or numbness. At least one of the symptoms of an aura develops gradually over 5 or more minutes and/or different symptoms occur in succession
truncal ataxia is a sign of ataxia chara: Truncal ataxia is a sign of ataxia characterized by instability of the trunk. It usually occurs during sitting
a type of kinetic tremor that occurs dur: A type of kinetic tremor that occurs during target directed movement is called intention tremor. That is, an oscillatory cerebellar ataxia that tends to be absent when the limbs are inactive and during the first part of voluntary movement but worsening as the movement continues and greater precision is required (e.g., in touching a target such as the patient's nose or a physician's finger)
repeated headache attacks lasting 4-72 h: Repeated headache attacks lasting 4-72 h fulfilling at least two of the following criteria: 1) unilateral location, 2) pulsating quality, 3) moderate or severe pain intensity, and 4) aggravation by or causing avoidance of routine physical activity such as climbing stairs. Headache attacks are commonly accompanied by nausea, vomiting, photophobia, or phonophobia
a neural tube defect characterized by sa: A neural tube defect characterized by sac-like protrusions of the brain and the membranes that cover it through openings in the skull
a type of encephalocele (that is, a a pr: A type of encephalocele (that is, a a protrusion of part of the cranial contents including brain tissue through a congenital opening in the cranium, typically covered with skin or mucous membrane) in the occipital region of the skull. Occipital encephalocele presents as a midline swelling over the occipital bone. It is usually covered with normal full-thickness scalp
an abnormality of the respiratory system: An abnormality of the respiratory system, which include the airways, lungs, and the respiratory muscles
an abnormality of the upper respiratory: An abnormality of the upper respiratory tract
any structural anomaly of the lung: Any structural anomaly of the lung
inflammation of any part of the lung par: Inflammation of any part of the lung parenchyma
a functional defect characterized by red: A functional defect characterized by reduced total lung capacity (TLC) not associated with abnormalities of expiratory airflow or airway resistance. Restrictive lung disease may be caused by alterations in lung parenchyma or because of a disease of the pleura, chest wall, or neuromuscular apparatus
pulmonary hypertension is defined mean p: Pulmonary hypertension is defined mean pulmonary artery pressure of 25mmHg or more and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure of 15mmHg or less when measured by right heart catheterisation at rest and in a supine position
difficulty in breathing. the physical pr: Difficulty in breathing. The physical presentation of respiratory distress is generally referred to as labored breathing, while the sensation of respiratory distress is called shortness of breath or dyspnea
asthma is characterized by increased res: Asthma is characterized by increased responsiveness of the tracheobronchial tree to multiple stimuli, leading to narrowing of the air passages with resultant dyspnea, cough, and wheezing
increased susceptibility to aspiration p: Increased susceptibility to aspiration pneumonia, defined as pneumonia due to breathing in foreign material, as manifested by a medical history of repeated episodes of aspiration pneumonia
defects in the formation of pulmonary lo: Defects in the formation of pulmonary lobules
an abnormality of the pulmonary pleura,: An abnormality of the pulmonary pleura, the thin, transparent membrane which covers the lungs and lines the inside of the chest walls
lack of breathing with no movement of th: Lack of breathing with no movement of the respiratory muscles and no exchange of air in the lungs. This term refers to a disposition to have recurrent episodes of apnea rather than to a single event
coughing up (expectoration) of blood or: Coughing up (expectoration) of blood or blood-streaked sputum from the larynx, trachea, bronchi, or lungs
accumulation of air in the pleural cavit: Accumulation of air in the pleural cavity leading to a partially or completely collapsed lung
pneumothorax occurring without traumatic: Pneumothorax occurring without traumatic injury to the chest or lung
persistent abnormal dilatation of the br: Persistent abnormal dilatation of the bronchi owing to localized and irreversible destruction and widening of the large airways
fev1 (forced expiratory volume in 1 seco: FEV1 (forced expiratory volume in 1 second) and FVC (forced vital capacity) less than 80 per cent
an increase in size of the ventricular s: An increase in size of the ventricular system of the brain
atrophy of the cortex of the cerebrum: Atrophy of the cortex of the cerebrum
absence seizures present with a sudden c: Absence seizures present with a sudden cessation of activity and awareness. Absence seizures tend to occur in younger age groups, have more sudden start and termination, and they usually display less complex automatisms than do focal seizures with impaired awareness, but the distinctions are not absolute
seizures with sudden, brief (< 100 msec): Seizures with sudden, brief (< 100 msec) involuntary single or multiple contraction(s) of muscles(s) or muscle groups of variable topography (axial, proximal limb, distal)
a congenital abnormality of the cerebral: A congenital abnormality of the cerebral hemisphere characterized by an excessive number of small gyri (convolutions) on the surface of the brain
any structural anomaly that affects the: Any structural anomaly that affects the upper motor neuron
periodic spells of incoordination and im: Periodic spells of incoordination and imbalance, that is, episodes of ataxia typically lasting from 10 minutes to several hours or days.n
a cavity within the cerebral hemisphere,: A cavity within the cerebral hemisphere, filled with cerebrospinal fluid, that communicates directly with the ventricular system
seizures lasting for more than 30 minute: Seizures lasting for more than 30 minutes or longer or multiple seizures repeated frequently without regaining consciousness between seizures
the presence of calcium deposition affec: The presence of calcium deposition affecting one or more structures of the basal ganglia
an abnormal gait pattern in which person: An abnormal gait pattern in which persons stand and walk with their feet spaced widely apart. This is often a component of cerebellar ataxia
hemorrhage occurring between the arachno: Hemorrhage occurring between the arachnoid mater and the pia mater
an abnormality of the spinal cord (myelo: An abnormality of the spinal cord (myelon)
during normal embryological development,: During normal embryological development, the spinal cord first occupies the entire length of the vertebral column but goes on to assume a position at the level of L1 due to differential growth of the conus medullaris and the vertebral column. The filum terminale is a slender, threadlike structure that remains after the normal regression of the distal embryonic spinal cord and attaches the spinal cord to the coccyx. A tethered cord results if there is a thickened rope-like filum terminale which anchors the cord at the level of L2 or below, potentially causing neurologic signs owing to abnormal tension on the spinal cord
a dementia associated with degeneration: A dementia associated with degeneration of the frontotemporal lobe and clinically associated with personality and behavioral changes such as disinhibition, apathy, and lack of insight. The hallmark feature of frontotemporal dementia is the presentation with focal syndromes such as progressive language dysfunction, or aphasia, or behavioral changes characteristic of frontal lobe disorders
an abnormally decreased phosphate concen: An abnormally decreased phosphate concentration in the blood
an abnormally high level of uric acid in: An abnormally high level of uric acid in the blood
abnormally increased level of blood lact: Abnormally increased level of blood lactate (2-hydroxypropanoic acid). Lactate is produced from pyruvate by lactate dehydrogenase during normal metabolism. The terms lactate and lactic acid are often used interchangeably but lactate (the component measured in blood) is strictly a weak base whereas lactic acid is the corresponding acid. Lactic acidosis is often used clinically to describe elevated lactate but should be reserved for cases where there is a corresponding acidosis (pH below 7.35)
an increased concentration of proteins i: An increased concentration of proteins in the blood
an abnormally increased potassium concen: An abnormally increased potassium concentration in the blood
an elevated concentration of glycine in: An elevated concentration of glycine in the blood
an abnormal increase in the level of tri: An abnormal increase in the level of triglycerides in the blood
an increased concentration of homocystin: An increased concentration of homocystine in the urine
an increased concentration of nitrogen c: An increased concentration of nitrogen compounds in the blood
an increased concentration of heparan su: An increased concentration of heparan sulfates in the urine
an increased concentration of homocystin: An increased concentration of homocystine in the blood
an increased concentration of lysine in: An increased concentration of lysine in the blood
hair on the neck extends more inferiorly: Hair on the neck extends more inferiorly than usual
the presence of developmental dysplasia: The presence of developmental dysplasia of the nail
inward advance of skin over the nail pla: Inward advance of skin over the nail plate
a decrease in the ability to perceive vi: A decrease in the ability to perceive vibration in the legs
a series of rhythmic and involuntary mus: A series of rhythmic and involuntary muscle contractions (at a frequency of about 5 to 7 Hz) that occur in response to an abruptly applied and sustained stretch
gliosis is the focal proliferation of gl: Gliosis is the focal proliferation of glial cells in the central nervous system
a tendency to fall or the inability to k: A tendency to fall or the inability to keep oneself from falling; imbalance. The retropulsion test is widely regarded as the gold standard to evaluate postural instability, Use of the retropulsion test includes a rapid balance perturbation in the backward direction, and the number of balance correcting steps (or total absence thereof) is used to rate the degree of postural instability. Healthy subjects correct such perturbations with either one or two large steps, or without taking any steps, hinging rapidly at the hips while swinging the arms forward as a counterweight. In patients with balance impairment, balance correcting steps are often too small, forcing patients to take more than two steps. Taking three or more steps is generally considered to be abnormal, and taking more than five steps is regarded as being clearly abnormal. Markedly affected patients continue to step backward without ever regaining their balance and must be caught by the examiner (this would be called true retropulsion). Even more severely affected patients fail to correct entirely, and fall backward like a pushed toy soldier, without taking any corrective steps
a type of tremors that is triggered by h: A type of tremors that is triggered by holding a limb in a fixed position
external mechanical compression of the s: External mechanical compression of the spinal cord
progressive loss of neural cells and tis: Progressive loss of neural cells and tissue
abnormal accumulation of fluid in the br: Abnormal accumulation of fluid in the brain
an abnormally heightened sensitivity to: An abnormally heightened sensitivity to loud sounds
pathological protein aggregates formed b: Pathological protein aggregates formed by hyperphosphorylation of a microtubule-associated protein known as tau, causing it to aggregate in an insoluble form
a defect in the understanding of complex: A defect in the understanding of complex motor commands and in the execution of certain learned movements, i.e., deficits in the cognitive components of learned movements
profound mental retardation is defined a: Profound mental retardation is defined as an intelligence quotient (IQ) below 20
delayed myelination in the central nervo: Delayed myelination in the central nervous system
a cyst occurring within the choroid plex: A cyst occurring within the choroid plexus within a cerebral ventricle
spasticity that increases in degree with: Spasticity that increases in degree with time
individuals with pseudobulbar signs ofte: Individuals with Pseudobulbar signs often also demonstrate abnormal behavioral symptoms such as inappropriate emotional outbursts of uncontrolled laughter or weeping etc
a type of motor delay characterized by a: A type of motor delay characterized by a delay in acquiring the ability to control the large muscles of the body for walking, running, sitting, and crawling
defective development of the vermis of c: Defective development of the vermis of cerebellum
seizures of with initial involvement of: Seizures of with initial involvement of both cerebral hemispheres
an abnormal dilatation of the fourth cer: An abnormal dilatation of the fourth cerebral ventricle
pseudobulbar signs result from injury to: Pseudobulbar signs result from injury to an upper motor neuron lesion to the corticobulbar pathways in the pyramidal tract. Patients have difficulty chewing, swallowing and demonstrate slurred speech (often initial presentation) as well as abnormal behavioral symptoms such as inappropriate emotional outbursts of uncontrolled laughter or weeping etc
the presence of an excessive amount of f: The presence of an excessive amount of fluid in the pleural cavity
inability to move the muscles of respira: Inability to move the muscles of respiration
an embolus (that is, an abnormal particl: An embolus (that is, an abnormal particle circulating in the blood) located in the pulmonary artery and thereby blocking blood circulation to the lung. Usually the embolus is a blood clot that has developed in an extremity (for instance, a deep venous thrombosis), detached, and traveled through the circulation before becoming trapped in the pulmonary artery
an increased susceptibility to respirato: An increased susceptibility to respiratory infections as manifested by a history of recurrent respiratory infections
replacement of normal lung tissues by fi: Replacement of normal lung tissues by fibroblasts and collagen
hair shafts are rough in texture: Hair shafts are rough in texture
decreased number of head hairs per unit: Decreased number of head hairs per unit area
a triangular depigmented region of white: A triangular depigmented region of white hairs located in the anterior midline of the scalp
hair that is fine or thin to the touch: Hair that is fine or thin to the touch
reduced number or density of axillary ha: Reduced number or density of axillary hair
development of gray hair at a younger th: Development of gray hair at a younger than normal age
hair whose growth is slower than normal: Hair whose growth is slower than normal
reduced number or density of pubic hair: Reduced number or density of pubic hair
white color (lack of pigmentation) of th: White color (lack of pigmentation) of the eyebrow
White color (lack of pigmentation) of the eyelashes
loss of round patches of scalp hair: Loss of round patches of scalp hair
abnormally increased hair growth over mu: Abnormally increased hair growth over much of the entire body
loss of scalp hair at an earlier than no: Loss of scalp hair at an earlier than normal age
upward and/or sideward growth of anterio: Upward and/or sideward growth of anterior hair
abnormally increased size of the liver: Abnormally increased size of the liver
an abnormality of the intestine. the clo: An abnormality of the intestine. The closely related term enteropathy is used to refer to any disease of the intestine
abnormal loss of protein from the digest: Abnormal loss of protein from the digestive tract related to excessive leakage of plasma proteins into the lumen of the gastrointestinal tract
an abnormality of the small intestine: An abnormality of the small intestine
meckel's diverticulum is a congenital di: Meckel's diverticulum is a congenital diverticulum located in the distal ileum
an abnormality of the duodenum, i.e., th: An abnormality of the duodenum, i.e., the first section of the small intestine
a developmental defect resulting in comp: A developmental defect resulting in complete obliteration of the duodenal lumen, that is, an abnormal closure of the duodenum
the passage of blackish, tarry feces ass: The passage of blackish, tarry feces associated with gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Melena occurs if the blood remains in the colon long enough for it to be broken down by colonic bacteria. One degradation product, hematin, imbues the stool with a blackish color. Thus, melena generally occurs with bleeding from the upper gastrointestinal tract (e.g., stomach ulcers or duodenal ulcers), since the blood usually remains in the gut for a longer period of time than with lower gastrointestinal bleeding
any abnormality of the large intestine: Any abnormality of the large intestine
an abnormality resulting from a lack of: An abnormality resulting from a lack of intestinal ganglion cells (i.e., an aganglionic section of bowel) that results in bowel obstruction with enlargement of the colon
the presence of multiple diverticula of: The presence of multiple diverticula of the colon
chronic inflammation of the nasal mucosa: Chronic inflammation of the nasal mucosa
more pronounced paramedian peaks and med: More pronounced paramedian peaks and median notch of the Cupid's bow
an exaggerated startle reaction in respo: An exaggerated startle reaction in response to a sudden unexpected visual or acoustic stimulus, or a quick movement near the face
a form of dystonia characterized by epis: A form of dystonia characterized by episodes of dystonia (often hemidystonia or generalized) lasting from minutes to hours. There are no dystonic symptoms between episodes
an abnormality resulting from an anomaly: An abnormality resulting from an anomaly of neuronal migration, i.e., of the process by which neurons travel from their origin to their final position in the brain
an abnormality of the autonomic nervous: An abnormality of the autonomic nervous system
an abnormality resulting from a lesion o: An abnormality resulting from a lesion of the sympathetic nervous system characterized by a combination of unilateral ptosis, miosis, and often ipsilateral hypohidrosis and conjunctival injection
increase in size of the cisterna magna,: Increase in size of the cisterna magna, one of three principal openings in the subarachnoid space between the arachnoid and pia mater, located between the cerebellum and the dorsal surface of the medulla oblongata
gray matter heterotopia is a neurologica: Gray matter heterotopia is a neurological disorder caused by clumps of grey matter being located in the wrong part of the brain. It is characterized as a type of cortical malformation. The neurons in heterotopia may appear to be normal, except for their mislocation; nuclear studies have shown glucose metabolism equal to that of normally positioned gray matter. The condition causes a variety of symptoms, but usually includes some degree of epilepsy or recurring seizures, and often affects the brain's ability to function on higher levels. Symptoms range from nonexistent to profound, in which case heterotopia can result in severe seizure disorder, loss of motor skills, and mental retardation
an anomaly resulting from disturbed neur: An anomaly resulting from disturbed neuronal migration and cortical organization
unlocalized atrophy of the brain with de: Unlocalized atrophy of the brain with decreased total brain matter volume and increased ventricular size
a lesser degree of hair pigmentation tha: A lesser degree of hair pigmentation than would otherwise be expected
loss of all hair on the entire body: Loss of all hair on the entire body
circumscribed depigmentation of the hair: Circumscribed depigmentation of the hair of the head or the eyelashes
absence of hair in the anterior midline: Absence of hair in the anterior midline and/or parietal areas
progressively reduced or lacking hair gr: Progressively reduced or lacking hair growth
fragile, easily breakable hair, i.e., wi: Fragile, easily breakable hair, i.e., with reduced tensile strength
paralysis (complete loss of muscle funct: Paralysis (complete loss of muscle function) in the arm, leg, and in some cases the face on one side of the body
inability to initiate changes in activit: Inability to initiate changes in activity or movement and to perform ordinary volitional movements rapidly and easily
a slow, continuous, involuntary writhing: A slow, continuous, involuntary writhing movement that prevents maintenance of a stable posture. Athetosis involves continuous smooth movements that appear random and are not composed of recognizable sub-movements or movement fragments. In contrast to chorea, in athetosis, the same regions of the body are repeatedly involved. Athetosis may worsen with attempts at movement of posture, but athetosis can also occur at rest
habitual flow of saliva out of the mouth: Habitual flow of saliva out of the mouth
arnold-chiari malformation consists of a: Arnold-Chiari malformation consists of a downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils and the medulla through the foramen magnum, sometimes causing hydrocephalus as a result of obstruction of CSF outflow
lack of physical coordination resulting: Lack of physical coordination resulting in an abnormal tendency to drop items or bump into objects
deterioration of the tissues of the late: Deterioration of the tissues of the lateral corticospinal tracts
cephalgia, or pain sensed in various par: Cephalgia, or pain sensed in various parts of the head, not confined to the area of distribution of any nerve
an abnormal sensation of spinning while: An abnormal sensation of spinning while the body is actually stationary
a resting tremor occurs when muscles are: A resting tremor occurs when muscles are at rest and becomes less noticeable or disappears when the affected muscles are moved. Resting tremors are often slow and coarse
a defect of development of the brain cha: A defect of development of the brain characterized by replacement of greater portions of the cerebral hemispheres and the corpus striatum by cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and glial tissue
attacks of disabling daytime drowsiness: Attacks of disabling daytime drowsiness and low alertness
repeated episodes of headache with rapid: Repeated episodes of headache with rapid onset, reaching a peak within minutes and of short duration (less than one hour) with pain that is throbbing, pulsating, or bursting in quality
loss of previously present motor (i.e.,: Loss of previously present motor (i.e., movement) abilities
an anomaly of the vermis of cerebellum: An anomaly of the vermis of cerebellum
congenital absence of the vermis of cere: Congenital absence of the vermis of cerebellum
any structural abnormality of the caudat: Any structural abnormality of the caudate nucleus
compression of the spinal cord in the ce: Compression of the spinal cord in the cervical region, generally manifested by paresthesias and numbness, weakness, difficulty walking, abnormalities of coordination, and neck pain or stiffness
moderate mental retardation is defined a: Moderate mental retardation is defined as an intelligence quotient (IQ) in the range of 35-49
a form of hydrocephalus characterized by: A form of hydrocephalus characterized by enlarged cerebral ventricles and normal cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) pressure upon lumbar puncture
a tremor present when the limbs are acti: A tremor present when the limbs are active, either when outstretched in a certain position or throughout a voluntary movement
an unintentional, oscillating to-and-fro: An unintentional, oscillating to-and-fro muscle movement affecting head movement
a type of focal-onset seizure in which a: A type of focal-onset seizure in which awareness is preserved. Awareness during a seizure is defined as the patient being fully aware of themself and their environment throughout the seizure, even if immobile
this term describes abnormality of the w: This term describes abnormality of the white matter of the cerebrum resulting from damage to the myelin sheaths of nerve cells
abnormality observed by electroencephalo: Abnormality observed by electroencephalogram (EEG), which is used to record of the brain's spontaneous electrical activity from multiple electrodes placed on the scalp
an impairment of memory as manifested by: An impairment of memory as manifested by a reduced ability to remember things such as dates and names, and increased forgetfulness
a focal dystonia of the fingers, hand, a: A focal dystonia of the fingers, hand, and/or forearm that appears when the affected person attempts to do a task that requires fine motor movements such as writing or playing a musical instrument
an abnormality of sleep including such p: An abnormality of sleep including such phenomena as 1) insomnia/hypersomnia, 2) non-restorative sleep, 3) sleep schedule disorder, 4) excessive daytime somnolence, 5) sleep apnea, and 6) restlessness
loss of previously present mental and mo: Loss of previously present mental and motor abilities
a type of gait (walking) characterized b: A type of gait (walking) characterized by by dragging one's feet along or without lifting the feet fully from the ground
any structural anomaly of the lower moto: Any structural anomaly of the lower motor neuron
lack of observable abnormal electroencep: Lack of observable abnormal electroencephalographic (EEG) patterns in an individual with a history of seizures. About half of individuals with epilepsy show interictal epileptiform discharges upon the first investigation. The yield can be increased by repeated studies, sleep studies, or by ambulatory EEG recordings over 24 hours. Normal interictal EEG is a sign that can be useful in the differential diagnosis
febrile seizures are convulsions induced: Febrile seizures are convulsions induced by a fever in infants or small children and are generally characterized by loss of consciousness and tonic-clonic movements. Most febrile seizures last a minute or two
abnormally diminished motor activity. in: Abnormally diminished motor activity. In contrast to paralysis, hypokinesia is not characterized by a lack of motor strength, but rather by a poverty of movement. The typical habitual movements (e.g., folding the arms, crossing the legs) are reduced in frequency
loss of developmental skills, as manifes: Loss of developmental skills, as manifested by loss of developmental milestones
an unintentional, oscillating to-and-fro: An unintentional, oscillating to-and-fro muscle movement affecting the hand
fasciculations are observed as small, lo: Fasciculations are observed as small, local, involuntary muscle contractions (twitching) visible under the skin. Fasciculations result from increased irritability of an axon (which in turn is often a manifestation of disease of a motor neuron). This leads to sporadic discharges of all the muscle fibers controlled by the axon in isolation from other motor units
an acquired language impairment of some: An acquired language impairment of some or all of the abilities to produce or comprehend speech and to read or write
a type of focal-onset saeizure character: A type of focal-onset saeizure characterized by impaired awareness. Awareness during a seizure is defined as the patient being fully aware of themself and their environment throughout the seizure, even if immobile. If awareness is impaired at any point during the seizure, the seizure is a focal impaired awareness seizure. The degree of loss of awareness may vary
weakness or partial paralysis in the low: Weakness or partial paralysis in the lower limbs
if the two laminae of the septum pelluci: If the two laminae of the septum pellucidum are not fused then a fluid-filled space or cavum is present. The cavum septum pellucidum is present at birth but usually obliterates by the age of 3 to 6 months. It is up to 1cm in width and the walls are parallel. It is an enclosed space and is not part of the ventricular system or connected with the subarachnoid space
the presence of complexes of repetitive: The presence of complexes of repetitive spikes and waves in EEG
a type of rigidity in which a muscle res: A type of rigidity in which a muscle responds with cogwheellike jerks to the use of constant force in bending the limb (i.e., it gives way in little, repeated jerks when the muscle is passively stretched)
no consensus exists on what a stroke-lik: No consensus exists on what a stroke-like episode is, but these episodes can be functionally defined as a new neurological deficit, occurring with or without the context of seizures, which last longer than 24 hours
the patient stands with the feet placed: The patient stands with the feet placed together and balance and is asked to close his or her eyes. A loss of balance upon eye closure is a positive Romberg sign and is interpreted as indicating a deficit in proprioception
increased width of the superior cerebell: Increased width of the superior cerebellar peduncle
a type of dysmetria involving the limbs: A type of dysmetria involving the limbs
an anomalous configuration of blood vess: An anomalous configuration of blood vessels that shunts arterial blood directly into veins without passing through the capillaries and that is located in the brain
stenosis of the cerebral aqueduct (also: Stenosis of the cerebral aqueduct (also known as the mesencephalic duct, aqueductus mesencephali, or aqueduct of Sylvius), which connects the third cerebral ventricle in the diencephalon to the fourth ventricle, which is between the pons and cerebellum
myokymia consists of involuntary, fine,: Myokymia consists of involuntary, fine, continuous, undulating contractions that spread across the affected striated muscle
incomplete closure of the embryonic neur: Incomplete closure of the embryonic neural tube, whereby some vertebral arches remain unfused and open. The mildest form is spina bifida occulta, followed by meningocele and meningomyelocele
leukodystrophy refers to deterioration o: Leukodystrophy refers to deterioration of white matter of the brain resulting from degeneration of myelin sheaths in the CNS. Their basic defect is directly related to the synthesis and maintenance of myelin membranes
cerebral cysts, usually located in the w: Cerebral cysts, usually located in the wall of the caudate nucleus or in the caudothalamic groove. They are found in up to 5.2% of all neonates, using transfontanellar ultrasound in the first days of life
an abnormality of the midbrain, which ha: An abnormality of the midbrain, which has as its parts the tectum, cerebral peduncle, midbrain tegmentum and cerebral aqueduct
an abnormal appearance of the midbrain i: An abnormal appearance of the midbrain in axial magnetic resonance imaging in which the elongated superior cerebellar peduncles give the midbrain an appearance reminiscent of a molar or wisdom tooth
difficulty to maintain correct position: Difficulty to maintain correct position of the head while standing or sitting
a defect in the motor ability that enabl: A defect in the motor ability that enables speech
impairment of expressive language and re: Impairment of expressive language and relative preservation of receptive language abilities. That is, the patient understands language (speech, writing) but cannot express it
protrusion of the meninges through a def: Protrusion of the meninges through a defect of the vertebral column
a herniation of meninges through a conge: A herniation of meninges through a congenital bone defect in the skull in the occipital region
a specific learning disability involving: A specific learning disability involving mathematics and arithmetic
the presence of a hamartoma of the hypot: The presence of a hamartoma of the hypothalamus
paralysis of all four limbs, and trunk o: Paralysis of all four limbs, and trunk of the body below the level of an associated injury to the spinal cord. The etiology of quadriplegia is similar to that of paraplegia except that the lesion is in the cervical spinal cord rather than in the thoracic or lumbar segments of the spinal cord
proliferation of astrocytes in the area: Proliferation of astrocytes in the area of a lesion of the central nervous system
any structural anomaly that affects the: Any structural anomaly that affects the motor neuron
a type of dystonia (abnormally increased: A type of dystonia (abnormally increased muscular tone causing fixed abnormal postures) that affects muscles of the limbs
an abnormality of the globus pallidus: An abnormality of the globus pallidus
the presence, on t2-weighted magnetic re: The presence, on T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging, of markedly low signal intensity if the globus pallidus that surrounds a central region of high signal intensity in the anteromedial globus pallidus, producing an eye-of-the-tiger appearance
reduced strength of the musculature of t: Reduced strength of the musculature of the distal extremities
language impairment is a deficit in comp: Language impairment is a deficit in comprehension or production of language that includes reduced vocabulary, limited sentence structure or impairments in written or spoken communication. Language abilities are substantially and quantifiably below age expectations
a type of dysarthria related to bilatera: A type of dysarthria related to bilateral damage of the upper motor neuron tracts of the pyramidal and extra- pyramidal tracts. Speech of affected individuals is slow, effortful, and has a harsh vocal quality
reduced size of the cerebral cortex: Reduced size of the cerebral cortex
a delay in the acquisition of the abilit: A delay in the acquisition of the ability to use language to communicate needs, wishes, or thoughts
protrusion of the meninges and portions: Protrusion of the meninges and portions of the spinal cord through a defect of the vertebral column
the primitive reflexes are a group of be: The primitive reflexes are a group of behavioural motor responses which are found in normal early development, are subsequently inhibited, but may be released from inhibition by cerebral, usually frontal, damage. They are thus part of a broader group of reflexes which reflect release phenomena, such as exaggerated stretch reflexes and extensor plantars. They do however involve more complex motor responses than such simple stretch reflexes, and are often a normal feature in the neonate or infant
central nervous system dysfunction in as: Central nervous system dysfunction in association with liver failure and characterized clinically (depending on degree of severity) by lethargy, confusion, nystagmus, decorticate posturing, spasticity, and bilateral Babinski reflexes
an involuntary and painless delay in the: An involuntary and painless delay in the relaxation of skeletal muscle following contraction or electrical stimulation
motor hyperactivity with excessive movem: Motor hyperactivity with excessive movement of muscles of the body as a whole
a clonal (malignant) hematopoietic disor: A clonal (malignant) hematopoietic disorder with an acute onset, affecting the bone marrow and the peripheral blood. The malignant cells show minimal differentiation and are called blasts, either myeloid blasts (myeloblasts) or lymphoid blasts (lymphoblasts)
increased concentration of lactate in th: Increased concentration of lactate in the cerebrospinal fluid
spasticity of one or more muscles innerv: Spasticity of one or more muscles innervated by the facial nerve
abnormality of the corticospinal tract,: Abnormality of the corticospinal tract, which is the chief element of the pyramidal system (the principle motor tract) and is the only direct connection between the cerebrum and the spinal cord
a functional anomaly of the upper motor: A functional anomaly of the upper motor neuron. The upper motor neurons are neurons of the primary motor cortex which project to the brainstem and spinal chord via the corticonuclear, corticobulbar and corticospinal (pyramidal) tracts. They are involved in control of voluntary movements.nDysfunction leads to weakness, impairment of fine motor movements, spasticity, hyperreflexia and abnormal pyramidal signs
abnormality of rem sleep. phases of rem: Abnormality of REM sleep. Phases of REM sleep are characterized by desynchronized EEG patterns, increases in heart rate and blood pressure, sympathetic activation, and a profound loss of muscle tonus except for the eye and middle-ear muscles. There are then phases of rapid eye movements
a decrease in the ability to perceive vi: A decrease in the ability to perceive vibration. Clinically, this is usually tested with a tuning fork which vibrates at 128 Hz and is applied to bony prominences such as the malleoli at the ankles or the metacarpal-phalangeal joints. There is a slow decay of vibration from the tuning fork. The degree of vibratory sense loss can be crudely estimated by counting the number of seconds that the examiner can perceive the vibration longer than the patient
an abnormality of the cerebral white mat: An abnormality of the cerebral white matter
deposition of calcium salts within small: Deposition of calcium salts within small blood vessels of the brain
diffuse unlocalised atrophy affecting th: Diffuse unlocalised atrophy affecting the cerebrum
a type of holoprosencephaly in which the: A type of holoprosencephaly in which the left and right frontal and parietal lobes are fused and the interhemispheric fissure is only present posteriorly
a developmental structural anomaly of th: A developmental structural anomaly of the stalk-like part of the brain that comprises the midbrain (aka mesencephalon), the pons (aka pons Varolii), and the medulla oblongata, and connects the cerebral hemispheres with the cervical spinal cord
spastic paralysis affecting all four lim: Spastic paralysis affecting all four limbs
a degenerative disease of the brain char: A degenerative disease of the brain characterized by the insidious onset of dementia. Impairment of memory, judgment, attention span, and problem solving skills are followed by severe apraxia and a global loss of cognitive abilities. The condition primarily occurs after age 60, and is marked pathologically by severe cortical atrophy and the triad of senile plaques, neurofibrillary tangles, and neuropil threads
the presence of calcium deposition withi: The presence of calcium deposition within brain structures
weakness of the hip girdle and upper thi: Weakness of the hip girdle and upper thigh muscles, for instance in myopathies, leads to an instability of the pelvis on standing and walking. If the muscles extending the hip joint are affected, the posture in that joint becomes flexed and lumbar lordosis increases. The patients usually have difficulties standing up from a sitting position. Due to weakness in the gluteus medius muscle, the hip on the side of the swinging leg drops with each step (referred to as Trendelenburg sign). The gait appears waddling. The patients frequently attempt to counteract the dropping of the hip on the swinging side by bending the trunk towards the side which is in the stance phase (in the German language literature this is referred to as Duchenne sign). Similar gait patterns can be caused by orthopedic conditions when the origin and the insertion site of the gluteus medius muscle are closer to each other than normal, for instance due to a posttraumatic elevation of the trochanter or pseudarthrosis of the femoral neck
an increase of the pressure inside the c: An increase of the pressure inside the cranium (skull) and thereby in the brain tissue and cerebrospinal fluid
hypsarrhythmia is abnormal interictal hi: Hypsarrhythmia is abnormal interictal high amplitude waves and a background of irregular spikes. There is continuous (during wakefulness), high-amplitude (>200 Hz), generalized polymorphic slowing with no organized background and multifocal spikes demonstrated by electroencephalography (EEG)
inability to elicit tendon reflexes in t: Inability to elicit tendon reflexes in the lower limbs
a sudden and transient episode of bilate: A sudden and transient episode of bilateral loss of muscle tone, often triggered by emotions
impaired ability to repeat non-word soun: Impaired ability to repeat non-word sounds. Nonword repetition (NWR) is a measure of short-term phonological memory
a type of dystonia that affects the midl: A type of dystonia that affects the midline muscles, i.e., the chest, abdominal, and back muscles
involuntary flexion or extension of the: Involuntary flexion or extension of the arms and legs
an abnormality of the gyri (i.e., the ri: An abnormality of the gyri (i.e., the ridges) of the cerebral cortex of the brain
an abnormality of the cerebral cortex: An abnormality of the cerebral cortex
the presence of developmental dysplasia: The presence of developmental dysplasia of the cerebral cortex
neuronal degeneration in the cerebellum,: Neuronal degeneration in the cerebellum, pontine nuclei, and inferior olivary nucleus
a form of torticollis in which the head: A form of torticollis in which the head is drawn back, either due to a permanent contractures of neck extensor muscles, or to a spasmodic contracture
patchy loss of myelin from nerve fibers: Patchy loss of myelin from nerve fibers in the central nervous system
parkinsonism is a clinical syndrome that: Parkinsonism is a clinical syndrome that is a feature of a number of different diseases, including Parkinson disease itself, other neurodegenerative diseases such as progressive supranuclear palsy, and as a side-effect of some neuroleptic medications. Some but not all individuals with Parkinsonism show responsiveness to dopaminergic medication defined as a substantial reduction of amelioration of the component signs of Parkinsonism (including mainly tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability) upon administration of dopaminergic medication
increased height of the central portion: Increased height of the central portion of the eyebrow, forming a crescent, semicircular, or inverted U shape
presence of more than two nipples: Presence of more than two nipples
congenital failure to develop, and absen: Congenital failure to develop, and absence of, the nipple
placement of the nipples at a lower than: Placement of the nipples at a lower than normal location
presence of a thickened, fibrotic perica: Presence of a thickened, fibrotic pericardium that forms a non-compliant shell around the heart, and resulting from chronic inflammation of the pericardium
an abnormality of the intestinal rotatio: An abnormality of the intestinal rotation and fixation that normally occurs during the development of the gut. This can lead to volvulus, or twisting of the intestine that causes obstruction and necrosis
