greater than normal amounts of fat in th: Greater than normal amounts of fat in the feces. This is a result of malabsorption of lipids in the small intestine and results in frothy foul-smelling fecal matter that floats
a disorder of esophageal motility charac: A disorder of esophageal motility characterized by the inability of the lower esophageal sphincter to relax during swallowing and by inadequate or lacking peristalsis in the lower half of the body of the esophagus
the passage of fresh (red) blood per anu: The passage of fresh (red) blood per anus, usually in or with stools. Most rectal bleeding comes from the colon, rectum, or anus
an intermittent form of abdominal pain: An intermittent form of abdominal pain
an abnormal connection (fistula) between: An abnormal connection (fistula) between the esophagus and the trachea
an abnormality of the intestine in which: An abnormality of the intestine in which part of the intestine invaginates (telescopes) into another part of the intestine
decreased strength of the muscle layer o: Decreased strength of the muscle layer of stomach, which leads to a decreased ability to empty the contents of the stomach despite the absence of obstruction
abnormal intestinal contractions, such a: Abnormal intestinal contractions, such as spasms and intestinal paralysis, related to the loss of the ability of the gut to coordinate muscular activity because of endogenous or exogenous causes
abnormal twisting of a portion of intest: Abnormal twisting of a portion of intestine around itself or around a stalk of mesentery tissue
a form of chronic gastritis associated w: A form of chronic gastritis associated with atrophic gastric mucous membrane
colitis refers to an inflammation of the: Colitis refers to an inflammation of the colon and is often used to describe an inflammation of the large intestine (colon, cecum and rectum). Colitides may be acute and self-limited or chronic, and broadly fit into the category of digestive diseases
vomiting that ejects the gastric content: Vomiting that ejects the gastric contents with great force
an erosion of the mucous membrane of in: An erosion of the mucous membrane of in a portion of the duodenum
a neurological anomaly with gross overea: A neurological anomaly with gross overeating associated with an abnormally strong desire or need to eat
an ulcer, that is, an erosion of an area: An ulcer, that is, an erosion of an area of the gastric mucous membrane
angiectasia of lymph vessels (i.e., dila: Angiectasia of lymph vessels (i.e., dilatation of lymphatic vessels) in the intestines
acute obstruction of the intestines prev: Acute obstruction of the intestines preventing passage of the contents of the intestines
a head tremor of moderate speed (3 to 4: A head tremor of moderate speed (3 to 4 Hz) in the anterior-posterior direction
reduced intensity of muscle tendon refle: Reduced intensity of muscle tendon reflexes in the lower limbs. Reflexes are elicited by stretching the tendon of a muscle, e.g., by tapping
the presence of necrosis affecting the l: The presence of necrosis affecting the liver
involuntary fecal soiling in adults and: Involuntary fecal soiling in adults and children who have usually already been toilet trained
celiac disease (cd) is an autoimmune con: Celiac disease (CD) is an autoimmune condition affecting the small intestine, triggered by the ingestion of gluten, the protein fraction of wheat, barley, and rye. Clinical manifestations of CD are highly variable and include both gastrointestinal and non-gastrointestinal features. The hallmark of CD is an immune-mediated enteropathy. This term is included because the occurence of CD is seen as a feature of a number of other diseases
the presence of fibrosis of that part of: The presence of fibrosis of that part of the liver with congenital onset
progressive destruction of the small-to-: Progressive destruction of the small-to-medium bile ducts of the intrahepatic biliary tree, which leads to progressive cholestasis and often end-stage liver disease
a type of hepatic necrosis that is conce: A type of hepatic necrosis that is concentrated around the necrosis of hepatocytes localized around the intrahepatic branch of portal vein
an abnormal localized widening (dilatati: An abnormal localized widening (dilatation) of the aortic root
abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatat: Abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatation in the wall of an atery, vein or the heart
a condition characterized by patchy athe: A condition characterized by patchy atheromas or atherosclerotic plaques which develop in the walls of medium-sized and large arteries and can lead to arterial stenosis with reduced or blocked blood flow
an overriding aorta is a congenital hear: An overriding aorta is a congenital heart defect where the aorta is positioned directly over a ventricular septal defect, instead of over the left ventricle. The result is that the aorta receives some blood from the right ventricle, which reduces the amount of oxygen in the blood. It is one of the four conditions of the Tetralogy of Fallot. The aortic root can be displaced toward the front (anteriorly) or directly above the septal defect, but it is always abnormally located to the right of the root of the pulmonary artery. The degree of override is quite variable, with 5-95% of the valve being connected to the right ventricle
Formation of a blot clot in a deep vein. The clot often blocks blood flow, causing swelling and pain. The deep veins of the leg are most often affected
elongated and tortuous mesenteric veins,: Elongated and tortuous mesenteric veins, which comprise the inferior mesenteric vein and the superior mesenteric vein
the aortic arch crosses the right mainst: The aortic arch crosses the right mainstem bronchus and not the left mainstem bronchus, but does not result in the creation of a vascular ring. The first branch is the left brachiocephalic artery which divides into the left carotid artery and left subclavian artery, the second branch is the right carotid artery, the third branch is the right subclavian artery
an anomalous configuration of blood vess: An anomalous configuration of blood vessels that shunts arterial blood directly into veins without passing through the capillaries and that is located in the gastrointestinal tract
abnormality of the absorption of fat fro: Abnormality of the absorption of fat from the gastrointestinal tract
sclerosis (hardening) of the arteries wi: Sclerosis (hardening) of the arteries with increased thickness of the wall of arteries as well as increased stiffness and a loss of elasticity
in type iv atherosclerotic lesions a den: In type IV atherosclerotic lesions a dense accumulation of extracellular lipid occupies an extensive but well-defined region of the intima. This type of extracellular lipid accumulation is known as the lipid core. A fibrous tissue increase is not a feature, and complications such as defects of the lesion surface and thrombosis are not present. The type IV lesion is also known as atheroma. Type IV is the first lesion considered advanced in this classification because of the severe intimal disorganization caused by the lipid core. The characteristic core appears to develop from an increase and the consequent confluence of the small isolated pools of extracellular lipid that characterize type III lesions. The increase in lipid is believed to result from continued insudation from the plasma. The nature and derivations of the lipid that constitutes the core are discussed in the section on the extracellular matrix at the end of this report. Type IV lesions, when they first appear in younger people, are found in the same locations as adaptive intimal thickenings of the eccentric type. Thus, atheroma is, at least initially, an eccentric lesion
abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatat: Abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatation in an artery that originates from he abdominal aorta
inflammation of a superficial vein assoc: Inflammation of a superficial vein associated with venous thrombosis (blood clot formation within the vein)
budd-chiari syndrome (bcs) is caused by: Budd-Chiari syndrome (BCS) is caused by obstruction of hepatic venous outflow at any level from the small hepatic veins to the junction of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with the right atrium, 1 and occurs in 1/100,000 of the general population worldwide. The most common presentation is with ascites, but can range from fulminant hepatic failure (FHF) to asymptomatic forms. Obstruction of hepatic venous outflow is mainly caused by primary intravascular thrombosis, which can occur suddenly or be repeated over time, accompanied by some revascularization, accounting for the variable parenchymal hepatic damage and histologic presentation. Budd-Chiari syndrome is thus a disease, but since it occurs as a manifestation of several other diseases, this term is kept for the present for convenience
a type of hypertension associated with p: A type of hypertension associated with pheochromocytoma
an abnormality of the bony pelvic girdle: An abnormality of the bony pelvic girdle, which is a ring of bones connecting the vertebral column to the femurs
the presence of extra bones within a cra: The presence of extra bones within a cranial suture. Wormian bones are irregular isolated bones which appear in addition to the usual centers of ossification of the cranium
aortic dissection refers to a tear in th: Aortic dissection refers to a tear in the intimal layer of the aorta causing a separation between the intima and the medial layers of the aorta
the presence of an abnormal shape of the: The presence of an abnormal shape of the calvaria (skullcap), that is, of that part of the skull that is made up of the superior portions of the frontal bone, occipital bone, and parietal bones and covers the cranial cavity that contains the brain
the presence of an abnormal lateral curv: The presence of an abnormal lateral curvature of the spine
a general term describing features chara: A general term describing features characterized by abnormal development of bones and connective tissues
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to bone
a disorder of bone growth affecting the: A disorder of bone growth affecting the vertebrae and the ends of the long bones (epiphyses)
an abnormally increased tendency to frac: An abnormally increased tendency to fractures of bones caused by an abnormal reduction in bone strength that is generally associated with an increased risk of fracture
an increased tendency to fractures follo: An increased tendency to fractures following trauma, with fractures occurring without pain
an organ or organ-system abnormality tha: An organ or organ-system abnormality that consists of uncontrolled autonomous cell-proliferation which can occur in any part of the body as a benign or malignant neoplasm (tumour)
a cancer originating in lymphocytes and: A cancer originating in lymphocytes and presenting as a solid tumor of lymhpoid cells
pheochromocytomas (also known as chromaf: Pheochromocytomas (also known as chromaffin tumors) produce, store, and secrete catecholamines. Pheochromocytomas usually originate from the adrenal medulla but may also develop from chromaffin cells in or about sympathetic ganglia. A common symptom of pheochromocytoma is hypertension owing to release of catecholamines
the presence of a nephroblastoma, which: The presence of a nephroblastoma, which is a neoplasm of the kidney that primarily affects children
a carotid body tumor (also called paraga: A carotid body tumor (also called paraganglionoma or chemodectoma) is a tumor found in the upper neck at the branching of the carotid artery. They arise from the chemoreceptor organ (paraganglion) located in the adventitia of the carotid artery bifurcation
a malignant bone tumor that usually deve: A malignant bone tumor that usually develops during adolescence and usually affects the long bones including the tibia, femur, and humerus. The typical symptoms of osteosarcoma comprise bone pain, fracture, limitation of motion, and tenderness or swelling at the site of the tumor
the presence of a basal cell carcinoma o: The presence of a basal cell carcinoma of the skin
coxa valga is a deformity of the hip in: Coxa valga is a deformity of the hip in which the angle between the femoral shaft and the femoral neck is increased compared to age-adjusted values (about 150 degrees in newborns gradually reducing to 120-130 degrees in adults)
trilobar skull configuration when viewed: Trilobar skull configuration when viewed from the front or behind
an abnormal narrowing of the foramen mag: An abnormal narrowing of the foramen magnum
abnormality of the sella turcica, a sadd: Abnormality of the sella turcica, a saddle-shaped depression in the sphenoid bone at the base of the human skull
a deformity of the sella turcica whereby: A deformity of the sella turcica whereby the sella extends further anterior than normal such that the anterior clinoid process appears to overhang it, giving the appearance of the letter J on imaging of the skull
abnormality of the calvaria, which is th: Abnormality of the calvaria, which is the roof of the skull formed by the frontal bone, parietal bones, and occipital bone
the presence of an abnormally thick calv: The presence of an abnormally thick calvaria
an abnormality of the frontal sinus, one: An abnormality of the frontal sinus, one of the mucosa-lined, normally air-filled paranasal sinuses of the bones of the skull. The frontal sinus is located within the frontal bone
an abnormal enlargement of the sella tur: An abnormal enlargement of the sella turcica
a developmental malformation of the occi: A developmental malformation of the occipital bone and upper end of the cervical spine, in which the latter appears to have pushed the floor of the occipital bone upward such that there is an abnormal flattening of the skull base
an abnormality of the base of the skull,: An abnormality of the base of the skull, which forms the floor of the cranial cavity and separates the brain from other facial structures. The skull base is made up of five bones: the ethmoid, sphenoid, occipital, paired frontal, and paired parietal bones, and is subdivided into 3 regions: the anterior, middle, and posterior cranial fossae. The petro-occipital fissure subdivides the middle cranial fossa into 1 central component and 2 lateral components
increased bone density of the skull base: Increased bone density of the skull base without significant changes in bony contour
any abnormality of the parietal bone of: Any abnormality of the parietal bone of the skull
the presence of symmetrical and circular: The presence of symmetrical and circular openings (foramina) in the parietal bone ranging in size from a few millimeters to several centimeters wide
any abnormality of the foramen magnum: Any abnormality of the foramen magnum
an abnormal increase in the size of the: An abnormal increase in the size of the foramen magnum
an abnormality of the process of ossific: An abnormality of the process of ossification of the skull
the presence of a high and narrow palate: The presence of a high and narrow palate
the presence of small (ca. 0.5-1.0 mm) d: The presence of small (ca. 0.5-1.0 mm) dilated blood vessels near the surface of the mucous membranes of the palate
unusual prominence of the median palatal: Unusual prominence of the median palatal raphe, which is the ridge formed by the fusion of the two plates of the skull that form the hard palate
a depression located at an oral commissu: A depression located at an oral commissure
increased depth of the median tongue fur: Increased depth of the median tongue furrow
a morphological abnormality of the mouth: A morphological abnormality of the mouth in which the angle of the mouth is downturned. The oral commissures are positioned inferior to the midline labial fissure
an abnormality of the immune system: An abnormality of the immune system
increased susceptibility to bacterial in: Increased susceptibility to bacterial infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of bacterial infection
failure of the immune system to protect: Failure of the immune system to protect the body adequately from infection, due to the absence or insufficiency of some component process or substance
an increased susceptibility to abscess f: An increased susceptibility to abscess formation, as manifested by a medical history of recurrent abscesses
an absence of the phase of elevated meta: An absence of the phase of elevated metabolic activity, during which oxygen consumption increases, that occurs in neutrophils, monocytes, and macrophages shortly after phagocytosing material. An enhanced uptake of oxygen leads to the production, by an NADH dependent system, of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals, which play a part in microbiocidal activity
an increased susceptibility to aspergill: An increased susceptibility to Aspergillus infections, as manifested by a history of recurrent episodes of Aspergillus infections
a chronic, relapsing, inflammatory, and: A chronic, relapsing, inflammatory, and often febrile multisystemic disorder of connective tissue, characterized principally by involvement of the skin, joints, kidneys, and serosal membranes
increased susceptibility to staphylococc: Increased susceptibility to Staphylococcus aureus infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of Staphylococcus aureus infection
recurrent or persistent superficial cand: Recurrent or persistent superficial Candida infections of the skin, mucous membranes, and nails
lymphadenopathy (enlargement of lymph no: Lymphadenopathy (enlargement of lymph nodes) owing to hyperplasia of follicular (germinal) centers
a chronic form of lymphadenopathy that i: A chronic form of lymphadenopathy that is not related to infection
a reduction in the rate of apoptosis in: A reduction in the rate of apoptosis in lymphocytes
increased susceptibility to infections w: Increased susceptibility to infections with Escherichia coli, as manifested by recurrent episodes of infection with this agent
increased susceptibility to serratia mar: Increased susceptibility to Serratia marcescens infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of Serratia marcescens infection
increased susceptibility to klebsiella i: Increased susceptibility to Klebsiella infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of Klebsiella infection
increased susceptibility to enteroviral: Increased susceptibility to enteroviral infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of enteroviral infection
cleft lip and cleft palate affecting bot: Cleft lip and cleft palate affecting both sides of the face
a thickened white patch on the oral muco: A thickened white patch on the oral mucosa that cannot be rubbed off
a condition that causes soft, weak bones: A condition that causes soft, weak bones. It is often attributed to a lack of vitamin D, which helps bones absorb important nutrients
osteomalacia is a general term for bone: Osteomalacia is a general term for bone weakness owing to a defect in mineralization of the protein framework known as osteoid. This defective mineralization is mainly caused by lack in vitamin D. Osteomalacia in children is known as rickets
A decreased rate of skeletal maturation. Delayed skeletal maturation can be diagnosed on the basis of an estimation of the bone age from radiographs of specific bones in the human body
an abnormal curvature of the spine in bo: An abnormal curvature of the spine in both a coronal (lateral) and sagittal (back-to-front) plane
abnormal thinning of the cortical region: Abnormal thinning of the cortical region of bones
osteomyelitis is an inflammatory process: Osteomyelitis is an inflammatory process accompanied by bone destruction and caused by an infecting microorganism
a pathologic fracture occurs when a bone: A pathologic fracture occurs when a bone breaks in an area that is weakened secondary to another disease process such as tumor, infection, and certain inherited bone disorders. A pathologic fracture can occur without a degree of trauma required to cause fracture in healthy bone
the repeated occurrence of bone fracture: The repeated occurrence of bone fractures (implying an abnormally increased tendency for fracture)
degeneration (wear and tear) of articula: Degeneration (wear and tear) of articular cartilage, i.e., of the joint surface. Joint degeneration may be accompanied by osteophytes (bone overgrowth), narrowing of the joint space, regions of sclerosis at the joint surface, or joint deformity
joint hypermobility (ability of a joint: Joint hypermobility (ability of a joint to move beyond its normal range of motion) affecting many or all joints of the body
presence of more than one exostosis. an: Presence of more than one exostosis. An exostosis is a benign growth the projects outward from the bone surface. It is capped by cartilage, and arises from a bone that develops from cartilage
a structural anomaly of the trachea: A structural anomaly of the trachea
increased resistance to the passage of a: Increased resistance to the passage of air in the upper airway
an increased susceptibility to lower res: An increased susceptibility to lower respiratory tract infections as manifested by a history of recurrent lower respiratory tract infections
weakness of the cartilage in the trachea: Weakness of the cartilage in the trachea and the bronchi, resulting in a floppy (non-rigid) airway. Affected persons may have difficulties to maintain patency of the airways
calcification (abnormal deposits of calc: Calcification (abnormal deposits of calcium) in the tracheal tissues
an increased susceptibility to upper res: An increased susceptibility to upper respiratory tract infections as manifested by a history of recurrent upper respiratory tract infections (running ears - otitis, sinusitis, pharyngitis, tonsillitis)
a reduction in the amount of air transpo: A reduction in the amount of air transported into the pulmonary alveoli by breathing, leading to hypercapnia (increase in the partial pressure of carbon dioxide)
an abnormal reduction on the vital capac: An abnormal reduction on the vital capacity, which is defined as the total lung capacity (volume of air in the lungs at maximal inflation) less the residual volume (i.e., volume of air in the lungs following maximal exhalation) of the lung
an anomaly of the rhythm or depth of bre: An anomaly of the rhythm or depth of breathing
osteolysis refers to the destruction of: Osteolysis refers to the destruction of bone through bone resorption with removal or loss of calcium
one or more flexion contractures (a bent: One or more flexion contractures (a bent joint that cannot be straightened actively or passively) that are present at birth
a non-progressive finding characterized: A non-progressive finding characterized by multiple joint contractures found throughout the body at birth
exaggerated anterior convexity of the th: Exaggerated anterior convexity of the thoracic vertebral column
coxa vara includes all forms of decrease: Coxa vara includes all forms of decrease of the femoral neck shaft angle (the angle between the neck and the shaft of the femur) to less than 120 degrees
any abnormality of bones of the arms or: Any abnormality of bones of the arms or legs
an abnormality of the knee joint or surr: An abnormality of the knee joint or surrounding structures
an abnormally increased extension of the: An abnormally increased extension of the knee joint, so that the knee can bend backwards
any anomaly of the structure of the femu: Any anomaly of the structure of the femur
the presence of a tail-like skin appenda: The presence of a tail-like skin appendage located adjacent to the sacrum
an anomalous radiographic appearance of: An anomalous radiographic appearance of the developing pelvis, in which the greater ischiadic noth (incisura ischiadica major) is shallow and the pelvis takes on the appearance said to resemble a halberd (a weapon especially of the 15th and 16th centuries consisting typically of a battle-ax and pike mounted on a handle)
displacement of the femur from its norma: Displacement of the femur from its normal location in the hip joint
an abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like: An abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) pattern of calcifications in soft tissues within or surrounding bones (as observed on radiographs)
disseminated multifocal hemangiomatous o: Disseminated multifocal hemangiomatous or lymphangiomatous lesions of the skeleton. The lesions are lytic, well-defined, round or oval lesions within the medullary cavity, and they have an intact cortex, and manifest variable peripheral sclerosis and may exhibit endosteal scalloping
inspiration of a foreign object into the: Inspiration of a foreign object into the airway
eversion of the posterior bladder wall t: Eversion of the posterior bladder wall through the congenitally absent lower anterior abdominal wall and anterior bladder wall
an increased susceptibility to bronchiti: An increased susceptibility to bronchitis as manifested by a history of recurrent bronchitis
abnormal function of a sphincter of the: Abnormal function of a sphincter of the urinary bladder
increased susceptibility to fungal infec: Increased susceptibility to fungal infections, as manifested by multiple episodes of fungal infection
increased susceptibility to infections w: Increased susceptibility to infections with Burkholderia cepacia, as manifested by recurrent episodes of infection with this agent
a structural abnormality of b cells: A structural abnormality of B cells
impaired production of memory cells, the: Impaired production of memory cells, the B cells that persist for years or an entire lifetime and which confer rapid and enhanced response to secondary challenge
the presence of normal overall immunoglo: The presence of normal overall immunoglobulin levels with deficiency of specific immunoglobulins directed against bacterial polysaccharides
absence of germinal centers in lymph nod: Absence of germinal centers in lymph nodes. Germinal centers are the parts of lymph nodes in which B lymphocytes proliferate, differentiate, mutate through somatic hypermutation and class switch during antibody responses
an abnormally decreased level of immunog: An abnormally decreased level of immunoglobulin M (IgM) in blood
an abnormally increased proportion of cd: An abnormally increased proportion of CD4-negative, CD8-negative (double negative or DN) alpha-beta regulatory T cells (Tregs) as compared to total number of T cells
an elevated proportion of t cells that e: An elevated proportion of T cells that express human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-DR. HLA-DR is an MHC class II cell surface receptor that presents antigens (peptides of at least 9 amino acids), thereby constituting a ligand for the T-cell receptor. HLA-DR can be upregulated in response to immune stimulation
the legs angle inward, such that the kne: The legs angle inward, such that the knees are close together and the ankles far apart
the presence of a meningioma, i.e., a be: The presence of a meningioma, i.e., a benign tumor originating from the dura mater or arachnoid mater
the presence of squamous cell carcinoma: The presence of squamous cell carcinoma of the skin
the presence of a melanoma, a malignant: The presence of a melanoma, a malignant cancer originating from pigment producing melanocytes. Melanoma can originate from the skin or the pigmented layers of the eye (the uvea)
the presence of a carcinoma of the urina: The presence of a carcinoma of the urinary bladder
clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorders: Clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorders characterized by dysplasia (ineffective production) in one or more hematopoietic cell lineages, leading to anemia and cytopenia
the presence of a medullary carcinoma of: The presence of a medullary carcinoma of the thyroid gland
an abnormality of the ilium, the largest: An abnormality of the ilium, the largest and uppermost bone of the pelvis
decreased width of the wing (or ala) of: Decreased width of the wing (or ala) of the ilium (which is the large expanded portion which bounds the greater pelvis laterally)
widening of the ilium ala, that is of th: Widening of the ilium ala, that is of the wing of the ilium, combined with external rotation, leading to a flared appearance of the iliac wing
a condition characterized by obstruction: A condition characterized by obstruction of the airway and by pauses in breathing during sleep occurring many times during the night. Obstructive sleep apnea is related to a relaxation of muscle tone (which normally occurs during sleep) leading to partial collapse of the soft tissues in the airway with resultant obstruction of the air flow
apnea resulting from depression of the r: Apnea resulting from depression of the respiratory centers in the medulla oblongata. There is a lack of respiratory effort rather than obstruction of airflow
recurrent episodes of apnea that are pre: Recurrent episodes of apnea that are precipitated by factors such as illness, fatigue, or stress
a severe form of respiratory insufficien: A severe form of respiratory insufficiency characterized by inadequate gas exchange such that the levels of oxygen or carbon dioxide cannot be maintained within normal limits
abnormally increased variability of the: Abnormally increased variability of the size of the vertebral bodies
recurrent bouts of sudden, severe apnea: Recurrent bouts of sudden, severe apnea that may be life-threatening
hyperventilation refers to an increased: Hyperventilation refers to an increased pulmonary ventilation rate that is faster than necessary for the exchange of gases. Hyperventilation can result from increased frequency of breathing, an increased tidal volume, or both, and leads to an excess intake of oxygen and the blowing off of carbon dioxide
a kind of neoplasm of the liver that ori: A kind of neoplasm of the liver that originates from immature liver precursor cells and macroscopically is composed of tissue resembling fetal or mature liver cells or bile ducts
a rapidly growing embryonic tumor arisin: A rapidly growing embryonic tumor arising in the posterior part of the cerebellar vermis and neuroepithelial roof of the fourth ventricle in children. More rarely, medulloblastoma arises in the cerebellum in adults
a tumor that develops in the retrostyloi: A tumor that develops in the retrostyloid compartment of the parapharyngeal space, arising from an island of paraganglion tissue derived from the neural crest that is located on the vagus nerve
the presence of an ependymoma of the cen: The presence of an ependymoma of the central nervous system
the presence of a carcinoma of the thyro: The presence of a carcinoma of the thyroid gland
the presence of a leiomyosarcoma of the: The presence of a leiomyosarcoma of the uterus
a benign epithelial tumor derived from i: A benign epithelial tumor derived from intrinsic cells of the adenohypophysis
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the pancreas
the presence of a papillary adenocarcino: The presence of a papillary adenocarcinoma of the thyroid gland
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the liver
a benign tumor of the parathyroid gland: A benign tumor of the parathyroid gland that can cause hyperparathyroidism
an abnormally decreased potassium concen: An abnormally decreased potassium concentration in the blood
an abnormally decreased calcium concentr: An abnormally decreased calcium concentration in the blood
an abnormally decreased sodium concentra: An abnormally decreased sodium concentration in the blood
an increased amount of bilirubin in the: An increased amount of bilirubin in the blood
an abnormally increased phosphate concen: An abnormally increased phosphate concentration in the blood
microscopic hematuria detected by dipsti: Microscopic hematuria detected by dipstick or microscopic examination of the urine
an increased concentration of all types: An increased concentration of all types of amino acid in the urine
elevations of the levels of sgot and sgp: Elevations of the levels of SGOT and SGPT in the serum. SGOT (serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase) and SGPT (serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase) are transaminases primarily found in the liver and heart and are released into the bloodstream as the result of liver or heart damage. SGOT and SGPT are used clinically mainly as markers of liver damage
increased concentration of methylmalonic: Increased concentration of methylmalonic acid in the blood
presence of myoglobin in the urine: Presence of myoglobin in the urine
an increased concentration of chloride i: An increased concentration of chloride in the urine
an abnormally decreased magnesium concen: An abnormally decreased magnesium concentration in the blood
an abnormally increased magnesium concen: An abnormally increased magnesium concentration in the blood
high levels of ketone bodies in the urin: High levels of ketone bodies in the urine
an abnormal reduction in the concentrati: An abnormal reduction in the concentration of corticotropin, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), in the blood
an abnormality of the cerebrospinal flui: An abnormality of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
increased concentration of protein in th: Increased concentration of protein in the cerebrospinal fluid
the presence in the serum of an autoanti: The presence in the serum of an autoantibody directed against the Fc portion of IgG
overproduction of thyroid-stimulating ho: Overproduction of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) by the anterior pituitary gland
an abnormal functionality of the thyroid: An abnormal functionality of the thyroid gland
an increased concentration of histidine: An increased concentration of histidine in the urine
thyroid hormone resistance because of a: Thyroid hormone resistance because of a defect in the thyroid hormone receptor
a reduction in aldehyde oxidase activity: A reduction in aldehyde oxidase activity
ventral hernia refers to a condition in: Ventral hernia refers to a condition in which abdominal contents protrude through a weakened portion of the abdominal wall
an abnormal reduction in sensation in th: An abnormal reduction in sensation in the distal portions of the extremities
absence of one half of the vertebral bod: Absence of one half of the vertebral body
an abnormal accentuation of the inward c: An abnormal accentuation of the inward curvature of the spine in the lumbar region
over curvature of the thoracic region, l: Over curvature of the thoracic region, leading to a round back or if sever to a hump
decreased height of the intervertebral d: Decreased height of the intervertebral disk
exaggerated convexity of the cervical ve: Exaggerated convexity of the cervical vertebral column, causing the cervical spine to bow outwards and take on a rounded appearance
a developmental defect leading to the un: A developmental defect leading to the union of two adjacent vertebrae
a congenital anomaly characterized by a: A congenital anomaly characterized by a joining (fusion) of two or more cervical vertebral bodies with one another
congenital absence of a part of the verm: Congenital absence of a part of the vermis of cerebellum
a granulomatous inflammation leading to: A granulomatous inflammation leading to multiple granuloma formation, which is a specific type of inflammation. A granuloma is a focal compact collection of inflammatory cells, mononuclear cells predominating, usually as a result of the persistence of a non-degradable product and of active cell mediated hypersensitivity
altered immune function characterized by: Altered immune function characterized by lymphoid proliferation, immune activation, and excessive autoreactivity often leading to autoimmune/inflammatory complications
an impairment of the class-switch recomb: An impairment of the class-switch recombination process that normally leads B lymphocytes to produce IgG, IgA, or IgE
the occurrence of an immune reaction aga: The occurrence of an immune reaction against the organism's own cells or tissues
selective deficiency of one or more, but: Selective deficiency of one or more, but not all, classes of immunoglobulins
delay in cutaneous immune reaction to sp: Delay in cutaneous immune reaction to specific antigens mediated not by antibodies but by cells. The delayed hypersensitivity test is an immune function test measuring the presence of activated T cells that recognize a specific antigen and is performed by injecting a small amount of the antigen into the skin. The area of the injection is examined 48-72 hours thereafter
inability to react to a delayed hypersen: Inability to react to a delayed hypersensitivity skin test
abnormal positioning in which the elbows: Abnormal positioning in which the elbows are turned out
a positional abnormality marked by outwa: A positional abnormality marked by outward bowing of the legs in which the knees stay wide apart when a person stands with the feet and ankles together
absence of the fingerlike protrusive, ac: Absence of the fingerlike protrusive, actin-dependent structures found on the surface of peripheral blood lymphocytes
decreased ability to react to a delayed: Decreased ability to react to a delayed hypersensitivity skin test
an abnormality of the lower arm: An abnormality of the lower arm
an abnormal osseous union (fusion) betwe: An abnormal osseous union (fusion) between the radius and the ulna
absence or underdevelopment of tissue in: Absence or underdevelopment of tissue in the central nervous system
a bending or abnormal curvature affectin: A bending or abnormal curvature affecting a long bone of the leg

a bending or abnormal curvature of the t: A bending or abnormal curvature of the tibia
the presence of abnormally small extremi: The presence of abnormally small extremities
a bending or abnormal curvature of the r: A bending or abnormal curvature of the radius
a chronic loss of elbow joint motion due: A chronic loss of elbow joint motion due to structural changes in muscle, tendons, ligaments, or skin that prevent normal movement of the joints of the elbow
an anomaly of the calf bone (fibula), on: An anomaly of the calf bone (fibula), one of the two bones of the calf
an abnormality of the ulna bone of the f: An abnormality of the ulna bone of the forearm
the kneecap normally is located within t: The kneecap normally is located within the groove termed trochlea on the distal femur and can slide up and down in it. Patellar dislocation occurs if the patella fully dislocates out of the groove
the presence of a carcinoma of the breas: The presence of a carcinoma of the breast
a benign neoplasm that usually arises fr: A benign neoplasm that usually arises from the sympathetic trunk in the mediastinum, representing a tumor of the sympathetic nerve fibers arising from neural crest cells
neuroblastoma is a solid tumor that orig: Neuroblastoma is a solid tumor that originate in neural crest cells of the sympathetic nervous system. Most neuroblastomas originate in the abdomen, and most abdominal neuroblastomas originate in the adrenal gland. Neuroblastomas can also originate in the thorax, usually in the posterior mediastinum
prolongation of the time taken for a sta: Prolongation of the time taken for a standardized skin cut of fixed depth and length to stop bleeding
abnormality originating in one or more m: Abnormality originating in one or more muscles, i.e., of the set of muscles of body
a morphological abnormality of epiphyses: A morphological abnormality of epiphyses whereby they are abnormally outwardly curving (protuberant)
the presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared): The presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared) metaphyseal segment of one or more long bones
abnormal widening of the metaphyseal reg: Abnormal widening of the metaphyseal regions of long bones
abnormality of the wrist, the structure: Abnormality of the wrist, the structure connecting the hand and the forearm
metaphyseal cupping refers to an inward: Metaphyseal cupping refers to an inward bulging of the metaphyseal profile giving the metaphysis a cup-like appearance
curvature of the shafts of the long bone: Curvature of the shafts of the long bones due to multiple fractures
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of the metaphyses
one or more abnormally short long bone: One or more abnormally short long bone
shortening of the middle parts of the li: Shortening of the middle parts of the limbs (forearm and lower leg) in relation to the upper and terminal segments
bending of the diaphysis (shaft) of the: Bending of the diaphysis (shaft) of the ulna
an elevation in bone density in one or m: An elevation in bone density in one or more diaphyses. Sclerosis is normally detected on a radiograph as an area of increased opacity
increase in size of one or more joints: Increase in size of one or more joints
an abnormal osseous union (fusion) betwe: An abnormal osseous union (fusion) between the radius and the humerus
dislocation of the distal humerus out of: Dislocation of the distal humerus out of the elbow joint, where the radius, ulna, and humerus meet
an abnormality of the shoulder, which is: An abnormality of the shoulder, which is defined as the structures surrounding the shoulder joint where the humerus attaches to the scapula
chronic reduction in active and passive: Chronic reduction in active and passive mobility of the shoulder joint due to structural changes in muscle, tendons, ligaments, or skin that prevents normal movement
partial dislocation of the head of the r: Partial dislocation of the head of the radius
abnormal increase in size of one or more: Abnormal increase in size of one or more metaphyses
an abnormality of the humerus (i.e., upp: An abnormality of the humerus (i.e., upper arm bone)
an anomaly related to partial closure, o: An anomaly related to partial closure, or failure of development of the ulnar side of the distal radial growth plate, which results in an arrest of epiphyseal growth of the medial and volar portions of the distal radius. This leads to shortening of the radius and relative overgrowth of the ulna
an abnormally increased calcium concentr: An abnormally increased calcium concentration in the blood
reduction in the concentration of albumi: Reduction in the concentration of albumin in the blood
an increased concentration of glucose in: An increased concentration of glucose in the blood
a decreased concentration of protein in: A decreased concentration of protein in the blood
an increased concentration of glucose in: An increased concentration of glucose in the urine
an elevated lipid concentration in the b: An elevated lipid concentration in the blood
an increased concentration of 4-hydroxy-: An increased concentration of 4-hydroxy-L-proline the urine
an increased concentration of potassium(: An increased concentration of potassium(1+) in the urine
a dislocation of the head of the radius: A dislocation of the head of the radius from its socket in the elbow joint
an increased tendency to fractures of th: An increased tendency to fractures of the long bones (Mainly, the femur, tibia, fibula,humerus, radius, and ulna)
underdevelopment of the proximal epiphys: Underdevelopment of the proximal epiphysis of the femur
trophic changes occurring in a limb: Trophic changes occurring in a limb
limitation of the extension of the hip,: Limitation of the extension of the hip, i.e., decreased ability to straighten the hip joint and thereby increase the angle between torso and thigh; moving the thigh or top of the pelvis backward
an abnormal shortening of the femur: An abnormal shortening of the femur
relatively increased growth of the fibul: Relatively increased growth of the fibula compared to that of the tibia
reduced diameter of a long bone: Reduced diameter of a long bone
an abnormal increase in the carrying ang: An abnormal increase in the carrying angle, which is the angle he long axis of the extended forearm as it lies lateral to the long axis of the arm
an abnormality of compact bone (also kno: An abnormality of compact bone (also known as cortical bone), which forms the dense surface of bones
the presence of multiple protuberances (: The presence of multiple protuberances (bulges, or knobs) at the ends of the long bones
loss of bone mass occurring beneath the: Loss of bone mass occurring beneath the periosteum (the periosteum is the connective-tissue membrane that surrounds all bones except at the articular surfaces). This process may create a serrated and lace-like appearance in periosteal cortical bone
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of cholesterol in the blood circulation
an increased concentration of glycine in: An increased concentration of glycine in the urine
an increased excretion of phosphates in: An increased excretion of phosphates in the urine
an abnormality of the composition of uri: An abnormality of the composition of urine or the levels of its components
abnormality of the homeostasis (concentr: Abnormality of the homeostasis (concentration) of a monoatomic ion
the presence of an abnormal decrease or: The presence of an abnormal decrease or increase of one or more amino acids in the blood circulation
an abnormally decreased chloride concent: An abnormally decreased chloride concentration in the blood
an abnormality detectable by sonography: An abnormality detectable by sonography of the heart (echocardiography)
an abnormal concentration of a hormone i: An abnormal concentration of a hormone in the blood
overproduction of the hormone of cortiso: Overproduction of the hormone of cortisol by the adrenal cortex, resulting in a characteristic combination of clinical symptoms termed Cushing syndrome, with truncal obesity, a round, full face, striae atrophicae and acne, muscle weakness, and other features
an abnormality in the of lipid metabolis: An abnormality in the of lipid metabolism
a contrqacture (chronic loss of joint mo: A contrqacture (chronic loss of joint motion due to structural changes in muscle, tendons, ligaments, or skin) that prevent normal movement of one or more joints of the limbs
an increased concentration of cholestero: An increased concentration of cholesterol in the blood
reduced activity of coagulation factor v: Reduced activity of coagulation factor VIII. Factor VIII (fVIII) is a cofactor in the intrinsic clotting cascade that is activated to fVIIIa in the presence of minute quantities of thrombin. fVIIIa acts as a receptor, for factors IXa and X
excretion in urine of proteins of a size: Excretion in urine of proteins of a size smaller than albumin (molecular weight 69 kD)
an abnormally decreased calcium concentr: An abnormally decreased calcium concentration in the urine
an abnormal buildup of lactic acid in th: An abnormal buildup of lactic acid in the body, leading to acidification of the blood and other bodily fluids
an abnormality of the myelination of mot: An abnormality of the myelination of motor and sensory peripheral nerves. These are axons for motor nerves and dendrites for sensory nerves in the strict anatomic sense
an increased concentration of cystine in: An increased concentration of cystine in the urine
an abnormality of the spinocerebellar tr: An abnormality of the spinocerebellar tracts, a set of axonal fibers originating in the spinal cord and terminating in the ipsilateral cerebellum. The spinocerebellar tract convey information to the cerebellum about limb and joint position (proprioception). They comprise the ventral spinocerebellar tract, the anterior spinocerebellar tract, and the posterior spinocerebellar tract
an abnormality of the conduction of elec: An abnormality of the conduction of electrical impulses by peripheral (motor or sensory) nerves. This finding is elicited by a nerve conduction study (NCS)
an increased concentration of proline in: An increased concentration of proline in the urine
an increased amount of nitrogen in the f: An increased amount of nitrogen in the form of urea in the blood
a reduction in the circulating levels of: A reduction in the circulating levels of all the major classes of immunoglobulin. is characterized by profound decreases in all classes of immunoglobulin with an absence of circulating B lymphocytes
an elevated concentration of low-density: An elevated concentration of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol in the blood
a increased concentration of serotonin i: A increased concentration of serotonin in the blood
decreased concentration of adenosylcobal: Decreased concentration of adenosylcobalamin. Adenosylcobalamin is one of the active forms of vitamin B12
an decreased concentration of cholestero: An decreased concentration of cholesterol in the blood
an abnormally high level of uric acid in: An abnormally high level of uric acid in the urine
an increased concentration of glutaric a: An increased concentration of glutaric acid in the urine
an elevated urinary concentration of cys: An elevated urinary concentration of cystathionine
an abnormal increased in the concentrati: An abnormal increased in the concentration of corticotropin, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), in the blood
abnormally increased serum levels of alk: Abnormally increased serum levels of alkaline phosphatase activity
an abnormally low urinary specific gravi: An abnormally low urinary specific gravity, i.e., reduced concentration of solutes in the urine
increased excretion of oxalates in the u: Increased excretion of oxalates in the urine
glycosylated transferrin concentrations: Glycosylated transferrin concentrations can be measured in serum as a marker of N-linked glycosylation fidelity. In the traditional nomenclature for congenital disorders of glycosylation, absence of entire glycans was designated type I, and loss of one or more monosaccharides as type II. These terms are retained for historical reasons but for new annotations the precise glycosylation defect should be recorded
increased concentration of pyruvic acid: Increased concentration of pyruvic acid in the urine
an increased concentration of 5-aminolev: An increased concentration of 5-aminolevulinic acid (CHEBI:17549) in the urine
an abnormal increased concentration of p: An abnormal increased concentration of parathyroid hormone
increased concentration of taurine in th: Increased concentration of taurine in the urine
an increased concentration of carnosine: An increased concentration of carnosine in the urine
an abnormality of the acetabulum, i.e.,: An abnormality of the acetabulum, i.e., the Acetabular part of hip bone, which together with the head of the femur forms the hip joint
an anomaly of the the pubic bone, i.e.,: An anomaly of the the pubic bone, i.e., of the ventral and anterior of the three principal components (publis, ilium, ischium) of the hip bone
underdevelopment of the pubis, which tog: Underdevelopment of the pubis, which together with the ilium and the ischium, is one of the three bones that make up the hip bone
an anomaly of the ischium, which forms t: An anomaly of the ischium, which forms the lower and back part of the hip bone
underdevelopment of the ischium, which f: Underdevelopment of the ischium, which forms the lower and back part of the hip bone
a shift from the normally round (convex): A shift from the normally round (convex) appearance of the iliac wing towards a square-like appearance
intrapelvic bulging of the medial acetab: Intrapelvic bulging of the medial acetabular wall
flattening of the superior part of the a: Flattening of the superior part of the acetabulum, which is a cup-shaped cavity at the base of the hipbone into which the ball-shaped head of the femur fits. The acetabular roof thereby appears horizontal rather than arched, as it normally does
abnormally increased width of the pubic: Abnormally increased width of the pubic symphysis is the midline cartilaginous joint uniting the superior rami of the left and right pubic bones
reduced ability to move the femur outwar: Reduced ability to move the femur outward to the side
the sacroiliac joint in the bony pelvis: The sacroiliac joint in the bony pelvis connects the sacrum and the ilium of the pelvis, which are joined by strong ligaments. The notch is located directly superior to the joint. This term refers to a reduction in the height of the notch
the presence of nipples that instead of: The presence of nipples that instead of pointing outward are retracted inwards
distance from nasion to subnasale more t: Distance from nasion to subnasale more than two standard deviations above the mean, or alternatively, an apparently increased length from the nasal root to the nasal base
the presence of a notch in the margin of: The presence of a notch in the margin of the ala nasi
it is characterized by one or more sympt: It is characterized by one or more symptoms including sneezing, itching, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea
distance from nasion to subnasale more t: Distance from nasion to subnasale more than two standard deviations below the mean, or alternatively, an apparently decreased length from the nasal root to the nasal tip
a disorder of muscle unrelated to impair: A disorder of muscle unrelated to impairment of innervation or neuromuscular junction
an abnormal appearance of muscle fibers: An abnormal appearance of muscle fibers observed on muscle biopsy. Ragged red fibers can be visualized with Gomori trichrome staining as irregular and intensely red subsarcolemmal zones, whereas the normal myofibrils are green. The margins of affect fibers appear red and ragged. The ragged-red is due to the accumulation of abnormal mitochondria below the plasma membrane of the muscle fiber, leading to the appearance of a red rim and speckled sarcoplasm
breakdown of muscle fibers that leads to: Breakdown of muscle fibers that leads to the release of muscle fiber contents (myoglobin) into the bloodstream
the presence of skeletal muscular atroph: The presence of skeletal muscular atrophy (which is also known as amyotrophy)
in the nbt test, neutrophils change the: In the NBT test, neutrophils change the colorless compound NBT into a compound with a deep blue color. If this test is negative (i.e., no blue color is produced), then this indicates a defect in superoxide-generating NADPH oxidase activity with inability to efficiently kill phagocytized bacteria
the intracellular accumulation of autofl: The intracellular accumulation of autofluorescent storage material
an intracellular accumulation of autoflu: An intracellular accumulation of autofluorescent lipopigment storage material in a curved pattern
pathological deposition of calcium salts: Pathological deposition of calcium salts in one or more arteries
an intracellular accumulation of autoflu: An intracellular accumulation of autofluorescent lipopigment storage material in a trabecular or fingerprint-like pattern
a decreased rate of pyruvate carboxylase: A decreased rate of pyruvate carboxylase activity
an abnormality of krebs cycle metabolism: An abnormality of Krebs cycle metabolism that is characterized by a decreased rate of methylmalonyl-CoA mutase activity
an abnormally increased level of immunog: An abnormally increased level of immunoglobulin E in blood
an increased concentration of dicarboxyl: An increased concentration of dicarboxylic acid in the urine
an increased concentration of glutamine: An increased concentration of glutamine in the blood
an increased concentration of orotic aci: An increased concentration of orotic acid in the urine
an increased concentration of ethylmalon: An increased concentration of ethylmalonic acid in the urine
a type of chromosomal aberration charact: A type of chromosomal aberration characterised by reduced resistance of chromosomes to change or deterioration
increased amount of chromosomal breaks i: Increased amount of chromosomal breaks in cultured blood lymphocytes or other cells induced by treatment with DNA cross-linking agents such as diepoxybutane and mitomycin C
decreased concentration of methylcobalam: Decreased concentration of methylcobalamin. Methylcobalamin is a form of vitamin B12
decreased activity of coagulation factor: Decreased activity of coagulation factor V
an intracellular accumulation of autoflu: An intracellular accumulation of autofluorescent lipopigment storage material in a straight or rectilinear pattern
an abnormally increased sodium concentra: An abnormally increased sodium concentration in the blood
an increased concentration of tyrosine i: An increased concentration of tyrosine in the blood
an decreased concentration of high-densi: An decreased concentration of high-density lipoprotein cholesterol in the blood
a decreased concentration of carnitine i: A decreased concentration of carnitine in the blood
an increased concentration of methionine: An increased concentration of methionine in the blood
an elevation of the level of the enzyme: An elevation of the level of the enzyme creatine kinase (also known as creatine phosphokinase, CPK; EC 2.7.3.2) in the blood. CPK levels can be elevated in a number of clinical disorders such as myocardial infarction, rhabdomyolysis, and muscular dystrophy
an abnormally increased level of immunog: An abnormally increased level of immunoglobulin G in blood
an increased concentration of phosphoeth: An increased concentration of phosphoethanolamine in the urine
underdevelopment of part or all of the e: Underdevelopment of part or all of the external reproductive organs
location of the urethral opening on the: Location of the urethral opening on the inferior aspect of the penis
increased size of the ridge of tissue th: Increased size of the ridge of tissue that extends along the midline of the scrotum
an abnormality of the process of dna rep: An abnormality of the process of DNA repair, that is, of the process of restoring DNA after damage
an abnormality of the coagulation cascad: An abnormality of the coagulation cascade, which is comprised of the contact activation pathway (also known as the intrinsic pathway) and the tissue factor pathway (also known as the extrinsic pathway) as well as cofactors and regulators
an increased amount of creatinine in the: An increased amount of creatinine in the blood
an increased concentration of hydroxypro: An increased concentration of hydroxyproline in the blood
an abnormally increased level of immunog: An abnormally increased level of immunoglobulin A in blood
the presence in serum of antibodies agai: The presence in serum of antibodies against smooth muscle
a type of hyperbilirubinemia with neonat: A type of hyperbilirubinemia with neonatal onset
an abnormal decrease in orotidine 5'-pho: An abnormal decrease in orotidine 5'-phosphate decarboxylase activity
a increased concentration of arginine in: A increased concentration of arginine in the urine
abnormal increased size of the viscera o: Abnormal increased size of the viscera of the abdomen
an abnormality of the hip bone: An abnormality of the hip bone
reduced side to side width of the pelvis: Reduced side to side width of the pelvis
a benign growth the projects outward fro: A benign growth the projects outward from the bone surface of the pelvis. Exostoses are capped by cartilage, and arise from a bone that develops from cartilage
an abnormally squared appearance of the: An abnormally squared appearance of the bony pelvis, a normally rounded or basin-shaped structure
widening of the femoral head and neck: Widening of the femoral head and neck
abnormal raised concentration of ferriti: Abnormal raised concentration of ferritin, a ubiquitous intracellular protein that stores iron, in the blood
abnormally reduced serum levels of alkal: Abnormally reduced serum levels of alkaline phosphatase activity
an increased concentration of cystathion: An increased concentration of cystathionine in the blood
a decreased concentration of leptin in t: A decreased concentration of leptin in the blood
an increased concentration of threonine: An increased concentration of threonine in the urine
an increased concentration of lysine in: An increased concentration of lysine in the urine
the closed form of spina bifida with inc: The closed form of spina bifida with incomplete closure of a vertebral body with intact overlying skin
when viewed in lateral radiographs, vert: When viewed in lateral radiographs, vertebral bodies have a roughly rectangular configuration. This term applies if the vertebral body appears rounded or oval
an irregular surface of the vertebral en: An irregular surface of the vertebral end plates, which are normally relatively smooth
complete bilateral fractures of the pars: Complete bilateral fractures of the pars interarticularis resulting in the anterior slippage of the vertebra
spondylolysis is an osseous defect of th: Spondylolysis is an osseous defect of the pars interarticularis, thought to be a developmental or acquired stress fracture secondary to chronic low-grade trauma
congenital synostosis between two or mor: Congenital synostosis between two or more adjacent vertebrae (partial or complete fusion of adjacent vertabral bodies)
reduced ability to move the vertebral co: Reduced ability to move the vertebral column with a resulting limitation of neck and trunk flexion
abnormally increased cuvature (anterior: Abnormally increased cuvature (anterior concavity) of the lumbar or cervical spine
a partial dislocation of one or more int: A partial dislocation of one or more intervertebral joints in the cervical vertebral column
abnormality of the dens of the axis, whi: Abnormality of the dens of the axis, which is also known as the odontoid process
developmental hypoplasia of the dens of: Developmental hypoplasia of the dens of the axis
in the orthopedic and radiological liter: In the orthopedic and radiological literature, sagittally cleft vertebra is generally known as a butterfly vertebra
any abnormality of the cervical vertebra: Any abnormality of the cervical vertebral column
a partial dislocation of the atlantoaxia: A partial dislocation of the atlantoaxial joints
generalized weakness or decreased streng: Generalized weakness or decreased strength of the muscles, affecting both distal and proximal musculature
weakness of the limb-girdle muscles (als: Weakness of the limb-girdle muscles (also known as the pelvic and shoulder girdles), that is, lack of strength of the muscles around the shoulders and the pelvis
reduced strength of the axial musculatur: Reduced strength of the axial musculature (i.e., of the muscles of the head and neck, spine, and ribs)
any anomaly in the composite material or: Any anomaly in the composite material or the layered arrangement of the bony skeleton
an abnormal increase in catecholamine co: An abnormal increase in catecholamine concentration in the blood
an abnormality of the process of endocho: An abnormality of the process of endochondral ossification, which is a type of replacement ossification in which bone tissue replaces cartilage
the prothrombin consumption test measure: The prothrombin consumption test measures the formation of intrinsic thromboplastin by determining the residual serum prothrombin after blood clotting is complete. If there is a defect in the process, less prothrombin will be converted to thrombin than normal (less prothrombin is consumed). This test may be abnormal with conditions including deficiency of factors VIII or IX, with circulating anticoagulants, thrombocytopenia
a type of megaloblastic anemia that impr: A type of megaloblastic anemia that improves upon administration of pyrimidine supplements such as uridylic acid and cytidylic acid
the formation of bullae (blisters) with: The formation of bullae (blisters) with cleavage in the lamina lucida layer of the skin
reduced activity of the enzyme glutathio: Reduced activity of the enzyme glutathione synthetase, which catalyzes the last step in the synthesis of glutathione and a deficiency results in low levels of glutathione. Acidosis is due to reduced feedback inhibition of gamma-glutamyl cysteine synthetase in the gamma-glutamyl cycle, which ultimately leads to overproduction and accumulation of 5-oxoproline
an increased concentration of noradrenal: An increased concentration of noradrenaline in the urine
normal peripheral blood lymphocytes, whe: Normal peripheral blood lymphocytes, when stimulated by phytohemagglutinin (PHA) are cytotoxic for homologous and heterologous cells but not for autologous cells in monolayer culture. The cytotoxic effect is thought to be indicative of the immunological competence of the lymphocytes
an increased concentration of alanine in: An increased concentration of alanine in the blood
a reduction in the rate of cholesterol e: A reduction in the rate of cholesterol esterification
an decreased level of renin (pro:0000138: An decreased level of renin (PRO:000013883) in the blood
an increase in the number of chromosome: An increase in the number of chromosome sets per cell in bone marrow cells
an abnormality of amino acid metabolism: An abnormality of amino acid metabolism characterized by a decreased rate of propionyl-CoA carboxylase activity
an increased concentration of threonine: An increased concentration of threonine in the blood
an increased concentration of an amino a: An increased concentration of an amino acid in the urine
a reduction in the level of thymic horom: A reduction in the level of thymic horomone
an increased concentration of cystine wi: An increased concentration of cystine within cells. This finding can be demonstrated on leukocytes, but is not specific to blood cells
decreased concentration of sulfate in th: Decreased concentration of sulfate in the urine
an increased concentration of tryptophan: An increased concentration of tryptophan in the urine
an increase in the amount of very-low-de: An increase in the amount of very-low-density lipoprotein cholesterol in the blood
a left-right reversal (or \"mirror refle: A left-right reversal (or "mirror reflection") of the anatomical location of the viscera of the abdomen
an abnormality of the femoral neck (whic: An abnormality of the femoral neck (which is the process of bone, connecting the femoral head with the femoral shaft)
an abnormality of the femoral head: An abnormality of the femoral head
an abnormal flattening of the proximal e: An abnormal flattening of the proximal epiphysis of the femur
an abnormal enlargement of the proximal: An abnormal enlargement of the proximal epiphysis of the femur
a narrowing of the sacrosciatic notch, i: A narrowing of the sacrosciatic notch, i.e., the deep indentation in the posterior border of the hip bone at the point of union of the ilium and ischium
an abnormal gait pattern that arises fro: An abnormal gait pattern that arises from weakness of the pretibial and peroneal muscles due to a lower motor neuron lesion. Affected patients have footdrop and are unable to dorsiflex and evert the foot. The leg is lifted high on walking so that the toes clear the ground, and there may be a slapping noise when the foot strikes the ground again
a pattern of simultaneous degeneration a: A pattern of simultaneous degeneration and regeneration of axons (see comment)
a loss of myelinated nerve fibers in the: A loss of myelinated nerve fibers in the peripheral nervous system (in general, this finding can be observed on nerve biopsy)
repeated episodes of segmental demyelina: Repeated episodes of segmental demyelination and remyelination lead to the accumulation of supernumerary Schwann cells around axons, which is referred to as onion bulb formation. This finding affects peripheral nerves
atrophic changes of axons of the periphe: Atrophic changes of axons of the peripheral nervous system
a reduced number of large myelinated ner: A reduced number of large myelinated nerve fibers
an axonal neuropathy of peripheral senso: An axonal neuropathy of peripheral sensory nerves
a phenomenon whereby patients are not ab: A phenomenon whereby patients are not able to stand up without the use of the hands owing to weakness of the proximal muscles of the lower limbs
wasting of thenar muscles, which are loc: Wasting of thenar muscles, which are located on palm of the hand at the base of the thumb
sudden and involuntary contractions of o: Sudden and involuntary contractions of one or more muscles
dilated, glial-lined cavity in spinal co: Dilated, glial-lined cavity in spinal cord. This cavity does not communicate with the central canal, and usually is between the dorsal columns unilaterally or bilaterally along the side of the cord
any abnormality of the neuromuscular jun: Any abnormality of the neuromuscular junction, which is the synapse between the motor end plate of a motor neuron and the skeletal muscle fibers
a type of onion bulb formation prominent: A type of onion bulb formation prominently affecting the area of the basal lamina
abnormal sensations such as tingling, pr: Abnormal sensations such as tingling, pricking, or numbness of the skin with no apparent physical cause
an abnormal reduction in the amplitude o: An abnormal reduction in the amplitude of the miniature endplate potentials, i.e. the postsynaptic response to transmitter released from an individual vesicle at the neuromuscular junction
a compound muscle action potential (cmap: A compound muscle action potential (CMAP) is a type of electromyography (EMG). CMAP refers to a group of almost simultaneous action potentials from several muscle fibers in the same area evoked by stimulation of the supplying motor nerve and are recorded as one multipeaked summated action potential. This abnormality refers to a greater than normal decrease in the amplitude during the course of the investigation
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of the proximal metaphysis of the femur
An anomaly of the atlantoaxial joint, i.e., of the joint between the first (atlas) and second (axis) cervical vertebrae
partial dislocation of the atlantoaxial: Partial dislocation of the atlantoaxial joint
an abnormal narrowing of the spinal cana: An abnormal narrowing of the spinal canal
frontal schisis (cleft or cleavage) of v: Frontal schisis (cleft or cleavage) of vertebral bodies
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the back
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the lower back
an abnormality related to a defect of ve: An abnormality related to a defect of vertebral separation during development
reduced amount of myelin in the central: Reduced amount of myelin in the central nervous system resulting from defective myelinogenesis
a type of decreased nerve conduction vel: A type of decreased nerve conduction velocity that affects the motor neuron
an abnormal prolongation of the miniatur: An abnormal prolongation of the miniature endplate potentials, i.e. the postsynaptic response to transmitter released from an individual vesicle at the neuromuscular junction
absence of the achilles reflex (also kno: Absence of the Achilles reflex (also known as the ankle jerk reflex), which can normally be elicited by tapping the tendon is tapped while the foot is dorsiflexed
a reduction in the size of nerve termina: A reduction in the size of nerve terminals
evidence of chronic denervation on elect: Evidence of chronic denervation on electromyography
the presence of characteristic findings: The presence of characteristic findings of denervation on electromyography (fibrillations, positive sharp waves, and giant motor unit potentials)
a reduction in the number of axons in th: A reduction in the number of axons in the peripheral nervous system
reduced speed of conduction of the actio: Reduced speed of conduction of the action potential along a sensory nerve
sudden and involuntary contractions of o: Sudden and involuntary contractions of one or more muscles brought on by exposure to cold temperatures
the presence of axonal regeneration foll: The presence of axonal regeneration following a previous axonal lesion
an increased rate of premature chromosom: An increased rate of premature chromosome condensation
the presence of autoantibodies in the se: The presence of autoantibodies in the serum that react against neutrophils
the presence in the serum of autoantibod: The presence in the serum of autoantibodies directed against thrombocytes
increased concentration of long-chain fa: Increased concentration of long-chain fatty acid
abnormal results of investigations using: Abnormal results of investigations using electromyography (EMG)
the presence of abnormal electromyograph: The presence of abnormal electromyographic patterns indicative of myopathy, such as small-short polyphasic motor unit potentials
a gammopathy in which there is a heterog: A gammopathy in which there is a heterogeneous increase in IgM immunoglobulins
undetectable serum immunoglobulin a leve: Undetectable serum immunoglobulin A level at a value < 5 mg/dL (0.05 g/L)
excretion of peptides conjugated to sial: Excretion of peptides conjugated to sialic acid in the urine
lipofuscin, a generic term applied to au: Lipofuscin, a generic term applied to autofluorescent lipopigment, is a mixture of protein and lipid that accumulates in most aging cells, particularly those involved in high lipid turnover (e.g., the adrenal medulla) or phagocytosis of other cell types (e g., the retinal pigment epithelium or RPE; macrophage). This term pertains if there is an increase in the extraneuronal accumulation of lipofuscin (also known as autofluorescent lipoprotein) more than expected for the age of the patient
abnormally increased movement at the jun: Abnormally increased movement at the junction between the first cervical (atlas) and the second cervical (axis) vertebrae as a result of either a bony or ligamentous anomaly
an abnormality of one or more of the ver: An abnormality of one or more of the vertebrae
defective structure and function of myel: Defective structure and function of myelin sheaths. Dysmyelination is distinguished from demyleination where there is destruction or damage of previously normal myelination
paralysis of voluntary muscles means los: Paralysis of voluntary muscles means loss of contraction due to interruption of one or more motor pathways from the brain to the muscle fibers. Although the word paralysis is often used interchangeably to mean either complete or partial loss of muscle strength, it is preferable to use paralysis or plegia for complete or severe loss of muscle strength, and paresis for partial or slight loss. Motor paralysis results from deficits of the upper motor neurons (corticospinal, corticobulbar, or subcorticospinal). Motor paralysis is often accompanied by an impairment in the facility of movement
hyperexcitability of the neuromuscular s: Hyperexcitability of the neuromuscular system related to abnormally low level of calcium in the blood, resulting in carpopedal or generalized spasms
a type of weakness that occurs after a m: A type of weakness that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions
an abnormality of the primary sensation: An abnormality of the primary sensation that is mediated by peripheral nerves (pain, temperature, touch, vibration, joint position). The word hypoesthesia (or hypesthesia) refers to a reduction in cutaneous sensation to a specific type of testing
an abnormality characterized by disrupti: An abnormality characterized by disruption of the normal functioning of peripheral axons
a segmental pattern of demyelination and: A segmental pattern of demyelination and regeneration (remyelination) affecting peripheral nerves
electromyographic (emg) findings charact: Electromyographic (EMG) findings characteristic of axonal neuropathy, with normal or slightly decreased nerve conduction velocities, normal or slightly prolonged distal latencies, but significantly reduced motor potentials and sensory amplitudes. There may be spontaneous activity upon needle EMG studies, such as increased insertional activity, positive sharp waves, and fibrillation potentials
weakness of the muscles of the arms: Weakness of the muscles of the arms
upturning of the big toe (and sometimes: Upturning of the big toe (and sometimes fanning of the other toes) in response to stimulation of the sole of the foot. If the Babinski sign is present it can indicate damage to the corticospinal tract
an abnormally increased diphosphate(4-): An abnormally increased diphosphate(4-) concentration in the urine. Diphosphate(4-), as ester with two phosphate groups, is also known as pyrophosphate
an elevated concentration of a gonadotro: An elevated concentration of a gonadotropin hormone (stimulating hormone or luteinizing hormone) in the urine, consistent with the diagnosis of primary hypogonadism
the presence of autoantibodies in the se: The presence of autoantibodies in the serum that react against nuclei or nuclear components
an abnormally increased level of immunog: An abnormally increased level of immunoglobulin M in blood
a kind of short stature in which differe: A kind of short stature in which different regions of the body are shortened to differing extents
a mild degree of short stature, more tha: A mild degree of short stature, more than -2 SD but not more than -3 SD from mean corrected for age and sex
a kind of short stature in which differe: A kind of short stature in which different regions of the body are shortened to a comparable extent
a severe degree of short stature, more t: A severe degree of short stature, more than -4 SD from the mean corrected for age and sex
a reduction of the ratio of renal calciu: A reduction of the ratio of renal calcium clearance to creatinine clearance to below 0.01
a type of disproportionate short stature: A type of disproportionate short stature characterized by a short trunk but a average-sized limbs
a reduction in methionine synthase activ: A reduction in methionine synthase activity
formation of crystals owing to an increa: Formation of crystals owing to an increased concentration of orotic acid in the urine
an increased concentration of prostaglan: An increased concentration of prostaglandin in the urine
an increase in the reabsorption of calci: An increase in the reabsorption of calcium by the renal tubulus that is not associated with increased parathormone levels
an increased concentration of glutaric a: An increased concentration of glutaric acid in the blood
an increased concentration of ornithine: An increased concentration of ornithine in the urine
decreased activity of acetaldehyde dehyd: Decreased activity of acetaldehyde dehydrogenase (ADH). ADH and alcohol dehydrogenase (ALDH) are the primary enzymes involved in alcohol metabolism
an abnormal reduction in xanthine dehydr: An abnormal reduction in xanthine dehydrogenase activity
an abnormality of krebs cycle metabolism: An abnormality of Krebs cycle metabolism that is characterized by a decreased rate of fumarate hydratase activity
an abnormally low level of uric acid in: An abnormally low level of uric acid in the blood
an increased rate of iduronate-2-sulfata: An increased rate of iduronate-2-sulfatase activity in the blood
an impairment in the rate and degree to: An impairment in the rate and degree to which platelets aggregate after the addition of an agonist that stimulates platelet clumping.
