Platelet aggregation is measured using aggregometer to measure the optical density of platelet-rich plasma, whereby platelet aggregation causes the plasma to become more transparent
excretion of glycosaminoglycan in the ur: Excretion of glycosaminoglycan in the urine. Glycosaminoglycans are long unbranched polysaccharides consisting of a repeating disaccharide unit
an increased concentration of pyruvate i: An increased concentration of pyruvate in the blood
a functional motor deficit where individ: A functional motor deficit where individuals whose responses to the challenges of exercise fail to achieve levels considered normal for their age and gender
the shoulder, or pectoral, girdle is com: The shoulder, or pectoral, girdle is composed of the clavicles and the scapulae. Shoulder-girdle weakness refers to lack of strength of the muscles attaching to these bones, that is, lack of strength of the muscles around the shoulders
an abnormally increased number of mitoch: An abnormally increased number of mitochondria in the cytoplasma adjacent to the sarcolemma (muscle cell membrane), whereby the mitochondria also possess an abnormal morphology
any abnormality of the soft tissues, inc: Any abnormality of the soft tissues, including both connective tissue (tendons, ligaments, fascia, fibrous tissues, and fat)
localized fluid retention and tissue swe: Localized fluid retention and tissue swelling caused by a compromised lymphatic system, affecting mainly the legs
a condition in which muscles cannot be m: A condition in which muscles cannot be moved quickly without accompanying pain or spasm
atrophy (wasting) affecting primary type: Atrophy (wasting) affecting primary type 2 muscle fibers. This feature in general can only be observed on muscle biopsy
fiber splitting or branching is a common: Fiber splitting or branching is a common finding in human and rat skeletal muscle pathology. Fiber splitting refers to longitudinal halving of the complete fiber, while branching originates from a regenerating end of a necrotic fiber as invaginations of the sarcolemma. In fiber branching, one end of the fiber remains intact as a single entity, while the other end has several branches
an abnormally high degree of muscle fibe: An abnormally high degree of muscle fiber size variation. This phenotypic feature can be observed upon muscle biopsy
rhabdomyolysis induced by a viral infect: Rhabdomyolysis induced by a viral infection
the term dystrophy means abnormal growth: The term dystrophy means abnormal growth. However, muscular dystrophy is used to describe primary myopathies with a genetic basis and a progressive course characterized by progressive skeletal muscle weakness and wasting, defects in muscle proteins, and histological features of muscle fiber degeneration (necrosis) and regeneration. If possible, it is preferred to use other HPO terms to describe the precise phenotypic abnormalities
an decreased concentration of low-densit: An decreased concentration of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol in the blood
the presence of a folate sensitive fragi: The presence of a folate sensitive fragile site at chromosome Xq28
an increased erythrocyte sedimentation r: An increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). The ESR a test that measures the distance that erythrocytes have fallen after one hour in a vertical column of anticoagulated blood under the influence of gravity. The ESR is a nonspecific finding. An elevation may indicate inflammation or may be caused by any condition that elevates fibrinogen
an increased concentration of prostaglan: An increased concentration of prostaglandin E2 in the blood
a decreased rate of glucose-6-phosphate: A decreased rate of glucose-6-phosphate isomerase activity
absence of molybdenum cofactor(2-), a co: Absence of molybdenum cofactor(2-), a cofactor for enzymes including sulfite oxidase, xanthine oxidoreductase, and aldehyde oxidase
a decreased concentration of citrulline: A decreased concentration of citrulline in the blood
increased concentration of total (conjug: Increased concentration of total (conjugated and unconjugated) bilirubin in the blood
a positive response to the regitine bloc: A positive response to the regitine blocking test consisting of a substantial reduction in blood pressure following administration of regitine, indicative of the presence of increased levels of epinephrine and norepinephrine in the circulation, which is seen in pheochromocytoma-associated hypertension
an abnormally increased sodium concentra: An abnormally increased sodium concentration in the cytosol
a phenotypic abnormality that is present: A phenotypic abnormality that is present at birth
onset of disease manifestations in adult: Onset of disease manifestations in adulthood, defined here as at the age of 16 years or later
a type of adult onset with onset of symp: A type of adult onset with onset of symptoms after the age of 60 years
gradual, very slow onset of disease mani: Gradual, very slow onset of disease manifestations
onset of signs or symptoms of disease be: Onset of signs or symptoms of disease between 28 days to one year of life
a type of adult onset with onset of symp: A type of adult onset with onset of symptoms at the age of 40 to 60 years
lack of urothione (the urinary metabolit: Lack of urothione (the urinary metabolite of molybdenum cofactor) in the urine
increased concentration of 4-hydroxyphen: Increased concentration of 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid in the urine
the presence of foam cells that contain: The presence of foam cells that contain lamellar inclusion bodies
increased cytoplasmic staining of fibrob: Increased cytoplasmic staining of fibroblasts with toluidine blue
if positive, the ferric chloride test in: If positive, the ferric chloride test indicates an increased concentration of phenols in the urine or blood
the presence of circulating autoantibodi: The presence of circulating autoantibodies to phospholipids
increased concentration of trimethylamin: Increased concentration of trimethylamine in the urine
onset of signs or symptoms of disease be: Onset of signs or symptoms of disease between the age of 5 and 15 years
onset of signs or symptoms of disease wi: Onset of signs or symptoms of disease within the first 28 days of life
congenital lack of development of the mu: Congenital lack of development of the muscles, which are then replaced by a mixture of dense fat and fibrous tissue
loss (disappearance) of previously prese: Loss (disappearance) of previously present subcutaneous fat tissue in arm or leg
an abnormal reduction in 4-hydroxyphenyl: An abnormal reduction in 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase activity
an increased concentration of adrenaline: An increased concentration of adrenaline in the urine
the presence of free hemoglobin in the u: The presence of free hemoglobin in the urine
abnormal transferrin isoform profile con: Abnormal transferrin isoform profile consistent with a type I congenital disorder of glycosylation. In the traditional nomenclature for congenital disorders of glycosylation, absence of entire glycans was designated type I, and loss of one or more monosaccharides as type II
increased time to coagulation in the par: Increased time to coagulation in the partial thromboplastin time (PTT) test, a measure of the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways. Phospholipid, and activator, and calcium are mixed into an anticoagulated plasma sample, and the time is measured until a thrombus forms
abnormally increased concentration of hy: Abnormally increased concentration of hydrogencarbonate in the urine
an increased concentration of lactic aci: An increased concentration of lactic acid in the urine
the presence of foam cells, a type of ma: The presence of foam cells, a type of macrophage that localizes to fatty deposits on blood vessel walls, where they ingest low-density lipoproteins and become laden with lipids, giving them a foamy appearance
recurring episodes of myoglobinuria, i.e: Recurring episodes of myoglobinuria, i.e., of the presence of myoglobin in the urine. This is usually a consequence of rhabdomyolysis, i.e., of the destruction of muscle tissue
metachromasia (also known as metachromac: Metachromasia (also known as metachromacy) is a characteristic color change which certain aniline dyes exhibit when bound to particular substances or when concentrated in solution. For example, the basic dye toluidine blue becomes distinctly pink when bound to cartilage matrix. In the sense used here, the metachromasia refers to a change in color not observed with normal tissues, anomalous staining with the cationic dyes toluidine blue O and Alcian blue resulting from excessive amounts of the polyanionic glycosaminoglycans
an abnormal reduction in dihydropyrimidi: An abnormal reduction in dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (NADP+) activity
an abnormality of glycoprotein metabolis: An abnormality of glycoprotein metabolism related to a decreased rate of alpha-1,6-mannosylglycoprotein 2-beta-N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase activity
reduced activity of the enzyme beta-gluc: Reduced activity of the enzyme beta-glucosidase, an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of glucosylceramide into ceramide and glucose
a decreased concentration of methionine: A decreased concentration of methionine in the blood
muscular atrophy affecting the muscles o: Muscular atrophy affecting the muscles of the pelvis
the age group in which disease manifesta: The age group in which disease manifestations appear
an abnormality in which the nuclei of sa: An abnormality in which the nuclei of sarcomeres take on an abnormally central localization (or in which this feature is found in an increased proportion of muscle cells)
an abnormally reduced activity of the en: An abnormally reduced activity of the enzyme cytochrome C oxidase in muscle tissue
the presence of multiple deletions of mi: The presence of multiple deletions of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA)
reduced strength and weakness of the mus: Reduced strength and weakness of the muscles of the arms and legs
abnormal protrusion of the scapula away: Abnormal protrusion of the scapula away from the surface of the back
muscular atrophy affecting muscles in th: Muscular atrophy affecting muscles in the distal portions of the extremities
lack of strength of the proximal muscula: Lack of strength of the proximal musculature occurring late in the clinical course
absence of the epiphysis located at the: Absence of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the distal phalanx of the 5th finger
muscular atrophy in the distribution of: Muscular atrophy in the distribution of shoulder girdle and peroneal muscles
generalized (diffuse, unlocalized) amyot: Generalized (diffuse, unlocalized) amyotrophy (muscle atrophy) affecting multiple muscles
a lack of strength of the proximal muscl: A lack of strength of the proximal muscles
enlargement of the muscles of the calf d: Enlargement of the muscles of the calf due to their replacement by connective tissue or fat
sudden and involuntary contractions of o: Sudden and involuntary contractions of one or more muscles brought on by physical exertion
hypertrophy (increase in size) of muscle: Hypertrophy (increase in size) of muscle cells (as opposed to hyperplasia, which refers to an increase in the number of muscle cells)
abnormal cell death involving muscle fib: Abnormal cell death involving muscle fibers usually associated with break in, or absence of, muscle surface fiber membrane and resulting in irreversible damage to muscle fibers
myofibrillar structural changes characte: Myofibrillar structural changes characterized by abnormal intracellular accumulation of the intermediate filament desmin and other proteins
hypertrophy (increase in size) of muscle: Hypertrophy (increase in size) of muscle cells in a generalized (not localized) distribution
weakness of the muscles involved in neck: Weakness of the muscles involved in neck flexion (sternocleidomastoid, longus capitus, longus colli, and scalenus anterior)
amyotrophy affecting the muscles of the: Amyotrophy affecting the muscles of the shoulder girdle
spontaneous, repetitive electrical activ: Spontaneous, repetitive electrical activity demonstrated by electromyography (EMG)
weakness of the quadriceps muscle (that: Weakness of the quadriceps muscle (that is, of the muscle fasciculus of quadriceps femoris)
the lysosomal-vacuolar pathway has a rol: The lysosomal-vacuolar pathway has a role in the controlled intracellular digestion of macromolecules such as protein complexes and organelles. This feature refers to the presence of an abnormally increased number of autophagic vacuoles in muscle tissue
a type of myopathy associated with mitoc: A type of myopathy associated with mitochondrial disease and characterized by findings on biopsy such as ragged red muscle fibers
the occurrence of an unusually high amou: The occurrence of an unusually high amount of muscle pain following exercise
myotonia that occurs after a period of r: Myotonia that occurs after a period of rest and decreases with continuing exercise
a mode of inheritance in which the sever: A mode of inheritance in which the severity of a disorder increases or the age of onset decreases as the disorder is passed from one generation to the next
a type of genetic anticipation observed: A type of genetic anticipation observed predominantly upon transmission from affected males
cases of the disease in question occur w: Cases of the disease in question occur without a previous family history, i.e., as isolated cases without being transmitted from a parent and without other siblings being affected
weakness of the muscles of the pelvic gi: Weakness of the muscles of the pelvic girdle (also known as the hip girdle), that is, lack of strength of the muscles around the pelvis
an abnormal, increased fatiguability of: An abnormal, increased fatiguability of the musculature
recurrent episodes of muscle flaccidity,: Recurrent episodes of muscle flaccidity, a type of paralysis in which a muscle becomes soft and yields to passive stretching
the presence of abnormal muscle fiber si: The presence of abnormal muscle fiber size such that type 1 fibers are smaller than type 2 fibers
a reduced amount of fat tissue in the lo: A reduced amount of fat tissue in the lowest layer of the integument. This feature can be appreciated by a reduced skinfold thickness
mechanical percussion (i.e., striking a: Mechanical percussion (i.e., striking a muscle with a reflex hammer) leads to spreading waves of muscle contractions that begin proximally and spread laterally across the muscle
formation of calcium deposits in any sof: Formation of calcium deposits in any soft tissue
a malformation of the uterus in which th: A malformation of the uterus in which the uterus is present as a paired organ as a result of the failure of fusion of the mullerian ducts during embryogenesis
bruxism is characterized by the grinding: Bruxism is characterized by the grinding of the teeth including the clenching of the jaw and typically occur during sleep
a nevus is a type of hamartoma that is a: A nevus is a type of hamartoma that is a circumscribed stable malformation of the skin
a skin abnormality characterized by redn: A skin abnormality characterized by redness and irritation, with thick, red skin that displays flaky, silver-white patches (scales)
multiple punctate calcifications in the: Multiple punctate calcifications in the dental pulp
pili (from latin pilus, hair) torti (fro: Pili (from Latin pilus, hair) torti (from Latin tortus, twisted) refers to short and brittle hairs that appear flattened and twisted when viewed through a microscope
a body habitus that is tall, slim and un: A body habitus that is tall, slim and underweight, with long legs and long arms (i.e., arm span exceeds height by 5 cm or more)
decreased concentration of homovanillic: Decreased concentration of homovanillic acid (HVA) in the cerebrospinal fluid. HVA is a metabolite of dopamine
multiple small zones of sarcomeric disor: Multiple small zones of sarcomeric disorganization and lack of oxidative activity (known as minicores) in type 1 and type 2 muscle fibers
Multiple small zones of sarcomeric disorganization and lack of oxidative activity (known as minicores) in muscle fibers
developmental hypoplasia (shortening) of: Developmental hypoplasia (shortening) of middle phalanx of toe
irregularity of the iliac crest, which i: Irregularity of the iliac crest, which is the superior border of the wing of the ilium
muscular atrophy affecting the muscles o: Muscular atrophy affecting the muscles of the limb girdle
nemaline rods are abnormal bodies that c: Nemaline rods are abnormal bodies that can occur in skeletal muscle fibers. The rods can be observed on histological analysis of muscle biopsy tissue or upon electron microscopy, where they appear either as extensions of sarcomeric Z-lines, in random array without obvious attachment to Z-lines (often in areas devoid of sarcomeres) or in large clusters localized at the sarcolemma or intermyofibrillar spaces
an abnormal predominance of type i muscl: An abnormal predominance of type I muscle fibers (in general, this feature can only be observed on muscle biopsy)
presence of abnormal vacuoles (membrane-: Presence of abnormal vacuoles (membrane-bound organelles) in the sarcolemma. On histological staining with hematoxylin and eosin, rimmed vacuoles are popcorn-like clear vacuoles with a densely blue rim. The vacuoles are often associated with cytoplasmic and occasionally intranuclear eosinophilic inclusions
an abnormally reduced amount of adipose: An abnormally reduced amount of adipose tissue in the thoracic cavity
death within the first 28 days of life: Death within the first 28 days of life
death in during childhood, defined here: Death in during childhood, defined here as between the ages of 2 and 10 years
death of the fetus in utero after at lea: Death of the fetus in utero after at least 20 weeks of gestation
a variable severity of phenotypic featur: A variable severity of phenotypic features
a situation in which mutation carriers d: A situation in which mutation carriers do not show clinically evident phenotypic abnormalities
a situation in which phenotypic abnormal: A situation in which phenotypic abnormalities become evident with age
a displacement or misalignment of the hu: A displacement or misalignment of the humerus with respect to the other bones of the should joint. Note that a subluxation is a partial dislocation
a partial dislocation of the shoulder jo: A partial dislocation of the shoulder joint
formation of calcified tissue in the sof: Formation of calcified tissue in the soft tissues surrounding the shoulder
a delay in the process of formation and: A delay in the process of formation and maturation of the epiphysis of one or more long bones of the upper limbs
the presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared): The presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared) metaphyseal segment of one or more long bones of the arm
increased width (breadth) of metaphyses: Increased width (breadth) of metaphyses of the arms
an elevation in bone density in one or m: An elevation in bone density in one or more diaphyses of the arms. Sclerosis is normally detected on a radiograph as an area of increased opacity
missing humerus bone associated with con: Missing humerus bone associated with congenital failure of development
a bending or abnormal curvature of the h: A bending or abnormal curvature of the humerus
presence of more than one exostosis orig: Presence of more than one exostosis originating in one or noth humerus bones. An exostosis is a benign growth the projects outward from the bone surface. It is capped by cartilage, and arises from a bone that develops from cartilage
destruction of an area of humerus bone d: Destruction of an area of humerus bone due to a disease process, such as cancer
reduction in diameter of the humerus: Reduction in diameter of the humerus
an anomaly of the humeral epiphysis: An anomaly of the humeral epiphysis
lack of formation of bone in the epiphys: Lack of formation of bone in the epiphysis of the humerus
ossification of the humeral epiphysis at: Ossification of the humeral epiphysis at an earlier age than normal
a delay in the process of formation and: A delay in the process of formation and maturation of the humeral epiphysis
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the humeral epiphysis
increased width of the humeral epiphysis: Increased width of the humeral epiphysis
increased width of the humeral epiphysea: Increased width of the humeral epiphyseal growth plate
fracture or fragmentation at the lateral: Fracture or fragmentation at the lateral portion of the metaphysis of a long bone. The radiographic appearance is that of a small corner of metaphysis separated from the metaphyseal edge by thin linear radiolucency. This feature can be observed in child abuse but fragmented appearance of the metaphysis or facture-like lesions can also be detected in the setting of certain skeletal dysplasias
flaring (increase of width with a splaye: Flaring (increase of width with a splayed appearance) of the humeral metaphysis
an anomaly of the humeral diaphysis: An anomaly of the humeral diaphysis
an abnormal irregularity of the cortical: An abnormal irregularity of the cortical surface of the diaphysis (shaft) of the humerus
increased width of the humeral diaphysis: Increased width of the humeral diaphysis
an abnormal osseous union (fusion) betwe: An abnormal osseous union (fusion) between the ulna and the humerus
a delay in the process of formation and: A delay in the process of formation and maturation of the epiphysis of one or more long bones that are part of the elbow
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of the metaphysis at the distal end of the humerus (at the elbow)
absence of one or more forearm bones ass: Absence of one or more forearm bones associated with congenital failure of development
a descriptive term for a forearm bone th: A descriptive term for a forearm bone that appears to have an additional bone within it on radiography
a bending or abnormal curvature affectin: A bending or abnormal curvature affecting either the radius, the ulna, or both
abnormally wide bone of the skeleton of: Abnormally wide bone of the skeleton of forearm
missing radius bone associated with cong: Missing radius bone associated with congenital failure of development
missing ulna bone associated with congen: Missing ulna bone associated with congenital failure of development
osteosclerosis (increased density relate: Osteosclerosis (increased density related to increased bone mass) of the ulna
reduction in diameter of the ulna: Reduction in diameter of the ulna
an injury of the wrist with displacement: An injury of the wrist with displacement of any of the eight carpal bones
the distal epiphysis (rounded portion of: The distal epiphysis (rounded portion of bone at the far end of the radius distal to the growth plate) has an abnormal cone-shaped appearance
a premature fusion of the epiphyseal pla: A premature fusion of the epiphyseal plates of the radius. Epiphyseal plates are located at the distal and proximal ends of the long bones, in this case of the radius and premature fusion will have an effect on the growh of the radial bone, inhibiting or at least disturbing the normal growth and development of the bone
abnormal increase in width of the epiphy: Abnormal increase in width of the epiphyseal growth plate of the radius
the presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared): The presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared) metaphyseal segment of the radius
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of the metaphysis of the radius
increase in width (breadth) of the radia: Increase in width (breadth) of the radial metaphysis
an anomaly of the radial diaphysis: An anomaly of the radial diaphysis
increase in width of the diaphysis of ra: Increase in width of the diaphysis of radius
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of the metaphysis of the ulna
a sloped configuration of the metaphysis: A sloped configuration of the metaphysis (shaft) of the ulna
increase in width (breadth) of the ulnar: Increase in width (breadth) of the ulnar metaphysis
the total absence of the hand, with no b: The total absence of the hand, with no bony elements distal to the radius or ulna
ossification of hand bones at an earlier: Ossification of hand bones at an earlier age than normal
ossification of hand bones is less advan: Ossification of hand bones is less advanced than would be expected according to age-adjusted norms
pattern of hand-wrist development does n: Pattern of hand-wrist development does not fit the normal sequence of ossification of the individual bones of the hand
osteosclerosis affecting one or more bon: Osteosclerosis affecting one or more bones of the hand
fusion of the hands and feet by a thin m: Fusion of the hands and feet by a thin membrane of skin (scarring) seen in forms of dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa and leading to a "mitten" hand deformity
wrist is bent inward toward the thumb be: Wrist is bent inward toward the thumb because of a congenital defect associated with shortening or absence of the radius
a hand in which the fingers are of nearl: A hand in which the fingers are of nearly equal length and deflected at the first interphalangeal joint, so as to give a forklike shape consisting of separation of the first and second as well as the third and fourth digits
deviated fingers is a term that should b: Deviated fingers is a term that should be used if one or more fingers of the hand are deviated from their normal position, either to the radial or ulnar side. A deviation of a finger can be caused by an abnormal form of one or more of the phalanges of the affected finger, or by a deviation or displacement of one or more phalanges
significant increase in the length and g: Significant increase in the length and girth of most or all of a digit compared to its contralateral digit (if unaffected) or compared to what would be expected for age/body build. The increased girth is accompanied by an increase in the dorso-ventral dimension AND the lateral dimension of the digit
an anomaly of the second finger, also kn: An anomaly of the second finger, also known as the index finger
an abnormal groove on the midline of the: An abnormal groove on the midline of the nose that may extend to the nasal tip
this term groups together three conditio: This term groups together three conditions that presumably represent different degrees of severity of a midline defect of the nose or nasal tip
an abnormal indentation of the skin in t: An abnormal indentation of the skin in the region of the nasal tip
an anomaly of the third finger: An anomaly of the third finger
hypoplasia (congenital reduction in size: Hypoplasia (congenital reduction in size) of the distal phalanx of the third finger
osteolytic defects of the phalanges of t: Osteolytic defects of the phalanges of the 4th (ring) finger
fusion of two or more bones of the 4th f: Fusion of two or more bones of the 4th finger
an abnormality affecting one or both 5th: An abnormality affecting one or both 5th fingers
clinodactyly refers to a bending or curv: Clinodactyly refers to a bending or curvature of the fifth finger in the radial direction (i.e., towards the 4th finger)
abnormality of the phalanges of the 5th: Abnormality of the phalanges of the 5th (little) finger
dissolution or degeneration of bone tiss: Dissolution or degeneration of bone tissue of the phalanges of the 5th finger
fusion of two or more bones of the 5th f: Fusion of two or more bones of the 5th finger
absence or underdevelopment (hypoplasia): Absence or underdevelopment (hypoplasia) of the middle phalanx of the fifth finger
a cone-shaped appearance of the epiphysi: A cone-shaped appearance of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the little finger of the hand, producing a 'ball-in-a-socket' appearance. This epiphysis is located at the proximal end of the phalanx and is normally nearly flat. The related entity 'angel-shaped' epiphysis refers to a pronounced cone-shaped epiphysis in combination with a pseudoepiphysis at the distal end of the phalanx
sclerosis of the epiphysis of the distal: Sclerosis of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the little finger, leading to an increased degree of radiopacity (white or ivory appearance) in X-rays
abnormality of the epiphysis of the midd: Abnormality of the epiphysis of the middle phalanx of the fifth finger. This epiphysis is located on the proximal end of the phalanx
abnormality of the distal phalanx of the: Abnormality of the distal phalanx of the 5th (little) finger
curved appearance of the distal phalanx: Curved appearance of the distal phalanx of the 5th (little) finger
hypoplastic/small distal phalanx of the: Hypoplastic/small distal phalanx of the fifth finger
a partial dislocation of the proximal in: A partial dislocation of the proximal interphalangeal joint of the little finger
congenital absence of a carpal bone: Congenital absence of a carpal bone
the presence of more than the normal num: The presence of more than the normal number of carpal bones
ossification of carpal bones at an abnor: Ossification of carpal bones at an abnormally early age
the bone-in-bone sign is a radiographic: The bone-in-bone sign is a radiographic finding produced by increased sclerosis (abnormally dense bone) occurring intermittently with zones of relatively normal bone density. This term should be used to describe such a finding in the carpal bones
carpal bones with irregular or fragmente: Carpal bones with irregular or fragmented margins
point-shaped (punctate) calcifications a: Point-shaped (punctate) calcifications affecting the carpal bones
formation of bone tissue of scaphoid is: Formation of bone tissue of scaphoid is less than expected for age
osseous fusion of the lunate and triquet: Osseous fusion of the lunate and triquetrum
formation of bone tissue of trapezium is: Formation of bone tissue of trapezium is less than expected for age
formation of bone tissue of trapezoid is: Formation of bone tissue of trapezoid is less than expected for age
a partial dislocation of some or all of: A partial dislocation of some or all of the small joints of the hand
abnormal formation of new bone on the su: Abnormal formation of new bone on the surface of a bone of the hand
an abnormal union between bones or parts: An abnormal union between bones or parts of bones of the hand
for children from birth to 4 years of ag: For children from birth to 4 years of age, the palm width is more than 2 SD below the mean; for children from 4 to 16 years of age the palm width is below the 5th centile; or, the width of the palm appears disproportionately narrow for its length
a partial dislocation affecting some or: A partial dislocation affecting some or all of the metacarpophalangeal joints
an abnormality of the gastric mucous mem: An abnormality of the gastric mucous membrane
an abnormality of the biliary system: An abnormality of the biliary system
the presence of any abnormality affectin: The presence of any abnormality affecting the abdominal wall
the presence of a hernia in the abdomina: The presence of a hernia in the abdominal wall
any abnormality of the skeletal muscle c: Any abnormality of the skeletal muscle cell. Muscle fibers are subdivided into two types. Type I fibers are fatigue-resistant and rich in oxidative enzymes (they stain light with the myosin ATPase reaction), and type II fibers are fast-contracting, fatigue-prone, and rich in glycolytic enzymes (these fibers stain darkly). Normal muscle tissue has a random distribution of type I and type II fibers
involuntary contractions of muscle leadi: Involuntary contractions of muscle leading to involuntary movements of extremities, neck, trunk, or face
thickening of the left ventricle wall wi: Thickening of the left ventricle wall with congenital onset
an abnormality in which the cardiac vent: An abnormality in which the cardiac ventricles depolarize too early as a result of an abnormality of cardiac conduction pathways such as an accessory pathway
an abnormally decreased level of immunog: An abnormally decreased level of immunoglobulin in blood
An abnormally decreased level of immunoglobulin G (IgG) in blood
the presence of a fistula of the vagina: The presence of a fistula of the vagina
the presence of a fistula connecting the: The presence of a fistula connecting the urinary bladder to another organ or the skin. The fistula can involve the bowel, the vagina, or rarely, the skin
a height below that which is expected ac: A height below that which is expected according to age and gender norms. Although there is no universally accepted definition of short stature, many refer to "short stature" as height more than 2 standard deviations below the mean for age and gender (or below the 3rd percentile for age and gender dependent norms)
an abnormal increase or decrease of weig: An abnormal increase or decrease of weight or an abnormal distribution of mass in the body
severe weight loss, wasting of muscle, l: Severe weight loss, wasting of muscle, loss of appetite, and general debility related to a chronic disease
any structural anomaly of the vitreous b: Any structural anomaly of the vitreous body
an abnormality of the anterior segment o: An abnormality of the anterior segment of the eyeball (which comprises the structures in front of the vitreous humour: the cornea, iris, ciliary body, and lens)
an increase in the magnitude or amount o: An increase in the magnitude or amount of ossification of the skull
a reduction in the magnitude or amount o: A reduction in the magnitude or amount of ossification of the skull
the presence of foam cells in the bone m: The presence of foam cells in the bone marrow, generally demonstrated by bone-marrow aspiration or biopsy. Foam cells have a vacuolated appearance due to the presence of complex lipid deposits, giving them a foamy or soap-suds appearance
partial or complete wasting (atrophy) of: Partial or complete wasting (atrophy) of the skin
the presence of excessive redundant myel: The presence of excessive redundant myelin in the peripheral nerve sheath
abnormality of an amino acid metabolic p: Abnormality of an amino acid metabolic process
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of a aromatic amino acid in the blood circulation
Any deviation from the normal concentration of a sulfur amino acid in the blood circulation
abnormality of galactoside metabolism. a: Abnormality of galactoside metabolism. A galactoside is a glycoside (a suger moiety bound to some other moiety) containing galactose
defects in the lysosomal glycosidases or: Defects in the lysosomal glycosidases or specific co-activators, result in accumulation of the substrates, such as glycosphingolipids, including gangliosides in GM1 gangliosidosis (Tay-Sachs disease) and GM2 gangliosidosis (Sandhoff disease)
reduced function of the muscles required: Reduced function of the muscles required to generate subatmospheric pressure in the thoracic cavity during breathing: the diaphragm, the external intercostal and the interchondral part of the internal intercostal muscles
this term applies to all changes in bone: This term applies to all changes in bone mineral density which (depending on severity) can be seen on x-rays as a change in density and or structure of the bone. Changes may affect all bones of the organism, just certain bones or only parts of bones and include decreased mineralisation as may be seen in osteoporosis or increased mineralisation and or ossification as in osteopetrosis, exostoses or any kind of atopic calicfications of different origin and distribution. The overall amount of mineralization of the bone-organ can be measured as the amount of matter per cubic centimeter of bones, usually measured by densitometry of the lumbar spine or hip. The measurements are usually reported as g/cm3 or as a Z-score (the number of standard deviations above or below the mean for the patient's age and sex). Note that measurement with this method does not reflect local changes in other bones, and as such might not be correct with regard the hole bone-organ
a reduction of bone mineral density, tha: A reduction of bone mineral density, that is, of the amount of matter per cubic centimeter of bones
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of a purine in the blood circulation
Any deviation from the normal concentration of a pyrimidine in the blood circulation
Any deviation from the normal concentration of a carboxylic acid in the blood circulation
any deviation from the normal circulatio: Any deviation from the normal circulation of leucine in the blood circulation
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of a fatty acid in the blood circulation
an abnormality of the balance or mainten: An abnormality of the balance or maintenance of the balance of acids and bases in bodily fluids, resulting in an abnormal pH
an abnormal concentration of leptin in t: An abnormal concentration of leptin in the blood
an abnormality of the enteric nervous sy: An abnormality of the enteric nervous system, which comprises two types of ganglia, the myenteric (Auerbach's) and submucosal (Meissner's) plexuses. The enteric nervous system functions to control gut movement, fluid exchange between the gut and its lumen, and local blood flow
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of calcium in the blood circulation
Any deviation from the normal concentration of a nitrogen compound in the blood circulation
Any deviation from the normal concentration of tryptophan in the blood circulation
a type of dystonia that is localized to: A type of dystonia that is localized to a specific part of the body
loss of strength in the arm, leg, and so: Loss of strength in the arm, leg, and sometimes face on one side of the body. Hemiplegia refers to a severe or complete loss of strength, whereas hemiparesis refers to a relatively mild loss of strength
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the nervous system
neoplasms located in the blood and blood: Neoplasms located in the blood and blood-forming tissue (the bone marrow and lymphatic tissue)
an abnormality of alkaline phosphatase a: An abnormality of alkaline phosphatase activity
deposition of calcium salts in the aorti: Deposition of calcium salts in the aortic valve
a pathological narrowing in the region a: A pathological narrowing in the region above the aortic valve associated with restricted left ventricular outflow
abnormal calcification of the mitral val: Abnormal calcification of the mitral valve
underdevelopment of the left side of the: Underdevelopment of the left side of the heart. May include atresia of the aortic or mitral orifice and hypoplasia of the ascending aorta
truncus arteriosus (single great artery: Truncus arteriosus (single great artery leaving the base of the heart, giving rise to the coronary, pulmonary, and systemic arteries) with a short pulmonary trunk arises from the truncus arteriosus, giving rise to both pulmonary arteries
inflammation of the alimentary part of t: Inflammation of the alimentary part of the gastrointestinal system
an inflammation of the colon and small i: An inflammation of the colon and small intestine. However, most conditions are either categorized as Enteritis (inflammation of the small intestine) or Colitis (inflammation of the large intestine)
a colon of abnormally small caliber: A colon of abnormally small caliber
a functional rather than mechanical obst: A functional rather than mechanical obstruction of the intestines, associated with manifestations that resemble those caused by an intestinal obstruction, including distension, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, constipation or diarrhea, in an individual in whom a mechanical blockage has been excluded
polyp-like protrusions which are histolo: Polyp-like protrusions which are histologically hamartomas. These can occur throughout the gastrointestinal tract. Hamartomatous polyps are composed of the normal cellular elements of the gastrointestinal tract, but have a markedly distorted architecture
a kind of congenital defect of the anter: A kind of congenital defect of the anterior abdominal wall in which the intestines are evident through the thin, lax, and protruding abdominal wall in affected infants
abnormal displacement or malposition of: Abnormal displacement or malposition of the anus
an ulcer of the gastrointestinal tract: An ulcer of the gastrointestinal tract
obstruction of the intestine due to abno: Obstruction of the intestine due to abnormally thick meconium
an anomaly in the ability to perceive an: An anomaly in the ability to perceive and distinguish scents (odors)
a decreased sensitivity to odorants (tha: A decreased sensitivity to odorants (that is, a decreased ability to perceive odors)
positioning of the nasal septum to the r: Positioning of the nasal septum to the right or left in contrast to the normal midline position of the nasal septum
an abnormality of the pulmonary artery: An abnormality of the pulmonary artery
an abnormal narrowing or constriction of: An abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches
intermittent claudication is a symptom o: Intermittent claudication is a symptom of peripheral arterial occlusive disease. After having walked over a distance which is individually characteristic, the patients experience pain or cramps in the calves, feet or thighs which typically subsides on standing still
inflammation of a vein associated with v: Inflammation of a vein associated with venous thrombosis (blood clot formation within the vein)
repeated episodes of inflammation of a v: Repeated episodes of inflammation of a vein associated with venous thrombosis (blood clot formation within the vein)
the formation of a blood clot inside an: The formation of a blood clot inside an artery

an abnormality of the cheek- one of two: An abnormality of the cheek- one of two bilateral soft tissue facial structures in the region of the face inferior to the eyes and between the nose and the ear. "Buccal" means relating to the cheek. The cheek is part of the midface
this is a description previously used to: This is a description previously used to describe a facial form characterized by a short, upturned nose, wide mouth, widely spaced eyes, and full cheeks. Because of the imprecision in this definition it is preferable to describe these features precisely. This term is retained because it was often used in the past, but it should not be used for new annotations

excessive growth of the bones of cranium: Excessive growth of the bones of cranium, i.e., of the skull

hepatic failure refers to the inability: Hepatic failure refers to the inability of the liver to perform its normal synthetic and metabolic functions, which can result in coagulopathy and alteration in the mental status of a previously healthy individual. a groove of the skin immediately in fron: A groove of the skin immediately in front of the ear

the anterior fontanelle generally ossifi: The anterior fontanelle generally ossifies by around the 18th month of life. A persistent open anterior fontanelle is diagnosed if closure is delayed beyond this age

a flattened vertebral body shape with re: A flattened vertebral body shape with reduced distance beween the vertebral endplates affecting the thoracic spine
three ossification sites are present in: Three ossification sites are present in typical vertebral bodies (C3-L5): a single ossification center in the vertebral body, and one each in the two neural arches. This term applies if there are additional vertebral ossification centers present during the development and maturation of the spine
the closed form of spina bifida with inc: The closed form of spina bifida with incomplete closure of the vertebra L5 with intact overlying skin

a progressive form of decreased height o: A progressive form of decreased height of the intervertebral disk

underdevelopment of one or more muscles: Underdevelopment of one or more muscles innervated by the facial nerve (the seventh cranial nerve)
reduced strength of the frontalis muscle: Reduced strength of the frontalis muscle (which is located on the forehead)

narrow, paramedian longitudinal depressi: Narrow, paramedian longitudinal depressions in the plantar skin of the forefoot

a type of atrial arrhythmia characterize: A type of atrial arrhythmia characterized by atrial rates of between 240 and 400 beats per minute and some degree of atrioventricular node conduction block. Typically, the ventricular rate is half the atrial rate. In the EKG; atrial flutter waves are observed as sawtooth-like atrial activity. Pathophysiologically, atrial flutter is a form of atrial reentry in which there is a premature electrical impulse creates a self-propagating circuit
episodes of ventricular tachycardia that: Episodes of ventricular tachycardia that have a sudden onset and ending
a form of atrioventricular (av) dissocia: A form of atrioventricular (AV) dissociation (i.e., the atria and the ventricles are under the control of two separate pacemakers) with congenital onset
af that cannot be successfully terminate: AF that cannot be successfully terminated by cardioversion, and longstanding (more than 1 year) AF, where cardioversion is not indicated or has not been attempted, is termed permanent
supraventricular tachycardia (svt) is an: Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is an abnormally increased heart rate (over 100 beats per minute at rest) with origin above the level of the ventricles

underdevelopment or reduced size of the: Underdevelopment or reduced size of the heart right ventricle, often due to a reduced number of cells

myxomatous mitral valve is defined as th: Myxomatous mitral valve is defined as the presence of excess leaflet tissue and leaflet thickening greater than 5 mm, resulting in a prolapse greater than 2 mm into the left atrium on parasternal long axis view
decreased number of head hairs per unit: Decreased number of head hairs per unit area on the anterior region of the scalp
excessive, increased hair growth located: Excessive, increased hair growth located in the elbow region

presence of multiple polyps in the duode: Presence of multiple polyps in the duodenum
the presence of multiple juvenile polyps: The presence of multiple juvenile polyps in the stomach and intestine. The term juvenile polyps refer to a special histopathology and not the age of onset as the polyp might be diagnosed at all ages. The juvenile polyp has a spherical appearance and is microscopically characterized by overgrowth of an oedematous lamina propria with inflammatory cells and cystic glands. Juvenile polyps are a specific type of hamartomatous polyps

sudden onset of thrombocytopenia (reduce: Sudden onset of thrombocytopenia (reduced platelet count) within 5-10 days of the transfusion of blood products. The clinical presentation is post-transfusion purpura (PTP), wigth severe thrmbocytopenia, epistaxis, and hemorrhages

a type of megaloblastic anemia that does: A type of megaloblastic anemia that does not improve upon administration of folate. Since vitamin B12 acts by promoting recycling of folate, administration of vitamin B12 also does not improve this type of anemia

repeated episodes of obstruction of bloo: Repeated episodes of obstruction of blood flow due to an embolus, i.e., blood clot that has traveled from its point of origin within the blood stream

bleeding that persists longer than the n: Bleeding that persists longer than the normal time following a surgical procedure

repeated episodes of the formation of a: Repeated episodes of the formation of a blot clot in a deep vein

the term maturity-onset diabetes of the: The term Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY) was initially used for patients diagnosed with fasting hyperglycemia that could be treated without insulin for more than two years, where the initial diagnosis was made at a young age (under 25 years). Thus, MODY combines characteristics of type 1 diabetes (young age at diagnosis) and type 2 diabetes (less insulin dependence than type 1 diabetes). The term MODY is now most often used to refer to a group of monogenic diseases with these characteristics. Here, the term is used to describe hyperglycemia diagnosed at a young age with no or minor insulin dependency, no evidence of insulin resistence, and lack of evidence of autoimmune destruction of the beta cells
repeated transient episodes of metabolic: Repeated transient episodes of metabolic acidosis, that is, of the buildup of acid or depletion of base due to accumulation of metabolic acids
an intermittent (discontinuous) form of: An intermittent (discontinuous) form of lactic acidemia
recurrent episodes of decreased concentr: Recurrent episodes of decreased concentration of glucose in the blood occurring during the infantile period

a chronic form of lactic acidemia: A chronic form of lactic acidemia

arteriosclerosis (increased thickness, i: Arteriosclerosis (increased thickness, increased stiffness, loss of elasticity) of the small arteries of the brain

formation of a blood clot (thrombus) ins: Formation of a blood clot (thrombus) inside a vein, causing the obstruction of blood flow

excessive bending, twisting, and winding: Excessive bending, twisting, and winding of a cerebral artery
calcification, that is, pathological dep: Calcification, that is, pathological deposition of calcium salts, affecting arteries distributed throughout the body
increased pressure in the pre-hepatic po: Increased pressure in the pre-hepatic portal vein
aortic dilatation refers to a dimension: Aortic dilatation refers to a dimension that is greater than the 95th percentile for the normal person age, sex and body size. In contrast, an aneurysm is defined as a localized dilation of the aorta that is more than 150 percent of predicted (ratio of observed to expected diameter 1.5 or more). Aneurysm should be distinguished from ectasia, which represents a diffuse dilation of the aorta less than 50 percent of normal aorta diameter
atherosclerosis which occurs in a person: Atherosclerosis which occurs in a person with certain risk factors (e.g., SLE, diabetes, smoking, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, family history of early heart disease) at an earlier age than would occur in another person without those risk factors

deterioration or loss of the tissues of: Deterioration or loss of the tissues of the cochlea
ossification affecting the external ear: Ossification affecting the external ear cartilage
any abnormality of the vertebral end pla: Any abnormality of the vertebral end plates, which are the top and bottom portions of the vertebral bodies that interface with the vertebral discs
an abnormality of the sacral bone: An abnormality of the sacral bone
an abnormality of the intervertebral dis: An abnormality of the intervertebral disk
an abnormality of the achilles tendon: An abnormality of the Achilles tendon

an abnormal localized widening (dilatati: An abnormal localized widening (dilatation) of the abdominal aorta

abnormal tortuous (i.e., twisted) form o: Abnormal tortuous (i.e., twisted) form of arteries
abnormal increase in diastolic blood pre: Abnormal increase in diastolic blood pressure
any structural abnormality of a cardiac: Any structural abnormality of a cardiac atrium
