muscular atrophy of distal arm muscles: Muscular atrophy of distal arm muscles
stiffness of the limbs (acondition in wh: Stiffness of the limbs (acondition in which muscles cannot be moved quickly without accompanying pain or spasm) occurring in an asymmetric pattern
a progressive degree of muscular rigidit: A progressive degree of muscular rigidity (continuous contraction of muscles with constant resistance to passive movement)
a diffuse loss of myelin from nerve fibe: A diffuse loss of myelin from nerve fibers in the central nervous system
a form of gray matter heterotopia were t: A form of gray matter heterotopia were the mislocalized gray matter is typically located periventricularly, also sometimes called subependymal heterotopia. Periventricular means beside the ventricles. This is by far the most common location for heterotopia. Subependymal heterotopia present in a wide array of variations. There can be a small single node or a large number of nodes, can exist on either or both sides of the brain at any point along the higher ventricle margins, can be small or large, single or multiple, and can form a small node or a large wavy or curved mass
episodic bouts of involuntary movements: Episodic bouts of involuntary movements with dystonic, choreic, ballistic movements, or a combination thereof. There is no loss of consciousness during the attacks

reduced amount of myelin in the nervous: Reduced amount of myelin in the nervous system resulting from defective myelinogenesis in the peripheral nervous system
a lighter than expected t2 signal on mag: A lighter than expected T2 signal on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the basal ganglia. This term refers to a localized hyperintensity affecting a particular region of the basal ganglia

muscular weakness and atrophy related to: Muscular weakness and atrophy related to loss of the motor neurons of the spinal cord and brainstem
an atypical absence seizure has less abr: An atypical absence seizure has less abrupt onset and offset of loss of awareness than typical absence seizures. They are often associated with other features such as loss of muscle tone of the head, trunk or limbs (often a gradual slump) and subtle myoclonic jerks. Atypical absence seizures often occur in individuals with intellectual impairment.The loss of awareness may be minimal with the patient continuing an activity, but more slowly or with mistakes

weakness of the muscles of the legs: Weakness of the muscles of the legs
any structural anomaly of the limbic sys: Any structural anomaly of the limbic system, a set of midline structures surrounding the brainstem of the mammalian brain, originally described anatomically, e.g., hippocampal formation, amygdala, hypothalamus, cingulate cortex. Although the original designation was anatomical, the limbic system has come to be associated with the system in the brain subserving emotional functions. As such, it is very poorly defined and doesn't correspond closely to the anatomical meaning any longer. [BirnLex]

seizures of which initial semiology indi: Seizures of which initial semiology indicates, or is consistent with, initial activation of only part of one cerebral hemisphere
the presence of atrophy (wasting) of the: The presence of atrophy (wasting) of the cerebrum, also known as the telencephalon, the largest and most highly developed part of the human brain

the presence of atrophy (wasting) of the: The presence of atrophy (wasting) of the corpus callosum

an abnormality of somatosensory evoked p: An abnormality of somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEP), i.e., of the electrical signals of sensation going from the body to the brain in response to a defined stimulus. Recording electrodes are placed over the scalp, spine, and peripheral nerves proximal to the stimulation site. Clinical studies generally use electrical stimulation of peripheral nerves to elicit potentials. SSEP testing determines whether peripheral sensory nerves are able to transmit sensory information like pain, temperature, and touch to the brain. Abnormal SSEPs can result from dysfunction at the level of the peripheral nerve, plexus, spinal root, spinal cord, brain stem, thalamocortical projections, or primary somatosensory cortex

an abnormally increased perspiration on: An abnormally increased perspiration on palms and soles
naevus flammeus localised in the skin of: Naevus flammeus localised in the skin of the forehead
cutaneous lesion that develops as a dry,: Cutaneous lesion that develops as a dry, scaly, red patch that evolves to an indurated and hyperpigmented plaque with adherent scale. Scarring may result in central white patches (loss of pigmentation) and skin atrophy
spontaneous development of hematomas (he: Spontaneous development of hematomas (hematoma) or bruises without significant trauma
telangiectases (small dilated blood vess: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) located near the surface of the skin of the cheeks
telangiectasia (that is, the presence of: Telangiectasia (that is, the presence of small dilated superficial blood vessels) of the oral mucosa
the presence of two to five cafe-au-lait: The presence of two to five cafe-au-lait macules
generalized abnormal accumulation of flu: Generalized abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin, or in one or more cavities of the body
an ichthyosiform abnormality of the skin: An ichthyosiform abnormality of the skin with congenital onset
hemangioma is a benign tumor of the vasc: Hemangioma is a benign tumor of the vascular endothelial cells. This term refers to facial hemangiomas that have a plaque-like morphology
the presence of multiple leiomyomas of t: The presence of multiple leiomyomas of the skin
absence of melanin pigment in various ar: Absence of melanin pigment in various areas, which is found at birth and is permanent. The lesions are known as leucoderma and are often found on the face, trunk, or limbs
a type of blistering that affects the sk: A type of blistering that affects the skin of the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet
lichenification affecting primarily flex: Lichenification affecting primarily flexural areas of the skin
keratinization of the bulbar conjunctiva: Keratinization of the bulbar conjunctiva near the limbus (corneoscleral junction), resulting in a raised spot
reduced number or density of facial hair: Reduced number or density of facial hair
increased amount of keratin (visible as: Increased amount of keratin (visible as white scales) surrounding hair follicles
cutis gyrata of palms and soles: Cutis gyrata of palms and soles
subcutaneous nodules that are located in: Subcutaneous nodules that are located in the vicinity of joints
an ichthyosiform abnormality of the skin: An ichthyosiform abnormality of the skin that presents at birth or shortly thereafter with generalized, erythema, blistering, erosions, and peeling. In the subsequent months, erythema and blistering improves but patients go on to develop hyperkeratotic scaling that is especially prominent along the joint flexures, neck, hands and feet
an abnormality of dermatoglyphs (fingerp: An abnormality of dermatoglyphs (fingerprints), which are present on fingers, palms, toes, and soles
the term collodion baby applies to newbo: The term collodion baby applies to newborns who appear to have an extra layer of skin (known as a collodion membrane) that has a collodion-like quality. It is a descriptive term, not a specific diagnosis or disorder (as such, it is a syndrome). Affected babies are born in a collodion membrane, a shiny waxy outer layer to the skin. This is shed 10-14 days after birth, revealing the main symptom of the disease, extensive scaling of the skin caused by hyperkeratosis. With increasing age, the scaling tends to be concentrated around joints in areas such as the groin, the armpits, the inside of the elbow and the neck. The scales often tile the skin and may resemble fish scales
telangiectases (small dilated blood vess: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) with a diffuse localization
hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the: Hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the hand and the sole of the foot in areas exposed to friction
increased susceptibility to staphylococc: Increased susceptibility to staphylococcal infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of staphylococcal infections
the presence of fewer than normal sweat: The presence of fewer than normal sweat glands
a skin condition characterized by excess: A skin condition characterized by excessive development of keratin in hair follicles, resulting in rough, cone-shaped, elevated papules resulting from closure of hair follicles with a white plug of sebum
tiny bumps of thickened skin (hyperkerat: Tiny bumps of thickened skin (hyperkeratosis) on the palms of the hands
an abnormal accumulation of fluid beneat: An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin on the back of the hands
loose and sagging skin of the fingers: Loose and sagging skin of the fingers
loose, wrinkled skin of hands and feet: Loose, wrinkled skin of hands and feet
loss of hair in the occipital region of: Loss of hair in the occipital region of the scalp with congenital onset
a severe degree of photosensitivity of t: A severe degree of photosensitivity of the skin
presence of a cutaneous lipoma on the fo: Presence of a cutaneous lipoma on the forehead
lack of skin pigmentation (coloring) of: Lack of skin pigmentation (coloring) of the anterior chest
piebaldism is characterized by stable an: Piebaldism is characterized by stable and persistent, well-circumscribed depigmented patches present at birth affecting the skin of the face, trunk, and extremities in a symmetrical distribution
hyperkeratosis affecting the sole of the: Hyperkeratosis affecting the sole of the foot
the presence of six or more cafe-au-lait: The presence of six or more cafe-au-lait spots
seborrheic dermatitis that is not locali: Seborrheic dermatitis that is not localized to any one particular region
hyperkeratosis lenticularis perstans (hl: Hyperkeratosis lenticularis perstans (HLP), also known as Flegel disease, is a keratinization abnormality characterized by small, asymptomatic erythematous papules that leave characteristic punctate bleeding when they become detached. The lesions generally occur symmetrically along the top of the foot and on the legs, appearing more rarely on the arms, forearms, palms, and soles, and even on the oral mucosa
a form of atopic dermatitis with onset i: A form of atopic dermatitis with onset in adulthood characterized by atopic red face, chronic lichenified eczema on the trunk, subacute or psoriasiform dermatitis
increased pigmentation of the skin with: Increased pigmentation of the skin with a netlike (reticular) pattern
a developmental defect resulting in the: A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin on the trunk or the limbs
absence or developmental hypoplasia of t: Absence or developmental hypoplasia of the eccrine sweat glands
the presence of multiple subcutaneous li: The presence of multiple subcutaneous lipoma that cause pain
the distal and proximal transverse palma: The distal and proximal transverse palmar creases are merged into a single transverse palmar crease on both hands
an abnormal accumulation of fluid beneat: An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin, or in one or more cavities of the body because of decreased osmotic pressure of plasma (hypoproteinemia)
naevus flammeus localised in the skin of: Naevus flammeus localised in the skin of the neck. This is one of the most common birthmarks and present in approximately 25% of all newborns
deposition of calcium salts in subcutane: Deposition of calcium salts in subcutaneous tissue (i.e., the the lowermost layer of the integument)
the presence of leiomyoma of the skin: The presence of leiomyoma of the skin
a developmental anomaly characterized by: A developmental anomaly characterized by abnormal smallness of both eyes
an acute condition characterized by sudd: An acute condition characterized by sudden visual loss (usually discovered in the morning), optic disc edema at onset, optic disc-related visual field defects. Nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy can be associated with flame hemorrhages on the swollen disc or nearby neuroretinal layer, and sometimes with nearby cotton-wool exudates
a form of colorblindness in which only t: A form of colorblindness in which only two of the three fundamental colors can be distinguished due to a lack of one of the retinal cone pigments
a nonprogressive (i.e., stationary) form: A nonprogressive (i.e., stationary) form of difficulties with night blindness with congenital onset
tractional retinal detachment at the per: Tractional retinal detachment at the periphery of the retina
lack of eyelashes on the lower lid: Lack of eyelashes on the lower lid
congenital abnormality of the extraocula: Congenital abnormality of the extraocular muscles
a type of cataract with punctate opaciti: A type of cataract with punctate opacities of the lens
sharply demarcated, congenital hyperpigm: Sharply demarcated, congenital hyperpigmentation of thr retinal pigment epithelium
a congenital defect of development chara: A congenital defect of development characterized by absence of the lacrimal gland
opacity of the entire lens nucleus: Opacity of the entire lens nucleus
degenerative changes of the peripheral r: Degenerative changes of the peripheral retina consisting of close-packed tiny cystic spaces at the outer plexiform/inner nuclear retinal level. The degeneration is very common in adult eyes and starts adjacent to the ora serrata and extends circumferentially and posteriorly
an abnormality of the vestibulo-ocular r: An abnormality of the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR). The VOR attempts to keep the image stable on the retina. Ideally passive or active head movements in one direction are compensated for by eye movements of equal magnitude
vitelliform maculopathy is a sharply dem: Vitelliform maculopathy is a sharply demarcated lesion caused by the accumulation of material, often lipofuscin in the subretinal space underlying the macula
narrowing of a tear duct (lacrimal duct): Narrowing of a tear duct (lacrimal duct)
a functional abnormality of the pupil: A functional abnormality of the pupil
absence of the combined rod-and-code res: Absence of the combined rod-and-code response on electroretinogram
an abnormality of the reflex that contro: An abnormality of the reflex that controls the diameter of the pupil, in response to the intensity of light that falls on the retina of the eye
abnormal development (dysgenesis) of the: Abnormal development (dysgenesis) of the anterior segment of the eye globe. These structures are mainly of mesenchymal origin
sudden-onset episode of abnormal, involu: Sudden-onset episode of abnormal, involuntary eye movements
absence of the crystalline lens of the e: Absence of the crystalline lens of the eye as a result of a developmental defect
a malignant melanoma originating within: A malignant melanoma originating within the eye. The tumor originates from the melanocytes in the uvea (which comprises the iris, ciliary body, and choroid)
a chronic irritative conjunctivitis, whi: A chronic irritative conjunctivitis, which commonly presents with general irritation and redness of the eyes, with a burning, dry, or foreign-body sensation of the eyes
cornea plana is an abnormally flat shape: Cornea plana is an abnormally flat shape of the cornea such that the normal protrusion of the cornea from the sclera is missing. The reduced corneal curvature can lead to hyperopia, and a hazy corneal limbus and arcus lipoides may develop at an early age
presence of multiple dilatations (sac-li: Presence of multiple dilatations (sac-like outpouchings) in the blood vessels of the conjunctiva
atrophy (loss or wasting) of the retinal: Atrophy (loss or wasting) of the retinal pigment epithelium observed on fundoscopy or fundus imaging
lack of transparency of the corneal epit: Lack of transparency of the corneal epithelium
abnormal (non-physiological) constrictio: Abnormal (non-physiological) constriction of the pupil of congenital onset
an abnormal reduction in the amount of p: An abnormal reduction in the amount of pigmentation of the iris
abnormal development of the choroid and: Abnormal development of the choroid and retina
pigment migration into the retina in a b: Pigment migration into the retina in a bone-spicule configuration (resembling the nucleated cells within the lacuna of bone)
macular dystrophy is a nonspecific term: Macular dystrophy is a nonspecific term for premature retinal cell aging and cell death, generally confied to the macula in which no clear extrinsic cause is evident
reduced transparency of the stroma of co: Reduced transparency of the stroma of cornea
a scotoma (area of diminished vision wit: A scotoma (area of diminished vision within the visual field) that surrounds the central fixation point
dilatation of small blood vessels of the: Dilatation of small blood vessels of the retina
increased depth of the anterior chamber,: Increased depth of the anterior chamber, i.e., the anteroposterior distance between the cornea and the iris is increased
underdevelopment of the optic disc, that: Underdevelopment of the optic disc, that is of the optic nerve head, where ganglion cell axons exit the eye to form the optic nerve
the presence of an increased number of t: The presence of an increased number of twists and turns of retinal blood vessels (arteries, arterioles, veins, venules)
an impairment of the ability to track ob: An impairment of the ability to track objects with the ocular smooth pursuit system, a class of rather slow eye movements that minimizes retinal target motion
ocular abnormality characterised by prem: Ocular abnormality characterised by premature degeneration of the vitreous and the retina that may be associated with increased risk of retinal detachment
fibrous connective tissue resulting from: Fibrous connective tissue resulting from incomplete healing of a wound (i.e., a scar) located in the choroid and retina or the eye
a type of retinal neovascularization tha: A type of retinal neovascularization that affects the posterior pole of the retina
a type of cataract characterized by punc: A type of cataract characterized by punctate, dust-like opacities within the cortical region of the lens
a type of cataract affecting the posteri: A type of cataract affecting the posterior pole of lens immediately adjacent to ('beneath') the Lens capsule
wasting (atrophy) of the retinal pigment: Wasting (atrophy) of the retinal pigment epithelium present in small, isolated areas
mottled (spotted or blotched with differ: Mottled (spotted or blotched with different shades) pigmentary abnormality of the macula lutea
a cataract that affects the anterior par: A cataract that affects the anterior part of the cortex of the lens
the presence of whitish deposits in the: The presence of whitish deposits in the conjunctiva resembling salt. May be related to calcinosis
abnormal largeness of the eye with an ax: Abnormal largeness of the eye with an axial length > 2.5 standard deviations from population mean
the presence of central, fine, whitish g: The presence of central, fine, whitish granular lesions in the stroma of the cornea. This type of corneal dystrophy is usually asymptomatic and begins in childhood and shows a slow progression. Later in the course, the corneal epithelium and Bowman's layer may be affected. Histologically, the cornea shows a uniform deposition of hyaline material
complete color blindness, a complete ina: Complete color blindness, a complete inability to distinguish colors. Affected persons cannot perceive colors, but only shades of gray
nystagmus consisting of horizontal to-an: Nystagmus consisting of horizontal to-and-fro eye movements of equal velocity
the presence of one or more dendritic co: The presence of one or more dendritic corneal epithelial ulcers characterized by a treelike branching linear pattern with feathery edges and terminal bulbs. Herpetiform corneal ulcers can be identified by fluorescein staining
a form of uveitis that is not associated: A form of uveitis that is not associated with the formation of granulomas
mottling (spots or blotches with differe: Mottling (spots or blotches with different shades) of the retinal pigment epithelium, i.e., localized or generalized fundal pigment granularity associated with processes at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium
a supranuclear gaze palsy is an inabilit: A supranuclear gaze palsy is an inability to look in a horizontal direction as a result of cerebral impairment. There is a loss of the voluntary aspect of eye movements, but, as the brainstem is still intact, all the reflex conjugate eye movements are normal
the presence of distinct colors in the c: The presence of distinct colors in the central (pupillary) zone of the iris than in the mid-peripheral (ciliary) zone
presenile cataract is a kind of cataract: Presenile cataract is a kind of cataract that occurs in early adulthood, that is, at an age that is younger than usual
congenital absence or closure of the ope: Congenital absence or closure of the opening of the lacrimal punctum
paralysis of both the extrinsic and intr: Paralysis of both the extrinsic and intrinsic ocular muscles
inability to see well at night or in poo: Inability to see well at night or in poor light with onset in adulthood
nonprogressive restriction of movement o: Nonprogressive restriction of movement of the external ocular muscles such that the eyes of affected individuals are partially or completely fixed in a strabismic position. Residual eye movements are significantly limited
a kind of cataract that progresses with: A kind of cataract that progresses with age
increased length of the upper eyelashes: Increased length of the upper eyelashes
deposition of hyaline extracellular mate: Deposition of hyaline extracellular material (amyloid) into the vitreous humor, which can manifest as vitreous opacities and reduced visual acuity
punctate opacification (reduced transpar: Punctate opacification (reduced transparency) of the corneal stroma
punched out lesions in the pigmented lay: Punched out lesions in the pigmented layer of the retina
horizontal nystagmus dating from or pres: Horizontal nystagmus dating from or present at birth
deposition of calcium salts in the retin: Deposition of calcium salts in the retina
fibrosis of only some of the external oc: Fibrosis of only some of the external ocular muscles such that the eyes of affected individuals are partially or completely fixed in a strabismic position
the presence of multiple hemangiomas in: The presence of multiple hemangiomas in the choroid. These are generally reddish or orange or can have increased pigmentation maiking them difficult to distinguish from choroidal melanomas
a shape created by an acute downward arc: A shape created by an acute downward arching of the upper eyelid and upward arching of the lower eyelid, toward the medial canthus, which gives the outline of the palpebral fissures the configuration of an almond. Thus, the maximum distance between the fissures is offset from, and medial to, the center point
allergic conjunctivitis is an allergic i: Allergic Conjunctivitis is an allergic inflammation of the conjunctiva
an abnormally slow velocity of horizonta: An abnormally slow velocity of horizontal saccadic eye movements
congenital absence of the extraocular mu: Congenital absence of the extraocular muscles
a type of posterior subcapsular cataract: A type of posterior subcapsular cataract characterized by an iridescent color
reduced pigmentation of the fundus, typi: Reduced pigmentation of the fundus, typically generalised. Fundoscopy may reveal a low level pigment in both RPE and choroid with clear visibility of choroidal vessels (pale/albinoid) or low pigment level in the RPE with deep pigment in choroid so that visible choroidal vessels are separated by deeply pigmented zones (tesselated/tigroid)
failure of attachment of the retina duri: Failure of attachment of the retina during development
bleeding within the vitreous compartment: Bleeding within the vitreous compartment of the eye
chorioretinal atrophy along the retinal: Chorioretinal atrophy along the retinal veins
intraocular pressure that is 2 standard: Intraocular pressure that is 2 standard deviations above the population mean
a type of of patterned retinal dystrophy: A type of of patterned retinal dystrophy that shows a reticular pattern of pigmentation
this corneal dystrophy affects the poste: This corneal dystrophy affects the posterior limiting membrane of the cornea and is characterized by polymorphous plaques of calcium deposits in the deep stromal layers of the cornea, and occasionally by vesicular lesions of the endothelium and edema of the deep corneal stroma
a type of retinal detachment arising due: A type of retinal detachment arising due to a combination of contracting retinal membranes, abnormal vitreoretinal adhesions, and vitreous changes. It is usually seen in the context of diseases that induce a fibrovascular response, e.g. diabetes
a congenital defect resulting in absence: A congenital defect resulting in absence of the lacrimal duct
anomaly of the visual evoked potentials: Anomaly of the visual evoked potentials elicited by a flash stimulus, generally a flash of light subtending an angle of at least 20 degrees of the visual field and presented in a dimly lit room
separation of the inner layers of the re: Separation of the inner layers of the retina (neural retina) from the pigment epithelium occuring near the outer limit (periphery) of the retina
congenital abnormal dilation of the pupi: Congenital abnormal dilation of the pupil on both sides
regional increase in the width (height): Regional increase in the width (height) of the lateral eyebrow
fibrosis of the external ocular muscles: Fibrosis of the external ocular muscles such that the eyes of affected individuals are partially or completely fixed in a strabismic position. Residual eye movements are significantly limited
a type of retinal reticular pigmentation: A type of retinal reticular pigmentation that forms a polygonal, netlike arrangement of hyperpigmented lines forming geometric patterns in the fundus
a form of monochromacy in which vision i: A form of monochromacy in which vision is derived from the remaining preserved blue (S) cones and rod photoreceptors
paralysis of the iris and ciliary appara: Paralysis of the iris and ciliary apparatus
a form of stapes ankylosis with congenit: A form of stapes ankylosis with congenital onset
a fixed reduction in the vertical distan: A fixed reduction in the vertical distance between the upper and lower eyelids with short palpebral fissures on one side only
a subtype of retinitis pigmentosa in whi: A subtype of retinitis pigmentosa in which, instead of the pathology starting in the mid-periphery like typical retinitis pigmentosa, the disease starts in the near periphery closer to the vascular arcades and tends to spare the far periphery
a type of posterior cortical cataract ch: A type of posterior cortical cataract characterized by dense lenticular opacities
chorioretinal atrophy concentrated aroun: Chorioretinal atrophy concentrated around the optic papilla (i.e., the optic nerve head)
a spectrum of fundoscopic appearances ch: A spectrum of fundoscopic appearances characterized by the development of a variety of patterns of deposits predominantly in the macular area. The deposits are typically bilateral, relatively symmetrical, yellow/white and associated with changes at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium. With time, retinal atrophy may occur. A number of pattern dystrophy subtypes have been described including butterfly-shaped dystrophy, reticular dystrophy (net-like pattern) and fundus pulverulentus (granular, mottled pigmentation)
persistence of the hyaloid artery, which: Persistence of the hyaloid artery, which is the embryonic artery that runs from the optic disk to the posterior lens capsule may persist; the site of attachment may form an opacity. The hyaloid artery is a branch of the ophthalmic artery, and usually regresses completely before birth. This features results from a failure of regression of the hyaloid vessel, which supplies the primary vitreous during embryogenesis and normally regresses in the third trimester of pregnancy, leading to a particular form of posterior cataract
a congenital cataract in which opacity i: A congenital cataract in which opacity is limited to layers of the lens external to the nucleus (i.e., the perinuclear region), i.e., between the nuclear and cortical layers of the lens
the presence of developmental dysplasia: The presence of developmental dysplasia of the retina
saccadic undershoot of horizontal saccad: Saccadic undershoot of horizontal saccadic eye movements, i.e., a horizontal saccadic eye movement that has less than the magnitude that would be required to gain fixation of the object
cerulean cataracts are a kind of congeni: Cerulean cataracts are a kind of congenital cataract having peripheral bluish and white opacifications in concentric layers with occasional central lesions arranged radially. Although the opacities may be observed during fetal development and childhood, usually visual acuity is only mildly reduced until adulthood, when lens extraction is generally necessary
horizontal nystagmus made apparent by lo: Horizontal nystagmus made apparent by looking to the right or to the left
a dark-adapted bright flash electroretin: A dark-adapted bright flash electroretinogram in which the b-wave that is of markedly lower amplitude than the associated a-wave (source: Holder GE., Inherited Chorioretinal Dystrophies: A Textbook and Atlas; 2014; p.17; ISBN 978-3-540-69466-3]
blockage of retinal arteriole, generally: Blockage of retinal arteriole, generally associated with interruption of blood flow and oxygen delivery to affected regions of the retina
decreased amount of pigmentation in the: Decreased amount of pigmentation in the macula lutea
retinal exudate within the retinal tissu: Retinal exudate within the retinal tissue itself
underdevelopment of the stroma of iris: Underdevelopment of the stroma of iris
congenital malformation of the lacrimal: Congenital malformation of the lacrimal duct associated with incomplete development of the bony nasolacrimal canal or craniofacial anomalies
loss of peripheral vision with retention: Loss of peripheral vision with retention of central vision, resulting in a constricted circular tunnel-like field of vision
an abnormally reduced response to stimul: An abnormally reduced response to stimulation of the cornea (by touch, foreign body, blowing air). The corneal reflex (also known as the blink reflex, normally results in an involuntary blinking of the eyelids
increased amount of pigmentation in the: Increased amount of pigmentation in the fovea centralis
abnormality of macular or foveal pigment: Abnormality of macular or foveal pigmentation
reduced transparency of the peripheral r: Reduced transparency of the peripheral region of the cornea
microscopic aneurysms of the retinal art: Microscopic aneurysms of the retinal arterioles near the central part of the fundus, visible as small round dark red dots on the retinal surface (not arising from visible vessels) that are by definition less than the diameter of the major optic veins as they cross the optic disc
partial dislocation of the lens in a sup: Partial dislocation of the lens in a superior direction
a form of macular degeneration character: A form of macular degeneration characterized by the presence of multiple cysts in the macula
a type of sutural cataract in which the: A type of sutural cataract in which the opacity follows the posterior Y suture
retinitis pigmentosa inversa is form of: Retinitis pigmentosa inversa is form of retinal degeneration characterized by areas of retinal/chorioretinal degeneration with pigment migration in the macular area (in contrast to retinitis pigmentosa which, at early disease stages, predominantly affects the retinal periphery)
absence of the anterior chamber of the e: Absence of the anterior chamber of the eye owing to a developmental defect
absence or underdevelopment of the lacri: Absence or underdevelopment of the lacrimal gland
decreased retinal arteriolar diameters,: Decreased retinal arteriolar diameters, which may decrease blood flow and slow oxygen delivery to regions of the retina
an arterial or venous retinal vascular a: An arterial or venous retinal vascular anomaly
an abnormality of the line of schwalbe: An abnormality of the line of Schwalbe
an abnormality of an extraocular muscle: An abnormality of an extraocular muscle
an anomaly of the space between the medi: An anomaly of the space between the medial and lateral canthi of the two open eyelids
a wrinkle of retinal tissue projecting o: A wrinkle of retinal tissue projecting outward from the surface of the retina and visible as a line on fundoscopy
absence or underdevelopment of the iris: Absence or underdevelopment of the iris
any abnormality of the blood vessels of: Any abnormality of the blood vessels of the conjunctiva
absence or underdevelopment of the uvea,: Absence or underdevelopment of the uvea, the pigmented middle layer of the eye consisting of the iris and ciliary body together with the choroid
congenital absence or underdevelopment o: Congenital absence or underdevelopment of the fovea centralis
absence or underdevelopment of the anter: Absence or underdevelopment of the anterior segment of the eye
absence or underdevelopment of the lens: Absence or underdevelopment of the lens
an abnormality of the skin characterized: An abnormality of the skin characterized the presence of excessive amounts of dry surface scales on the skin resulting from an abnormality of keratinization
the presence of one or more bullae on th: The presence of one or more bullae on the skin, defined as fluid-filled blisters more than 5 mm in diameter with thin walls
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the skin
increased blood pressure during a pregna: Increased blood pressure during a pregnancy
virilization (deepening of voice, facial: Virilization (deepening of voice, facial hirsutism and scalp hair loss) with onset during pregnancy (usually towards the end of the first trimester) and regression several months post-partum
an abnormally high concentration of seru: An abnormally high concentration of serum conjugated estriol as compared to normal values for gestational-age
the development of pes cavus that is pro: The development of Pes cavus that is progressive with age
reduction in bone mineral density affect: Reduction in bone mineral density affecting any or all of the tarsal bones, seven bones of the foot comprising the calcaneus, talus, cuboid, navicular, and the cuneiform bones
a developmental abnormality characterize: A developmental abnormality characterized by the absence of the fifth metatarsal bone
medial deviation of the great toe owing: Medial deviation of the great toe owing to a deformity of the great toe joint causing the hallux to deviate medially
an outward deviation of the foot at the: An outward deviation of the foot at the talocalcaneal or subtalar joint
the presence of a fifth metatarsal bone: The presence of a fifth metatarsal bone that has not undergone ossification at an age when ossification is usually visible
an anomaly of the fifth metatarsal bone: An anomaly of the fifth metatarsal bone
an overall widening of the spaces betwee: An overall widening of the spaces between the digits
medial deviation of the second toe: Medial deviation of the second toe
delayed maturation and calcification of: Delayed maturation and calcification of any of the tarsal bones, seven bones of the foot comprising the calcaneus, talus, cuboid, navicular, and the cuneiform bones
the presence of unusually deep creases (: The presence of unusually deep creases (ridges/wrinkles) on the skin of sole of foot located between the first and second toe
precocious (accelerated) maturation and: Precocious (accelerated) maturation and calcification of any of the tarsal bones, seven bones of the foot comprising the calcaneus, talus, cuboid, navicular, and the cuneiform bones
osteosclerosis of the endosteal surface: Osteosclerosis of the endosteal surface of the diaphyses (shafts) of the metatarsal bones
bending or curvature of a third toe in t: Bending or curvature of a third toe in the tibial direction (i.e., towards the big toe)
limitation of the ability to bend the to: Limitation of the ability to bend the toes
synostosis of the calcaneus with the nav: Synostosis of the calcaneus with the navicular bone
a congenital deformity characterized by: A congenital deformity characterized by a dorsiflexed, inverted, and adducted foot, i.e., a combination of talipes calcaneus and talipes varus
a two-part calcaneus, a finding that pro: A two-part calcaneus, a finding that probably results from delayed coalescence of two primary calcaneal centers of ossification
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in one or more tarsal bones
defective ossification in an irregular p: Defective ossification in an irregular pattern of the seven bones of the foot comprising the calcaneus, talus, cuboid, navicular, and the cuneiform bones
abnormal plantar flexion of the calcaneu: Abnormal plantar flexion of the calcaneus relative to the longitudinal axis of the tibia. This results in the angle between the long axis of the tibia and the long axis of the heel bone (calcaneus) being greater than 90 degrees
delayed maturation and calcification of: Delayed maturation and calcification of the calcaneus
abnormal response to epinephrine as mani: Abnormal response to epinephrine as manifested by reduced or lacking aggregation of platelets upon addition of epinephrine
elevations of the levels of sgot (serum: Elevations of the levels of SGOT (serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase) and SGPT (serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase) that occur during infections
increased time to coagulation in the pro: Increased time to coagulation in the prothrombin time test, which is a measure of the extrinsic pathway of coagulation. The results of the prothrombin time test are often expressed in terms of the International normalized ratio (INR), which is calculated as a ratio of the patient's prothrombin time (PT) to a control PT standardized for the potency of the thromboplastin reagent developed by the World Health Organization (WHO) using the formula: INR is equal to Patient PT divided by Control PT
episodes of muscle weakness associated w: Episodes of muscle weakness associated with reduced levels of potassium in the blood
excessive amounts of mucopolysaccharide: Excessive amounts of mucopolysaccharide in the urine
hyperapobetalipoproteinemia is defined a: Hyperapobetalipoproteinemia is defined as the combination of a normal low density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol in the face of an increased LDL apolipoprotein B (apoB) protein
an increase in the level of 3-hydroxydic: An increase in the level of 3-hydroxydicarboxylic acid in the urine
a decrease in alkaline phosphatase activ: A decrease in alkaline phosphatase activity measured within leukocytes below detectable levels
an increased concentration of ammonia in: An increased concentration of ammonia in the blood not associated with symptoms such as encephalopathy
abnormally reduced concentration of cort: Abnormally reduced concentration of cortisol in the blood
reduced proportion of helper t cells rel: Reduced proportion of helper T cells relative to the total number of T cells
abnormally decreased rate of beta-galact: Abnormally decreased rate of beta-galactosidase activity. Beta-galactosidase activity can be measured in leukocyte, fibroblast, or plasma
reduced activity of coagulation factor v: Reduced activity of coagulation factor VII. Factor VII is part of the extrinsic coagulation pathway, which is initiated at the site of injury in response to the release of tissue factor (fIII). a reduced ability to secrete prolactin,: A reduced ability to secrete prolactin, a protein hormone that is secreted by lactotrophs in the anterior pituitary and that stimulates mammary gland development and milk production
ketosis-resistant diabetes is a synonym: Ketosis-resistant diabetes is a synonym for type II diabetes. This term thus refers to a form of type II diabetes in which patients are dependent on insulin
insufficient production of steroid hormo: Insufficient production of steroid hormones (primarily cortisol) by the adrenal glands as a result of a primary defect in the glands themselves
amenorrhea due to loss of ovarian functi: Amenorrhea due to loss of ovarian function before the age of 40. Primary ovarian inssuficiency (POI) is a state of female hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. It can manifest as primary amenorrhea with onset before menarche or secondary amenorrhea
a reduced ability to secrete gonadotropi: A reduced ability to secrete gonadotropins, which are protein hormones secreted by gonadotrope cells of the anterior pituitary gland, including the hormones follitropin (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH)

abnormal development of the adrenal glan: Abnormal development of the adrenal gland
a condition in which the pituitary gland: A condition in which the pituitary gland is partially resistant to thyroid hormone, so that it continues to secrete thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) until the blood level of thyroid hormone rises higher than normal

developmental hypoplasia of the adrenal: Developmental hypoplasia of the adrenal medulla

In cartilage, collagen II, actually a collagen II:IX:XI heterofibril, is by far the most important type of collagen. A number of abnormalities may be appreciated by electron micrography or biochemical investigations, including sparse collagen fibers in the cartilage matrix

reduced activity of coagulation factor x: Reduced activity of coagulation factor X. The extrinsic and intrinsic pathways converge at factor X (fX). Factor Xa in turn activates prothrombin (factor II) to thrombin (factor IIa)

a type of renal tubular acidosis charact: A type of renal tubular acidosis characterized by a failure of acid secretion by the alpha intercalated cells of the cortical collecting duct of the distal nephron. The urine cannot be acidified below a pH of 5.3, associated with acidemia and hypokalemia

The adherent platelets then release granules that lead to platelet activation and aggregation

any abnormal process of ossification of: Any abnormal process of ossification of the metatarsal bones, which normally are each ossified from two centers: one for the body, and one for the head (metatarsal II,III,IV, and V) and one for the body and one for the base (metatarsal I). The ossification process begins in the center of the body about the ninth week, and extends toward either extremity. The center for the base of the first metatarsal appears about the third year, and the centers for the heads of the other bones between the fifth and eighth years. They join the bodies between the eighteenth and twentieth years

narrowing (becoming gradually narrower): Narrowing (becoming gradually narrower) of the distance between vertebral pedicles that gets progressively more severe towards to caudal (lower) end of the vertebral column. pulsatile tinnitus is generally classifi: Pulsatile tinnitus is generally classified a kind of objective tinnitus, meaning that it is not only audible to the patient but also to the examiner on auscultation of the auditory canal and/or of surrounding structures with use of an auscultation tube or stethoscope. Usually, pulsatile tinnitus is heard as a lower pitched thumping or booming, a rougher blowing sound which is coincidental with respiration, or as a clicking, higher pitched rhythmic sensation. Pulsatile tinnitus may be associated with vascular abnormalities such as arterioevenous shunts or glomus tumors or the jugular vein, arterial bruits related to a high-riding carotid artery (close to the auditory areas) or carotid stenosis, or venous abnormalities such as a dehiscent jugular bulb or to hypertension. Finally, in some patients, mechanical abnormalities such a spatulous eustachian tubes, palatomyoclonus (small spasms of muscles in the soft palate area), or idiopathic stapedial muscle spasm may represent the underlying cause of pulsatile tinnitus

impotence (inability to develop or maint: Impotence (inability to develop or maintain an erection) resulting from abnormal functioning of the autonomic nervous system

fusion of the proximal phalanx of the 2n: Fusion of the proximal phalanx of the 2nd finger with another bone
triangular shaped proximal phalanx of th: Triangular shaped proximal phalanx of the 2nd finger. A triangular or so called delta shaped phalanx is a typical result after a bracket epiphysis of the affected phalanx
a vestibular schwannoma (also known as a: A vestibular Schwannoma (also known as acoustic neuroma, acoustic neurinoma, or acoustic neurilemoma) is a benign, usually slow-growing tumor that develops from the VIIIth cranial nerve supplying the inner ear
abnormality of the vestibulocochlear ner: Abnormality of the vestibulocochlear nerve, the eighth cranial nerve, which is involved in transmitting sound and equilibrium information from the inner ear to the brain
astrocytoma is a neoplasm of the central: Astrocytoma is a neoplasm of the central nervous system derived from astrocytes. Astrocytes are a type of glial cell, and thus astrocytoma is a subtype of glioma
the presence of a peripheral schwannoma: The presence of a peripheral schwannoma
a hamartoma (a benign, focal malformatio: A hamartoma (a benign, focal malformation consisting of a disorganized mixture of cells and tissues) of the retina
neurofibromas present in a smaller numbe: Neurofibromas present in a smaller number than usually seen in neurofibromatosis type 1
absence of the proximal phalanx of the 2: Absence of the proximal phalanx of the 2nd finger
hypoplasia (congenital reduction in size: Hypoplasia (congenital reduction in size) of the proximal phalanx of the second finger
fusion of the proximal phalanx of the 2n: Fusion of the proximal phalanx of the 2nd finger with the 2nd metacarpal
abnormality of one or all of the epiphys: Abnormality of one or all of the epiphyses of the proximal, and distal phalanges of the thumb and/or the 1st metacarpal
chronic loss of joint motion in the thum: Chronic loss of joint motion in the thumb due to structural changes in non-bony tissue. The term camptodactyly is used if the distal and/or proximal interphalangeal joints are affected
a structural anomaly of one or more phal: A structural anomaly of one or more phalanges of the thumb
displacement of the thumb from its norma: Displacement of the thumb from its normal position
complete duplication of the distal phala: Complete duplication of the distal phalanx of the thumb. On x-ray two separate bones appear side to side
complete duplication of the proximal pha: Complete duplication of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. On x-ray two separate bones appear side to side. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
partail or complete duplication of the f: Partail or complete duplication of the first metacarpal bone
partial duplication of the distal phalan: Partial duplication of the distal phalanx of the thumb. Depending on the severity, the appearance on x-ray can vary from a notched phalanx (the duplicated bone is almost completely fused with the phalanx) to a partially fused appearance of the two bones
complete or partial duplication of the d: Complete or partial duplication of the distal phalanx of the thumb. Depending on the severity, the appearance on x-ray can vary from a notched phalanx (the duplicated bone is almost completely fused with the phalanx), a partially fused appearance of the two bones, or two separate bones appearing side to side
complete or partial duplication of the p: Complete or partial duplication of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. Depending on the severity, the appearance on x-ray can vary from a notched phalanx (the duplicated bone is almost completely fused with the phalanx), a partially fused appearance of the two bones, or two separate bones appearing side to side. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
this term applies if the proximal phalan: This term applies if the proximal phalanx of the thumb is partially duplicated. Depending on the severity, the appearance on x-ray can vary from a notched phalanx (the duplicated bone is almost completely fused with the phalanx) to a partially fused appearance of the two bones. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
complete duplication of the first metaca: Complete duplication of the first metacarpal bone
partial duplication of the first metacar: Partial duplication of the first metacarpal bone
any anomaly of the distal phalanx of thu: Any anomaly of the distal phalanx of thumb
any anomaly of the proximal phalanx of t: Any anomaly of the proximal phalanx of thumb
insertion of thumb at a more distal loca: Insertion of thumb at a more distal location than normal
chronic loss of joint motion of the carp: Chronic loss of joint motion of the carpometacarpal joint of the thumb due to structural changes in non-bony tissue. This joint is formed by the first metacarpal and the trapezial bone and is also called Articulatio carpometacarpalis pollicis, carpometacarpal articulation of thumb, carpometacarpal joint of thumb or first carpometacarpal articulation. Seldom referred to as thumb saddle joint
chronic loss of joint motion of the meta: Chronic loss of joint motion of the metacarpophalangeal joint of the thumb due to structural changes in non-bony tissue. This joint is also called Articulatio metacarpophalangealis pollicis
chronic loss of joint motion of the inte: Chronic loss of joint motion of the interphalangeal joint of the thumb due to structural changes in non-bony tissue. This joint is also called Articulatio interphalangealis pollicis
this term applies if the proximal phalan: This term applies if the proximal phalanx of the thumb is either small/hypoplastic or absent. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
increased width of the proximal phalanx: Increased width of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
bullet-shaped phalanx refers to a short: Bullet-shaped phalanx refers to a short and wide phalanx that tapers distally. Bullet-shaped phalanges lack the normal diaphyseal constriction. This term is used if the proximal phalanx of the thumb is affected
a deviation from the normal straight sha: A deviation from the normal straight shape of the proximal phalanx of the thumb
dissolution or degeneration of bone tiss: Dissolution or degeneration of bone tissue of the proximal phalanx of the thumb
an uneven increase in bone density of th: An uneven increase in bone density of the proximal phalanx of the thumb
fusion of a phalanx of the thumb with an: Fusion of a phalanx of the thumb with another bone
triangular shaped proximal phalanx of th: Triangular shaped proximal phalanx of the thumb
absence of the proximal phalanx of the t: Absence of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
hypoplastic (short) proximal phalanx of: Hypoplastic (short) proximal phalanx of the thumb. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
fusion of the proximal phalanx of the th: Fusion of the proximal phalanx of the thumb with the 1st metacarpal
increased width of the distal phalanx of: Increased width of the distal phalanx of thumb
bullet-shaped phalanx refers to a short: Bullet-shaped phalanx refers to a short and wide phalanx that tapers distally. Bullet-shaped phalanges lack the normal diaphyseal constriction. This term is used if the distal phalanx of the thumb is affected
a deviation from the normal straight sha: A deviation from the normal straight shape of the distal phalanx of the thumb
dissolution or degeneration of bone tiss: Dissolution or degeneration of bone tissue of the distal phalanx of the thumb
an uneven increase in bone density of th: An uneven increase in bone density of the distal phalanx of the thumb
triangular shaped distal phalanx of the: Triangular shaped distal phalanx of the thumb. A triangular or so called delta shaped phalanx is a typical result after a bracket epiphysis of the affected phalanx
absence of the distal/terminal phalanx o: Absence of the distal/terminal phalanx of the thumb
hypoplastic (short) distal phalanx of th: Hypoplastic (short) distal phalanx of the thumb
an abnormal morphology of one or more ph: An abnormal morphology of one or more phalanges of the thumb, with a short and wide phalanx that tapers distally. Bullet-shaped phalanges lack the normal diaphyseal constriction
a deviation from the normal straight sha: A deviation from the normal straight shape of a thumb phalanx
dissolution or degeneration of bone tiss: Dissolution or degeneration of bone tissue of one or more phalanges of the thumb
an uneven increase in bone density of on: An uneven increase in bone density of one or more of the phalanges of the thumb
congenital fusion (ankylosis) of the int: Congenital fusion (ankylosis) of the interphalangeal joint of the thumb
abnormal shape of one or more phalanges: Abnormal shape of one or more phalanges of the thumb such that affected phalanges resemble a triangle
the absence of a phalangeal segment of a: The absence of a phalangeal segment of a thumb
abnormality of the epiphysis of the dist: Abnormality of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb. This epiphysis is located on the proximal end of the phalanx
this term applies if the epiphysis of th: This term applies if the epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the thumb, which is located at the proximal end of the phalanx, does not appear in concordance with gender and age dependant norms as seen on x-rays. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
absence of the epiphysis located at the: Absence of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
an abnormality of the proximal phalanx o: An abnormality of the proximal phalanx of the thumb in which the epiphysis surrounds a phalangeal bone, having a bracket-like form and reaching from the proximal side of a phalanx to the distal side
a cone-shaped appearance of the epiphysi: A cone-shaped appearance of the epiphysis of the middle phalanx of the thumb of the hand, producing a 'ball-in-a-socket' appearance. This epiphysis is located at the proximal end of the phalanx and is normally nearly flat. The related entity 'angel-shaped' epiphysis refers to a pronounced cone-shaped epiphysis in combination with a pseudoepiphysis at the distal end of the phalanx. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
abnormally large size of the epiphysis l: Abnormally large size of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the proximal phalanx of the thumb with respect to age-dependent norms. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the: Epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the thumb having multiple bony fragments
irregular radiographic opacity of the ep: Irregular radiographic opacity of the epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
sclerosis of the epiphysis of the proxim: Sclerosis of the epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the thumb, leading to an increased degree of radiopacity (white or ivory appearance) in X-rays
a pseudoepiphysis (which is a secondary: A pseudoepiphysis (which is a secondary ossification center distinct from the normal epiphysis) of the proximal phalanx of the thumb
abnormally small size of the epiphysis l: Abnormally small size of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the proximal phalanx of the thumb with respect to age-dependent norms
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
a triangular appearance of the epiphysis: A triangular appearance of the epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the thumb of the hand. This epiphysis is located at the proximal end of the phalanx and is normally nearly flat. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2-5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
absence of the epiphysis located at the: Absence of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the distal phalanx of the thumb
an abnormality of the distal phalanx of: An abnormality of the distal phalanx of the thumb in which the epiphysis surrounds a phalangeal bone, having a bracket-like form and reaching from the proximal side of a phalanx to the distal side
a cone-shaped appearance of the epiphysi: A cone-shaped appearance of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb of the hand, producing a 'ball-in-a-socket' appearance. This epiphysis is located at the proximal end of the phalanx and is normally nearly flat. The related entity 'angel-shaped' epiphysis refers to a pronounced cone-shaped epiphysis in combination with a pseudoepiphysis at the distal end of the phalanx
abnormally large size of the epiphysis l: Abnormally large size of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the distal phalanx of the thumb with respect to age-dependent norms
epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the t: Epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb having multiple bony fragments
uneven radiographic opacity of the epiph: Uneven radiographic opacity of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb
sclerosis of the epiphysis of the distal: Sclerosis of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb, leading to an increased degree of radiopacity (white or ivory appearance) in X-rays
a pseudoepiphysis (which is a secondary: A pseudoepiphysis (which is a secondary ossification center distinct from the normal epiphysis) of the distal phalanx of the thumb
abnormally small size of the epiphysis l: Abnormally small size of the epiphysis located at the proximal end of the distal phalanx of the thumb with respect to age-dependent norms
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb
a triangular appearance of the epiphysis: A triangular appearance of the epiphysis of the distal phalanx of the thumb of the hand. This epiphysis is located at the proximal end of the phalanx and is normally nearly flat
absence of one or more epiphyses of the: Absence of one or more epiphyses of the thumb
an abnormality of the thumb in which the: An abnormality of the thumb in which the epiphysis surrounds a phalangeal bone, having a bracket-like form and reaching from the proximal side of a phalanx to the distal side
a cone-shaped appearance of the epiphyse: A cone-shaped appearance of the epiphyses of the thumb, producing a 'ball-in-a-socket' appearance. The related entity 'angel-shaped' epiphysis refers to a pronounced cone-shaped epiphysis in combination with a pseudoepiphysis at the distal end of a phalanx
abnormally large size of the epiphyses o: Abnormally large size of the epiphyses of the thumb with respect to age-dependent norms
epiphysis of the thumb having multiple b: Epiphysis of the thumb having multiple bony fragments
uneven radiographic opacity of the one o: Uneven radiographic opacity of the one or more epiphyses of the thumb
sclerosis of one or more of the epiphyse: Sclerosis of one or more of the epiphyses of the thumb, leading to an increased degree of radiopacity (white or ivory appearance) in X-rays
a pseudoepiphysis (which is a secondary: A pseudoepiphysis (which is a secondary ossification center distinct from the normal epiphysis) of one or more phalanges of the thumb
abnormally small size of one or more of: Abnormally small size of one or more of the epiphyses of the thumb with respect to age-dependent norms
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in one or more of the epiphyses of the thumb
chronic loss of joint motion in one or m: Chronic loss of joint motion in one or more distal interphalangeal joints of the fingers
an abnormal union between bones or parts: An abnormal union between bones or parts of bones of the fingers. The synonymous term "symphalangism of the hand" may be translated as fusions of bones of varying digree, that involve at least one phalangeal bone of the hand. If bony fusions are referred to as "Symphalangism" the fusion occurs in a proximo-distal axis. Fusions of bones of the fingers in a radio-ulnar axis are referred to as "bony" Syndactyly
fusion involving two or more metacarpal: Fusion involving two or more metacarpal bones (A synostosis of the first metacarpal and the proximal phalanx of the thumb can also be observed, note that the first metacarpal bone corresponds to a proximal phalanx)
synostosis (bony fusion) involving one o: Synostosis (bony fusion) involving one or more bones of the carpus (scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate, pisiform)
fusion of the 1st metacarpal with anothe: Fusion of the 1st metacarpal with another bone. In contrast to the proximal phalanges of the digits 2 to 5, the proximal phalanx of the thumb is embryologically equivalent to the middle phalanges of the other digits, whereas the first metacarpal is embryologically of phalangeal origin and as such equivalent to the proximal phalanges of the other digits
chronic cerebrospinal fluid (csf) lympho: Chronic cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) lymphocytosis is defined as the finding, in at least two serial CSF examinations, of more than 5 cells per cubic millimeter
increased concentration of interferon al: Increased concentration of interferon alpha in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
chilblains, also called perniosis, are a: Chilblains, also called perniosis, are an inflammatory skin condition related to an abnormal vascular response to the cold. We are unaware of a reliable estimate of incidence. It typically presents as tender, pruritic red or bluish lesions located symmetrically on the dorsal aspect of the fingers, toes, ears and nose. Less commonly, reports describe involvement of the thighs and buttocks. The lesions present hours after exposure to cold and usually resolve spontaneously in one to three weeks
a benign vascular tumor of the retina wi: A benign vascular tumor of the retina without any neoplastic characteristics
a cystadenoma, an epithelial tumor, that: A cystadenoma, an epithelial tumor, that originates within the head of the epididymis
small nodular masses which originate in: Small nodular masses which originate in the subependymal region of the lateral ventricles and protrude into the ventricular cavity. They may represent subependymal hamartomas of tuberous sclerosis or nodular heterotopia of grey matter
cortical tubers in the brain are hamarto: Cortical tubers in the brain are hamartomatous lesions typically located at the gray-white matter interface, commonly in the frontal and parietal lobes. Cortical tubers are composed of abnormal glial and neural cells, and the size, number, and location vary among patients
a demarcated, largely intraventricular t: A demarcated, largely intraventricular tumor in the region of the foramen of Monro composed of spindle to large plump or ganglion-like cells with eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm and somewhat pleomorphic nuclei with occasional prominent nucleoli. These tumors are almost always associated with tuberous sclerosis
hypomelanotic macules (\"ash leaf spots\: Hypomelanotic macules ("ash leaf spots") are white or lighter patches of skin that may appear anywhere on the body and are caused by a lack of melanin. White ash leaf-shaped macules are considered to be characteristic of tuberous sclerosis
the presence of a sebaceous adenoma with: The presence of a sebaceous adenoma with origin in the sebum secreting cells of the skin
a plaque representing a connective-tissu: A plaque representing a connective-tissue nevus. Connective tissue naevi are uncommon skin lesions that occur when the deeper layers of the skin do not develop correctly or the components of these layers occur in the wrong proportion. Shagreen patches are oval-shaped and nevoid, skin-colored or occasionally pigmented, smooth or crinkled, The word shagreen refers to a type of roughened untanned leather
the presence of small depressions in the: The presence of small depressions in the dental enamel
a lesion located beneath a fingernail or: A lesion located beneath a fingernail or toenail
the presence of fibromata beneath finger: The presence of fibromata beneath finger or toenails
the presence of a neoplasm of the urinar: The presence of a neoplasm of the urinary bladder
the presence of a neoplasm of the kidney: The presence of a neoplasm of the kidney
areas of the retina lacking pigmentation: Areas of the retina lacking pigmentation. Punched out areas of chorioretinal hypopigmentation less than 1 disc diameter in size and tending to be located in the midperiphery of the retina
a benign or malignant neoplasm (tumour): A benign or malignant neoplasm (tumour) originating in striated muscle, either skeletal muscle or cardiac muscle
a benign tumor of cardiac striated muscl: A benign tumor of cardiac striated muscle
a benign tumor of striated muscle: A benign tumor of striated muscle
the presence of a hamartoma of the cereb: The presence of a hamartoma of the cerebrum
a neurofibroma in which schwann cells pr: A neurofibroma in which Schwann cells proliferate inside the nerve sheath, producing an irregularly thickened, distorted, tortuous structure
the presence of a glioma, which is a neo: The presence of a glioma, which is a neoplasm of the central nervous system originating from a glial cell (astrocytes or oligodendrocytes)
a glioma originating in the optic nerve: A glioma originating in the optic nerve or optic chiasm
pseudarthrosis, or \"false joint\" of th: Pseudarthrosis, or "false joint" of the tibia is the result of a developmental failure in the tibia progressing to spontaneous fracture and subsequent fibrous nonunion. The fracture is rarely present at birth but commonly develops during the first 18 months of life
the presence of pigmented, oval and dome: The presence of pigmented, oval and dome-shaped raised hamartomatous nevi of the iris.
nephrosclerosis refers to thickening or: Nephrosclerosis refers to thickening or scarring ("sclerosis") resulting from damage to the renal arterioles, also referred to as arteriosclerosis of the kidney arteries
shoulder joint stiffness is a perceived: Shoulder joint stiffness is a perceived sensation of tightness in shoulders when attempting to move them after a period of inactivity
an abnormality of the spinal dura mater,: An abnormality of the spinal dura mater, which is the outermost of the three layers of the meninges surrounding the spinal cord
presence of arachnoid cysts of the spina: Presence of arachnoid cysts of the spinal canal extradurally in the epidural space
abnormally increased thickness of the na: Abnormally increased thickness of the nasal septum
abnormally increased hair growth in the: Abnormally increased hair growth in the lumbosacral region
increased volume of the earlobe, that is: Increased volume of the earlobe, that is, abnormally prominent ear lobules
absence of the pectoralis major muscle: Absence of the pectoralis major muscle
a bony defect in the skull base: A bony defect in the skull base
complete or nearly complete soft tissue: Complete or nearly complete soft tissue fusion of the alveolar ridges
partial fusion of the upper and lower ey: Partial fusion of the upper and lower eyelid margins by single or multiple bands of tissue
a pterygium (or pterygia) occurring in t: A pterygium (or pterygia) occurring in the popliteal region (the back of the knee)
a pterygium (or pterygia) in the intercr: A pterygium (or pterygia) in the intercrural (groin) region
pyramidal skinfold extending from the ba: Pyramidal skinfold extending from the base to the top of the nails is a rare and distinctive anomaly seen in popliteal pterygia syndrome
pterygium affecting the elbow. this is a: Pterygium affecting the elbow. This is a cutaneous web that can lead to severe flexion contracture of the elbow joint. Antecubital pterygium can be unilateral, bilateral, symmetric, or asysmmetric
anterior schisis (cleft or cleavage) of: Anterior schisis (cleft or cleavage) of vertebral bodies
excessive wrinkling of the skin of the f: Excessive wrinkling of the skin of the face
chronic pain in the limbs with no clear: Chronic pain in the limbs with no clear focal etiology
columella extending inferior to the leve: Columella extending inferior to the level of the nasal base, when viewed from the side
small or missing phalangeal bones of the: Small or missing phalangeal bones of the fingers of the hand
increased width of the phalanges of the: Increased width of the phalanges of the hand
the presence of short and wide phalanges: The presence of short and wide phalanges which taper distally ("bullet shaped")
dissolution or degeneration of bone tiss: Dissolution or degeneration of bone tissue of the phalanges of the hand
uneven (irregular) increase in bone dens: Uneven (irregular) increase in bone density of one or more of the phalanges of the hand
fusion of two or more phalangeal bones o: Fusion of two or more phalangeal bones of the hand
annular constrictions around the digits,: Annular constrictions around the digits, limbs, or trunk, occurring congenitally (sometimes causing intrauterine autoamputation) and also associated with a wide variety of disorders. Constrictive amniotic bands are the result of primary amniotic rupture, which can lead to entanglement of fetal tissue (especially limbs) in fibrous amniotic strands
the absence of all phalanges of all the: The absence of all phalanges of all the digits of a limb and the associated soft tissues
absent thumb, i.e., the absence of both: Absent thumb, i.e., the absence of both phalanges of a thumb and the associated soft tissues
hypoplasia (congenital reduction in size: Hypoplasia (congenital reduction in size) of the thumb
syndactyly with fusion of toes three and: Syndactyly with fusion of toes three and four
horn-like malformations of the iliac cre: Horn-like malformations of the iliac crests with symmetrical bilateral central posterior iliac processes. A characteristic finding in the Nail-Patella syndrome. Iliac horns are visible on X-ray and may be palpable, but are asymptomatic
a zone of darker pigmentation around the: A zone of darker pigmentation around the central part of the iris with a roughly cloverleaf or flower shape
absence or underdevelopment of the bicep: Absence or underdevelopment of the biceps muscle
absence or underdevelopment of the trice: Absence or underdevelopment of the triceps muscle
absence or underdevelopment involving th: Absence or underdevelopment involving the musculature of the thigh
absence or underdevelopment of the quadr: Absence or underdevelopment of the quadriceps muscle
the presence of an abscess located aroun: The presence of an abscess located around the anus
a hemisacral defect involving the sacral: A hemisacral defect involving the sacral vertebrae S2 to S5. In hemisacrum, the first sacral vertebra is intact and there is agenesis involving only S2-S5
presence of a bifid sacral bone: Presence of a bifid sacral bone
a type of sacrococcygeal teratoma locate: A type of sacrococcygeal teratoma located anterior to the sacrum and entirely inside the body (Altman type IV)
congenital developmental defect arising: Congenital developmental defect arising from the primitive branchial apparatus
a congenital fistula in the neck resulti: A congenital fistula in the neck resulting from incomplete closure of a branchial cleft
a branchial cyst is a remnant of embryon: A branchial cyst is a remnant of embryonic development resulting from a failure of obliteration of a branchial cleft and consists of a subcutaneous cystic mass. Cysts are located anterior or posterior to the ear or in the submandibular region
cholesteatoma is a benign but potentiall: Cholesteatoma is a benign but potentially destructive growth consisting of keratinizing epithelium located in the middle ear and/or mastoid process. In cholesteatoma, a skin cyst grows into the middle ear and mastoid. The cyst is not cancerous but can erode tissue and cause destruction of the ear
a goiter that is not associated with fun: A goiter that is not associated with functional thyroid abnormalities
the presence of two or more accessory sp: The presence of two or more accessory spleens
maternal diabetes can either be a gestat: Maternal diabetes can either be a gestational, mostly type 2 diabetes, or a type 1 diabetes.
