complete duplication of a phalanx of sec: Complete duplication of a phalanx of second toe
reduced length of one or more phalanx of: Reduced length of one or more phalanx of second toe as a result of developmental hypoplasia
absence of distal phalanx of the second: Absence of distal phalanx of the second toe as a result of developmental aplasia
reduced length of the distal phalanx of: Reduced length of the distal phalanx of the second toe as a result of developmental hypoplasia
reduced length of the middle phalanx of: Reduced length of the middle phalanx of second toe as a result of developmental hypoplasia
reduced length of the proximal phalanx o: Reduced length of the proximal phalanx of second toe as a result of developmental hypoplasia
a structural abnormality of the interven: A structural abnormality of the interventricular septum
in contrast to forms of polydactyly wher: In contrast to forms of polydactyly where the supernumerary digit (this can either be a rudimentary or a completely 'normal' digit) is either located postaxial (on the fibular side of the foot, next top the little toe), preaxial (on the tibial side of the foot, next to the big toe) or mesoaxial (somewhere central, between big and little toe), a supernumerary digit may also be placed ectopically, meaning anywhere else except post-,meso- or preaxial. In the literature this is sometimes refered to as Disorganisation-like Syndrome (OMIM223200)

In contrast to forms of polydactyly where the supernumerary digit (this can either be a rudimentary or a completely 'normal' digit) is either located postaxial (on the ulnar side of the hand, next to the little finger), preaxial (on the radial side of the hand, next to the thumb) or mesoaxial (somewhere central, between thumb and little finger), a supernumerary digit may also be placed ectopically, meaning anywhere else except post-,meso- or preaxial. In the literature this is sometimes refered to as Disorganisation-like Syndrome (OMIM223200)
a congenital anomaly characterized by th: A congenital anomaly characterized by the presence of supernumerary fingers or toes
a bifid or bifurcated appearance of the: A bifid or bifurcated appearance of the femur as seen on x-rays, possible appearing as a more or less severe bowing of the upper leg. Might be associated with hip dysplasia on the affected side
the retrograde (backwards) flow of blood: The retrograde (backwards) flow of blood through the pulmonary valve into the right ventricle during diastole
an ostium primum atrial septal defect is: An ostium primum atrial septal defect is located in the most anterior and inferior aspect of the atrial septum. The ostium primum refers to an anterior and inferior opening (ostium) within the septum primum, which divides the rudimentary atrium during fetal development. The ostium primum is normally sealed by fusion of the superior and inferior endocardial cushions around 5 weeks' gestation. Ostium primum defects result from a failure of the fusion of the embryologic endocardial cushion and septum primum
a narrowing of the orifice of the tricus: A narrowing of the orifice of the tricuspid valve of the heart
an abnormal connection between the epith: An abnormal connection between the epithelialised surface of the anal canal and the perianal skin
a developmental defect resulting in comp: A developmental defect resulting in complete obliteration of the lumen of the colon. That is, there is an abnormal closure, or atresia of the tubular structure of the colon
an abnormal narrowing of the lumen of th: An abnormal narrowing of the lumen of the esophagus
absence or underdevelopment of the splee: Absence or underdevelopment of the spleen
an abnormal (non-anatomic) location of t: An abnormal (non-anatomic) location of the spleen
pelvic asymmetry refers to asymmetric po: Pelvic asymmetry refers to asymmetric positioning of landmarks on the two sides of the pelvis and may have a structural or functional etiology
the presence of osteophytes (bone spurs): The presence of osteophytes (bone spurs), i.e., of bony projections originating from the acetabulum
an exaggeration of the normal arched for: An exaggeration of the normal arched form of the acetabular roof such that it takes on a steep appearance
an abnormality of the sacrosciatic notch: An abnormality of the sacrosciatic notch, i.e., the deep indentation in the posterior border of the hip bone at the point of union of the ilium and ischium
abnormally increased width of the greate: Abnormally increased width of the greater sacrosciatic notch
hermaphroditism refers to a discrepancy: Hermaphroditism refers to a discrepancy between the morphology of the gonads and that of the external genitalia. In female pseudohermaphroditism, the genotype is female (XX) and the gonads are ovaries, but the external genitalia are virilized
the presence of both ovarian and testicu: The presence of both ovarian and testicular tissues either in the same or in opposite gonads. Affected persons have ambiguous genitalia and may have 46,XX or 46,XY karyotypes or 46,XX/XY mosaicism
abnormality of the female genital system: Abnormality of the female genital system
abnormality of the male genital system: Abnormality of the male genital system
aplasia or developmental hypoplasia of t: Aplasia or developmental hypoplasia of the ovary
aplasia, that is failure to develop, of: Aplasia, that is failure to develop, of the ovary
a developmental disorder characterized b: A developmental disorder characterized by the progressive loss of primordial germ cells in the developing ovaries of an embryo, leading to hypoplastic ovaries composed of wavy connective tissue with occasional clumps of granulosa cells, and frequently mesonephric or hilar cells
the onset of puberty before the age of 8: The onset of puberty before the age of 8 years in girls
absence or underdevelopment of the teste: Absence or underdevelopment of the testes
testis not palpable in the scrotum or in: Testis not palpable in the scrotum or inguinal canal
the presence of more than two testes: The presence of more than two testes
increased urinary excretion of oligosacc: Increased urinary excretion of oligosaccharides (low molecular weight carbohydrate chains composed of at least three monosaccharide subunits), derived from a partial degradation of glycoproteins
an abnormality in the synthesis or catab: An abnormality in the synthesis or catabolism of heme. Heme is composed of ferrous iron and protoporphyrin IX and is an essential molecule as the prosthetic group of hemeproteins such as hemoglobin, myoglobin, mitochondrial and microsomal cytochromes
abnormally increased excretion of porphy: Abnormally increased excretion of porphyrins in the urine
buildups of minerals that form in the ur: Buildups of minerals that form in the urinary bladder
cloacal exstrophy is a severe anterior a: Cloacal exstrophy is a severe anterior abdominal wall defect in which the two hemibladders are visible and are separated by a midline intestinal plate, an omphalocele, and an imperforate anus
absence or underdevelopment of the urina: Absence or underdevelopment of the urinary bladder
persistence of the urachal canal resulti: Persistence of the urachal canal resulting in a canal between the bladder and the umbilicus
the presence of an abnormal connection b: The presence of an abnormal connection between the urethra and another organ or the skin
the presence of an abnormal membrane obs: The presence of an abnormal membrane obstructing the urethra
shortening of the arms predominantly aff: Shortening of the arms predominantly affecting terminal parts of the arm in relation to the upper and middle limb segments
amniotic constriction rings affecting th: Amniotic constriction rings affecting the arms
abnormal increase in size of the upper l: Abnormal increase in size of the upper limbs (due to an increase of the size of cells)
the ability of the elbow joint to move b: The ability of the elbow joint to move beyond its normal range of motion
an abnormality of the hypothenar eminenc: An abnormality of the hypothenar eminence, i.e., of the muscles on the ulnar side of the palm of the hand (i.e., on the side of the little finger)
reduced muscle mass on the ulnar side of: Reduced muscle mass on the ulnar side of the palm, that is, reduction in size of the hypothenar eminence
absence or underdevelopment of the palma: Absence or underdevelopment of the palmar creases
the absence of the major creases of the: The absence of the major creases of the palm (distal transverse crease, proximal transverse crease, or thenar crease)
an abnormality of the creases of the ski: An abnormality of the creases of the skin of palm of hand
a narrow segment of significantly reduce: A narrow segment of significantly reduced circumference of a digit
webbing or fusion of the fingers, involv: Webbing or fusion of the fingers, involving soft parts and including fusion of individual finger bones. Bony fusions are referred to as "bony" Syndactyly if the fusion occurs in a radio-ulnar axis. Fusions of bones of the fingers in a proximo-distal axis are referred to as "Symphalangism"
an abnormally increased length of the me: An abnormally increased length of the metacarpal bones
shortening of the legs predominantly aff: Shortening of the legs predominantly affecting terminal parts of the leg in relation to the upper and middle arm segments
amniotic constriction rings affecting th: Amniotic constriction rings affecting the legs
abnormal increase in size of the lower l: Abnormal increase in size of the lower limbs (due to an increase of the size of cells)
a developmental defect in which the legs: A developmental defect in which the legs are fused together
a developmental defect that occurs if th: A developmental defect that occurs if the two halves of the patella fail to fuse in early childhood
the kneecap normally is located within t: The kneecap normally is located within the groove termed trochlea on the distal femur and can slide up and down in it. Patellar subluxation refers to an unstable kneecap that does not slide centrally within its groove, i.e., a partial dislocation of the patella
the ability of the knee joint to extend: The ability of the knee joint to extend beyond its normal range of motion (the lower leg is moved beyond a straight position with respect to the thigh)
an abnormal limitation of knee joint mob: An abnormal limitation of knee joint mobility
a bending or abnormal curvature of the f: A bending or abnormal curvature of the fibula
duplication of the fibula. this may occu: Duplication of the fibula. This may occur as a part of diplopodia (accessory tarsal or metatarsal bone). Diplopodia with double fibula is an extremely rare condition
an abnormal increase in the length of th: An abnormal increase in the length of the tibia
an abnormal limitation of the mobility o: An abnormal limitation of the mobility of the ankle joint
an abnormality of dermatoglyphs on the t: An abnormality of dermatoglyphs on the toes and soles, i.e., an abnormality of the patterns of ridges of the skin of sole of foot
a difference in size or shape between th: A difference in size or shape between the left and right foot
a condition in which the anterior part o: A condition in which the anterior part of the foot rotates outward away from the midline of the body and the heel remains straight
an ability of the toe joints to move bey: An ability of the toe joints to move beyond their normal range of motion
digits that appear disproportionately lo: Digits that appear disproportionately long compared to the foot
deposition of calcium salts in the pitui: Deposition of calcium salts in the pituitary gland
hypersecretion of one or more pituitary: Hypersecretion of one or more pituitary hormones. This can occur in conditions in which deficiency in the target organ leads to decreased hormonal feedback, or as a primary condition most usually in connection with a pituitary adenoma
absence or underdevelopment of the thymu: Absence or underdevelopment of the thymus
the presence of ectopic thymus tissue. n: The presence of ectopic thymus tissue. Normally, cells of the ventral bud of the third pharyngeal pouch detach and migrate in the eighth gestational week caudally and medially towards the location of the mature thyroid. They migrate further retrosternally into the superior mediastinum. There are two main ways ectopic thymus tissue can develop. Either cells detach along the descensus path and proliferate, thereby forming accessory thymus tissue, or the entire gland fails to descend
an abnormality of the thyroid gland owin: An abnormality of the thyroid gland owing to the presence of a fibrous cyst resulting from the persistence of the thyroglossal duct
an abnormal increase in quantity or stre: An abnormal increase in quantity or strength of fetal movements
gait apraxia affecting the ability to ma: Gait apraxia affecting the ability to make walking movements with the legs
a learning disorder characterized primar: A learning disorder characterized primarily by difficulties in learning to read and spell. Dyslectic children also exhibit a tendency to read words from right to left and to confuse letters such as b and d whose orientation is important for their identification. Children with dyslexia appear to be impaired in phonemic skills (the ability to associate visual symbols with the sounds they represent)
an acquired type of sensory aphasia wher: An acquired type of sensory aphasia where damage to the brain leads to the loss of the ability to read
inability to recognize objects not becau: Inability to recognize objects not because of sensory deficit but because of the inability to combine components of sensory impressions into a complete pattern. Thus, agnosia is a neurological condition which results in an inability to know, to name, to identify, and to extract meaning from visual, auditory, or tactile impressions
an inability or difficulty differentiati: An inability or difficulty differentiating among the fingers of either hand as well as the hands of others
a writing disability in the absence of m: A writing disability in the absence of motor or sensory deficits of the upper extremities, resulting in an impairment in the ability to write regardless of the ability to read and not due to intellectual impairment
inability to recognize the form of objec: Inability to recognize the form of objects by touch without visual input. That is, an impairment in the recognition of objects based only on the texture, size, weight and three-dimensional form of the object in the absence of any major somatosensory deficit
inability to recognize faces of familiar: Inability to recognize faces of familiar persons
the tendency to repeat vocalizations mad: The tendency to repeat vocalizations made by another person
palatal myoclonus is characterized by my: Palatal myoclonus is characterized by myoclonic (rhythmic involuntary jerky) movements of the soft palate
spinal myoclonus is generally due to a t: Spinal myoclonus is generally due to a tumor, infection, injury, or degenerative process of the spinal cord, and is characterized by involuntary rhythmic muscle contractions, usually at a rate of more than one per second. Myoclonus occurs synchronously in several muscles and can be increased in severity and frequency by fatigue or stress, but is usually unaffected by sensory stimuli. Spinal myoclonus ceases during sleep or anesthesia
the combination of pendular nystagmus, h: The combination of pendular nystagmus, head nodding, and torticollis
a paroxysmal, transient loss of memory f: A paroxysmal, transient loss of memory function with preservation of immediate recall and remote memory but with a severe impairment of memory for recent events and ability to retain new information
an intermittent cessation of airflow at: An intermittent cessation of airflow at the mouth and nose during sleep. Apneas of at least 10 seconds are considered important, but persons with sleep apnea may have apneas of 20 seconds to up to 2 or 3 minutes. Patients may have up to 15 events per hour of sleep
sleep apnea resulting from a transient a: Sleep apnea resulting from a transient abolition of the central drive to the ventilatory muscles
an abnormally increased width of the cra: An abnormally increased width of the cranial sutures for age-related norms (generally resulting from delayed closure)
the presence of an abnormally thin calva: The presence of an abnormally thin calvarium
a degree of pneumatization that is incre: A degree of pneumatization that is increased compared to age-related norms
the presence of convoluted folds and fur: The presence of convoluted folds and furrows formed from thickened skin of the scalp, resembling cerebriform pattern. The scalp has convoluted and elevated folds, 1 to 2 cm in thickness. The convolutions generally cannot be flattened by traction
nystagmus due to disturbance of the vest: Nystagmus due to disturbance of the vestibular system; eye movements are rhythmic, with slow and fast components
bursts of large-amplitude multidirection: Bursts of large-amplitude multidirectional saccades without intersaccadic interval
vertical nystagmus may present with eith: Vertical nystagmus may present with either up-beating or down-beating eye movements or both. When present in the straight-ahead position of gaze it is referred to as upbeat nystagmus or downbeat nystagmus
downbeat nystagmus is a type of fixation: Downbeat nystagmus is a type of fixation nystagmus with the fast phase beating in a downward direction. It generally increases when looking to the side and down and when lying prone
fine, rapid twitching of individual musc: Fine, rapid twitching of individual muscle fibers with little or no movement of the muscle as a whole. If a motor neuron or its axon is destroyed, the muscle fibers it innervates undergo denervation atrophy. This leads to hypersensitivity of individual muscle fibers to acetyl choline so that they may contract spontaneously. Isolated activity of individual muscle fibers is generally so fine it cannot be seen through the intact skin, although it can be recorded as a short-duration spike in the EMG
a type of paralysis in which a muscle be: A type of paralysis in which a muscle becomes soft and yields to passive stretching, which results from loss of all or practically all peripheral motor nerves that innervated the muscle. Muscle tone is reduced and the affected muscles undergo extreme atrophy within months of the loss of innervation
a localized myotonic contraction in a mu: A localized myotonic contraction in a muscle in reaction to percussion (tapping with the examiner's finger, a rubber percussion hammer, or a similar object)
paralysis of voluntary muscles means los: Paralysis of voluntary muscles means loss of contraction due to interruption of one or more motor pathways from the brain to the muscle fibers. Although the word paralysis is often used interchangeably to mean either complete or partial loss of muscle strength, it is preferable to use paralysis or plegia for complete or severe loss of muscle strength, and paresis for partial or slight loss. Paralysis due to lesions of the principle motor tracts is related to a lesion in the corticospinal, corticobulbar or brainstem descending (subcorticospinal) neurons
severe or complete weakness of both lowe: Severe or complete weakness of both lower extremities with sparing of the upper extremities
weakness of both lower extremities with: Weakness of both lower extremities with sparing of the upper extremities. Paraplegia refers to a severe or complete loss of strength, whereas paraparesis refers to a relatively mild loss of strength
an acute dystonic reaction with blepharo: An acute dystonic reaction with blepharospasm, periorbital twitches, and protracted fixed staring episodes. There may be a maximal upward deviation of the eyes in the sustained fashion. Oculogyric crisis can be triggered by a number of factors including neuroleptic medications
a soft tissue continuity in the a/p axis: A soft tissue continuity in the A/P axis between two fingers that extends distally to at least the level of the proximal interphalangeal joints, or a soft tissue continuity in the A/P axis between two fingers that lies significantly distal to the flexion crease that overlies the metacarpophalangeal joint of the adjacent fingers
a finger resting on the dorsal surface o: A finger resting on the dorsal surface of an adjacent digit when the hand is at rest
an abnormality of the clivus, which is t: An abnormality of the clivus, which is the inclined bony region of the posterior cranial fossa located between the sella turcica and the foramen magnum
an abnormal vertical orientation of the: An abnormal vertical orientation of the clivus (which normally forms a kind of slope from the sella turcica down to the region of the foramen magnum)
an abnormally wavy surface or edge of th: An abnormally wavy surface or edge of the clavicles
An abnormally wavy surface or edge of the ribs
a midline anterior-posterior cleft of th: A midline anterior-posterior cleft of the epiglottis that involves at least two-thirds of the epiglottic leaf. It is a useful feature for clinical diagnosis because it appears to be very rare in syndromes other than Pallister-Hall-Syndrome and is also rare as an isolated malformation
this term applies if the epiglottis is a: This term applies if the Epiglottis is absent or hypoplastic
a disordered proliferation of mature tis: A disordered proliferation of mature tissues that is native to the site of origin, e.g., exostoses, nevi and soft tissue hamartomas. Although most hamartomas are benign, some histologic subtypes, e.g., neuromuscular hamartoma, may proliferate aggressively such as mesenchymal cystic hamartoma, Sclerosing epithelial hamartoma, Sclerosing metanephric hamartoma
y-shaped metatarsals are the result of a: Y-shaped metatarsals are the result of a partial fusion of two metatarsal bones, with the two arms of the Y pointing in the distal direction. Y-shaped metatarsals may be seen in combination with polydactyly
a hamartoma (disordered proliferation of: A hamartoma (disordered proliferation of mature tissues) which can originate from any tissue of the eye
an abnormally low concentration of serum: An abnormally low concentration of serum alpha-fetoprotein as compared to normal values for gestational-age
an abnormal elevation of phytanic acid: An abnormal elevation of phytanic acid
any abnormality of the proximal epiphysi: Any abnormality of the proximal epiphysis of the femur
the presence of developmental dysplasia: The presence of developmental dysplasia of the femoral head
a cystic lesion originating within the b: A cystic lesion originating within the brain
cone-shaped epiphyses (also known as con: Cone-shaped epiphyses (also known as coned epiphyses) are epiphyses that invaginate into cupped metaphyses. That is, the epiphysis has a cone-shaped distal extension resulting from increased growth of the central portion of the epiphysis relative to its periphery
an alteration of the normally smooth con: An alteration of the normally smooth contour of the epiphysis leading to an irregular appearance
sclerosis of the epiphyses, leading to a: Sclerosis of the epiphyses, leading to an increased degree of radiopacity (white or ivory appearance) in X-rays
reduction in the size or volume of epiph: Reduction in the size or volume of epiphyses
stop of growth at the epiphyseal plate t: Stop of growth at the epiphyseal plate the hyaline cartilage plate in the metaphysis at each end of a long bone, at an earlier than normal age, resulting in growth arrest and shortening of the involved bone
any abnormality of the distal epiphysis: Any abnormality of the distal epiphysis of the femur
any abnormality of the proximal epiphysi: Any abnormality of the proximal epiphysis of the tibia
Any abnormality of the proximal epiphysis of the fibula
any abnormality of the distal epiphysis: Any abnormality of the distal epiphysis of the fibula
any abnormality of the proximal epiphysi: Any abnormality of the proximal epiphysis of the radius
any abnormality of the distal epiphysis: Any abnormality of the distal epiphysis of the radius
any abnormality of the proximal epiphysi: Any abnormality of the proximal epiphysis of the humerus
any abnormality of the distal epiphysis: Any abnormality of the distal epiphysis of the humerus
Any abnormality of the distal epiphysis of the ulna
any abnormality of the proximal epiphysi: Any abnormality of the proximal epiphysis of the ulna
an abnormal predominance of type ii musc: An abnormal predominance of type II muscle fibers (in general, this feature can only be observed on muscle biopsy)
a benign uni- or multicystic, intraosseo: A benign uni- or multicystic, intraosseous tumor of odontogenic origin, with a characteristic lining of parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelium and potential for aggressive, infiltrative behaviour
a chronic epithelioid cell granulomatous: A chronic epithelioid cell granulomatous inflammation of the meibomian gland caused by inflammation of a blocked meibomian gland. A chalazion or meibomian cyst appears as a painless tuberous swelling in the upper lid without loss of eyelashes
an acute purulent infection of the sebac: An acute purulent infection of the sebaceous glands of Zeis at the base of the eyelashes, of the apocrine sweat glands of Moll or the meibomian sebacious glands often caused by staphylococcus infections. Hordeola can either occur as Hordeola externa affecting the sebaceous glands of Zeis or the apocrine sweat glands of Moll or as Hordeola interna affecting the meibomian sebacious glands. In contrast to chalazia, hordeola are extremely painful and can cause extreme local swelling
hordeola externa are acute purulent infe: Hordeola externa are acute purulent infections affecting the sebaceous glands of Zeis or the apocrine sweat glands of Moll, often caused by staphylococcus infections. In contrast to chalazia, hordeola are extremely painfull and can cause extreme local swelling
hordeola interna are acute purulent infe: Hordeola interna are acute purulent infections affecting the meibomian sebacious glands, often caused by staphylococcus infections. In contrast to chalazia (chronic epithelioid cell granulomatous inflammation of the meibomian gland caused by inflammation of a blocked meibomian gland), hordeola are extremely painfull and can cause extreme local swelling
cutaneous skin tags also known as acroch: Cutaneous skin tags also known as acrochorda or fibroepithelial polyps are small benign tumours that may either form secondarily over time primarily in areas where the skin forms creases, such as the neck, armpit or groin or may also be present at birth, in which case they usually occur in the periauricular region
the presence of multiple pits (small, pi: The presence of multiple pits (small, pinpoint-large indentations on the surface of the skin) located on the skin of sole of foot
benign tumors that are composed of fibro: Benign tumors that are composed of fibrous or connective tissue. They can grow in all organs, arising from mesenchyme tissue. The term "fibroblastic" or "fibromatous" is used to describe tumors of the fibrous connective tissue. When the term fibroma is used without modifier, it is usually considered benign, with the term fibrosarcoma reserved for malignant tumors
angiofibroma consist of many often dilat: Angiofibroma consist of many often dilated vessels
the presence of a lung fibroma, a benign: The presence of a lung fibroma, a benign neoplasm that can present as a mass causing airway obstruction, cough, and hemoptysis, or present without symptoms as a solitary pulmonary nodule
the presence of a fibroma of the ovary: The presence of a fibroma of the ovary
a benign biphasic tumor of the breast wi: A benign biphasic tumor of the breast with epithelial and stromal components
prominence of the malar process of the m: Prominence of the malar process of the maxilla and infraorbital area appreciated in profile and from in front of the face
a soft tissue continuity in the anteropo: A soft tissue continuity in the anteroposterior axis between adjacent foot digits that involves at least half of the proximodistal length of one of the two involved digits; or, a soft tissue continuity in the A/P axis between two digits of the foot that does not meet the prior objective criteria
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the skeleton
absence or underdevelopment of the toena: Absence or underdevelopment of the toenail
absence or underdevelopment of the anter: Absence or underdevelopment of the anterior pituitary gland, also known as the adenohypophysis
absence of the pituitary gland resulting: Absence of the pituitary gland resulting from a developmental defect
underdevelopment of the anterior pituita: Underdevelopment of the anterior pituitary gland
facial nerve palsy is a dysfunction of c: Facial nerve palsy is a dysfunction of cranial nerve VII (the facial nerve) that results in inability to control facial muscles on the affected side with weakness of the muscles of facial expression and eye closure. This can either be present in unilateral or bilateral form
any abnormality affecting the cortex of: Any abnormality affecting the cortex of the humerus
any abnormality of a metatarsal bone epi: Any abnormality of a metatarsal bone epiphysis
any abnormality of the epiphyses of the: Any abnormality of the epiphyses of the feet
inability to detect any qualitative olfa: Inability to detect any qualitative olfactory sensation
inability to perceive certain odorants (: Inability to perceive certain odorants (implies that the sense of smell is maintained for other classes of odorants)
reduced ability to detect any qualitativ: Reduced ability to detect any qualitative olfactory sensation
reduced ability to perceive certain odor: Reduced ability to perceive certain odorants (implies that the sense of smell is maintained for other classes of odorants)
the presence of a cleft in the cerebral: The presence of a cleft in the cerebral cortex unilaterally or bilaterally, usually located in the frontal area
a form of amyloidosis that affects the c: A form of amyloidosis that affects the conjunctiva
an abnormally increased level of liver i: An abnormally increased level of liver isoforms of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally increased level of bone is: An abnormally increased level of bone isoforms of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
abnormality of the nasal cavity (the cav: Abnormality of the nasal cavity (the cavity includes and starts at the nares and reaches all the way through to the and includes the choanae, the posterior nasal apertures)
abnormality of the midnasal cavity which: Abnormality of the midnasal cavity which includes the cavity between the nares and the choanae
absence or abnormal closure of the midna: Absence or abnormal closure of the midnasal cavity
abnormal narrowing (stenosis) of the mid: Abnormal narrowing (stenosis) of the midnasal cavity, i.e., of the middle nasal meatus, which in neonates can cause respiratory distress
absence of the distal phalanges of the t: Absence of the distal phalanges of the toes
an abnormal lack of stability of the cer: An abnormal lack of stability of the cervical spine
any abnormal increase or reduction in sk: Any abnormal increase or reduction in skin elasticity
an abnormally increased ability of the s: An abnormally increased ability of the skin to permit light to pass through (translucency) such that subcutaneous structures such as veins display an increased degree of visibility
an abnormal degree of flatness of the al: An abnormal degree of flatness of the Ala of nose, which can be defined as a reduced nasal elevation index (lateral depth of the nose from the tip of the nose to the insertion of the nasal ala in the cheek x 100 divided by the side-to-side breadth of the nasal alae)
an abnormality of the premaxilla (the em: An abnormality of the premaxilla (the embryonic structure that forms the anterior part of the maxilla) causing it to appear relatively small in size compared to the other parts of the maxilla or other facial structures
an abnormality of the meninges, includin: An abnormality of the Meninges, including any abnormality of the Dura mater, the Arachnoid mater, and the Pia mater
an abnormality of the dura mater: An abnormality of the Dura mater
an abnormality of the falx cerebri: An abnormality of the Falx cerebri
a developmental defect characterized by: A developmental defect characterized by aplasia of the Falx cerebri
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in one or more epiphyses
an abnormality of the formation and mine: An abnormality of the formation and mineralization of an epiphysis
patchy (irregular) reduction in bone den: Patchy (irregular) reduction in bone density. This can take on many forms depending on severity and distribution as can be seen on x-rays
patchy (irregular) changes in bone miner: Patchy (irregular) changes in bone mineral density. These changes can either be patchy reduction or increase of mineral density as seen on x-rays. Depending on the pathomechanism and the underlying disease, these changes can either appear solely as reduction or increase or as a combination of both (patches of bone showing an increased density while others are affected by reduction of mineral density)

Patchy (irregular) changes in bone mineral density with patches of bone showing an increased density side to side with patches that are affected by reduction of mineral density. This is sometimes referred to as a moth-eaten appearance on x-rays
an abnormality of the formation and mine: An abnormality of the formation and mineralization of any bone of the skeleton of hand
a developmental defect characterized by: A developmental defect characterized by the absence of the Third ventricle
an abnormality of the diencephalon, whic: An abnormality of the Diencephalon, which together with the cerebrum (telencephalon) makes up the forebrain
a developmental defect characterized by: A developmental defect characterized by fusion of the left and right halves of the thalamus
underdevelopment of the anterior nasal s: Underdevelopment of the anterior nasal spine of maxilla
a congenital defect characterized by abs: A congenital defect characterized by absence of the Maxilla
an abnormality of the zygomatic bone: An abnormality of the zygomatic bone
underdevelopment of the zygomatic bone [: Underdevelopment of the zygomatic bone [UBERON_0001683]. That is, a reduction in size of the zygomatic bone, including the zygomatic process of the temporal bone of the skull, which forms part of the zygomatic arch
an abnormality of the third metatarsal b: An abnormality of the third metatarsal bone
the presence of an abnormal curvature of: The presence of an abnormal curvature of the vertebral column
an abnormality of the formation and mine: An abnormality of the formation and mineralization of any bone of the skeleton of foot
enuresis occurring during waking hours o: Enuresis occurring during waking hours of the day
an abnormally increased level of alkalin: An abnormally increased level of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally increased level of kidney: An abnormally increased level of kidney isoforms of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally increased level of alkalin: An abnormally increased level of alkaline phosphatase, intestinal type in the blood
An abnormally increased level of alkaline phosphatase, placental type in the blood
an abnormally reduced level of alkaline: An abnormally reduced level of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally reduced level of bone isof: An abnormally reduced level of bone isoforms of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally reduced level of kidney is: An abnormally reduced level of kidney isoforms of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally reduced level of liver iso: An abnormally reduced level of liver isoforms of alkaline phosphatase, tissue-nonspecific isozyme in the blood
an abnormally reduced level of alkaline: An abnormally reduced level of alkaline phosphatase, intestinal type in the blood
An abnormally reduced level of alkaline phosphatase, placental type in the blood
a hand or foot with more than five digit: A hand or foot with more than five digits that has a recognizable A/P axis of symmetry. The axis can lie within a normally formed or partially duplicated digit resembling a middle finger, index finger, thumb, toe, or hallux. Alternatively, the axis can be in an interdigital space with a flanking pair of digits that resemble a middle finger, index finger, thumb, toe or hallux. The most lateral digits on each side of the hand typically resemble fifth fingers/toes
mirror image duplication of digits affec: Mirror image duplication of digits affecting the hands only
Mirror image duplication of digits affecting the feet
syndactyly with fusion of fingers two to: Syndactyly with fusion of fingers two to five
a kind of congenital cataract that is ch: A kind of congenital cataract that is characterized by a hollow sphere of punctate opacities involving the fetal nucleus and that usually occurs bilaterally
a lamellar cataract with a pulverulent (: A Lamellar cataract with a pulverulent (punctate, "dust-like" opacities) appearance
a type of congenital cataract in which t: A type of congenital cataract in which the opacity follows the anterior or posterior Y suture
A type of Congenital cataract in which the opacities occupy the subcapsular cortex at the anterior or posterior pole of the lens
a type of anterior polar cataract which: A type of anterior polar cataract which projects as a conical opacity into the anterior chamber
a type of nuclear cataract involving con: A type of nuclear cataract involving congenital dust-like (pulverulent) opacity of the embryonal and fetal nucleus
a nuclear cataract with a triangular for: A nuclear cataract with a triangular form
an abnormal deviation from normal levels: An abnormal deviation from normal levels of immunoglobulins in blood
an increased level of gamma globulin (im: An increased level of gamma globulin (immunoglobulin) in the blood
syndactyly with fusion of fingers one an: Syndactyly with fusion of fingers one and two
syndactyly with fusion of fingers four a: Syndactyly with fusion of fingers four and five
syndactyly with fusion of fingers one to: Syndactyly with fusion of fingers one to three
Syndactyly with fusion of fingers one to four
Syndactyly with fusion of fingers one to five (complete syndactyly of all fingers of the hand)
syndactyly with fusion of the fingers tw: Syndactyly with fusion of the fingers two to four
syndactyly with fusion of fingers three: Syndactyly with fusion of fingers three to five
syndactyly with fusion of toes one and t: Syndactyly with fusion of toes one and two
syndactyly with fusion of toes one to fo: Syndactyly with fusion of toes one to four
syndactyly with fusion of toes one to fi: Syndactyly with fusion of toes one to five (complete syndactyly of all toes of the foot)
syndactyly with fusion of toes two to fo: Syndactyly with fusion of toes two to four
syndactyly with fusion of toes two to fi: Syndactyly with fusion of toes two to five
syndactyly with fusion of toes three to: Syndactyly with fusion of toes three to five
webbing or fusion of the toes, involving: Webbing or fusion of the toes, involving soft parts and including fusion of individual bones of the toes. Bony fusions are referred to as "bony" Syndactyly if the fusion occurs in a tibial-fibular axis. Fusions of bones of the toes in a proximo-distal axis are referred to as "Symphalangism"
an abnormality of the texture of the hai: An abnormality of the texture of the hair
an abnormality of the distribution of ha: An abnormality of the distribution of hair growth
an abnormal hair whorl (that is, a patch: An abnormal hair whorl (that is, a patch of hair growing in the opposite direction of the rest of the hair)
an asymmetriy, i.e., difference in size,: An asymmetriy, i.e., difference in size, shape or position between the left and right ear
an abnormally advanced degree of pneumat: An abnormally advanced degree of pneumatization (i.e., formation of air cells) in the mastoid process with respect to age-dependent norms
abnormal prominence of the corneal nerve: Abnormal prominence of the corneal nerve fibers
rupture of the eyeball not due to trauma: Rupture of the eyeball not due to trauma
a developmental defect characterized by: A developmental defect characterized by absence of the retina
pallor of the perifoveal macula of the r: Pallor of the perifoveal macula of the retina with appearance of a small circular reddish choroid shape as seen through the fovea centralis due to relative transparancy of the macula
this may present as a partial or complet: This may present as a partial or complete duplication of the eyebrows
the eyebrows extend towards - or even al: The eyebrows extend towards - or even all the way down to - the margin of the upper eyelid
nodular changes affecting the eyelids ma: Nodular changes affecting the eyelids may have many different causes such as cystic lesions (chalaziae, hordeolae), lipogranulomas, melanomas, infectious diseases (Molluscum contagiosum) and many more
naevus flammeus localised in the skin of: Naevus flammeus localised in the skin of the eyelid
tumor-like growths that consist of repla: Tumor-like growths that consist of replacement of the medullary bone with fibrous tissue, causing the expansion and weakening of the areas of bone involved. Especially when involving the skull or facial bones, the lesions can cause externally visible deformities. The skull is often, but not necessarily, affected, and any other bone or bones may be involved. Fibrous dysplasia can either effect isolated bones (Monostotic fibrous dysplasia) or also generalized all bones of the body (Polyostotic fibrous dysplasia)
fibrous dysplasia of the bones were lesi: Fibrous dysplasia of the bones were lesions are localized in many bones throughout of the body. Polyostotic fibrous dysplasia is a cardinal feature of McCune-Albright syndrome
Fibrous dysplasia of the bones were lesions are localized in only one bone
osteopoikilosis is a benign, asymptomati: Osteopoikilosis is a benign, asymptomatic sclerotic dysplasia of the bones. It affects both male and female and may be seen at any age. Radiographically sclerotic circular or ovoid lesions are usually symmetrically distributed in a periarticular location. Lesions can increase or decrease in size and number in serial radiographs or even disappear and do not have increased bone radiotracer uptake
a lamellar pattern visible on radiograph: A lamellar pattern visible on radiographs and mainly localized at the metaphyses of the long tubular bones. Pathologic-anatomical studies revealed that these benign signs on x-rays are the result of a juvenile metaphyseal bone necrosis. Calcifications in the necrotic marrow lead to this lamellar or lattice-like appearance
an abnormal accumulation of excess fluid: An abnormal accumulation of excess fluid in the lower extremity resulting in swelling of the feet and extending upward to the lower leg
an abnormal accumulation of fluid beneat: An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin of the arms
diminished length of a metatarsal bone,: Diminished length of a metatarsal bone, with resultant proximal displacement of the associated toe
a developmental abnormality characterize: A developmental abnormality characterized by the absence (aplasia) of a metatarsal bone
absence of a digit or of one or more pha: Absence of a digit or of one or more phalanges of a toe
an abnormal distribution of eyebrow hair: An abnormal distribution of eyebrow hair growth in the medial direction
positioning of a lacrimal punctum other: Positioning of a lacrimal punctum other than at the medial margins of the eyelid
blepharochalasis is characterized by rec: Blepharochalasis is characterized by recurrent, non-painful, nonerythematous episodes of eyelid edema. It has been divided into hypertrophic and atrophic forms. In the hypertrophic form recurrent edema results in orbital fat herniation through a weakened orbital septum. Most patients who have blepharochalasis present in an atrophic condition with atrophy of redundant eyelid skin and superior nasal fat pads
loss of elasticity of the upper and lowe: Loss of elasticity of the upper and lower eyelids causing the skin to sag and bulge
a persistent midline depression of the s: A persistent midline depression of the skin over the fat pad of the chin
midline deficiency of the mandible and s: Midline deficiency of the mandible and some or all overlying tissues
an anomaly of the temporomandibular join: An anomaly of the temporomandibular joint
asymmetry between the left and right sid: Asymmetry between the left and right sides of the maxilla
absence or underdevelopment of the prema: Absence or underdevelopment of the premaxilla
absence of the premaxilla, which is the: Absence of the Premaxilla, which is the embryonic structure that forms the anterior part of the maxilla
an abnormality of the premaxilla [uberon: An abnormality of the premaxilla [UBERON_0002244], the most anterior part of the maxilla that usually bears the central and lateral incisors and includes the anterior nasal spine and inferior aspect of the piriform rim. The premaxilla contains the bone and teeth of the primary palate
prominent positioning of the premaxilla: Prominent positioning of the premaxilla in relation to the rest of the maxilla, the facial skeleton, or mandible. Not necessarily caused by an increase in size (hypertrophy of) the premaxilla
aplasia of a toe. that is, absence of al: Aplasia of a toe. That is, absence of all phalanges of a non-hallux digit of the foot and the associated soft tissues
a chordoma is a tumors that arises from: A chordoma is a tumors that arises from embryonic remnants of the notochord along the length of the neuraxis. Chordomas generally occur in the sacrum, intracranially at the clivus, or along the spinal axis
insertion of the posterior columella bel: Insertion of the posterior columella below the nasal base
hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the: Hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the hand
deposition of calcium salts in a tissue: Deposition of calcium salts in a tissue or location in which calcification does not normally occur
the presence of a cyst, fistula, or absc: The presence of a cyst, fistula, or abscess in the sacrococcygeal region (gluteal crease) characteristically accompanied by hair and skin folds
a sinus in the coccygeal region (the reg: A sinus in the coccygeal region (the region of the intergluteal cleft). A pilonidal sinus often contains hair and skin debris
a hair-containing cyst or sinus usually: A hair-containing cyst or sinus usually in the coccygeal region
a developmental defect characterized by: A developmental defect characterized by abnormal connection of one or more pulmonary veins to the superior or inferior vena cava, the right atrium, or the coronary sinus, resulting in a left-to-right shunt of oxygenated blood
a form of anomalous pulmonary venous ret: A form of anomalous pulmonary venous return in which not all pulmonary veins drain abnormally. Partial anomalous pulmonary venous return frequently involves one or both of the veins from one lung
the presence of an additional membrane i: The presence of an additional membrane in the left or right cardiac atrium which results in the subdivision of the affected atrium (and thus in total three atria, whence the name)
a developmental defect of the aortic arc: A developmental defect of the aortic arch system in which the trachea and esophagus are completely encircled by connected segments of the aortic arch and its branches. This occurs if the normal process of regression and persistence of the bilateral embryonic aortic arches fails
marked widening of the trachea and major: Marked widening of the trachea and major bronchi that may be predispose to chronic respiratory tract infection
marked widening of the major bronchi tha: Marked widening of the major bronchi that may be predispose to chronic respiratory tract infection
the presence of an abnormally large pelv: The presence of an abnormally large pelvis
over-sensitivity to certain frequency ra: Over-sensitivity to certain frequency ranges of sound
skin dimples are cutaneous indentations: Skin dimples are cutaneous indentations that are the result of tethering of the skin to underlying structures (bone) causing an indentation
a subtype of skin dimples occurring in t: A subtype of skin dimples occurring in the shoulder region
redness of the skin, caused by hyperemia: Redness of the skin, caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the uterus
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of a: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of a gonad
the presence of a neoplasm of the urinar: The presence of a neoplasm of the urinary system
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the genital system
the presence of a neoplasm of the testis: The presence of a neoplasm of the testis
underdevelopment of the interstitial (le: Underdevelopment of the interstitial (Leydig) cells of the testis. These cells produce testosterone
hypertrophy or overdevelopment of the in: Hypertrophy or overdevelopment of the interstitial (Leydig) cells of the testis. These cells produce testosterone
a digit with two nails, with at least so: A digit with two nails, with at least some soft tissue between them
reduced ability affecting mainly visuosp: Reduced ability affecting mainly visuospatial cognition which may be tested using pattern construction (for example by Differential Ability Scales, which test a person's strengths and weaknesses across a range of intellectual abilities)
a hemangioblastoma is a benign vascular: A hemangioblastoma is a benign vascular neoplasm that arises almost exclusively in the central nervous system. Hemangioblastomas consist of a tightly packed cluster of small blood vessels forming a mass of up to 1 or 2 cm in diameter
increased focal pigmentation of the verm: Increased focal pigmentation of the vermilion of the lips
a neoplasm of the pineal gland: A neoplasm of the pineal gland
lack of paramedian peaks and median notc: Lack of paramedian peaks and median notch of the upper lip vermilion
reduced bulkiness of the crease or fold: Reduced bulkiness of the crease or fold of skin running from the lateral margin of the nose, where nasal base meets the skin of the face, to a point just lateral to the corner of the mouth (cheilion or commissure)
increased pigmentation, either focal or: Increased pigmentation, either focal or generalized, of the skin surrounding the vermilion of the lips
inner aspect of the upper lip vermilion: Inner aspect of the upper lip vermilion (normally apposing the teeth) visible in a frontal view, i.e., the presence of an everted upper lip
triangular appearance of the oral apertu: Triangular appearance of the oral aperture with the apex in the midpoint of the upper vermilion and the lower vermilion forming the base
oral commissures positioned superior to: Oral commissures positioned superior to the midline labial fissure
gentle upward curve of the upper lip ver: Gentle upward curve of the upper lip vermilion such that the center is placed well superior to the commissures
visible space between the dental arches: Visible space between the dental arches in occlusion
tongue extending beyond the alveolar rid: Tongue extending beyond the alveolar ridges or teeth at rest
increased width of the uvula (subjective: Increased width of the uvula (subjective finding)
more than two clockwise hair whorls: More than two clockwise hair whorls
hair growth from a single point on the s: Hair growth from a single point on the scalp in any location other than lateral to the midline and close to the vertex of the skull
a congenital, hairless plaque consisting: A congenital, hairless plaque consisting of overgrown epidermis, sebaceous glands, hair follicles, apocrine glands and connective tissue. They are a variant of epidermal naevi. Sebaceous naevi most often appear on the scalp, but they may also arise on the face, neck or forehead. At birth, a sevaceous nevus typically appears as a solitary, smooth, yellow-orange hairless patch. Sebaceous naevi become more pronounced around adolescence, often appearing bumpy, warty or scaly
epidermal naevi are due to an overgrowth: Epidermal naevi are due to an overgrowth of the epidermis and may be present at birth (50%) or develop during childhood
a type of nevus sebaceous with a linear: A type of nevus sebaceous with a linear form, raised borders and yellowish color
generalized seizures with sustained incr: Generalized seizures with sustained increase in muscle contraction lasting a few seconds to minutes
seizures characterized by a sudden loss: Seizures characterized by a sudden loss or diminution of muscle tone, which may be fragmentary, confined to a segment (limb, jaw, head), or massive, leading to a slumping to the ground
a type of seizure characterized by cryin: A type of seizure characterized by crying or an outburst of crying as a major feature
a type of seizure characterized by laugh: A type of seizure characterized by laughing or an outburst of laughing as a major feature
a scintillating scotoma is a common visu: A scintillating scotoma is a common visual aura that can preced a migraine, whereby a spot of flickering light near the center of the visual fields occurs. The spot prevents vision, and is thus termed scotoma. The scotoma can extend into one or more shimmering arcs of white or colored flashing lights
an overlap of the bony plates of the sku: An overlap of the bony plates of the skull in an infant, with or without early closure
any structural abormality of the fifth c: Any structural abormality of the fifth cranial nerve
abnormality of the eleventh cranial nerv: Abnormality of the eleventh cranial nerve
abnormality of the twelfth cranial nerve: Abnormality of the twelfth cranial nerve
abnormality of the seventh cranial nerve: Abnormality of the seventh cranial nerve sometimes also referred to as the facial nerve
intermittent clonic or tonic contraction: Intermittent clonic or tonic contraction of muscles supplied by facial nerve. Muscles are relaxed in between contractions
a reduced ability to discriminate betwee: A reduced ability to discriminate between different temperatures
a reduced sense of touch (tactile sensat: A reduced sense of touch (tactile sensation). This is usually tested with a wisp of cotton or a fine camel's hair brush, by asking patients to say 'now' each time they feel the stimulus
a loss or impairment of the sensation of: A loss or impairment of the sensation of the relative position of parts of the body and joint position
pain is an unpleasant sensation that can: Pain is an unpleasant sensation that can range from mild, localized discomfort to agony, whereby the physical part of pain results from nerve stimulation and is often accompanied by an emotional component. This term groups abnormalities in pain sensation presumed to result from abnormalities related to the specific nerve fibers that carry the pain impulses to the brain
spontaneous pain is a kind of neuropathi: Spontaneous pain is a kind of neuropathic pain which occurs without an identifiable trigger
trophic changes is a term used to descri: Trophic changes is a term used to describe abnormalities in the area of pain that include primarily wasting away of the skin, tissues, or muscle, thinning of the bones, and changes in how the hair or nails grow, including thickening or thinning of hair or brittle nails
a pattern of sensory loss with selective: A pattern of sensory loss with selective loss of touch sensation and proprioception without loss of pain and temperature, or vice-versa
decreased concentration of ceruloplasmin: Decreased concentration of ceruloplasmin in the blood
an increased concentration of non cerulo: An increased concentration of non ceruloplasmin bound copper in the blood
an increased concentration of copper in: An increased concentration of copper in the urine
an abnormality in cerebral electrical ac: An abnormality in cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG) and being identified at multiple locations (foci)
focal (localized) slow activity reflects: Focal (localized) slow activity reflects focal dysfunction, not diffuse dysfunction (i.e., encephalopathy)
multifocal slowing of cerebral electrica: Multifocal slowing of cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG)
diffuse slowing of cerebral electrical a: Diffuse slowing of cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG)
the presence of complexes of slow spikes: The presence of complexes of slow spikes and slow waves (<2.5 Hz) in electroencephalography (EEG)
the presence of complexes of spikes and: The presence of complexes of spikes and waves (2.5-3.5 Hz) in electroencephalography (EEG)
The presence of complexes of spikes and waves (>3.5 Hz) in electroencephalography (EEG)
complexes of spikes (<70 ms) and sharp w: Complexes of spikes (<70 ms) and sharp waves (70-200 ms), which are sharp transient waves that have a strong association with epilepsy, in cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG)
the burst suppression pattern in electro: The burst suppression pattern in electroencephalography refers to a characteristic periodic pattern of low voltage (<10 microvolts) suppressed background and a relatively shorter pattern of higher amplitude slow, sharp, and spiking complexes
eeg abnormalities (epileptiform discharg: EEG abnormalities (epileptiform discharges) evoked by flashing lights or black and white striped patterns
periodic lateralized epileptiform discha: Periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs)are periodic, lateralized, and epileptiform. PLEDs show a relatively constant interval between discharges (0.5 to 3 seconds)
an abnormal generalized reduction in amp: An abnormal generalized reduction in amplitude of the cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG)
an abnormal localized reduction in ampli: An abnormal localized reduction in amplitude of the cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG)
periodically recurring abnormalities in: Periodically recurring abnormalities in the EEG
epileptiform discharges induced by hyper: Epileptiform discharges induced by hyperventilation (overbreathing) in cerebral electrical activity recorded along the scalp by electroencephalography (EEG)
a kind of breech presentation in which t: A kind of breech presentation in which the hips are flexed and the knees are extended
A kind of breech presentation in which the hips are flexed and the knees are flexed
a kind of breech presentation in which o: A kind of breech presentation in which one or both hips are extended and one or both of the fetus' feet are pointing down and entering the birth canal
a type of motor delay characterized by a: A type of motor delay characterized by a delay in acquiring the ability to control the fingers and hands
a delay in the acquisition of the abilit: A delay in the acquisition of the ability to understand the speech of others
severe mental retardation is defined as: Severe mental retardation is defined as an intelligence quotient (IQ) in the range of 20-34
an enduring pattern of uncooperative, de: An enduring pattern of uncooperative, defiant, and hostile behavior toward authority figures that does not involve major antisocial violations, is not accounted for by the child's developmental stage, and results in significant functional impairment. A certain level of oppositional behavior is common in children and adolescents
an incomplete closure of the abdominal w: An incomplete closure of the abdominal wall
a type of ataxia characterized by the im: A type of ataxia characterized by the impairment of the ability to smoothly perform the elements of a voluntary movement in the appropriate order and speed. With dyssynergia, a voluntary movement appears broken down into its component parts
a type of dyssynergia affecting eye move: A type of dyssynergia affecting eye movements and characterized by the inability to smoothly follow a visual target across the visual field
a type of dyssynergy characterized by th: A type of dyssynergy characterized by the lack of the ability to smoothly perform the elements of a voluntary movement in the appropriate order and speed
incoordination of movement caused by a d: Incoordination of movement caused by a deficit in the sensory nervous system. Sensory ataxia can be distinguished from cerebellar ataxia by asking the patient to close his or her eyes. Persons with cerebellar ataxia show only a minimal worsening of symptoms, whereas persons with sensory ataxia show a marked worsening of symptoms
an inversion of the t-wave (which is nor: An inversion of the T-wave (which is normally positive)
atrophy of the cervical segment of the s: Atrophy of the cervical segment of the spinal cord
the presence of xanthomas (intra-and ext: The presence of xanthomas (intra-and extra-cellular accumulations of cholesterol) extensor tendons (typically over knuckles, Achilles tendon, knee, and elbows)
a diagnostic reflex elicited by stimulat: A diagnostic reflex elicited by stimulation of the skin over the surface of the lateral malleolus of the foot. The Chaddock refelx is present if there is extension of one or more or all of the toes with or without fanning of them when the external inframalleolar skin is stimulated. The Chaddock sign, similar to the Babinski sign, is taken to be an indication of disease of the spinocortical (pyramidal) tract
an abnormal level of a circulating prote: An abnormal level of a circulating protein in the blood
a type of strabismus in which the fixati: A type of strabismus in which the fixating eye is always the same one, while the other eye is constantly deviated. Monocular strabismus is to be distinguished from alternating strabismus, in which either of the eyes 'squints' at different times
the presence during the prenatal period: The presence during the prenatal period of a cystic mass with multiple septa with multiple, asymmetric, thin-walled cysts near the posterior aspect of the neck. Fetal cystic hygroma can be defined as nuchal translucency with or without septations measuring greater than 3.0 mm
the presence of an abnormally large hypo: The presence of an abnormally large hypoechoic space in the posterior fetal neck (usually detected on prenatal ultrasound examination)
an abnormality of the umbilical cord, wh: An abnormality of the umbilical cord, which is the cord connecting the developing embryo or fetus to the placenta
a congenital disorder of the pulmonary v: A congenital disorder of the pulmonary valve in which the orifice of the valve fails to develop
a congenital disorder of the aortic valv: A congenital disorder of the aortic valve in which the orifice of the valve fails to develop
shortening of the extremities affecting: Shortening of the extremities affecting primarily the distal parts of the limbs (hands and feet) in relation to the other segments of the limbs
a disease where there is cellular death: A disease where there is cellular death (necrosis) of bone components due to interruption of the blood supply
a joint disorder caused by blood depriva: A joint disorder caused by blood deprivation in the subchondral bone causing the subchondral bone to die in a process called avascular necrosis. The bone is then reabsorbed by the body, leaving the articular cartilage it supported prone to damage. The result is fragmentation (dissection) of both cartilage and bone, and the free movement of these osteochondral fragments within the joint space, causing pain and further damage
morbus koehler is a juvenile aseptic nec: Morbus Koehler is a Juvenile aseptic necrosis affecting the Os naviculare pedis
morbus kienboeck is a juvenile aseptic n: Morbus Kienboeck is a Juvenile aseptic necrosis affecting the Os lunatum
morbus osgood-schlatter is a juvenile as: Morbus Osgood-Schlatter is a Juvenile aseptic necrosis affecting the Tuberositas tibiae
a developmental growth retardation of th: A developmental growth retardation of the vertebral end plates that may lead to secondary destruction of the vertebral end plates and protrusion of the nucleus pulposus into the vertebral body (so called Schmorl's nodes as seen on x-rays)
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of a branched chain family amino acid in the blood circulation

zonular cataracts are defined to be cata: Zonular cataracts are defined to be cataracts that affect specific regions of the lens
a 'coral-like' pattern of opacity in the: A 'coral-like' pattern of opacity in the lens of the eye. That is, a cataract with an irregular, stellate form
a form of cataract in which the lens sub: A form of cataract in which the lens substance has shrunk, leaving a collapsed, flattened capsule with little or no cortex or epithelium on the lens
a type of cataract affecting the anterio: A type of cataract affecting the anterior pole of lens immediately adjacent to ('beneath') the lens capsule
a cataract that affects the posterior pa: A cataract that affects the posterior part of the cortex of the lens
a kind of nuclear cataract characterized: A kind of nuclear cataract characterized by fiberglasslike or needlelike crystals projecting in different directions, through or close to the axial region of the lens

an abnormality of the nasal bone, compri: An abnormality of the nasal bone, comprising the left nasal bone and the right nasal bone
absence or underdevelopment of the nasal: Absence or underdevelopment of the nasal bone
a finding of a focus of increased echoge: A finding of a focus of increased echogenicity upon prenatal ultrasound examination of the fetus. The foci may be present in one or both ventricles. Echogenic intracardiac focus (EICF) is defined as a focus of echogenicity comparable to bone, in the region of the papillary muscle in either or both ventricles of the fetal heart
echogenic bowel is defined as fetal bowe: Echogenic bowel is defined as fetal bowel with homogenous areas of echogenicity that are equal to or greater than that of surrounding bone
an abnormality of the renal pelvis: An abnormality of the renal pelvis
mild pyelectasis is defined as a hypoech: Mild pyelectasis is defined as a hypoechoic spherical or elliptical space within the renal pelvis that measures at least 5mm and not more than 10 mm. The measurement is taken on a transverse section through the fetal renal pelvis using the maximum anterior-to-posterior measurement
the presence of dilatation of the renal: The presence of dilatation of the renal pelvis
a first-trimester prenatal ultrasound fi: A first-trimester prenatal ultrasound finding of abnormal blood flow in the ductus venosus
an abnormality of the fetal circulation: An abnormality of the fetal circulation system or fetal echocardiogram
a first-trimester prenatal ultrasound fi: A first-trimester prenatal ultrasound finding of abnormal blood flow in the umbilical vein
an abnormality of the fourth ventricle: An abnormality of the fourth ventricle
an abnormality of the third ventricle: An abnormality of the third ventricle
a kind of ventriculomegaly occurring in: A kind of ventriculomegaly occurring in the fetal period and usually diagnosed by prenatal ultrasound. Cerebral ventriculomegaly is defined by atrial measurements 10 mm or more. Mild ventriculomegaly (MVM) is defined as measurements between 10 and 15 mm. Measurements are obtained from an axial plane at the level of the thalamic nuclei just below the standard image to measure the BPD (pmid:16100637)
a form of hydrocephalus in which the flo: A form of hydrocephalus in which the flow of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) within the cerebral ventricular system or in the outlets of the CSF to the arachnoid space is obstructed
underdevelopment of the right-sided stru: Underdevelopment of the right-sided structures of the heart
the presence of a dilated urinary bladde: The presence of a dilated urinary bladder
fetal megacystis is an abnormally enlarg: Fetal megacystis is an abnormally enlarged bladder identified at any gestational age
a developmental defect resulting in an o: A developmental defect resulting in an obstructing membrane in the posterior male urethra
a bilateral form of agenesis of the kidn: A bilateral form of agenesis of the kidney
congenital cystic adenomatoid malformati: Congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation (CCAM) can be diagnosed prenatally if ultrasound shows a cystic or solid lung tumor. A CCAM does not have systemic arterial blood supply (in contrast to bronchopulmonary sequenstration). It is a cystic area within the lung that originates from abnormal embryogenesis
the presence of microscopic cystic masse: The presence of microscopic cystic masses of nonfunctioning pulmonary tissue that lack an obvious communication with the tracheobronchial tree
a kind of bronchopulmonary sequestration: A kind of bronchopulmonary sequestration that is incorporated into the normal surrounding lung
A kind of bronchopulmonary sequestration that is completely discrete from the normal lung and is surrounded by separate pleura
by the 14th week of gestation it is near: By the 14th week of gestation it is nearly always possible to visualized the fluid-filled fetal stomach bubble on prenatal sonography. absence of the lutheran antigen (a type: Absence of the Lutheran antigen (a type I integral membrane glycoprotein) from the surface of red blood cells
a kind of anemia characterized by inadeq: A kind of anemia characterized by inadequate production of erythrocytes
a deviation from the normal count of b c: A deviation from the normal count of B cells, i.e., the cells that are formed in the bone marrow, migrate to the peripheral lymphatic system, and mature into plasma cells or memory cells

an abnormality of blood coagulation, com: An abnormality of blood coagulation, common pathway

involuntary urine leakage synchronous wi: Involuntary urine leakage synchronous with exertion, or actions such as sneezing, or coughing

abnormality of the striatum, which is th: Abnormality of the striatum, which is the largest nucleus of the basal ganglia, comprising the caudate, putamen and ventral striatum, including the nucleus accumbens

an increase in the number of spontaneous: An increase in the number of spontaneous sister chromatid exchanges observed in cell culture of lymphocytes or other cells

abnormally increased formation of dense: Abnormally increased formation of dense trabecular bone tissue. Despite the increased density of bone tissue, osteopetrotic bones tend to be more fracture-prone than normal
a severe form of myopia with greater tha: A severe form of myopia with greater than -6.00 diopters

a typical absence seizure is a generaliz: A typical absence seizure is a generalized seizure with abrupt onset and offset of altered awareness which can vary in severity (see specific syndromes). Clonic movements of eyelids, head, eyebrows, chin, perioral or other facial parts may occur, most typically at 3Hz. Myoclonus of limbs can rarely occur. Oral and manual automatisms are common and there may be perseveration of behaviors occurring prior to seizure onset

Seizures are typically very brief (less than 6s in duration) and multiple seizures occur on a daily basis. Mostly awareness is retained
rhythmic myoclonic jerks of the shoulder: Rhythmic myoclonic jerks of the shoulders and arms with tonic abduction that results in progressive lifting of the arms during the seizure. The myoclonic jerks are typically bilateral but may be unilateral or asymmetric. Perioral myoclonias and rhythmic jerks of the head and legs may occur. Seizures last 10-60 seconds and typically occur daily. Level of awareness varies from complete loss of awareness to retained awareness

an abnormality of the skin that is restr: An abnormality of the skin that is restricted to a particular body region
an abnormality of the density of hair gr: An abnormality of the density of hair growth
hair that lacks the lustre (shine or gle: Hair that lacks the lustre (shine or gleam) of normal hair
an abnormality of the distribution of ha: An abnormality of the distribution of hair growth that is acquired during the course of life
a congenital abnormality of the distribu: A congenital abnormality of the distribution of hair growth
hair whose growth rate deviates from the: Hair whose growth rate deviates from the norm
reduced pigmentation of hair in patches: Reduced pigmentation of hair in patches
thickening of the epidermal layer of the: Thickening of the epidermal layer of the skin
congenital deep dermal melanosis in the: Congenital deep dermal melanosis in the sacral area
increased susceptibility to cutaneous fu: Increased susceptibility to cutaneous fungal infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of cutaneous fungal infections
increased susceptibility to viral skin i: Increased susceptibility to viral skin infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of viral skin infections
absence of the inner ear due to a develo: Absence of the inner ear due to a developmental defect
incomplete formation of the cochlear par: Incomplete formation of the cochlear partition. The scala vestibuli and scala tympani separated by the cochlear partition, except in the apical turn where the two scalae are in continuity via the helicotrema
incomplete partition i is also known as: Incomplete partition I is also known as cystic cochleovestibular malformation, where the cochlea has no bony modiolus, resulting in an empty cystic cochlea. This is accompanied by a dilated cystic vestibule with developmental arrest at the fifth week of gestation
absence of the cochlea, a spiral shaped: Absence of the cochlea, a spiral shaped cavity in the inner ear, owing to a developmental defect
a morphological abnormality of the vesti: A morphological abnormality of the vestibule, the central part of the osseous labyrinth that is situated medial to the tympanic cavity, behind the cochlea, and in front of the semicircular canals
complete absence of the vestibule of the: Complete absence of the vestibule of the inner ear
underdevelopment of the vestibule of the: Underdevelopment of the vestibule of the inner ear
dilatation of the vestibule of the inner: Dilatation of the vestibule of the inner ear
an abnormality of the morphology of the: An abnormality of the morphology of the semicircular canal
increased size of the semicircular canal: Increased size of the semicircular canal
an abnormality of the internal acoustic: An abnormality of the Internal acoustic meatus, i.e., of the canal in the petrous part of the temporal bone through which the cranial nerve VII and cranial nerve VIII traverse
aplasia of the internal auditory canal: Aplasia of the internal auditory canal
reduction in diameter of the internal au: Reduction in diameter of the internal auditory canal
increased size of the vestibular aqueduc: Increased size of the vestibular aqueduct
increased size of the cochlear duct, i.e: Increased size of the cochlear duct, i.e., of a duct that communicates between the perilymphatic space and the subarachnoid space, and transmits a vein from the cochlea to join the internal jugular
an abnormality of the function of the in: An abnormality of the function of the inner ear
a structural anomaly of the internal par: A structural anomaly of the internal part of the ear
absence or underdevelopment of the cochl: Absence or underdevelopment of the cochlea, a spiral shaped cavity in the inner ear, owing to a developmental defect
An abnormality of the dorsal columns, i.e., of the dorsal portion of the gray substance of the spinal cord. The dorsal column consists of the fasciculus gracilis and fasciculus cuneatus and itself is part of the dorsal funiculus
reduced muscle tone secondary to an abno: Reduced muscle tone secondary to an abnormality of the central nervous system
an abnormality of myelination of nerves: An abnormality of myelination of nerves in the central nervous system
delayed myelination in the peripheral ne: Delayed myelination in the peripheral nervous system
a type of disproportionate short stature: A type of disproportionate short stature characterized by a short trunk but a average-sized limbs that is lethal at birth
A type of disproportionate short stature characterized by a short trunk but a average-sized limbs with onset in infancy
intrauterine growth retardation that is: Intrauterine growth retardation that is at least 3 standard deviations (SD) below average, but not as low as 4 SD, corrected for sex and gestational age
delivery of a fetus through surgical inc: Delivery of a fetus through surgical incisions made through the abdominal wall (laparotomy) and the uterine wall (hysterotomy)
delivery of newborn by means of a ventou: Delivery of newborn by means of a ventouse, a vacuum device used to assist the delivery of a baby when the second stage of labour has not progressed adequately
shoulder dystocia occurs when the fetal: Shoulder dystocia occurs when the fetal anterior shoulder impacts against the maternal symphysis following delivery of the vertex
an abnormality of the placenta in which: An abnormality of the placenta in which there are numerous cystic spaces within the placenta as well as placental enlargement
increased length of the umbilical cord: Increased length of the umbilical cord
separation of the placenta from the uter: Separation of the placenta from the uterus wall before delivery
the age group when the cessation of life: The age group when the cessation of life happens
death during adolescence, the period bet: Death during adolescence, the period between childhood and adulthood (roughly between the ages of 10 and 19 years)
an abnormality of chloride homeostasis o: An abnormality of chloride homeostasis or concentration in the body
an abnormally increased chloride concent: An abnormally increased chloride concentration in the blood
an increased consentration of zinc in th: An increased consentration of zinc in the blood
an finding upon obstetric ultrasound exa: An finding upon obstetric ultrasound examination performed at around 16 to 20 weeks of gestation that is abnormal but not clearly identifiable as a fetal anatomic malformation or growth restriction. Such findings are known as soft markers since they are associated with increased risk for fetal aneuploidy or other disorders
fetal choroid plexus cysts (cpcs) are so: Fetal choroid plexus cysts (CPCs) are sonographically discrete, small cysts found in the choroid plexus within the lateral cerebral ventricles of the developing fetus at 14 to 24 weeks gestation. Imaging of the choroid plexus is performed in the transverse plane of the fetal head at the same level that the lateral cerebral ventricle is evaluated. The choroid plexus should be inspected bilaterally for the presence of cysts. The size of CPCs is not of clinical relevance (pmid:16100637)
