the cisterna magna is measured on a tran: The cisterna magna is measured on a transaxial view of the fetal head angled 15 degrees caudal to the canthomeatal line. The anterior/posterior diameter is taken between the inferior/posterior surface of the vemis of the cerebellum to the inner surface of the cranium. An enlarged cisternal magna is defined by an anterior/posterior diameter of 10 mm or more (pmid:16100637)
a short femur length is defined as eithe: A short femur length is defined as either a measurement below the 2.5th percentile for gestational age or a measurement that is less than 0.9 of that predicted by the measured biparietal diameter. The femur should be measured with the bone perpendicular to the ultrasound beam and with epiphyseal cartilages visible but not included in the measurement (pmid:16100637)
a short humerus length is defined as a l: A short humerus length is defined as a length below the 2.5th percentile for gestational age or as a measurement less than 0.9 of that predicted by the measured biparietal diameter. The humerus should be measured with the bone perpendicular to the ultrasound beam and with epiphyseal cartilages visible but not included in the measurement (pmid:16100637)
on prenatal ultrasound, the nasal bone i: On prenatal ultrasound, the nasal bone is a thin echogenic line within the bridge of the fetal nose. The fetus is imaged facing the transducer with the fetal face strictly in the midline. The angle of insonation is 90 degrees, with the longitudinal axis of the nasal bone as the reference line. Calibres are placed at each end of the nasal bone. Absence of the nasal bone or measurements below 2.5th percentile are considered significant (pmid:16100637)
fifth finger clinodactyly is defined by: Fifth finger clinodactyly is defined by a hypoplastic or absent mid-phalanx of the fifth digit. Ultrasound identification of the fetal hand must first be undertaken and then appropriate magnification accomplished. The evaluation requires stretching of the 5 fingers. The diagnosis is established when the middle phalanx of the fifth finger is markedly smaller than normal or absent, which often causes the finger to be curved inward (pmid:16100637)
an abnormally high concentration of seru: An abnormally high concentration of serum alpha-fetoprotein as compared to normal values for gestational-age
an abnormally high concentration of mate: An abnormally high concentration of maternal serum human chorionic gonadotropin as compared to normal values for gestational-age
an abnormally low concentration of mater: An abnormally low concentration of maternal serum human chorionic gonadotropin as compared to normal values for gestational-age
an abnormally low concentration of serum: An abnormally low concentration of serum PAPP-A (pregnancy associated plasma protein A), as compared to normal values for gestational-age
an abnormally elevated or decreased leve: An abnormally elevated or decreased level of a maternal serum marker analytes used in screening for aneuploidy
a medical history of a fetus or child bo: A medical history of a fetus or child born to a mother with an autoimmune disease
a medical history of exposure of the mot: A medical history of exposure of the mother of a child or fetus to a teratogenic substance during pregnancy
rhabdomyolysis induced by intake of alco: Rhabdomyolysis induced by intake of alcohol
an abnormality of the medulla oblongata,: An abnormality of the medulla oblongata, the lower half of the brainstem
an anomaly of the control or production: An anomaly of the control or production of movement in the central nervous system
a type of rigidity in which the arms are: A type of rigidity in which the arms are in flexion and adduction and the legs are extended. This signifies a lesion in the cerebral white matter, internal capsules, or thalamus
a type of cerebral palsy characterized b: A type of cerebral palsy characterized by slow, involuntary muscle movement and mixed muscle tone
clonus is an involuntary tendon reflex t: Clonus is an involuntary tendon reflex that causes repeated flexion and extension of the foot. Ankle clonus is tested by rapidly flexing the foot upward
Clonus is an involuntary tendon reflex that causes repeated flexion and extension of the foot. Knee clonus can be tested by rapidly pushing the patella towards the toes
a type of infection of the central nervo: A type of infection of the central nervous system that can be regarded as a sign of a pathological susceptibility to infection
microcephaly (hp:0000252) that is presen: Microcephaly (HP:0000252) that is present already at the time of birth
an abnormality of the function of the mi: An abnormality of the function of the middle ear
an abnormality of the incus, an ossicle: An abnormality of the incus, an ossicle in the middle ear
an abnormality of the malleus, an ossicl: An abnormality of the malleus, an ossicle in the middle ear
this term refers to the loss of eyelashe: This term refers to the loss of eyelashes that were previously present
the presence of a carcinoma of the esoph: The presence of a carcinoma of the esophagus
onset of disease at up to 8 weeks of ges: Onset of disease at up to 8 weeks of gestation
onset prior to birth but after 8 weeks o: Onset prior to birth but after 8 weeks of gestation
onset of disease at the age of between 1: Onset of disease at the age of between 16 and 40 years
Onset of disease at the age of between 1 and 5 years
a lack of intestinal ganglion cells (i.e: A lack of intestinal ganglion cells (i.e., an aganglionic section of bowel) affecting the small intestine
A lack of intestinal ganglion cells (i.e., an aganglionic section of bowel) affecting the duodenum
absence or underdevelopment of the gallb: Absence or underdevelopment of the gallbladder
a developmental defect in which the gall: A developmental defect in which the gallbladder fails to form
sudden, repetitive, nonrhythmic motor mo: Sudden, repetitive, nonrhythmic motor movements (spasms), involving the eyes and muscles of the face
regurgitation of milk through the nose: Regurgitation of milk through the nose
feeding problem necessitating nasogastri: Feeding problem necessitating nasogastric tube feeding
feeding problem necessitating gastrostom: Feeding problem necessitating gastrostomy tube feeding
the enteric villi are atrophic or absent: The enteric villi are atrophic or absent
sensorineural hearing impairment with ch: Sensorineural hearing impairment with childhood onset
persistence of the stapedial artery, whi: Persistence of the stapedial artery, which normally regresses during embryonic life
complete loss of hearing related to a se: Complete loss of hearing related to a sensorineural defect
in primary position, the eyes drift slow: In primary position, the eyes drift slowly downward and then spontaneously beat upward. Upward gaze accentuates the nystagmus. The associated oscillopsias are often very irritating, but the symptoms are usually transient
absence of globe, optic nerve, chiasm an: Absence of globe, optic nerve, chiasm and optic tracts. No evidence of ocular tissue on MRI scan or examination
an abnormality of the lacrimal punctum,: An abnormality of the lacrimal punctum, an opening on the eyelid close to the medial canthus that drains tears from the conjunctival sac into the lacrimal duct in the same eyelid
a developmental anomaly characterized by: A developmental anomaly characterized by abnormal smallness of one eye
an abnormality of the lacrimal duct, a d: An abnormality of the lacrimal duct, a duct that drain tears from the conjunctiva, via the lacrimal puncta, into the lacrimal sac
abnormality of the lacrimal gland, i.e.,: Abnormality of the lacrimal gland, i.e., of the almond-shaped gland that secretes the aqueous layer of the tear film for each eye
adhesions between the iris and the corne: Adhesions between the iris and the cornea
adhesions between the iris and the lens: Adhesions between the iris and the lens
developmental abnormality in which the l: Developmental abnormality in which the lens and cornea are not separated
an abnormal anteroposterior thickness of: An abnormal anteroposterior thickness of the cornea
a increased anteroposterior thickness of: A increased anteroposterior thickness of the cornea
abnormality of the corneal endothelium,: Abnormality of the corneal endothelium, that is, the single layer of cells on the inner surface of the cornea
abnormality of descemet's membrane, whic: Abnormality of Descemet's membrane, which is the basement membrane of the corneal endothelium
a reduction in the number of corneal end: A reduction in the number of corneal endothelial cells
an abnormality of the stroma of cornea,: An abnormality of the stroma of cornea, also known as the substantia propria of cornea
reduced transparency of the central port: Reduced transparency of the central portion of the corneal stroma
generalized reduced transparency of the: Generalized reduced transparency of the stroma of the cornea
abnormality of the corneal epithelium, t: Abnormality of the corneal epithelium, that is of the epithelial tissue that covers the front of the cornea
ingrowth of new blood vessels into the c: Ingrowth of new blood vessels into the cornea
new growth of vessels on the surface of: New growth of vessels on the surface of the iris
a conical projection of the anterior sur: A conical projection of the anterior surface of the lens, occurring as a developmental anomaly
a conical projection of the posterior su: A conical projection of the posterior surface of the lens, occurring as a developmental anomaly
progressive maculopathy characterized by: Progressive maculopathy characterized by concentric regions of hyper- and hypo-pigmentation
cystoid macular edema (cme) is any type: Cystoid macular edema (CME) is any type of macular edema that involves cyst formation
choroidal neovascularization (cnv) is th: Choroidal neovascularization (CNV) is the creation of new blood vessels in the choroid layer of the eye
pale often indistinct lesions of the mac: Pale often indistinct lesions of the macula
a macular hole is a small break in the m: A macular hole is a small break in the macula, located in the center of the retina
increased amount of pigmentation in the: Increased amount of pigmentation in the macula lutea
drusen (singular, 'druse') are tiny yell: Drusen (singular, 'druse') are tiny yellow or white accumulations of extracellular material (lipofuscin) that build up in Bruch's membrane of the eye
splitting of the retina in the macular r: Splitting of the retina in the macular region
a benign tumor of the retina that appear: A benign tumor of the retina that appears as a grouping of blood-filled saccules within the inner retinal layers or on the surface of the optic disc. Retinal cavernous angioma are described as having a 'cluster of grapes' appearance
an abnormality of binocular vision, that: An abnormality of binocular vision, that is of the ability to synthesize the visual inputs from both eyes to a single image with perception of depth
inability to make fine depth discriminat: Inability to make fine depth discriminations from parallax provided by the two eyes' different positions on the head
a condition where the retina contains no: A condition where the retina contains no functional cone cells, so that in addition to the absence of color discrimination, vision in lights of normal intensity is difficult
the condition of having both rods and co: The condition of having both rods and cones, but only a single kind of cone. Affected individuals have good pattern vision in daylight, but cannot distinguish between colors
individuals affected by dichromacy posse: Individuals affected by dichromacy possess only two types of cones, instead of three
individuals with anomalous trichromacy p: Individuals with anomalous trichromacy possess three types of cones, but one of the three types of cones has an abnormal spectral sensitivity compared to normal cones
a type of anomalous trichromacy associat: A type of anomalous trichromacy associated with abnormal M photopigment, such that the absorption spectrum is shifted toward L wavelengths. Affected individuals have difficulties distinguishing between red and green
complete lack of the m photopigment, whi: Complete lack of the M photopigment, which is replaced with the L photopigment. Affected individuals tend to confuse red and green
blue and green cones only; no functional: Blue and green cones only; no functional red cones
an iris cyst is composed of a single cel: An iris cyst is composed of a single cell layer of epithelium and is filled with fluid
malignant tumor of melanocytes affecting: Malignant tumor of melanocytes affecting the iris
a benign brown pigmented area over the i: A benign brown pigmented area over the iris representing proliferation of melanocyte cells in the stromal layer of the iris. An iris nevus can be flat or occasionally slightly elevated
an abnormal shape of the lens: An abnormal shape of the lens
exaggerated curvature of the lens of the: Exaggerated curvature of the lens of the eye, producing an anterior or posterior spherical bulging
sharply demarcated hyperpigmentation whi: Sharply demarcated hyperpigmentation which is congenital found in around 3-5% of the population and of no functional significance
Sharply demarcated hyperpigmentation which is congenital
a small break in the retina: A small break in the retina
inflammation of the vitreous body, chara: Inflammation of the vitreous body, characterized by the presence of inflammatory cells and protein exudate in the vitreous cavity
a type of retinal exudate located in the: A type of retinal exudate located in the subretinal space between the sensory retina and the retinal pigment epithelium
the appearance of yellow/white crystalli: The appearance of yellow/white crystalline-like (hence the name) spots in the retina and thickening of the peripheral part of the vitreous
abnormality of the spatial relationship: Abnormality of the spatial relationship of the cardiac segments to other components of the heart
Abnormality of the spatial relationship of the atria to other components of the heart
right atrial isomerism is characterized: Right atrial isomerism is characterized by bilateral triangular, morphologically right atrial, appendages, both joining the atrial chamber along a broad front with internal terminal crest
in left atrial isomerism there is a bila: In left atrial isomerism there is a bilateral small finger-shaped morphologically left atrial appendage joining the atrial chamber along a narrow front without an internal terminal crest
mirror image atrial arrangement, with mo: Mirror image atrial arrangement, with morphologic right atrium on the left hand side and morphologic left atrium on the right hand side
common atrium without defining morpholog: Common atrium without defining morphologic features
the essence of the lesion is the combina: The essence of the lesion is the combination of discordant atrioventricular and ventriculo-arterial connections. Thus, the morphologically right atrium is connected to a morphologically left ventricle across the mitral valve, with the left ventricle then connected to the pulmonary trunk. The morphologically left atrium is connected to the morphologically right ventricle across the tricuspid valve, with the morphologically right ventricle connected to the aorta
crossing of the inflow streams of the tw: Crossing of the inflow streams of the two ventricles, due to an apparent twisting of the heart about its long axis
criss-cross atrioventricular valves with: Criss-cross atrioventricular valves with a rare cardiac malformation characterized by the two ventricles lying one above the other instead of side by side
a deviance in the normal connections bet: A deviance in the normal connections between two cardiac segements
an abnormality of the circulatory connec: An abnormality of the circulatory connection between atria and ventricles
a defect where there is no connection be: A defect where there is no connection between the left atrium and left ventricle
A defect where there is no connection between the right atrium and right ventricle
with left or right cardiac isomerism in: With left or right cardiac isomerism in a biventricular, the atrioventricular connections are perforce ambiguous, in that one of the connections is concordant (e.g., right-sided morphologic right atrium connected to a morphologic right ventricle) and one of the connections is discordant (e.g., left-sided morphologic right atrium connected to a morphologic left ventricle)
connection of the right atrium to the le: Connection of the right atrium to the left ventricle and of the left atrium to the right ventricle in a biventricular heart
the condition in which both atria are jo: The condition in which both atria are joined to a single ventricle each by its own atrioventricular valve

The condition in which both atria are joined to the left ventricle each by its own atrioventricular valve. Usually there is a hypoplastic right ventricle, which may be on the opposite side of the heart as usual
The condition in which both atria are joined to the right ventricle each by its own atrioventricular valve. Usually, the left ventricle is hypoplastic

The condition in which both atria are joined to a single ventricle each by its own atrioventricular valve. The morphology of this ventricle does not allow one to determine if it corresponds to the left or right ventricle
a congenital defect with failure to open: A congenital defect with failure to open of the mitral valve orifice
an atrioventricular valve that empties i: An atrioventricular valve that empties into both ventricles. The valve overrides the interventricular septum above a ventricular septum defect
anomalous insertion of the chordae tendi: Anomalous insertion of the chordae tendinae or papillary muscles into the contralateral ventricle in the presence of a ventricular septum defect
an abnormality of the circulatory connec: An abnormality of the circulatory connection between the ventricles and the pulmonary artery and aorta
anomalous mitral valve arcade is diagnos: Anomalous mitral valve arcade is diagnosed based on the following features (1) An adequately sized mitral valve orifice; (2) short, thick, and poorly differentiated chordae with direct union of the papillary muscles to the anterior leaflet; (3) narrow or nearly nonexistent spaces between the abnormal chordae; and (4) greater differentiation of the chordae attached to the posterior papillary muscle
complete absence of the interatrial sept: Complete absence of the interatrial septum with common atrioventricular valve and two atrioventricular connections
a congenital anomaly with partitioning o: A congenital anomaly with partitioning of the right atrium to form a triatrial heart caused by persistence of the right valve of the sinus venosus. Typically, the right atrial partition is due to exaggerated fetal eustachian and thebesian valves, which together form an incomplete septum across the lower part of the atrium. This septum may range from a reticulum to a substantial sheet of tissue
an interatrial communication caused by a: An interatrial communication caused by a deficiency of the common wall between the superior vena cava (SVC) and the right-sided pulmonary veins. SVASD is commonly associated with anomalous pulmonary venous connection (APVC) of some or all of the pulmonary veins, which produces additional left-to-right shunting
the left atrio-ventricular connection co: The left atrio-ventricular connection consists of two anatomically distinct orifices separated by accessory fibrous tissue
cleft in the anterior mitral valve leafl: Cleft in the anterior mitral valve leaflet not associated with an atrioventricular canal defect
abnormality of the mitral valve apparatu: Abnormality of the mitral valve apparatus, whereby chordae attach to a single papillary muscle or hypoplastic papillary muscles
a congenital stenotic mitral valvular an: A congenital stenotic mitral valvular anomaly with a ring of tissue above the mitral valve
congenital defect characterized by under: Congenital defect characterized by underdevelopment of the tricuspid valve
an atrioventricular valve that has faile: An atrioventricular valve that has failed to open (atretic)
a tricuspid valve that has failed to ope: A tricuspid valve that has failed to open
a specific combination of heart defects: A specific combination of heart defects with a primum atrial septal defect, cleft anterior mitral valve leaflet, and inlet ventricular defect. There is one valve annulus and two valve orifices

A specific combination of heart defects including a primum atrial septal defect and cleft anterior mitral valve leaflet. There is not an inlet ventricular septal defect present. There are two valve annuluses and two valve orifices

A specific combination of heart defects with a primum atrial septal defect, cleft anterior mitral valve leaflet, and an inlet ventricular septal defect. There are two valve annuli and two valve orifices
anatomic features of unbalanced atrioven: Anatomic features of unbalanced atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD) include varying amounts of ventricular hypoplasia, as well as malalignment of the atrioventricular junction. In complete AVSD, the common AV valve can be situated either equally over the right and left ventricles (balanced) or unequally over the ventricles (unbalanced)
abnormally short chordae tendineae of th: Abnormally short chordae tendineae of the mitral valve
a congenital defect of heart development: A congenital defect of heart development characterized by origin of both pulmonary artery and aorta from the morphological left ventricle
displacement of the heart outside the th: Displacement of the heart outside the thoracic cavity and into the abdomen
a type of ectopia cordis with the heart: A type of ectopia cordis with the heart partially in the cervical region and without a defect of the sternum
A type of ectopia cordis with the heart partially in the cervical region with a defect of the superior portion of the sternum
congenital malformation of the thoracic: Congenital malformation of the thoracic wall with partial or total displacement of the heart outside the thoracic cavity. This feature is associated with sternal cleft or absence of the sternum
congenital malformation of the ventral w: Congenital malformation of the ventral wall with partial or total evisceration of the heart outside the thoracic cavity and displacement partially into the abdominal cavity
a deviance from the norm of the origin o: A deviance from the norm of the origin or course of the right brachiocephalic artery, the left common carotid artery, the left subclavian artery or the proximal vertebral arteries
the aortic arch extends into the soft ti: The aortic arch extends into the soft tissues of the neck before turning down into to become the descending aorta
the left common carotid artery has a com: The left common carotid artery has a common origin with the innominate artery
a conenital abnormality of the aortic ar: A conenital abnormality of the aortic arch in which the two embryonic aortc arches form a vascular ring that surrounds the trachea or esophagus and then join to form the descending aorta. Double aortic arch can cause symptoms because of compression of the esophagus (dysphagia, cyanosis while eating) or trachea (stridor)
the subclavian artery arises from ductus: The subclavian artery arises from ductus arteriosus. While the ductus arteriosus is patent its blood supply comes from the ductus, hence from the pulmonary artery. After it closes, the blood supply is retrogradely from the vertebral artery via the circle of Willis
a patent ductus arteriosus or ductal lig: A patent ductus arteriosus or ductal ligament completes the ring
aortic arch crosses the right mainstem b: Aortic arch crosses the right mainstem bronchus. The left carotid artery is the first branch, right carotid artery the second branch and right subclavian artery as the third branch
aortic arch crosses the left mainstem br: Aortic arch crosses the left mainstem bronchus. The first branch is the right carotid artery, the second branch is the left carotid artery, the third branch is the subclavian artery, the fourth branch is the right subclavian artery arising from the posteromedial aspect of the distal aortic arch and continuing posterior to the esophagus to the right hand side of the body
the ring may be completed by the ductal: The ring may be completed by the ductal ligament
mesocardia is an abnormal location of th: Mesocardia is an abnormal location of the heart in which the heart is in a midline position and the longitudinal axis of the heart lies in the mid-sagittal plane
abnormal plane of direction of the heart: Abnormal plane of direction of the heart from the base to the apex. Left sided is normal
Abnormal plane of direction of the heart from the base to the apex towards the right. Left sided is normal
Abnormal plane of direction of the heart from the base to the apex in the midline. Left sided is normal
defect or defects of the morphogenesis o: Defect or defects of the morphogenesis of the aorta and pulmonary arteries
a congenital anomaly with an abnormal co: A congenital anomaly with an abnormal connection between the aorta and the main pulmonary artery resulting in an aortopulmonary shunt
a congenitally corrected transposition o: A congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries with a ventricular septal defect: a hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart. The ventricular septal defect is centered around the most superior aspect of the ventricular septum
truncus arteriosus (single great artery: Truncus arteriosus (single great artery leaving the base of the heart, giving rise to the coronary, pulmonary, and systemic arteries) with each pulmonary artery arising separate from each other on the posterior or lateral aspect of the truncus

Truncus arteriosus (single great artery leaving the base of the heart, giving rise to the coronary, pulmonary, and systemic arteries) whereby one of the two pulmonary artery branched does not arise from the common pulmonary trunk, but instead from the ductus arteriosus or directly from the aorta

Truncus arteriosus (single great artery leaving the base of the heart, giving rise to the coronary, pulmonary, and systemic arteries) whereby the aortic arch is hypoplastic or interrupted, and a large patent ductus arteriosus is present
non-continuity of the arch of aorta with: Non-continuity of the arch of aorta with an atretic point or absent segment
non-continuity of the aortic arch with a: Non-continuity of the aortic arch with an atretic point or absent segment at the level of the isthmus
Non-continuity of the aortic arch with an atretic point or absent segment between the left carotid and subclavian arteries
Non-continuity of the aortic arch with an atretic point or absent segment between the innominate and left carotid arteries
an abnormality of the pulmonary situs, i: An abnormality of the pulmonary situs, i.e., of the sidedness of the morphological right and left lungs, which is defined by characteristics such as the number of lobes per lung and the relationship of the pulmonary arteries to their bronchi
mirror image arrangement of the mainstem: Mirror image arrangement of the mainstem bronchi with the right pulmonary artery posterior to the right upper lobe bronchus and the left pulmonary artery anterior to the left upper lobe bronchus
an abnormality of the pulmonary situs, i: An abnormality of the pulmonary situs, i.e., of the sidedness of the morphological right and left lungs, in which the morphology of both left and right lungs is the same
An abnormality of the pulmonary situs, i.e., of the sidedness of the morphological right and left lungs, in which both lungs have the morphology of a right lung
An abnormality of the pulmonary situs, i.e., of the sidedness of the morphological right and left lungs, in which both lungs have the morphology of a left lung
an abnormality of the abdominal situs, i: An abnormality of the abdominal situs, i.e., of the sidedness of the abdomen and its organs
a type of ventricular septal defect comm: A type of ventricular septal defect communicating directly between the left ventricle and right atrium. This is anatomically possible because the normal tricuspid valve is more apically displaced than the mitral valve
a ventricular septal defect that involve: A ventricular septal defect that involves the inlet of the right ventricular septum immediately inferior to the AV valve apparatus
the trabecular septum is the largest par: The trabecular septum is the largest part of the interventricular septum. It extends from the membranous septum to the apex and superiorly to the infundibular septum. A defect in the trabecular septum is called muscular VSD if the defect is completely rimmed by muscle
a muscular ventricular septal defect loc: A muscular ventricular septal defect located at the apex of the heart
a type of muscular ventricular septal de: A type of muscular ventricular septal defect characterized by the presence of multiple small defects in the ventricular septum
right pulmonary venous return to the inf: Right pulmonary venous return to the inferior vena cava
aorto-ventricular tunnel is a congenital: Aorto-ventricular tunnel is a congenital, extracardiac channel which connects the ascending aorta above the sinutubular junction to the cavity of the left, or (less commonly) right ventricle
a developmental defect of the pericardiu: A developmental defect of the pericardium with congenital onset
no pericardium around the heart, occurri: No pericardium around the heart, occurring as a congenital defect, not the result of a surgical pericardectomy
no pericardium over the diaphragmatic su: No pericardium over the diaphragmatic surface of the heart. It is a congenital defect, not the result of a pericardectomy. Pericardium is present on other parts of the heart
no pericardium is present on the rightha: No pericardium is present on the righthand side of the heart. It is a congenital absence of pericardium rather than the result of a pericardectomy
a congenital anomaly with lack of part o: A congenital anomaly with lack of part of the pericardium on the righthand side of the heart
a congenital anomaly with complete lack: A congenital anomaly with complete lack of the pericardium on the lefthand side of the heart
a congenital anomaly with lack of part o: A congenital anomaly with lack of part of the pericardium on the lefthand side of the heart
lack of a part of the pericardium over t: Lack of a part of the pericardium over the diaphragmatic surface of the heart. It is a congenital defect, not the result of a pericardectomy. Pericardium is present on other parts of the heart
isolated abnormalities of the coronary a: Isolated abnormalities of the coronary artery origins. This may be in associated with other structural heart malformations but not the patterns of complex structural heart malformations which result in abnormal course of the coronary arteries
a coronary artery begins (branches off f: A coronary artery begins (branches off from) the pulmonary artery rather than as normal from the root of the aorta
left main coronary artery begins (branch: Left main coronary artery begins (branches off from) the pulmonary artery rather than as normal from the root of the aorta, above the left cusp of the aortic valve
right coronary artery begins (branches o: Right coronary artery begins (branches off from) the pulmonary artery rather than as normal from the root of the aorta, above the right cusp of the aortic valve
the presence of a single coronary artery: The presence of a single coronary artery ostium from which both coronary arteries arise
a congenital malformation with abnormal: A congenital malformation with abnormal connection between one of the coronary arteries and a heart chamber or another blood vessel
an abnormality of the coronary sinus, wh: An abnormality of the coronary sinus, which is formed by the union of the great cardiac vein and the left marginal vein and terminates in the right atrium. The coronary sinus functions to o collect deoxygenated blood from the myocardium of the heart and drain it into the right atrium
an atrial septal defect characterized by: An atrial septal defect characterized by a deficiency in the tissue separating the coronary sinus from the left atrium (LA). This results in partial or complete unroofing of the coronary sinus leading to a predominantly left-to-right shunt through the coronary sinus (LA to coronary sinus to right atrium [RA]). The orifice of the ostium is frequently large because of the increased flow. From the RA side, the defect is located at the level of the coronary sinus ostium and may also include some deficiency in atrial tissue around the ostium. From the LA side, the size can be variable depending on the degree of unroofing of the coronary sinus
a venous pouch within the left ventricul: A venous pouch within the left ventricular wall, with a neck opening into the coronary sinus
abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatat: Abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatation of one of the anatomic dilations of the ascending aorta, which occurs just above the aortic valve
narrowing or constriction of the aorta l: Narrowing or constriction of the aorta localized at the insertion of the ductus arteriosus, i.e., to the juxtaductal region of aortic arch
Narrowing or constriction of the aorta localized distal to the ductus arteriosus, i.e., to the postductal region of aortic arch
abnormal persistent patency of the ductu: Abnormal persistent patency of the ductus arteriosus in postnatal life when birth was at 37 completed weeks of gestation or greater
Abnormal persistent patency of the ductus arteriosus when birth was at less than 37 weeks completed gestation
the presence of both a left and a right: The presence of both a left and a right ductus arteriosus
a double outlet right ventricle with a s: A double outlet right ventricle with a subaortic ventritricular septal defect (a hotel between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart), that extends anterosuperiorly and arere closely related to the pulmonary artery as well, are considered to be doubly committed. There is associated pulmonary stenosis, the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches

A double outlet right ventricle with a subaortic ventritricular septal defect (a hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart), that extends anterosuperiorly and arere closely related to the pulmonary artery as well, are considered to be doubly committed. There is not associated pulmonary stenosis, the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches
a double outlet right ventricle with a n: A double outlet right ventricle with a non-committed ventricular septal defect (VSD), which is a VSD that is anatomically related to, or close to, neither great vessel, being separated from both by considerable muscle, and also has a pulmonary stenosis; abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches

A double outlet right ventricle with a non-committed ventricular septal defect (VSD), which is a VSD that is anatomically related to, or close to, neither great vessel, being separated from both by considerable muscle, but there is not accompanying pulmonary stenosis; the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches
a double outlet right ventricle with a v: A double outlet right ventricle with a ventricular spetal defect (a hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart), that is considered to be closely related to the aortic origin. There is associated pulmonary stenosis, the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches

A double outlet right ventricle with a ventricular spetal defect (a hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart), that is considered to be closely related to the aortic origin. There is not associated pulmonary stenosis, the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches

A double outlet right ventricle with a ventricular spetal defect (a hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart), that is considered to be closely related to the pulmonary origin. There is associated pulmonary stenosis, the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches

A double outlet right ventricle with a ventricular spetal defect (a hole between the two bottom chambers (ventricles) of the heart), that is considered to be closely related to the pulmonary origin. There is not associated pulmonary stenosis, the abnormal narrowing or constriction of the pulmonary artery, in the main pulmonary artery and/or in the left or right pulmonary artery branches
features of tetralogy of fallot with eit: Features of tetralogy of Fallot with either rudimentary ridges or the complete absence of pulmonic valve tissue
anomalous origin of one pulmonary artery: Anomalous origin of one pulmonary artery from the ascending aorta with the contralateral pulmonary artery arising from the right ventricle
the left pulmonary artery originates fro: The left pulmonary artery originates from the ascending aorta in the presence of a pulmonary valve and main pulmonary artery
failure to develop of the tricuspid valv: Failure to develop of the tricuspid valve and thus lack of the normal connection between the right atrium and the right ventricle
right ventricular dysfunction (global or: Right ventricular dysfunction (global or regional) with functional and morphological right ventricular abnormalities, with or without left ventricular disease
left ventricular non-compaction (lvnc) i: Left ventricular non-compaction (LVNC) is characterized by prominent left ventricular trabeculae and deep inter-trabecular recesses. The myocardial wall is often thickened with a thin, compacted epicardial layer and a thickened endocardial layer. In some patients, LVNC is associated with left ventricular dilatation and systolic dysfunction, which can be transient in neonates
transient left ventricular apical balloo: Transient left ventricular apical ballooning syndrome or takotsubo cardiomyopathy is characterized by transient regional systolic dysfunction involving the left ventricular apex and/or mid-ventricle in the absence of obstructive coronary disease on coronary angiography. Patients present with an abrupt onset of angina-like chest pain, and have diffuse T-wave inversion, sometimes preceded by ST-segment elevation, and mild cardiac enzyme elevation
absence of the right superior vena cava: Absence of the right superior vena cava (RSVC). An absent RSVC is always associated with a persistent left superior vena cava (PLSVC). During normal fetal development, the left-sided anterior venous cardinal system regresses, leaving the coronary sinus (CS) and the ligament of Marshall. Failure of the closure of the left anterior cardinal vein results in PLSVC. In general, PLSVC is associated with the right superior vena cava (RSVC) and drains into the RA via a dilated CS. When developmental arrest occurs at an earlier stage, the CS is absent and the PLSVC drains into the left atrium
a persistent left superior vena cava (pl: A persistent left superior vena cava (PLSVC) that drains into the left atrium instead of the right atrium via the coronary sinus, resulting in a right to left sided shunt
A persistent left superior vena cava (PLSVC) that drains into the right atrium via the coronary sinus. This is the case in 80-92% of cases of PLSVC and results in no hemodynamic consequence
interrupted inferior vena cava with azyg: Interrupted inferior vena cava with azygous continuation is the result of connection failure between the right subcardinal vein and the right vitelline vein. Consequently, venous blood from the caudal part of the body reaches the heart via the azygous vein and superior vena cava
a myxoma (tumor of primitive connective: A myxoma (tumor of primitive connective tissue) of the heart. Cardiac myxomas consist of stellate to plump, cytologically bland mesenchymal cells set in a myxoid stroma. Cardiac myxomas are of endocardial origina and general project from the endocardium into a cardiac chamber
abnormal proliferation of blood vessels: Abnormal proliferation of blood vessels within the cardiac cavities attached to the endocardium
a teratoma within the heart. most common: A teratoma within the heart. Most commonly, these tumors are detected in the pericardial cavity attached to the pulmonary artery and aorta. The tumour size within the heart varies from 2 to 9 cm in diameter, and intrapericardial tumors as large as 15 cm have been reported. Intracardiac tumors arise from the atrial or ventricular wall as nodular masses protruding into the cardiac chambers. Cardiac and pericardial teratomas are easily detected in the fetus and neonate by two-dimensional echocardiography as heterogeneous and encapsulated cystic masses. Histologically, cardiac teratomas contain multiple immature elements including epithelium, neuroglial tissue, thyroid, pancreas, smooth and skeletal muscle, cartilage and bone
any cardiac rhythm other than the normal: Any cardiac rhythm other than the normal sinus rhythm. Such a rhythm may be either of sinus or ectopic origin and either regular or irregular. An arrhythmia may be due to a disturbance in impulse formation or conduction or both
a type of tetralogy of fallot with pulmo: A type of tetralogy of Fallot with pulmonary atresia in which all pulmonary blood flow is derived from major aortopulmonary collateral arteries (MAPCA)
the commonest form of tetralogy of fallo: The commonest form of tetralogy of Fallot characterized by pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, ventricular septum defect, and right ventricular hypertrophy, without pulmonary atresia, absent pulmonary valve, atrioventricular canal defect or absent subarterial conus
a ventricular septal defect that lies be: A ventricular septal defect that lies beneath the semilunar valve(s) in the conal or outlet septum
a ventricular septal defect that is conf: A ventricular septal defect that is confluent with and involves the membranous septum and is bordered by an atrioventricular valve, not including the type 3 VSDs
any ventricular septal defect (vsd) that: Any ventricular septal defect (VSD) that is small enough to restrict flow across it such that a pressure gradient exists between the two sides of the VSD
Any ventricular septal defect (VSD) that does not restrict flow across it sufficiently to generate a pressure gradient between the two sides of the VSD
the superior vena cava passes below the: The superior vena cava passes below the aortic arch
an abnormal path of a coronary artery: An abnormal path of a coronary artery
a type of supraventricular tachycardia t: A type of supraventricular tachycardia that originates in the atrioventricular node
supraventricular tachycardia in which an: Supraventricular tachycardia in which an accessory pathway connecting the atria and ventricles, apart from the AV node, participates as a necessary part of a reentrant mechanism
supraventricular tachycardia with an acc: Supraventricular tachycardia with an accessory connection mediated pathway that is called concealed becasue it is not seen on the ECG during sinus rhythm
an incessant orthodromic tachycardia wit: An incessant orthodromic tachycardia with anterograde conduction over the atrioventricular node and by retrograde conduction via an accessory pathway usually located in the posteroseptal region with slow and decremental conduction
hemorrhage into the parenchyma of the ce: Hemorrhage into the parenchyma of the cerebellum
chronic supraventricular tachycardia pre: Chronic supraventricular tachycardia predominantly seen in childhood
multifocal atrial tachycardia is a rare: Multifocal atrial tachycardia is a rare supraventricular arrhythmia in neonates and young infants that is characterized by multiple P waves with varying P wave morphology and is usually asymptomatic
an abnormality of the sinoatrial (sa) no: An abnormality of the sinoatrial (SA) node in the right atrium. THe SA node acts as the pacemaker of the heart
heart rate of greater than 100 beats per: Heart rate of greater than 100 beats per minute
an abnormality involving the generation: An abnormality involving the generation of the action potential by the sinus node and is characterized by an atrial rate inappropriate for physiological requirements. Manifestations include severe sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses or arrest, sinus node exit block, chronic atrial tachyarrhythmias, alternating periods of atrial bradyarrhythmias and tachyarrhythmias, and inappropriate responses of heart rate during exercise or stress
delay of conduction through the atrioven: Delay of conduction through the atrioventricular node, which is manifested as prolongation of the PR interval in the electrocardiogram (EKG). All atrial impulses reach the ventricles
an intermittent atrioventricular block w: An intermittent atrioventricular block with failure of some atrial impulses to conduct to the ventricles, i.e., some but not all atrial impulses are conducted through the atrioventricular node and trigger ventricular contraction
progressive pr interval prolongation wit: Progressive PR interval prolongation with the subsequent occurrence of a single nonconducted P wave that results in a pause. The pause that follows the nonconducted impulse is less than fully compensatory (less than the sum of two normal sinus intervals)
a type of second degree atrioventricular: A type of second degree atrioventricular (AV) block characterized by sudden failure to conduct an impulse through the AV node without a preceding change in the PR interval
atrioventricular (av) dissociation is pr: Atrioventricular (AV) dissociation is present if the atria and the ventricles are under the control of two separate pacemakers. AV dissociation can occur in the absence of a primary AV conduction disturbance
block of conduction of electrical impuls: Block of conduction of electrical impulses along the Bundle of His or along one of its bundle branches
conduction block in the anterior divisio: Conduction block in the anterior division of the left bundle branch of the bundle of His
a conduction block of the right branch o: A conduction block of the right branch of the bundle of His. This manifests as a prolongation of the QRS complex (greater than 0.12 s) with delayed activation of the right ventricle and terminal delay on the EKG
a conduction block of the left branch of: A conduction block of the left branch of the bundle of His. This manifests as a generalized disturbance of QRS morphology on EKG
libman-sacks valvular lesions are steril: Libman-Sacks valvular lesions are sterile fibrofibrinous vegetations that favor the left-sided heart valves and usually form on the ventricular surface of the mitral valve
abnormal conduction in all three divisio: Abnormal conduction in all three divisions of the intraventricular conducting tissue
junctional ectopic tachycardia (jet) is: Junctional ectopic tachycardia (JET) is a unique type of supraventricular arrhythmia defined by narrow QRS complex and atrioventricular (AV) dissociation or retrograde atrial conduction in a 1:1 pattern
accessory pathway-related atrioventricul: Accessory pathway-related atrioventricular reentrant tachycardia (AVRT) involves an abnormal electrical conduction of the accessory pathway. The accessory pathway connecting impulses between the atrium and the ventricle can be seen at any site in the AV groove
an abnormality of the pulmonary veins: An abnormality of the pulmonary veins
type 1 total anomalous pulmonary venous: Type 1 total anomalous pulmonary venous connection
type 2 total anomalous pulmonary venous: Type 2 total anomalous pulmonary venous connection
type 3 total anomalous pulmonary venous: Type 3 total anomalous pulmonary venous connection
type 4 total anomalous pulmonary venous: Type 4 total anomalous pulmonary venous connection
defect or defects of the morphogenesis o: Defect or defects of the morphogenesis of the right heart identifiable at birth
uhl anomaly of the right ventricle refer: Uhl anomaly of the right ventricle refers to the almost complete absence of right ventricular myocardium, normal tricuspid valve, and preserved septal and left ventricular myocardium
systemic desaturation of a liveborn baby: Systemic desaturation of a liveborn baby resulting from persistent pulmonary hypertension with a patent ductus arteriosus and patent foramen ovale, such that the circulation in postnatal life follows the fetal course
clonus at the elbow joint, i.e., an exag: Clonus at the elbow joint, i.e., an exaggerated phasic stretch reflex characterized by repetitive, rhythmic contractions at the elbow, generated by rapid passive stretch at the elbow joint
an abnormality in the range and ease of: An abnormality in the range and ease of motion of joints across their normal range
an abnormality of cortical sensation. as: An abnormality of cortical sensation. Assuming the primary sensory modalities are intact and the patient is alert and cooperative, the presence of an abnormality of sensory function usually indicates a lesion of a parietal cortex or of the thalamocortical projections to the parietal cortex
an abnormality of the concentration of c: An abnormality of the concentration of cortisol in the blood
any structural anomaly of the adrenal gl: Any structural anomaly of the adrenal glands
a functional abnormality of the adrenal: A functional abnormality of the adrenal glands
a form of adrenal insufficiency related: A form of adrenal insufficiency related to a lack of ACTH, which leads to a decrease in the production of cortisol by the adrenal glands. Aldosterone production is not usually affected
adrenal insufficiency secondary to a def: Adrenal insufficiency secondary to a defect in ACTH production
a form of hyperaldosteronism caused by a: A form of hyperaldosteronism caused by a defect within the adrenal gland
adrenal insufficiency secondary to a def: Adrenal insufficiency secondary to a defect in corticotropin-releasing hormone production
Adrenal insufficiency secondary to a defect in the corticotropin-releasing hormone receptor
a form of primary hyperaldosteronism in: A form of primary hyperaldosteronism in which the overproduction of aldosterone can be suppressed by the administration of dexamethasone
A form of primary hyperaldosteronism in which the overproduction of aldosterone cannot be suppressed by the administration of dexamethasone or similar glucocorticoids
a form of hyperaldosteronism caused by a: A form of hyperaldosteronism caused by abnormally increased renin levels
abnormal anatomical location of the adre: Abnormal anatomical location of the adrenal gland
absent development of the adrenal gland: Absent development of the adrenal gland
hypercortisolemia associated with a over: Hypercortisolemia associated with a overproduction of ACTH (often from a tumor), leading secondarily to overproduction of cortisol
an hormonally inactive adrenocortical ad: An hormonally inactive adrenocortical adenoma, that is, an adenoma that does not secrete excessive amounts of adrenal hormones
an hormonally active adrenocortical aden: An hormonally active adrenocortical adenoma, that is, an adenoma that secretes excessive amounts of adrenal hormones
an abnormality of the adenohypophysis, w: An abnormality of the adenohypophysis, which is also known as the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland
a reduced ability to secrete adrenocorti: A reduced ability to secrete adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), a hormone that stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete of glucocorticoids such as cortisol
overproduction of adrenocorticotropic ho: Overproduction of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), which generally leads secondarily to overproduction of cortisol by the adrenal cortex
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the adenohypophysis, which is also known as the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland
an abnormality of the neurohypophysis, w: An abnormality of the neurohypophysis, which is also known as the posterior lobe of the hypophysis
the presence of a neoplasm (tumour) in t: The presence of a neoplasm (tumour) in the neurohypophysis, which is also known as the posterior lobe of the hypophysis
abnormal development of the neurohypophy: Abnormal development of the neurohypophysis during embryonic growth and development
a solid, low grade, spindle cell, glial: A solid, low grade, spindle cell, glial neoplasm of adults that originates in the neurohypophysis or infundibulum. Clinical signs and symptoms include visual disturbance, headache and features of hypopituitarism. Pituicytomas are well-circumscribed, solid masses that can measure up to several centimeters. Histologically, they show a compact architecture consisting of elongate, bipolar spindle cells arranged in interlacing fascicles or assuming a storiform pattern
an abnormal anatomical location of the p: An abnormal anatomical location of the posterior lobe of the hypophysis, also known as the neurohypophysis. The posterior pituitary is normally present in the dorsal portion of the sella turcica, but when ectopic is usually near the median eminence. This defect is likely to be due to abnormal migration during embryogenesis
absence of the neurohypophysis owing to: Absence of the neurohypophysis owing to a developmental defect
a type of pituitary adenoma that produce: A type of pituitary adenoma that produces gonadotropins
A type of pituitary adenoma that produces growth hormone
a type of pituitary adenoma that is of u: A type of pituitary adenoma that is of unknown cellular origin and that lacks immunocytochemical or fine structural markers. Null cell adenomas are not associated with hormone excess
a type of pituitary adenoma that produce: A type of pituitary adenoma that produces thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH)
a pituitary tumor with subarachnoid, bra: A pituitary tumor with subarachnoid, brain, or systemic metastasis. The diagnosis of a pituitary carcinoma requires evidence of metastatic disease, either outside the central nervous system (CNS) or as separate noncontiguous foci within the CNS
a spindled-to-epithelioid, oncocytic, no: A spindled-to-epithelioid, oncocytic, nonendocrine neoplasm of the anterior hypophysis that manifests in adults and follows a benign clinical course. Pituitary spindle cell oncocytomas are firm, fibrous, and adherent to surrounding structures and are highly vascular
a structural abnormality of the parathyr: A structural abnormality of the parathyroid gland
a functional abnormality of the parathyr: A functional abnormality of the parathyroid gland
abnormal embryonic development of the pa: Abnormal embryonic development of the parathyroid gland
an abnormal anatomical location of the p: An abnormal anatomical location of the parathyroid gland
a type of hyperparathyroidism that occur: A type of hyperparathyroidism that occurs following kidney transplantation, which is a treatment for secondary hyperparathyroidism. Although kidney transplantation leads to a normalization of serum calcium and parathyroid hormone in most patients. The state of persistent hypercalcemia and hyperparathyroidism is referred to as tertiary hyperparathyroidism
a type of hypoparathyroidism with circul: A type of hypoparathyroidism with circulating antiparathyroid or anti-calcium sensing receptor antibodies indicative of autoimmunity
a structural abnormality of the thyroid: A structural abnormality of the thyroid gland
enlargement of the thyroid gland related: Enlargement of the thyroid gland related to a singular nodule in the thyroid gland
absence of a lobe of the thyroid gland r: Absence of a lobe of the thyroid gland related to a failure of its embryologic development
an abnormal growth of parafollicular (c-: An abnormal growth of parafollicular (C-cells) cells
a type of hypothyroidism due to an insuf: A type of hypothyroidism due to an insufficient stimulation of an otherwise normal thyroid gland. Central hypothyroidism is caused by either pituitary (secondary hypothyroidism) or hypothalamic (tertiary hypothyroidism) defects
an elevated concentration of free 3,3',5: An elevated concentration of free 3,3',5-triiodo-L-thyronine in the blood circulation
neoplasm categorized according to type o: Neoplasm categorized according to type of histological abnormality
neoplasm categorized according to the an: Neoplasm categorized according to the anatomical site of origin of the neoplasm
the presence of an embryonal neoplasm of: The presence of an embryonal neoplasm of the kidney that primarily affects children
presence of persistent islands of renal: Presence of persistent islands of renal blastema in the postnatal kidney, anywhere within a renal lobe (a portion of a kidney consisting of a renal pyramid and the renal cortex above it)
abnormally persistent foci of embryonal: Abnormally persistent foci of embryonal immature blastema located in the superficial cortical region (perilobar)
a type of papillary renal cell carcinoma: A type of papillary renal cell carcinoma that is characterized by small cuboidal cells covering thin papillae with a single line of uniform nuclei and small nucleoli
a renal tumor originating from an oncocy: A renal tumor originating from an oncocyte, which is an epithelial cell characterized by an excessive amount of mitochondria, resulting in an abundant acidophilic, granular cytoplasm
posterior positions and/or vertical shor: Posterior positions and/or vertical shortening of the infraorbital and perialar regions, or increased concavity of the face and/or reduced nasolabial angle
increased size of the parotid gland: Increased size of the parotid gland
a benign (noncancerous) tumorlike malfor: A benign (noncancerous) tumorlike malformation made up of an abnormal mixture of cells and tissues that originates in the tongue
visually assessable vertical indentation: Visually assessable vertical indentation, cleft, or depression of the nasal bridge, ridge and tip
a functional abnormality of a skeletal m: A functional abnormality of a skeletal muscle
a structural abnormality of a skeletal m: A structural abnormality of a skeletal muscle
atrophy (wasting) affecting primary type: Atrophy (wasting) affecting primary type 1 muscle fibers. This feature in general can only be observed on muscle biopsy
decreased intensity of the patellar refl: Decreased intensity of the patellar reflex (also known as the knee jerk reflex)
a type of myotonia that worsens with rep: A type of myotonia that worsens with repeated muscle contractions
a reduced ability to distinguish tactile: A reduced ability to distinguish tactile sensations at points that are very close to one another. This can be tested by using special calipers whose points can be set from 2mm to several centimeters apart
a reduced ability to identify precisely: A reduced ability to identify precisely the site of a touch. This test is usually carried out by asking a patient, whose eyes are closed or covered, to touch the same site with a fingertip
impaired ability to recognize letters or: Impaired ability to recognize letters or numbers drawn by an examiner's fingertip on the patient's skin (the patients eyes are closed or covered throughout this examination)
lipofuscin (age pigment) is a brown-yell: Lipofuscin (age pigment) is a brown-yellow, electron-dense, autofluorescent material that accumulates progressively over time in lysosomes of postmitotic cells, such as neurons and cardiac myocytes. This term pertains if there is an increase in the accumulation of lipofuscin (also known as autofluorescent lipoprotein) more than expected for the age of the patient
an increased level of hypoxanthine in th: An increased level of hypoxanthine in the urine
a congenital defect in the skull, whereb: A congenital defect in the skull, whereby there is a protrusion of part of the cranial contents through a congenital defect in the cranium, usually covered with skin or mucous membrane. The term encephalocele refers to a subclass of these lesions in which brain tissue protrudes through the defect
an encephalocele located between bregma: An encephalocele located between bregma and lambda
basal encephalocele is an encephalocele: Basal encephalocele is an encephalocele that occurs along the cribriform plate or through the sphenoid bone. The mass may appear in the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, epipharynx, sphenoid sinus, posterior orbit, or pterygopalatine fossa. The important distinction from other types is that no external tumor is visible except in those rare instances of herniations so large that they protrude through the mouth or nares
a cleft of the muscular (soft) portion o: A cleft of the muscular (soft) portion of the palate that is covered by mucous membrane. Soft-palate submucous clefts are characterized by a midline deficiency or lack of muscle tissue
absence of the normal opening of the cho: Absence of the normal opening of the choana (the posterior nasal aperture) as a result of an obstructing choanal membrane that may be thin and strandlike or thick and pluglike
an abnormality of one or more of the set: An abnormality of one or more of the set of bones that make up the facial skeleton
increased width of the midpoint of the m: Increased width of the midpoint of the mandible (mental protuberance) and overlying soft tissue
horizontal crease or fold situated below: Horizontal crease or fold situated below the vermilion border of the lower lip and above the fatty pad of the chin, with the face at rest
h-shaped crease in the fat pad of the ch: H-shaped crease in the fat pad of the chin
prominence of a triangular soft tissue a: Prominence of a triangular soft tissue area of the philtrum with the apex to the columella
narrow ridge in the midline of the philt: Narrow ridge in the midline of the philtral groove
absence of the usual parallel position o: Absence of the usual parallel position of philtral ridges
pit in the midline of the philtral groov: Pit in the midline of the philtral groove
distance between the philtral ridges, me: Distance between the philtral ridges, measured just above the vermilion border, more than 2 standard deviations below the mean. Alternatively, an apparently decreased distance between the ridges of the philtrum
nasal tip positioned to one side of the: Nasal tip positioned to one side of the midline
decrease in width of the nasal tip: Decrease in width of the nasal tip
positioning of the nasal tip inferior to: Positioning of the nasal tip inferior to the nasal base
a noninflammatory, progressive occlusion: A noninflammatory, progressive occlusion of the intracranial carotid arteries owing to the formation of netlike collateral arteries arising from the circle of Willis
delayed maturation and calcification of: Delayed maturation and calcification of the talus
detectable but decreased iga levels that: Detectable but decreased IgA levels that are more than 2 standard deviations below normal age-adjusted means
localized thickening and tightness of th: Localized thickening and tightness of the skin of the fingers or toes
a deviation from the normal count of t c: A deviation from the normal count of T cells
a functional anomaly of t cells: A functional anomaly of T cells
a potentially lethal cardiac arrhythmia: A potentially lethal cardiac arrhythmia characterized by an extremely rapid, hemodynamically unstable ventricular tachycardia (150-300 beats/min) with a large oscillating sine-wave appearance
an abnormality of the form, structure, o: An abnormality of the form, structure, or size of the skeletal system
an abnormality of the function of the sk: An abnormality of the function of the skeletal system
an abnormality of the appendicular skele: An abnormality of the appendicular skeletal system, consisting of the of the limbs, shoulder and pelvic girdles
a benign, painful, tumor of bone charact: A benign, painful, tumor of bone characterized by the formation of osteoid tissue, primitive bone and calcified tissue
a bone tumor composed of cellular spindl: A bone tumor composed of cellular spindle-cell stroma containing scattered multinucleated giant cells resembling osteoclasts
a type of abdominal pain that comes and: A type of abdominal pain that comes and goes in waves, most often starting and ending suddenly and being of severe intensity
any anomaly in the formation of bone or: Any anomaly in the formation of bone or of a bony substance, or the conversion of fibrous tissue or of cartilage into bone or a bony substance
accumulation of blood within the pericar: Accumulation of blood within the pericardial sac
accumulation of chyle (the whitish fluid: Accumulation of chyle (the whitish fluid taken up by the lacteals in the intestine, consisting of an emulsion of lymph and triglyceride fat thatpasses into the veins by the thoracic duct) in the pericardium. Chylopericardium is generally caused by obstruction of or trauma to the thoracic duct
accumulation of serous fluid (pale yello: Accumulation of serous fluid (pale yellow and transparent fluid) in the pericardial sac
accumulation of blood in the peritoneal: Accumulation of blood in the peritoneal cavity owing to internal hemorrhage
abnormal accumulation of fluid leading t: Abnormal accumulation of fluid leading to swelling of the pharynx
an appetite for and the persistent inges: An appetite for and the persistent ingestion of non-food substances such as clay. In order to diagnose pica, this behavior must have persisted over a period of at least one month
a discrete mass of neoplastic monoclonal: A discrete mass of neoplastic monoclonal plasma cells either in the bone marrow or in an extramedullary location
decreased activity of coagulation factor: Decreased activity of coagulation factor IX. Factor IX, which itself is activated by factor Xa or factor VIIa to form factor IXa, activates factor X into factor Xa
a type of keratitis characterized by inf: A type of keratitis characterized by inflammation in pinpoint areas of the corneal epithelium
the presence of spots or rounded patches: The presence of spots or rounded patches of abnormally increased density of metaphyseal bone
both lungs have three lobes. normally, t: Both lungs have three lobes. Normally, the left lung has two lobes, whereas the right lung has three lobes
any structural anomaly of the connective: Any structural anomaly of the connective tissue bundles in the extracellular matrix of bone tissue that are composed of collagen, and play a role in tissue strength and elasticity
any anomaly in the formation of the bony: Any anomaly in the formation of the bony substance of the sternum
an abnormally increased diphosphate(4-): An abnormally increased diphosphate(4-) concentration in the blood. Diphosphate(4-), as ester with two phosphate groups, is also known as pyrophosphate
an anomaly of the ilium ala. this is the: An anomaly of the ilium ala. This is the large expanded portion of the ilum which bounds the greater pelvis laterally
pain in the lower back and hip radiating: Pain in the lower back and hip radiating in the distribution of the sciatic nerve
any anomaly in the function of thrombocy: Any anomaly in the function of thrombocytes
abnormal response to arachidonic acid as: Abnormal response to arachidonic acid as manifested by reduced or lacking aggregation of platelets upon addition of arachidonic acid
abnormal response to ristocetin as manif: Abnormal response to ristocetin as manifested by reduced or lacking aggregation of platelets upon addition of ristocetin
abnormal response to thrombin or thrombi: Abnormal response to thrombin or thrombin mimetics as manifested by reduced or lacking aggregation of platelets upon addition of thrombin (or thrombin mimetics)
abnormal number of platelets per volume: Abnormal number of platelets per volume of blood. In a healthy adult, a normal platelet count is between 150,000 and 450,000 per microliter of blood
low platelet count following administrat: Low platelet count following administration of unfractionated or (less commonly) low-molecular weight heparin
an anomaly in platelet form, ultrastruct: An anomaly in platelet form, ultrastructure, or intracellular organelles
anomalous size of platelets. most normal: Anomalous size of platelets. Most normal sized platelets are 1.5 to 3 micrometers in diameter. Large platelets are 4 to 7 micrometers. Giant platelets are larger than 7 micrometers and usually 10 to 20 micrometers
average platelet volume above the upper: Average platelet volume above the upper limit of the normal reference interval
presence of reduced amount of a membrane: Presence of reduced amount of a membrane protein on the cell membrane of platelets. This feature is typically measured by flow cytometry
decreased cell membrane concentration of: Decreased cell membrane concentration of the glycoprotein complex Ib-IX-V
an acute form of disseminated intravascu: An acute form of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Acute DIC can occur following sudden exposure of blood to procoagulants, with the compensatory hemostatic mechanisms becoming overwhelmed
decreased cell membrane concentration of: Decreased cell membrane concentration of glycoprotein VI
Decreased cell membrane concentration of P2Y12 receptor
an anomaly of alpha or dense granules or: An anomaly of alpha or dense granules or platelet lysosomes
abnormal bleeding of the umbilical stump: Abnormal bleeding of the umbilical stump following separation of the cord at approximately 7-10 days after birth
bleeding from vessels of the various tis: Bleeding from vessels of the various tissues of the eye
bleeding in the anterior chamber of the: Bleeding in the anterior chamber of the eye
hemorrhage from the vessels of the choro: Hemorrhage from the vessels of the choroid
bleeding sufficiently severe as to requi: Bleeding sufficiently severe as to require red cell transfusion (WHO Grade 3 or 4)
significant bleeding or hemorrhage witho: Significant bleeding or hemorrhage without significant precipitating factor
prolonged or protracted bleeding followi: Prolonged or protracted bleeding following an invasive procedure or intervention
significant maternal haemorrhage/blood l: Significant maternal haemorrhage/blood loss following deilvery of a child
number of leukocytes per volume of blood: Number of leukocytes per volume of blood beyond normal limits
abnormal response to thromboxane as mani: Abnormal response to thromboxane as manifested by reduced or lacking aggregation of platelets upon addition of thromboxane A2 receptor agonists
a type of anemia related to a reduction: A type of anemia related to a reduction in the average life span of red blood cells in the peripheral circulation, which is normally around 120 days
bleeding beneath the mucous membrane tha: Bleeding beneath the mucous membrane that lines the inner surface of the eyelid
increased number of neutrophils circulat: Increased number of neutrophils circulating in blood
an abnormality of the level of activity: An abnormality of the level of activity of circulating fibrinogen
increased concentration of fibrinogen in: Increased concentration of fibrinogen in the blood
decreased concentration of fibrinogen in: Decreased concentration of fibrinogen in the blood
anomaly in the level or the function of: Anomaly in the level or the function of hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying protein of erythrocytes
hemoglobin h (hbh) contains four beta-gl: Hemoglobin H (HbH) contains four beta-globin chains. It is normally not present at all in blood, but may make up about 1-40 percent of all hemoglobin in HbH disease, a subform of alpha thalassemia
hemoglobin f (hbf) contains two globin a: Hemoglobin F (HbF) contains two globin alpha chains and two globin gamma chains. It is the main form of hemoglobin in the fetus during the last seven months of intrauterine development and in the half year of postnatal life. In adults it normally makes up less than one percent of all hemoglobiin. This term refers to an increase in HbF above this limit. In beta thalassemia major, it may represent over 90 percent of all hemoglobin, and in beta thalassemia minor it may make up between 0.5 to 4 percent
hemoglobin a (hba) contains two globin a: Hemoglobin A (HbA) contains two globin alpha chains and two globin beta chains. HbA is normally the main adult hemoglobin, representing about 96-98 percent of all hemoglobin. This term represents a decreased in the proportion of HbA below this limit, and can be seen in various forms of thalassemia
a reduction in the ratio of production o: A reduction in the ratio of production of beta globin to that of alpha globin. This is the major abnormality in the various forms of beta thalassemia
A reduction in the ratio of production of alpha globin to that of beta globin. This is the major abnormality in the various forms of alpha thalassemia
missing radius bone on one side only ass: Missing radius bone on one side only associated with congenital failure of development
abnormally flat shape of the heads of th: Abnormally flat shape of the heads of the metacarpal bones
abnormal reduction in length affecting a: Abnormal reduction in length affecting all phalanges
an anomaly of a metacarpophalangeal join: An anomaly of a metacarpophalangeal joint
an anomaly of the glenoid fossa, also kn: An anomaly of the glenoid fossa, also known as the glenoid cavity, which is the articular surface of the scapula that articulates with the head of the humerus
excessive, increased hair growth located: Excessive, increased hair growth located in the lumbar region
Excessive, increased hair growth located in the thoracic region
abnormal calcification in the cardiovasc: Abnormal calcification in the cardiovascular system
atrophy of the extensor digitorum longus: Atrophy of the extensor digitorum longus muscles, which mediate extension of the toes
bending or curvature of a fourth toe in: Bending or curvature of a fourth toe in the tibial direction (i.e., towards the big toe)
accumulation of pus in the pleural cavit: Accumulation of pus in the pleural cavity
a type of pleural effusion with a transu: A type of pleural effusion with a transudate (extravascular fluid with low protein content and a low specific gravity). Pleural effusions can be classified as transudates or exudates based on Light's criteria, which classify an effusion as exudate if one or more of the following are present: (1) the ratio of pleural fluid protein to serum protein is greater than 0.5, (2) the ratio of pleural fluid lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) to serum LDH is greater than 0.6, or (3) the pleural fluid LDH level is greater than two thirds of the upper limit of normal for serum LDH
a type of pleural effusion with a exudat: A type of pleural effusion with a exudate (extravascular fluid that has exuded out of a tissue or its capillaries due to injury or inflammation). Pleural effusions can be classified as transudates or exudates based on Light's criteria, which classify an effusion as exudate if one or more of the following are present: (1) the ratio of pleural fluid protein to serum protein is greater than 0.5, (2) the ratio of pleural fluid lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) to serum LDH is greater than 0.6, or (3) the pleural fluid LDH level is greater than two thirds of the upper limit of normal for serum LDH
an increased or decreased activity of th: An increased or decreased activity of the mitochondrial respiratory chain
a reduction in the activity of the mitoc: A reduction in the activity of the mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I, which is part of the electron transport chain in mitochondria
A reduction in the activity of the mitochondrial respiratory chain complex III, which is part of the electron transport chain in mitochondria

A reduction in the activity of the mitochondrial proton-transporting ATP synthase complex, which makes ATP via oxidative phosphorylation, and is sometimes described as Complex V of the electron transport chain
proximal mislocalization of the big toe: Proximal mislocalization of the big toe from its normal position
one or more digit that appears dispropor: One or more digit that appears disproportionately short compared to the hand/foot, whereby either the entire digit or a specific phalanx is shortened
developmental hypoplasia (shortening) of: Developmental hypoplasia (shortening) of proximal phalanx of toe
presence of an additional phalanx-like b: Presence of an additional phalanx-like bone, producing an extra, wedge-shaped bone at the base of the proximal phalanx of the third finger
An anomaly of the cerebellar peduncles. The superior, middle, and inferior cerebellar peduncles emerge from the cerebellum. The superior cerebellar penduncles connect the cerebellum to the midbrain, the middle cerebellar peduncles connect the cerebellum to the pons, and the inferior cerebellar peduncle connects the medulla spinalis and medulla oblongata with the cerebellum
an anomaly of the superior cerebellar pe: An anomaly of the superior cerebellar peduncle
increased length of the superior cerebel: Increased length of the superior cerebellar peduncle
abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatat: Abnormal outpouching or sac-like dilatation in the wall of the inferior mesenteric artery or superior mesenteric artery
decreased concentration of urate in the: Decreased concentration of urate in the urine
a decreased concentration of total carni: A decreased concentration of total carnitine in the blood
underdeveloped nails of the fifth toes: Underdeveloped nails of the fifth toes
a soft tissue continuity in the a/p axis: A soft tissue continuity in the A/P axis between fingers 3 and 4
an abnormality of the shape of the thora: An abnormality of the shape of the thoracic vertebra T12 such that it is wedge-shaped (narrow towards the front)
an abnormality of the shape of the lumba: An abnormality of the shape of the lumbar vertebra L2 such that it is wedge-shaped (narrow towards the front)
Increased concentration of SO3(2-), i.e., sulfite, in the urine
increased concentration of thiosulfate(2: Increased concentration of thiosulfate(2-) in the urine
a type of vasculitis (inflammation of bl: A type of vasculitis (inflammation of blood vessel walls) that affects blood vessels that are smaller than arteries, i.e., arterioles, venules, and capilllaries
bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneu: Bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneumonia (BOOP) is and interstitial lung abnormalitiy characterized histopathologically by plugs of granulation tissue lying within small airways, alveolar ducts, and alveoli and by chronic inflammatory cell infiltration in alveolar walls. Patients with BOOP generally present with subacute illness, including shortness of breath, fever, malaise, and weight loss
inflammation and fibrosis of the bronchi: Inflammation and fibrosis of the bronchioles leading to partial or complete obstruction of these airways
an infection of the upper or lower respi: An infection of the upper or lower respiratory tract
