a history of repeated acute infections o: A history of repeated acute infections of the upper or lower respiratory tract
acute inflammation of the lung due to an: Acute inflammation of the lung due to an infection
pneumonia due to the aspiration (breathi: Pneumonia due to the aspiration (breathing in) of food, liquid, or gastric contents into the upper respiratory tract
an acute episode of pneumonia due to the: An acute episode of pneumonia due to the aspiration (breathing in) of food, liquid, or gastric contents into the upper respiratory tract
diffuse benign transformation of the hep: Diffuse benign transformation of the hepatic parenchyma into small regenerative nodules with minimal or no fibrosis
the presence of multiple granulomas in t: The presence of multiple granulomas in the liver as based on pathological examination. Granulomas are small 0.5 to 2 mm collections of modified macrophages called epithelioid cells usually surrounded by lymphocytes
a lymphoproliferative abnormality of the: A lymphoproliferative abnormality of the intestine characterized by numerous visible mucosal nodules measuring up to, and rarely exceeding, 0.5 cm in diameter Histologically, hyperplastic lymphoid follicles with large germinal centres are seen in the lamina propria and superficial submucosa. There is enlargement of the mucosal B cell follicles caused by hyperplasia of the follicle centres; surrounded by a normal appearing mantle zone. Disease may involve the stomach, the entire small intestine, and the large intestine
an abnormality of the pectoral muscle, c: An abnormality of the pectoral muscle, comprising the pectoralis major, a thick, fan-shaped muscle of the anterior chest and the pectoralis minor, a thin, triangular muscle situated underneath the pectoralis major
a small hole through the whole thickness: A small hole through the whole thickness of the retina
hypoplasia (underdevelopment) of the pec: Hypoplasia (underdevelopment) of the pectoralis minor on only one side of the chest
focal proliferation of glial cells in th: Focal proliferation of glial cells in the substantia nigra
absence of any measurable level of sperm: Absence of any measurable level of sperm in his semen, resulting from a defect in the production of spermatozoa in the testes. Can be differentiated from obstructive azoospermia on the basis of testicular biopsy

Absence of any measurable level of sperm in his semen, resulting from post-testicular obstruction or retrograde ejaculation. Can be differentiated from obstructive azoospermia on the basis of testicular biopsy

Absence of any measurable level of sperm in his semen, due to a hypothalamic or pituitary abnormality diagnosed with hypo-gonadotropic-hypogonadism. The diagnosis is made on the basis of low LH and FSH levels and low or normal testosterone levels
history of repeated intermittent involun: History of repeated intermittent involuntary muscle contractions that were painful
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of citrulline in the blood circulation
an increased concentration of citrulline: An increased concentration of citrulline in the blood
a reduced concentration of copper in the: A reduced concentration of copper in the blood
impaired ability to eat related to probl: Impaired ability to eat related to problems gathering food and getting ready to suck, chew, or swallow it
an elevated concentration of luteinizing: An elevated concentration of luteinizing hormone in the blood
amyloid deposition in the walls of lepto: Amyloid deposition in the walls of leptomeningeal and cortical arteries, arterioles, and less often capillaries and veins of the central nervous system
an exaggerated whealing tendency when th: An exaggerated whealing tendency when the skin is stroked, that is, formation of red, itchy bumps and lines on the skin as a result of pressure on the skin (for instance, stroking the skin with a pen or tongue depressor)
abnormally low glucose concentration in: Abnormally low glucose concentration in the cerebrospinal fluid
repeated episodes of sudden-onset and tr: Repeated episodes of sudden-onset and transient lethargy
replacement of bone marrow by fibrous ti: Replacement of bone marrow by fibrous tissue
partial or complete loss of hearing foll: Partial or complete loss of hearing following ingestion of aminoglycoside antibiotics
an increased concentration of catecholam: An increased concentration of catecholamine in the urine
an increased concentration of homovanill: An increased concentration of homovanillic acid in the urine
an increased concentration of vanillylma: An increased concentration of vanillylmandelic acid in the urine
an increased concentration of dopamine i: An increased concentration of dopamine in the urine
gallstones composed primarily of cholest: Gallstones composed primarily of cholesterol, usually about 2-3 cm in length with an oval form and a yellow or green/brown color
gallstones composed primarily of bilirub: Gallstones composed primarily of bilirubin and calcium salts (calcium bilirubinate) with a low cholesterol concentration
a type of pigment gallstone that is hard: A type of pigment gallstone that is hard and black, containing calcium carbonate and calcium phosphates
a type of pigment gallstone that is brow: A type of pigment gallstone that is brown, containing calcium fatty acids. These stones are softer than black pigment gallstones
failure of formation of the lumen of the: Failure of formation of the lumen of the gallbladder, often associated with gallbladder hypoplasia
clay colored stools lacking bile pigment: Clay colored stools lacking bile pigment
formation of abnormal, extraskeletal bon: Formation of abnormal, extraskeletal bony tissue, i.e., the presence of bone in soft tissue where bone normally does not exist
formation of abnormal bony tissue within: Formation of abnormal bony tissue within muscle tissue
Formation of abnormal bony tissue within tendon tissue
Formation of abnormal bony tissue within ligament tissue
a deviation from the normal range of neu: A deviation from the normal range of neutrophil cell counts in the circulation
an abnormal form or size of neutrophils: An abnormal form or size of neutrophils
a reduction in the ability of neutrophil: A reduction in the ability of neutrophils to kill bacteria
an abnormality of the interatrial septum: An abnormality of the interatrial septum
a bulging of the interatrial septum towa: A bulging of the interatrial septum towards one side. In adults, atrial septal aneurysm can be defined as a protrusion of the aneurysm of >10 mm beyond the plane of the atrial septum as measured by transesophageal echocardiography
increased activity of coagulation factor: Increased activity of coagulation factor V, Factor V, which is activated to factor Va by means of minute amounts of thrombin (and inactivated by larger amounts of thrombin). Activated factor V (fVa) is a cofactor in the formation of the prothrombinase complex
abnormally increased level of blood lact: Abnormally increased level of blood lactate following a meal
an increased concentration of glucose in: An increased concentration of glucose in the blood following a meal
a persecutory delusion of supposed hosti: A persecutory delusion of supposed hostility of others
eeg with generalized sharp transient wav: EEG with generalized sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec
eeg with repetitive generalized sharp tr: EEG with repetitive generalized sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec
affective, mnemonic or composite percept: Affective, mnemonic or composite perceptual auras with subjective qualities similar to those experienced in life but are recognized by the subject as occurring outside of actual context
affective auras with subjective qualitie: Affective auras with subjective qualities similar to those experienced in life but are recognized by the subject as occurring outside of actual context
auras which reflect ictal dysmnesia such: Auras which reflect ictal dysmnesia such as: feelings as familiarity (deja-vu) and unfamiliarity (jamais-vu)
a subjective feeling that an experience: A subjective feeling that an experience which is occurring for the first time has been experienced before
A subjective feeling that an experience which has occurred before is being experienced for the first time
auras with creation of composite percept: Auras with creation of composite perceptions without corresponding external stimuli involving visual, auditory, somatosensory, olfactory and/or gustatory phenomena
auras with an alteration of actual perce: Auras with an alteration of actual percepts involving the visual, auditory, somatosensory, olfactory or gustatory systems
eeg with focal sharp transient waves in: EEG with focal sharp transient waves in the central region, i.e., focal sharp waves of a duration less than 80 msec followed by a slow wave
EEG with focal sharp transient waves in the frontal region, i.e., focal sharp waves of a duration less than 80 msec followed by a slow wave
EEG with focal sharp transient waves in the occipital region, i.e., focal sharp waves of a duration less than 80 msec followed by a slow wave
EEG with focal sharp transient waves in the parietal region, i.e., focal sharp waves of a duration less than 80 msec followed by a slow wave
EEG with focal sharp transient waves in the temporal region, i.e., focal sharp waves of a duration less than 80 msec followed by a slow wave
eeg with focal sharp transient waves of: EEG with focal sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec in the central region
EEG with focal sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec in the frontal region
EEG with focal sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec in the occipital region
EEG with focal sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec in the parietal region
EEG with focal sharp transient waves of a duration less than 80 msec in the temporal region
complete dislocation of the lens of the: Complete dislocation of the lens of the eye
aorta descends on right instead of on th: Aorta descends on right instead of on the left
persistence of blood flow through the du: Persistence of blood flow through the ductus venosus for longer than the normal time after birth
a congenital defect of the vasculature s: A congenital defect of the vasculature such that there is a shunt (by-pass) of blood directly from the portal vein to the vena cava (i.e., the blood from the portal vein is not filtered through the liver)
elevated concentration of galactose in t: Elevated concentration of galactose in the urine
Elevated concentration of galactose in the blood
deviation from the normal concentration: Deviation from the normal concentration of ornithine in the blood circulation
increased concentration of ornithine in: Increased concentration of ornithine in the blood
an abnormal accumulation of fluid and sw: An abnormal accumulation of fluid and swelling in the tissues of the larynx
a benign tumor of the liver of presumabl: A benign tumor of the liver of presumably epithelial origin
an abnormal concentration of a hormone i: An abnormal concentration of a hormone in the urine
abnormally increased concentration of co: Abnormally increased concentration of cortisol in the urine
benign neoplasia derived from lipoblasts: Benign neoplasia derived from lipoblasts or lipocytes of white or brown fat. May be angiomatous or hibernomatous
presence of a lipoma in the region of th: Presence of a lipoma in the region of the sacrum
malignant neoplasms which probably origi: Malignant neoplasms which probably originate in primitive mesenchymal stem cell populations differentiating down a lipomatous pathway
multiple, localized or widespread, asymp: Multiple, localized or widespread, asymptomatic or inflammatory dermal cysts involving the pilosebaceous units. Lesions can appear anywhere on the body, but steatocystoma multiplex is more commonly involved with those areas of the skin with a high density of developed pilosebaceous units (e.g., the axilla, groin, neck, and proximal extremities)
wasting of the sternocleidomastoid muscl: Wasting of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, the muscle in the anterior part of the neck that acts to flex and rotate the head
wasting of the pectoral muscles, i.e., o: Wasting of the pectoral muscles, i.e., of the pectoralis major and pectoralis minor
corneal guttata are droplet-like accumul: Corneal guttata are droplet-like accumulations of non-banded collagen on the posterior surface of Descemet's membrane. The presence of focal thickenings of Descemet's membrane histologically named guttae. Cornea guttata can be easily diagnosed in vivo and ex vivo by means of specular microscopy as it gives dark areas where no endothelial cells are visible
presence of folds in the descement membr: Presence of folds in the Descement membrane, which is the basement membrane of the endothelial (inner) cell layer of the cornea. Descement membrane folds are generally a manifestation of inflammation or edema of the cornea
abnormal accumulation of fluid and swell: Abnormal accumulation of fluid and swelling of the stroma of cornea
a type of asthma in which aspirin and ot: A type of asthma in which aspirin and other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) that inhibit cyclooxygen-ase 1 (COX-1) exacerbate bronchoconstriction
rhythmic, involuntary sinusoidal oscilla: Rhythmic, involuntary sinusoidal oscillations of one or both eyes. The waveform of pendular nystagmus may occur in any direction
seesaw nystagmus is a type of pendular n: Seesaw nystagmus is a type of pendular nystagmus where a half cycle consists of the elevation and intorsion of one eye, concurrently with the depression and extortion of the fellow eye. In the other half cycle, there is an inversion of the ocular movements
presence of multiple yellowish-white les: Presence of multiple yellowish-white lesions of various size and configuration on the retina not related to vascular lesions
inability to elicit tendon reflexes in t: Inability to elicit tendon reflexes in the upper limbs
a visual defect characterized by the ina: A visual defect characterized by the inability to see as clearly in bright light as in dim light. The word hemeralopia literally means day blindness
a kind of focal dystonia characterized b: A kind of focal dystonia characterized by forceful contractions of the face, jaw, and/or tongue causing difficulty in opening and closing the mouth and often affecting chewing and speech
a form of focal dystonia that affects th: A form of focal dystonia that affects the vocal cords, associated with involuntary contractions of the vocal cords causing interruptions of speech and affecting the voice quality and often leading to patterned, repeated breaks in speech
an extreme form of generalized edema wit: An extreme form of generalized edema with widespread and massive edema due to effusion of fluid into the extracellular space
hypoglycermia following a meal (or more: Hypoglycermia following a meal (or more generally, after intake of glucose)
a reduced concentration of calcitriol in: A reduced concentration of calcitriol in the blood. Calcitriol is also known as 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol or 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3
a reduced concentration of calcifediol i: A reduced concentration of calcifediol in the blood. Calcifediol is also known as calcidiol, 25-hydroxycholecalciferol and 25-Hydroxyvitamin D3
malignant tumor of melanocytes of the ch: Malignant tumor of melanocytes of the choroid. The classic appearance of choroidal melanoma is a pigmented dome-shaped or collar button-shaped tumor with an associated exudative retinal detachment. Choroidal melanoma is usually pigmented, but can be variably pigmented and even amelanotic (non-pigmented)
malignant tumor of melanocytes of the ci: Malignant tumor of melanocytes of the ciliary body
the presence of a melanoma of the skin: The presence of a melanoma of the skin
a type of melanoma that is flat and irre: A type of melanoma that is flat and irregular in shape and color, with different shades of black and brown
a type of melanoma that starts as a rais: A type of melanoma that starts as a raised area that is usually dark blackish-blue or bluish-red but may not have any color
a subtype of melanoma in situ that typic: A subtype of melanoma in situ that typically develops on sun-damaged skin. The lesion is typically a large, irregularly pigmented macule that has developed from an ordinary lentigo (a small pigmented spot on the skin with a clearly-defined edge). Change to a malignant lentigo typically takes place over 20 years or more, and many patients accept the change as a consequence of aging
a type of cutaneous melanoma localized t: A type of cutaneous melanoma localized to the palm, sole, or beneath the nail (subungual melanoma). Acral lentiginous melanoma starts as a slowly-enlarging flat patch of discoloured skin and usually displays a size above 6 mm and often several centimetres or more in diameter upon diagnosis and variable pigmentation with a mixutre of colors including brown, and blue-grey, black and red. The surface of the lesion is initially smooth but later in the course may become thicker and irregular, and may ulcerate or bleed
excretion of oligosaccharides conjugated: Excretion of oligosaccharides conjugated to sialic acid in the urine
a fluid filled cavity that develops with: A fluid filled cavity that develops with a bone
radiographic features include a dilated,: Radiographic features include a dilated, radiolucent lesion typically located eccentrically within the metaphyseal portion of the bone, with fluid levels visible on magnetic resonance imaging
a benign fluid filled simple cyst of bon: A benign fluid filled simple cyst of bone filled with serous fluid
presence of multiple cystic changes in m: Presence of multiple cystic changes in multiple areas or multiple bones
increased concentration of disaccharide: Increased concentration of disaccharide in the urine
increased excretion of glycopeptides in: Increased excretion of glycopeptides in the urine. Glycopeptides are peptides with carbohydrate moieties covalently attached to the side chains of the amino acid residues
excretion of excess amounts of aspartylg: Excretion of excess amounts of aspartylglucosamine in the urine
an increased concentration of keratan su: An increased concentration of keratan sulfate in the urine
an increased concentration of chondroiti: An increased concentration of chondroitin sulfate (CHEBI:37397) in the urine
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration in the blood circulation of acylcarnitine, which is produced by reversible esterification of the 3-hydroxyl group of carnitine
excretion of urine with an acid ph: Excretion of urine with an acid pH
an abnormal distribution of n-acylglycin: An abnormal distribution of N-acylglycines in the urine. There are numerous different N-acylglycines, and this term refers to pathological alterations in their level or distribution
an abnormality of the pupillary light re: An abnormality of the pupillary light reaction characterized by a marked slowing of the light reaction of usually just one pupil. The pupil tends to be relatively dilated, and there is reduced accommodation
an abnormality of mental functioning aff: An abnormality of mental functioning affecting the personality and behavioural tendencies of an individual and characterized by a rigid and unhealthy pattern of thinking and behavior. The definition of a personal disorder implies that the abnormality is not the result of damage or insult to the brain or from another psychiatric disorder
a personality disorder characterized by: A personality disorder characterized by impulsive behavior and unpredictable and capricious mood. Affected individuals show a liability to outbursts of emotion and an incapacity to control the behavioural explosions

A personality disorder characterized by shallow and labile affectivity, self-dramatization, theatricality, exaggerated expression of emotions, suggestibility, egocentricity, self-indulgence, lack of consideration for others, easily hurt feelings, and continuous seeking for appreciation, excitement and attention
blockade of impulses at a focal site alo: Blockade of impulses at a focal site along the course of a motor axon
any anomaly of the conduction of motor n: Any anomaly of the conduction of motor nerve impulses in the central nervous system
atrophy of the cerebellum affecting prim: Atrophy of the cerebellum affecting primarily the granular cell layer
an abnormally increased size of the cere: An abnormally increased size of the cerebellum compared to other brain structures
atrophy of the cerebellum affecting prim: Atrophy of the cerebellum affecting primarily the Purkinje cell layer
nuclear or cytoplasmic aggregates that s: Nuclear or cytoplasmic aggregates that show positive staining with antibodies against ubiquitin within cells of the brain
any abnormality of the size of the skele: Any abnormality of the size of the skeletal muscle cell
presence of an increased number of neutr: Presence of an increased number of neutrophils in the urine
an abnormal color of the urine, that is,: An abnormal color of the urine, that is, the color of the urine appears different from the usual straw-yellow color
an anomaly in the surface contour of mit: An anomaly in the surface contour of mitochondria
a deviation from the normal odor of the: A deviation from the normal odor of the urine
an anomaly of the function of the pancre: An anomaly of the function of the pancreas
a functional anomaly of the acinar gland: A functional anomaly of the acinar gland portion of the pancreas that secretes digestive enzymes
a deviation from the normal size of the: A deviation from the normal size of the pancreas
a congenital inclusion cysts that arises: A congenital inclusion cysts that arises from ectodermal cells that normally form skin cells being left behind in the nervous system during development

A congenital inclusion cysts that arises from the inclusion of ectodermally committed cells at the time of neural tube closure (3rd-5th week of embryogenesis). The capsule of dermoid cysts consists of simple epithelium supported by collagen. In thicker parts, the lining is supplemented with dermis containing hair follicles, sebaceous glands, and apocrine glands
an abnormal accumulation of fluid beneat: An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin on the back of the feet
an abnormal catecholamine concentration: An abnormal catecholamine concentration in the blood
an abnormal concentration of creatinine: An abnormal concentration of creatinine in the blood
an abnormally reduced amount of creatini: An abnormally reduced amount of creatinine in the blood
a deviation from the normal number of mi: A deviation from the normal number of mitochondria per cell

The deposition of calcium salts in the parenchyma of the renal cortex (the outer portion of the kidney between the renal capsule and the renal medulla)

increased width of the skin of vermilion: Increased width of the skin of vermilion border region of upper lip

noises from the temporomandibular joint: Noises from the temporomandibular joint during mandibular movement (e.g., chewing). Temporomandibular joint crepitus is often described as a clicking, popping, grating sound

The definition of VBD includes: (i) diameter of basilar or vertebral artery over 4.5 mm; or (ii) deviation of any portion more than 10 mm from the shortest expected course; and (iii) length of basilar artery over 29.5 mm or length of intracranial vertebral artery over 23.5 mm
a type of incomitant strabismus in which: A type of incomitant strabismus in which both elevator muscles (i.e., the inferior oblique and superior rectus muscles) of the same eye are weak leading to restricted elevation and hypotropia
an ocular movement abnormality character: An ocular movement abnormality characterised by simultaneous weakness of the inferior rectus muscle and superior oblique muscle of the same eye
a type of incomitant strabismus in which: A type of incomitant strabismus in which the angle of deviation varies as the patient's gaze shifts upwards and/or downwards
a manifest or latent ocular deviation in: A manifest or latent ocular deviation in which one or both eyes tends to deviate nasally
a form of esotropia (convergent deviatio: A form of esotropia (convergent deviation of the eyes) associated with activation of the accommodative reflex
any structural anomaly of a cell of the: Any structural anomaly of a cell of the monocyte, granulocyte, mast cell, megakaryocyte, or erythroid lineage
a reduction in the numbers of pro-b cell: A reduction in the numbers of pro-B cells (defined by coexpression of CD34 and CD19). Earlier B-cell precursors are defined by expressing surface CD34 and cytoplasmic TdT in the absence of CD19
a manifest or latent ocular deviation in: A manifest or latent ocular deviation in which one or both eyes tends to deviate temporally
the presence of autoantibodies in the se: The presence of autoantibodies in the serum that react against granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor
any functional abnormality of erythrocyt: Any functional abnormality of erythrocytes (red-blood cells)
any deviation from the normal number of: Any deviation from the normal number of red blood cells per volume in the circulation
an abnormal elevation above the normal n: An abnormal elevation above the normal number of red blood cells per volume in the circulation
an abnormal reduction below the normal n: An abnormal reduction below the normal number of red blood cells per volume in the circulation
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of hemoglobin in the blood
an abnormal reduction below normal hemog: An abnormal reduction below normal hemoglobin concentration in the circulation
an abnormal elevation above normal hemog: An abnormal elevation above normal hemoglobin concentration in the circulation
any deviation from the normal number of: Any deviation from the normal number of eosinophils per volume in the blood circulation
the presence of virus in the blood: The presence of virus in the blood
persistent presence of epstein-barr viru: Persistent presence of Epstein-Barr virus in the blood
a white or lighter patch of skin that ma: A white or lighter patch of skin that may appear anywhere on the body and are caused by decreased skin pigmentation
osteoarthritis of the metatarsophalangea: Osteoarthritis of the metatarsophalangeal joint of the first toe
widening of the wall of capillary blood: Widening of the wall of capillary blood vessels in the glomerulus. This feature may be produced by deposits and other changes affecting either subepithelial and subendothelial regions or the glomerular basement membrane itself
a structural anomaly of the capillary bl: A structural anomaly of the capillary blood vessels in the renal glomerulus
an abnormal anatomic position of the fov: An abnormal anatomic position of the fovea, the small, central pit composed of closely packed cones that is located in the macula of the retina
downward movement of the trachea during: Downward movement of the trachea during inspiration due to downward traction on the tracheobronchial tree
the upper incisors deviate from the norm: The upper incisors deviate from the normal angle of being roughly parallel to the surface of the face and instead slant outwards
partial or complete loss of foveal tissu: Partial or complete loss of foveal tissue that was once present
narrowing of the anterior nasal aperture: Narrowing of the anterior nasal aperture (piriform or pyriform aperture), which is a pear-shaped opening in the skull that forms the bony inlet of the nose
diffusely widened perivascular spaces in: Diffusely widened perivascular spaces in the basal ganglia, affecting especially the corpus striatum. Status cribrosum is usually symmetrical, with the perivascular spaces showing CSF signal and without diffusion restriction. The word cribriform means sievelike, with multiple perforations
a type of rigidity that is manifested by: A type of rigidity that is manifested by an exaggerated extensor posture of all extremities
small, hard cyst-like nodules, freely mo: Small, hard cyst-like nodules, freely moveable in the subcutis over the bony prominences of the legs and arms, which have an outer calcified layer with a translucent core on x-ray
a structural anomaly of the tiny blood v: A structural anomaly of the tiny blood vessels that connect arterioles with venules and whose walls act as semipermeable membranes that mediate the diffusion of fluids and gases between the blood circulation and body tissues
reduced resistance to rupture of capilla: Reduced resistance to rupture of capillary blood vessels. Capillary fragility may manifest as a bleeding diathesis with spontaneous ecchymoses (bruises)
a functional anomaly of the tiny blood v: A functional anomaly of the tiny blood vessels that connect arterioles with venules and whose walls act as semipermeable membranes that mediate the diffusion of fluids and gases between the blood circulation and body tissues
sudden breakage of an artery leading to: Sudden breakage of an artery leading to leakage of blood from the circulation
an increased concentration of prostate s: An increased concentration of prostate specific antigen (PSA) in the circulation
a deviation from normal range of the ery: A deviation from normal range of the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), a test that measures the distance that erythrocytes have fallen after one hour in a vertical column of anticoagulated blood under the influence of gravity. The ESR is a nonspecific finding. An elevation may indicate inflammation or may be caused by any condition that elevates fibrinogen. A decreased ESR may be seen in polycythemia or in certain blood diseases in which red blood cells have an irregular or smaller shape that causes slower settling
a reduced erythrocyte sedimentation rate: A reduced erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). The ESR is a test that measures the distance that erythrocytes have fallen after one hour in a vertical column of anticoagulated blood under the influence of gravity. The ESR is a nonspecific finding. A decreased ESR may be seen in polycythemia or in certain blood diseases in which red blood cells have an irregular or smaller shape that causes slower settling
a developmental defect resulting in comp: A developmental defect resulting in complete obliteration of the lumen of the rectum. That is, there is an abnormal closure, or atresia of the tubular structure of the rectum
an abnormal dilation of the rectum. ther: An abnormal dilation of the rectum. There is a large filled rectum as a result of underlying innervation or muscular abnormalities, which remains after disimpaction of the rectum
a congenital malformation characterized: A congenital malformation characterized by an abnormal connection (fistula) between the rectum and the vulval vestibule, at the lower aspect of the vaginal opening
rectovestibular fistula with a normal an: Rectovestibular fistula with a normal anus is known as H-type fistula or double termination of the alimentary tract
the term osteoma refers to the anomalous: The term osteoma refers to the anomalous presence of ossification (bone formation) in the interior of the dermis or epidermis. The dermal or subcutaneous bone formation presents as stony hard nodules. The osteomata appear as irregular, hardened small nodules that are well circumscribed and generally of the same color as the skin
a structural anomaly of nerves of the en: A structural anomaly of nerves of the enteric nervous system
deterioration of enteric neurons with im: Deterioration of enteric neurons with impairment of enteric neuronal structure. Typical neuropathological findings include qualitative (e.g., neuronal swelling, intranuclear inclusions, axonal degeneration) and quantitative (e.g., reduction in the number of neurons) abnormalities of the enteric neurons
a functional anomaly of the digestive sy: A functional anomaly of the digestive system
a structural anomaly of the digestive sy: A structural anomaly of the digestive system
abnormal form of the progenitor cells co: Abnormal form of the progenitor cells committed to the erythroid lineage
anomalous form of the proerythroblast, i: Anomalous form of the proerythroblast, i.e., the immature, nucleated erythrocyte occupying the stage of erythropoeisis that follows formation of erythroid progenitor cells. This cell is CD71-positive, has both a nucleus and a nucleolus, and lacks hematopoeitic lineage markers
focal proliferation of glial cells in th: Focal proliferation of glial cells in the hypothalamus
a collection of pus within a testicle. u: A collection of pus within a testicle. Ultrasonographic features include shaggy, irregular walls, intratesticular location, low-level internal echoes, and occasionally, hypervascular margins
swelling within the basal ganglia due to: Swelling within the basal ganglia due to the accumulation of fluid
swelling within the thalamus due to the: Swelling within the thalamus due to the accumulation of fluid
a morphological anomaly of lymph nodes i: A morphological anomaly of lymph nodes in the mesenteric root or throughout the mesentery
increase in size of one or more mesenter: Increase in size of one or more mesenteric lymph nodes
a circumscribed area of pus or necrotic: A circumscribed area of pus or necrotic debris in lung parenchyma, which leads to a cavity, and after formation of bronchopulmonary fistula, can manifest as an air-fluid level inside the cavity
a deviation from normal of the level of: A deviation from normal of the level of lactate in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
a decrease in the level of lactate in th: A decrease in the level of lactate in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
a deviation from normal in the level of: A deviation from normal in the level of choline-containing compounds in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
an decrease in the level of choline-cont: An decrease in the level of choline-containing compounds in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
an increase in the level of creatine in: An increase in the level of creatine in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
a decrease in the level of creatine in t: A decrease in the level of creatine in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
a deviation from normal in the level of: A deviation from normal in the level of N-acetyl aspartate in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
an increase in the level of n-acetyl asp: An increase in the level of N-acetyl aspartate in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
a structural anomaly of the olfactory lo: A structural anomaly of the olfactory lobe, the structure within the brain that receives neural input from the nasal cavity and thereby processes the sense of smell
a circumscribed area of pus or necrotic: A circumscribed area of pus or necrotic debris in the parenchyma of the spleen
multiple abscess lesions in the spleen: Multiple abscess lesions in the spleen
the practice of eating earth or soil-lik: The practice of eating earth or soil-like substrates such as clay or chalk
the anterior abdominal wall is sunken an: The anterior abdominal wall is sunken and presents a concave rather than a convex contour
a deviation from normal of the mean corp: A deviation from normal of the mean corpuscular volume, or mean cell volume (MCV) of red blood cells, usually taken to be 80 to 100 femtoliters
a reduction from normal of the mean corp: A reduction from normal of the mean corpuscular volume, or mean cell volume (MCV) of red blood cells (usually defined as an MCV below 80 femtoliters)
strabismus in which the angle of deviati: Strabismus in which the angle of deviation differs depending upon the direction of gaze or according to which eye is fixing, associated with: (i) defective movement of the eye, (ii) asymmetrical accommodative effort
Strabismus in which the angle of deviation of the squiting eye remains the same in relation to the other eye, in all directions of gaze, and whichever eye is fixing
an anomaly of the u wave of the electroc: An anomaly of the U wave of the electrocardiogram (EKG). The U wave is a small (0.5 mm) deflection immediately following the T wave, usually in the same direction as the T wave. It is best seen in leads V2 and V3
direction of the u wave opposite to the: Direction of the U wave opposite to the T wave (i.e., below baseline) in leads with upright T waves
increased amplitude of the u wave, defin: Increased amplitude of the U wave, defined as an amplitude grerater than 1-2mm or 25 percent of the height of the T wave
u wave inversion that is induced by exer: U wave inversion that is induced by exercise stress testing
an anomaly of the complex formed by the: An anomaly of the complex formed by the Q, R, and S waves, which occur in rapid succession on the electrocardiogram
elevation of the voltage (height) of the: Elevation of the voltage (height) of the QRS complex. There are several criteria in use, but the most common is the Sokolov-Lyon criterion (S wave depth in V1 + tallest R wave height in V5-V6 greater than 35 mm)
abnormal amplitude of the qrs complex of: Abnormal amplitude of the QRS complex of the electrocardiogram (EKG)
reduced amplitude (height) of the qrs co: Reduced amplitude (height) of the QRS complex of the electrocardiogram (EKG), defined as amplitudes of all the QRS complexes in the limb leads are less than 5 mm or amplitudes of all the QRS complexes in the precordial leads less than 10 mm
the qrs complexes of the electrocardiogr: The QRS complexes of the electrocardiogram alternate in height
a circumscribed area of pus or necrotic: A circumscribed area of pus or necrotic debris in the parenchyma of the pancreas
a form of hyperkeratosis characterized b: A form of hyperkeratosis characterized by thickening of the cornified layer without retained nuclei
a skin change elicited by briskly rubbin: A skin change elicited by briskly rubbing the skin lesion in urticaria pigmentosa (UP), whereby the area begins to itch and becomes raised and surrounded by erythema. Unlike other forms of dermatographism, Darier's sign refers to urtication that is limited to the UP involved areas and, as in this case, spares the skin unaffected by UP
any structural anomaly of the elastic fi: Any structural anomaly of the elastic fibers of the skin. Elastic fibers are the essential extracellular matrix macromolecules comprising an elastin core surrounded by a mantle of fibrillin-rich microfibrils
an increased amount of desmosine measure: An increased amount of desmosine measure in the skin. Desmosine is a cross-linking amino acid formed from lysyl residues in elastin
inflammatory cells within the wall and o: Inflammatory cells within the wall and ostia of the hair follicle, creating a follicular-based pustule
passage of many stools containing blood: Passage of many stools containing blood
Passage of many stools containing blood and mucus
area of skin requiring an increased amou: Area of skin requiring an increased amount of time to return to its original shape after being stretched
complete shedding (separation) of the na: Complete shedding (separation) of the nail from the proximal matrix. Onychomadesis is the proximal separation of the nail plate from the nail matrix due to a temporary cessation of nail growth
vomiting of material that is of fecal or: Vomiting of material that is of fecal origin
a structural anomaly of the mucous linin: A structural anomaly of the mucous lining of the large intestine
diffuse hypertrophy or thickening of the: Diffuse hypertrophy or thickening of the stratum spinosum of the epidermis (prickle cell layer of the skin)
a retinal exudate in the area surroundin: A retinal exudate in the area surrounding the optic nerve head
a subretinal scar with a disc-like shape: A subretinal scar with a disc-like shape in the region of the macula
a sudden violent, spasmodic, audible exp: A sudden violent, spasmodic, audible expiration of breath through the nose and mouth
marked, involuntary jerking of the eyeli: Marked, involuntary jerking of the eyelids
structural abnormality of the hypothalam: Structural abnormality of the hypothalamus related to defective development
structural abnormality of the thalamus r: Structural abnormality of the thalamus related to defective development
any structural anomaly of the hippocampu: Any structural anomaly of the hippocampus,
structural abnormality of the hippocampu: Structural abnormality of the hippocampus related to defective development
structural abnormality of the basal gang: Structural abnormality of the basal ganglia related to defective development
a type of skin nodule that has a small d: A type of skin nodule that has a small depression that resembles a navel (i.e., is umbilicated)
a capillary malformation is a flat, shar: A capillary malformation is a flat, sharply defined vascular stain of the skin. It may cover a large surface area or it may be scattered and appear as little islands of color. In a cpillary maformation, the predominant vessels are small, slow-flow vessels (i.e., arterioles and postcapillary venules)
a congenital skin lesion characterized b: A congenital skin lesion characterized by irregular hypopigmented macules that coalesce to form plaques and occur particularly on the chest. It is generally present at birth or develops in the first days of life. It is more common in females. Diagnosis is confirmed by applying gentle friction to the lesion and the surrounding skin and checking that the erythema produced in the healthy skin does not appear in the hypopigmented lesion. This pale macule becomes more conspicuous when the lesion and its surroundings are rubbed. The margin of the naevus is ill-defined and consists of an archipelago of small anaemic spots/
a variant of port-wine stain characteriz: A variant of port-wine stain characterized by a pale red or even pink tone, in contrast to the darker hue of the port-wine stain. By analogy with the term port-wine stain, this variant rose-wine stain, or nevus roseus. Nevus roseus, however, cannot be definitely diagnosed until adulthood as port-wine stains are sometimes pink in children. While the natural history of port-wine stains includes hypertrophy, darkening, and nodularity, nevus roseus remains unchanged for life
a congenital vascular malformation that: A congenital vascular malformation that presents as localized or generalized erythematous-telangiectatic lesions with a reticular pattern; the lesions are almost always present at birth or develop in the first days of life. Cutis marmorata telangiectatica congenita (CMTC) appears as marble-like pattern (mottling) on the surface of the skin. In contrast to cutis marmorata, the marbling is more severe and always visible
angioma serpiginosum consists of punctat: Angioma serpiginosum consists of punctate, tightly packed telangiectatic lesions. Characteristic histopathological features are dilated and tortuous capillaries involving the uppermost part of the dermis
decrease in the activity of pyruvate kin: Decrease in the activity of pyruvate kinase (PK) within erythrocytes. PK catalyzes the reaction: ATP + pyruvate = ADP + phosphoenolpyruvate
yellow/white, sharply delineated lesion,: Yellow/white, sharply delineated lesion, typically of inflammatory nature, involving the macula
an adverse response (dislike) to sound n: An adverse response (dislike) to sound no matter what volume the sound is, characterized by a strong negative reaction to soft sounds that can sometimes be further triggered by seeing the source of the offending sound
hypergranulosis is an increased thicknes: Hypergranulosis is an increased thickness of the stratum granulosum
eosinophilic hyaline ovoid bodies which: Eosinophilic hyaline ovoid bodies which are often found in the subepidermal papillary regions or sometimes in the epidermis. Civatte bodies (CBs) are seen as rounded, homogenous, eosinophilic masses on routine H and E staining lying in the deeper parts of epidermis/epithelium and more frequently in dermis/connective tissue. They are known as CBs (in epithelium/epidermis), colloid bodies, or hyaline bodies (in connective tissue). They are 10-25 micrometers in diameter and situated mostly within or above the inflammatory cell infiltrate. In lichen planus, the number of necrotic keratinocytes may be so large that they are seen lying in clusters in the uppermost dermis. These bodies show a positive periodic acid Schiff reaction and are diastase resistant
an intrauterine state characterized by s: An intrauterine state characterized by suboptimal values in the fetal heart rate, oxygenation of fetal blood, or other parameters indicative of compromise of the fetus. Signs of fetal distress include repetitive variable decelerations, fetal tachycardia or bradycardia, late decelerations, or low biophysical profile
rete pegs (or ridges) are the epithelial: Rete pegs (or ridges) are the epithelial extensions that project into the underlying connective tissue in both skin and mucous membranes. Rete ridge flattening refers to the loss of these projections so that the skin epithelium acquires a relatively flat appearance
lightening or darkening of the lips from: Lightening or darkening of the lips from their usual coloring
an alteration of the color of the lip to: An alteration of the color of the lip to take on a violet color. This term does not include cyanosis
a type of focal seizure (i.e., affecting: A type of focal seizure (i.e., affecting initially only one hemisphere of the brain) that is simple (not resulting in alteration of consciousness) that originates in the occipital lobe. Visual hallucinations are the hallmark of occipital seizures, but are not invariably present. Hallucinations typically commence in the visual field contralateral to the affected visual cortex and then spread to involve the entire visual field. Elementary visual seizures are characterized by fleeting visual manifestations which may be either positive (flashes, phosphenes) or, less commonly, negative (scotoma, hemianopia, amaurosis). Positive phenomena are usually flashes of colour or light, which are simple in shape and may be static or mobile
a type of epidermal acanthosis character: A type of epidermal acanthosis characterized by a jagged (sawtooth) appearance of the rete ridges of the epidermis
areas of white discoloration visible on: Areas of white discoloration visible on the surface of the teeth (enamel) in the form of streaks or specks
a tendency of teeth to fracture as manif: A tendency of teeth to fracture as manifested by a history of repeated fracture of the dental enamel without adequate trauma
white lesions of the oral mucosa are gen: White lesions of the oral mucosa are generally caused by a condition that increases the thickness of the epithelium. This increases the distance to the vascular bed and thereby tends to change the usual reddish color of the oral mucosa to white. Common causes include hyperkeratosis (thickening of the keratin layer), acanthosis (thickening of the spinous cell layer), increased edema in the epithelium (leukoedema), and reduced vascularity of the underlying lamina propria. Additionally, fibrin caps od surface ulcerations and collapsed bullae can appear white
a corrugated white lesion of the oral mu: A corrugated white lesion of the oral mucosa that usually occurs on the lateral or ventral surfaces of the tongue and may have a shaggy or frayed appearance
a scaly, crusty lesion caused by damage: A scaly, crusty lesion caused by damage from the ultraviolet radiation of the sun, with typical location on sun-exposed areas of the skin. Actinic keratosis lesions are often elevated, rough, and wartlike, and may be red, or occasionally tan, pink, or flesh-toned in color
an abnormally reduced amount of adipose: An abnormally reduced amount of adipose tissue in the abdominal cavity
a structural anomaly of the mucous linin: A structural anomaly of the mucous lining of the small intestine
lactase is produced in the small intesti: Lactase is produced in the small intestine in humans, Lactase is a member of the beta-galactosidase family of enzymes, and hydrolyzes D-lactose to form D-galactose and D-glucose, which can be absorbed by the small intestine. There are many ways of assessing lactase activity. In one test, an endoscopic biopsy from the postbulbar duodenum is incubated with lactose on a test plate, and a color reaction develops within 20 min as a result of hydrolyzed lactose (a positive result) in patients with normolactasia, whereas no reaction (a negative result) develops in patients with severe hypolactasia. Other, less direct, tests include the hydrogen breath test, and blood tests following lactose challenges
enlargement of the soft tissues of one o: Enlargement of the soft tissues of one or more fingers
a deviation from normal concentration of: A deviation from normal concentration of the hormone estrogen in the blood circulation
a deviation from normal concentrations o: A deviation from normal concentrations of estradiol in the circulation
an elevation above normal limits of the: An elevation above normal limits of the concentration of estradiol in the circulation
a deviation from normal concentration of: A deviation from normal concentration of estriol in the circulation
an elevation above normal limits of estr: An elevation above normal limits of estriol concentration in the circulation
a reduction below normal limits of estri: A reduction below normal limits of estriol in the circulation
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of circulating estrone
an elevation above normal limits of the: An elevation above normal limits of the concentration of estrone in the circulation
a reduction below normal limits of the c: A reduction below normal limits of the concentration of estrone in the circulation
ectopic deposition of calcium salts foun: Ectopic deposition of calcium salts found in the gingiva
a symptom or manifestation indicating a: A symptom or manifestation indicating a systemic or general effect of a disease and that may affect the general well-being or status of an individual
a sudden sensation of feeling cold: A sudden sensation of feeling cold
involuntary contraction or twitching of: Involuntary contraction or twitching of the muscles
severe chills with violent shivering. a: Severe chills with violent shivering. A rigor is an episode of shaking or exaggerated shivering which can occur with a high fever
deterioration of the tissue of the fovea: Deterioration of the tissue of the fovea, i.e.,the region of sharpest vision within the macula of the retina
a shiny appearance of the macula, which: A shiny appearance of the macula, which is often called a beaten bronze appearance
a fluorescein angiographic finding of ab: A fluorescein angiographic finding of absence of the normal background fluorescence (a dark choroid)
partial or complete wasting (loss) of mu: Partial or complete wasting (loss) of muscularois propria tissue that was once present. The atrophy may involve a marked vacuolar degeneration of myocytes, loss of muscle fibers and some cases a highly characteristic honeycomb fibrosis
hyperplastic submucosal and myenteric pl: Hyperplastic submucosal and myenteric plexus containing an increased number of ganglion cells, glial cells and nerve fibers
lack of visual responsiveness or decreas: Lack of visual responsiveness or decrease in visual capabilities suggesting a lack of visual responsiveness or decrease in visual capabilities in an infant or young child in which visual behavior fails to meet normal developmental milestones
short-lived and not permanent. this term: Short-lived and not permanent. This term applies to a phenotypic abnormality that is temporary and of short duration
presence of biliary veins that serve as: Presence of biliary veins that serve as a collateral channel to the systemic circulation
a functional anomaly of the hepatobiliar: A functional anomaly of the hepatobiliary system
inability to be weaned from intravenous: Inability to be weaned from intravenous (parenteral) nutrition, as judged by the hydration status (urine output, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, urine sodium concentration), ability to maintain weight, stool output, and serum electrolyte status
an increased concentration of sedoheptul: An increased concentration of sedoheptulose in the urine. Sedoheptulose is a monosaccharide with seven carbon atoms and a ketone functional group
increased amount of autofluorescence in: Increased amount of autofluorescence in the retina as ascertained by fundus autofluorescence imaging
decreased amount of autofluorescence in: Decreased amount of autofluorescence in the retina as ascertained by fundus autofluorescence imaging
a temper tantrum is an emotional outburs: A temper tantrum is an emotional outburst usually triggered by a sense of frustration and manifested as whining and crying, screaming, kicking, hitting, and breath holding. Temper tantrums are normal in toddlers and young children and usually happen between the ages of one to three years. Temper tantrums may be considered abnormal if they occur at an unusually high frequency, are of unusual severity, or occur at an old age than usual
temper tantrums that occur more frequent: Temper tantrums that occur more frequently than usual
temper tantrums whose severity is more s: Temper tantrums whose severity is more severe than usual. For instance, a temper tantrum might be considered to be severe if a child loses control so completely that the child cannot control the tantrum on its own, continuing until it becomes exhausted or a parent intervenes
a structural abnormality of the optic ch: A structural abnormality of the optic chiasm.The optic chiasm, located below the hypothalamus, is a partial crossing of the optic nerves
an elevated number of elastic fibers, th: An elevated number of elastic fibers, that is of bundles of proteins and glycoproteins in the extracellular matrix in the reticular dermis. Elastic fibers can stretch and recoil back to their original length. This feature can be appreciated on histology with hematoxylin and eosin or other staining methods
formation of clumps or aggregates that m: Formation of clumps or aggregates that make up small protuberances from elastic fibers within the dermis (especially the reticular dermis)
an increase of the diameter of elastic f: An increase of the diameter of elastic fibers in the dermis
elastic fibers in the dermis exhibit an: Elastic fibers in the dermis exhibit an increased number of breaks associated with disorganization of the structure of the elastic fibers
abnormal function of the left ventricule: Abnormal function of the left ventricule during left ventricular relaxation and filling
abnormality of left ventricular contract: Abnormality of left ventricular contraction, often defined operationally as an ejection fraction of less than 40 percent
a central nervous system neoplasm with n: A central nervous system neoplasm with neuronal and, less consistently, glial differentiation
a tumor of the central nervous system th: A tumor of the central nervous system that has components of both neurocytic and glial areas, whereby usually the glial component of the tumour predominates. Rossette-forming glioneuronal tumors (RGNT) have biphasic cytoarchitecture with two elements; neurocytic rosettes resembling Homer-Wright rosettes, and astrocytic component resembling a pilocytic astrocytoma. RGNTs are low-grade tumors that lack histopathological signs of malignancy
thickening of the interlobular septa of: Thickening of the interlobular septa of the lungs as seen on a high-resolution computed tomography scan with a smooth appearance of the interlobular septa
Thickening of the interlobular septa of the lungs as seen on a high-resolution computed tomography scan with a nodular or beaded appearance of the interlobular septa

Thickening of the interlobular septa of the lungs as seen on a high-resolution computed tomography scan with an irregular appearance of the interlobular septa. THis feature is often associated with distortion of lung architecture
extensive interstitial fibrosis with alv: Extensive interstitial fibrosis with alveolar disruption and bronchiolectasis
a fine reticular pattern on high-resolut: A fine reticular pattern on high-resolution computeed tomography, with the visible lines separated by a few millimeters. Regions of the lung with intralobular interstitial thickening characteristically show a fine lacelike or netlike appearance
thickening of the peribronchovascular in: Thickening of the peribronchovascular interstitium, a connective tissue sheath that surrounds the central bronchi and pulmonary arteries. The peribronchovascular interstitium extends from the level of the pulmonary hila into the peripheral lung. This feature may be ascertained on high-resolution computer tomography
increase in thickness of the subpleural: Increase in thickness of the subpleural interstitium
a descriptive term that is applied to co: A descriptive term that is applied to computer tomography imaging and that refers to a hazy area of increased attenuation in the lung with preserved bronchial and vascular markings
a hazy area of increased attenuation in: A hazy area of increased attenuation in centrilobular areas of the lung with preserved bronchial and vascular markings seen on a computer tomography scan. Centrilobular refers to a location that is central within secondary pulmonary lobules
an abscess-like lesion located within th: An abscess-like lesion located within the abdomen. The lesions are localized in the spleen, liver, abdominal lymph nodes. The lesions represent visceral sterile collections of mature neutrophils that do not respond to antibiotics but regress quickly when treated with corticosteroids, but relapses occur frequently
a confined region of lax skin that hangs: A confined region of lax skin that hangs below the level of the surrounding skin. Histopatholigically, there is a loss of elastic fibers in the dermis of the affected region
unilateral ptosis with associated upper: Unilateral ptosis with associated upper eyelid contraction and contraction of either the external or the internal pterygoid muscle. It is thought to occur because of congenital miswiring of a branch of the fifth cranial nerve into the branch of the third cranial nerve supplying the levator muscle. In Marcus Gunn jaw winking synkinesis, elevation and even retraction of the affected eyelid is triggered by chewing, suction, lateral mandible movement, smiling, sternocleidomastoid contraction, protruding tongue, Valsalva manoeuvre and even by breathing
inflammation of retinal blood vessels as: Inflammation of retinal blood vessels as manifested by perivascular sheathing or cuffing, vascular leakage and/or occlusion
a type of generalized tonic-clonic seizu: A type of generalized tonic-clonic seizure that occurs in and rapidly engages bilaterally distributed networks
areas of brighter than expected signal o: Areas of brighter than expected signal on magnetic resonance imaging emanating from the cerebral white matter that surrounds the fourth cerebral ventricle (which is located beneath the tentorium of the cerebellum)
a posterolateral defect in the diaphragm: A posterolateral defect in the diaphragm, commonly referred to as a Bochdalek hernia, which is often accompanied by herniation of the stomach, intestines, liver, and/or spleen into the chest cavity
an anterior retrosternal or parasternal: An anterior retrosternal or parasternal hernia that can result in the herniation of liver or intestines into the chest cavity
a congenital diaphragm defect involving: A congenital diaphragm defect involving the central tendinous (e.g., amuscular) portion of the diaphragm, whereby the entire rim of diaphragmatic musculature is present
an elevation in the total-iron binding c: An elevation in the total-iron binding capacity, which measures how much serum iron is bound if an excess of radioactive iron is added. A high TIBC corresponds to a high transferrin concentration. The latent (or free) iron binding capacity is the difference between the TIBC and the measured serum iron, corresponding to the transferrin not bound to iron, i.e., free iron binding capacity
a benign tumor made up of mostly myofibr: A benign tumor made up of mostly myofibroblasts that appears almost exclusively on the digits of the hands and feet, rarely involving the thumb or big toe. The lesion displays a proliferation of bland intradermal spindle cells arranged in whorls, fascicles, or a storiform pattern in a collagenous background of varying degrees. Also usually present are perpendicular tumor cell fascicles that extend to the epidermis. The small intracytoplasmic inclusions are said to appear similar to red blood cells. The inclusion bodies have been shown to be made up of densely packed vimentin and actin filaments. The tumor often causes a dome-shaped elevation of the overlying structures, forming a protuberant or polypoid nodule. The overlying epidermis can display a host of changes, including acanthosis, hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, rete ridge flattening, entrapment of adnexal structures, and, rarely, ulceration
a non-malignant sessile or pedunculated: A non-malignant sessile or pedunculated polyp in the colon and rectum that displays a cap of inflammatory granulation tissue with fibrinopurulent exudate that covers the polyp
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration in blood of an apolipoprotein, i.e., of a protein that binds lipids to form lipoprotein and is thereby responsible for the transport of lipids in the blood and lymph circulation
an increased concentration in blood of a: An increased concentration in blood of apolipoprotein A-IV, a major component of HDL and chylomicrons that has a role in VLDL secretion and catabolism and is required for efficient activation of lipoprotein lipase by ApoC-II
distended and engorged umbilical veins w: Distended and engorged umbilical veins which are seen radiating from the umbilicus across the abdomen to join systemic veins
a trigger is defined as an external fact: A trigger is defined as an external factor that leads to the manifestation of a sign or symptom in a person with a susceptibility to developing that manifestation
applies to a sign or symptom that is pro: Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by breast feeding in an infant
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by exposure to cold surroundings
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by being dehydrated, i.e., by a deficit in total body water
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating or drinking carbohydrates
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating or drinking fructose
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating or drinking glucose
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by drinking or otherwise ingesting ethanol
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by abstaining from eating food (fasting)

Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating or drinking galactose. Galactose usually is ingested as lactose, which is composed of equimolar amounts of glucose and galactose
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by exposure to heat
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by febrile illness
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating large quantities of food, for instance, by a heavy meal
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating a diet high in lipids
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by excessively rapid and deep breathing
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by a vaccination
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by menstruation in a female
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by pregnancy in a female
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by a lack of sufficient sleep
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by smoking
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by eating or drinking sodium
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by exposure to sound or noise
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by a physical, mental, or emotional factor associated with bodily or mental tension
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by a a state of excitement or by being startled
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by a sudden movement
Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by vestibular stimulation, including head turning, cold calorics, postural changes, or rotating chair
a structural anomaly of a synovial bursa: A structural anomaly of a synovial bursa
an inability to move the body at sleep o: An inability to move the body at sleep onset or upon awakening from sleep lasting seconds to a few minutes
an undesirable physical event or experie: An undesirable physical event or experience that occur during entry into sleep, during sleep, or during arousal from sleep
a parasomnia that occurs in non-rapid ey: A parasomnia that occurs in non-rapid eye movement (NREM) sleep. This refers to a disorder of arousal that occurs during slow-wave sleep (ie, NREM stage 3 sleep)
ambulation or other complex motor behavi: Ambulation or other complex motor behaviors after getting out of bed in a sleep-like state. During sleepwalking episodes, the sonambulating individual appears confused or dazed, the eyes are usually open, and he or she might mumble or give inappropriate answers to questions, or occasionally appear agitated
a nocturnal episode characterized by dis: A nocturnal episode characterized by disorientation, grogginess, and, at times, substantial agitation upon awakening from slow-wave sleep or following forced awakenings. These characteristics might present as agitation, crying or moaning, disorientation, and particularly slow mentation on arousal from sleep (i.e., sleep inertia). The duration of episodes is typically 5 to 15 min but they might last up to several hours
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the foot
a localized detachment of the vitreous f: A localized detachment of the vitreous from the retina due to the accumulation of blood. When localized in the macular area, it results in sudden profound loss of vision. Subhyaloid premacular hemorrhage is typically characterized by a circumscribed, round or dumb-bell shaped, bright red mound of blood beneath the internal limiting membrane (ILM) or between the ILM and hyaloid face, in or near to the central macular area
an accumulation of blood between the neu: An accumulation of blood between the neurosensory retina and the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) arising from the choroidal or retinal circulation
a type of retinal hemorrhage that is loc: A type of retinal hemorrhage that is located within the nerve fiber layer (NFL) of the retina and that exhibits a characteristic flame shape which results from constraints by the structure of the NFL (axons of the ganglion cells)
accumulation of blood located in the ret: Accumulation of blood located in the retina's inner nuclear and outer plexiform layers, and having a dot-like or blot-like shape. THe shape results from intraretinal compression, restricting the hemorrhages within a specific location
accumulation of blood located beneath th: Accumulation of blood located beneath the neurosensory retina in the space between the neurosensory retina and the retinal pigment epithelium
an accumulation of blood located between: An accumulation of blood located between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch's membrane
a hollow mass located in the skin that i: A hollow mass located in the skin that is surrounded by an epithelium-lined wall and is well demarcated from the adjacent tissue. Cysts are often said to be sac-like and may contain serous liquid or semisolid material
nontender, round and firm, but slightly: Nontender, round and firm, but slightly compressible, intradermal or subcutaneous cyst measuring 0.5-5 cm in diameter. Trichilemmal cysts are acquired rather than congenital, and tend to appear on the scalp rather than the face, and to be intradermal rather than subcutaneous
a congenital subcutaneous cyst that aris: A congenital subcutaneous cyst that arises from entrapment of skin along the lines of embryonic fusion. In contrast to epidermal cysts, dermoid cysts tend to contain various adnexal structures such as hair, sebaceous, eccrine or apocrine glands. Dermoid cysts are present at birth, and are indolent, firm, deep, subcutaneous nodules. They are often located on the head and neck, and rarely in the anogenital area. Dermoid cysts arenslowly progressive and can grow to a size of 1 to 4 cm
a cutaneous cyst that is small (one or t: A cutaneous cyst that is small (one or two millimeters in diameter) and painless, presenting as a follicular papule that usually is skin colored but may have a reddish or brownish tinge
a clogged cutaneous sebaceous follicle,: A clogged cutaneous sebaceous follicle, which is a cutaneous gland that secretes sebum (usually into a hair follicle)
a comedo in which the top of the pore is: A comedo in which the top of the pore is not stretched open and thus does not expose the clogged portion (which would appear black), hence the name whitehead
a comedo in which the part of the pore a: A comedo in which the part of the pore at the surface of the skin is stretched and open, exposing the contents of the comedo, which appear black
an anomaly of the tongue characterized b: An anomaly of the tongue characterized by loss (atrophy) of filiform papillae of the tongue, leaving areas of erythema (redness), surrounded by a serpiginous, white, hyperkeratotic border. The name geographic tongue refers to an appearance that is said to be similar to a map
an abnormal fear of being in a closed or: An abnormal fear of being in a closed or narrow space with no escape
an ameliorating factor is defined as an: An ameliorating factor is defined as an external factor that leads to a sign or symptom that is already present improving or becoming more bearable
applies to a sign or symptom that is imp: Applies to a sign or symptom that is improved or made more bearable by pregnancy in a female
Applies to a sign or symptom that is improved or made more bearable by heat (including fever)
Applies to a sign or symptom that is improved or made more bearable by eating or drinking carbohydrates including glucose (sugar)
a sensation of tightness in the neck whe: A sensation of tightness in the neck when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity. Neck stiffness often involves soreness and difficulty moving the neck, especially when trying to turn the head to the side
a sensation of tightness in the elbow jo: A sensation of tightness in the elbow joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
a sensation of tightness in the wrist jo: A sensation of tightness in the wrist joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
a sensation of tightness in a finger joi: A sensation of tightness in a finger joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
a sensation of tightness in the hip join: A sensation of tightness in the hip joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
a sensation of tightness in the knee joi: A sensation of tightness in the knee joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
a sensation of tightness in the ankle jo: A sensation of tightness in the ankle joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
a sensation of tightness in a toe joint: A sensation of tightness in a toe joint when attempting to move it, especially after a period of inactivity
degeneration (wear and tear) of the arti: Degeneration (wear and tear) of the articular cartilage of the neck joints
deep, noisy breathing during sleep accom: Deep, noisy breathing during sleep accompanied by hoarse or harsh sounds caused by the vibration of respiratory structures (especially the soft palate) resulting in sound due to obstructed air movement during breathing while sleeping
disruptions in the production of speech: Disruptions in the production of speech sounds, with involuntary repetitions of words or parts of words, prolongations of speech sounds, or complete blockage of speech production for several seconds
a sudden episode of intense fear in a si: A sudden episode of intense fear in a situation in which there is no danger or apparent cause. The panic attack is accompanied by symptoms such as palpitations, sweating and chills or hot flushes. There may be a sensation of dyspnea (being out of breath), chest pain, or abdominal distress. Some indiviudals with panic attacks may experience depersonalization, a fear of going crazy, or a fear of dying
any physiological abnormality of the eso: Any physiological abnormality of the esophagus
involuntary contractions of the esophagu: Involuntary contractions of the esophagus that are irregular, uncoordinated, and painful
an cystic ovarian teratoma composed of d: An cystic ovarian teratoma composed of dermal and epidermal elements and containing tissue components including hair, teeth, bone, thyroid, and others
applies to an abnormality that is locate: Applies to an abnormality that is located farther from the median plane or midline of the body or of the referenced structure
any functional anomaly of the skin adnex: Any functional anomaly of the skin adnexa (skin appendages), which are specialized skin structures located within the dermis and focally within the subcutaneous fatty tissue, comprising three histologically distinct structures: (1) the pilosebaceous unit (hair follicle and sebaceous glands); (2) the eccrine sweat glands; and (3) the apocrine glands
hyperhidrosis that occurs with gustatory: Hyperhidrosis that occurs with gustatory stimulation (e.g., moisture on face from sweating that occurs after eating)
sweating provoked by cold temperature ra: Sweating provoked by cold temperature rather than by heat
a pain characteristic is defined as a su: A pain characteristic is defined as a subjective category or type of pain
applied to pain that is described as sha: Applied to pain that is described as sharp, i.e., sudden and severe
applied to pain that is dull, i.e., not: Applied to pain that is dull, i.e., not severe but that continues over a long period of time
applied to pain that is tender, i.e., el: Applied to pain that is tender, i.e., elicited by touching the affected body part
applied to pain that wakes the affecting: Applied to pain that wakes the affecting individual from sleep
an aggravating factor is defined as an e: An aggravating factor is defined as an external factor that leads to a sign or symptom that is already present getting worse or becoming more severe
applied to a sign or symptom that is agg: Applied to a sign or symptom that is aggravated by activity, exertion, or exercise
applies to an abnormality that is situat: Applies to an abnormality that is situated in the central part of the body, in the head and trunk as distinguished from the limbs
enlarged lymph nodes in the neck: Enlarged lymph nodes in the neck
applies to an abnormality that affects t: Applies to an abnormality that affects the arms, trunk, head more than the legs
Applies to an abnormality that affects the legs more than the arms, trunk, head
Applies to an abnormality that affects the distal portions of limbs (hand, foot) and head (ears, nose)
applies to an abnormality whose localiza: Applies to an abnormality whose localization corresponds to the lines of Blashko, which are thought to reflect the pathways of epidermal cell migration and proliferation during the development of the fetus
Applies to an abnormality whose localization corresponds to the dermatomes, i.e., the nerve root distribution
applies to an abnormality whose distribu: Applies to an abnormality whose distribution and appearance resembles that of the grouped umbilicated vesicles seen in herpes simplex and herpes zoster infections
Applies to an abnormality whose distribution and appearance resembles that of measles, i.e., maculopapular lesions that are red and roughly 2 to 10 mm in diameter and may be partially confluent
applied to an abnormality whose duration: Applied to an abnormality whose duration is extended over a longer period of time than is expected or usual (e.g., prolonged fever lasts longer than one usually sees with an infection)
an erythematous (red), flat facial rash: An erythematous (red), flat facial rash that affects the skin in the malar area (over the cheekbones) and extends over the bridge of the nose
applies to an abnormality that occurs in: Applies to an abnormality that occurs in or is exacerbated during the night
applies to a sign, symptom, or other abn: Applies to a sign, symptom, or other abnormality that occurs in or is exacerbated in the day time
applied to a sign, symptom, or other man: Applied to a sign, symptom, or other manifestation that occurs multiple times at usually irregular intervals. The occurences are separated by an interval in which the sign, symptom, or manifestation is not present
applies to a sign, symptom, or other man: Applies to a sign, symptom, or other manifestation that recurs with a fixed time interval, i.e., the symptom-free periods are always of the same length
Applies to a sign, symptom, or other manifestation that is episodic with a fixed time interval of one day (24 hours)
acute appearance of disease manifestatio: Acute appearance of disease manifestations in a period of minutes
Acute appearance of disease manifestations in a period of hours
Acute appearance of disease manifestations in a period of days
a deviation from the normal circular sha: A deviation from the normal circular shape of the pupil
an abnormal pupil shape that is elliptic: An abnormal pupil shape that is elliptical, i.e., egg-like
a closed sac, having a distinct membrane: A closed sac, having a distinct membrane and division compared to the nearby tissue located within the anterior chamber. The sac that may contain air, fluids, or semi-solid material
a form of strabismus with both eyes turn: A form of strabismus with both eyes turned inward to a relatively mild degree, usually defined as less than 10 prism diopters
a form of strabismus with one or both ey: A form of strabismus with one or both eyes deviated outward to a milder degree than with exotropia
a benign, flat or slightly elevated mela: A benign, flat or slightly elevated melanocytic lesions of the posterior uveawith clearly defined margins. Choroidal nevi tend they remain stable in size, and to display features such as overlying drusen as well as retinal pigment epithelial atrophy, hyperplasia or fibrous metaplasia
