applies to a sign or symptom that is wor: Applies to a sign or symptom that is worsened, aggravated, or exacerbated by head trauma
a deformity of the elbow in which there: A deformity of the elbow in which there is a deviation of the forearm toward the midline of the body
a malignant neoplasm originating from th: A malignant neoplasm originating from the surface ovarian epithelium
formation of new blood vessels on the ir: Formation of new blood vessels on the iris. The new vessels do not display the typical radially symmertic growth pattern of normal iris blood vessels, but rather appear disorganized. Rubeosis usually starts from the pupillary border with tiny tufts of dilated capillaries or red spots that can only be appreciated with high magnification
leakage of fluorescein dye observed upon: Leakage of fluorescein dye observed upon retinal fluorescein angiography. Areas of leakage can be appreciated as showing gradual enlargement with blurring of margins
an anomalous build up of copper (cu) in: An anomalous build up of copper (Cu) in the liver
blockage of venous return (flow of blood: Blockage of venous return (flow of blood from the periphery back towards the right atrium) in a vein
blockage of blood flow through an artery: Blockage of blood flow through an artery
decreased density/number and/or decrease: Decreased density/number and/or decreased diameter of medial eyebrow hairs
blockage of the retinal artery, generall: Blockage of the retinal artery, generally associated with interruption of blood flow and oxygen delivery to the retina
reduced dimension of the solid part of t: Reduced dimension of the solid part of the kidney (parenchyma, the renal cortex and medulla) as measured from the collecting system (renal calyces and pelvis) to the border of the kidney. This measurement can be performed by measuring the thickness of the parenchyma in computed tomography scans
significant maternal hemorrhage/bleed in: Significant maternal hemorrhage/bleed in the second half of pregnancy and prior to the birth of the baby
the presence of autoantibodies (immunogl: The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against glutamic acid decarboxylase
a limitation of the ability to direct on: A limitation of the ability to direct one's gaze below the horizontal meridian
A limitation of the ability to direct one's gaze above the horizontal meridian
an anomaly of the outer shell (cortex) o: An anomaly of the outer shell (cortex) of a foot bone
a reduction in the thickness of the oute: A reduction in the thickness of the outer shell (cortex) of foot bones
applies to a sign or symptom that is pro: Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by a strong spontaneously arising mental state, reaction or feeling (emotion)
a failure to achieve the ability to stan: A failure to achieve the ability to stand up at an appropriate developmental stage. Most children begin to walk alone at 11 to 15 months of age. On average, children can stand while holding on at the age of 9 to 10 months, can pull up to stand and walk with one hand being held at 12 months, and can stand alone and walk well at 18 months
a failure to achieve the ability to sit: A failure to achieve the ability to sit at an appropriate developmental stage. Most children sit with support at 6 months of age and sit steadily without support at 9 months of age
a reddish appearance over the white part: A reddish appearance over the white part (sclera) of the eye ranging from a few enlarged blood vessels appearing as wiggly lines over the sclera to a bright red color completely covering to sclera
a ring of redness at the limbus of the e: A ring of redness at the limbus of the eye, the border between the cornea and the sclera
prominence of blood vessels of the super: Prominence of blood vessels of the superficial episcleral tissues
prominence of blood vessels of the deep: Prominence of blood vessels of the deep episcleral tissues
an inflammatory cellular deposit deposit: An inflammatory cellular deposit deposited on the corneal endothelium and visible as spots on the cornea
blockage of blood flow in the central re: Blockage of blood flow in the central retinal artery
presence of lupus anticoagulant (la) aut: Presence of lupus anticoagulant (LA) autoantibodies. LA represent a heterogeneous group of autoantibodies, IgG, IgM, or a mixture of both classes, that interfere with standard phospholipid-based coagulant tests (this is only an in vitro phenomenon, LA do not cause reduction of coagulation in vivo). The antibodies are directed against plasma proteins which also bind to phospholipid surfaces
damage to and obliteration of intrahepat: Damage to and obliteration of intrahepatic bile ducts (bile ducts that transport bile between the Canals of Hering and the interlobar bile ducts)
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of beta-2-microglobulin in the blood
elevated concentration of beta-2-microgl: Elevated concentration of beta-2-microglobulin in the blood
reduced concentration of beta-2-microglo: Reduced concentration of beta-2-microglobulin in the blood
an anomaly of the margin of the cornea o: An anomaly of the margin of the cornea overlapped by the sclera
swelling of the margin of the cornea ove: Swelling of the margin of the cornea overlapped by the sclera
conjunctival papillae with a diameter gr: Conjunctival papillae with a diameter greater than 1 millimeter. They characteristically have flattened tops which sometimes demonstrate staining with fluorescein
a history of repeated fungal infections: A history of repeated fungal infections located between the fingers or toes, usually manifested by scaling, maceration, and itching. The toes are more commonly affected than the fingers
being related to a mutation that gamete: Being related to a mutation that gamete that participates in fertilization. All cells of the emerging organism will be affected and the variant canl be passed on to the next generation
a type of antinuclear antibody (ana) pos: A type of antinuclear antibody (ANA) positivity revealed by indirect immunofluorescence (IFL). The multiple nuclear dots (MND) pattern is immunomorphologically characterized by the staining of 3-20 dots of variable size distributed all over the cell nucleus, but sparing the nucleoli, and, in contrast to the anticentromere pattern, MND reactivity does not stain the chromosomes in mitotic cells
an anomaly of cellular morphology or phy: An anomaly of cellular morphology or physiology
acquired focal dilatations of branches o: Acquired focal dilatations of branches of the retinal artery, usually second-order retinal arterioles, that range in size from 100 to 200 micrometers in diameter. Macroaneurysms are generally located at the termporal retina and may be hemorrhagic or exudative
a type of myoclonus in which the myoclon: A type of myoclonus in which the myoclonias shift from body region to another in a random and asynchronous fashion. Erratic myoclonus can affect the face or limbs, are brief, single or repetitive, very frequent and nearly continuous
presence of iris pigment epithelium on t: Presence of iris pigment epithelium on the anterior surface of the iris
an abnormal polygonal shape of the calic: An abnormal polygonal shape of the calices of the kidney (which normally have a rounded or cup-shaped appearance)
increased number of calices of the kidne: Increased number of calices of the kidney
a structural anomaly of the pyramid of t: A structural anomaly of the pyramid of the adult kidney, cone-shaped structures with a broad base adjacent to the renal cortex and the narrow apex that is termed papilla
undergrowth of the pyramid of the adult: Undergrowth of the pyramid of the adult kidney, cone-shaped structures with a broad base adjacent to the renal cortex and the narrow apex that is termed papilla
hypercellularity due to increased number: Hypercellularity due to increased number of cells within glomerular capillary lumina, causing narrowing of the lumina
hypercellularity (increased number of ce: Hypercellularity (increased number of cells) in the renal glomerulus but external to the glomerular capillaries, i.e., in the Bowman space or more than one layer of parietal or visceral epithelial cells
a benign hair follicle tumor whose tumor: A benign hair follicle tumor whose tumor cells form rudimentary hair follicles but not actual hair shafts. A trichoepithelioma is usually less than one centimeter, firm, round, and shihy with yellow, pink, brown, or bluish color. They may occur multiply, usually on the face, and may gradually increase in number with age
a structural anomaly of the growth plate: A structural anomaly of the growth plates (epiphyseal plates), areas of cartilage located near the ends of long bones that are located between the metaphysis (widened part of the shaft of the bone) and the epiphysis (end of the bone) and in which growth occurs in the developing bone. After conclusion of bone growth, the growth plates ossify (harden into solid bone)
increased thickness (dimension along the: Increased thickness (dimension along the axis of the bone) of the growth plate
abnormal bone tissue formation (ossifica: Abnormal bone tissue formation (ossification) affecting the sacrum
formation of the sacrum bone tissue occu: Formation of the sacrum bone tissue occurs later than age-adjusted norms
particularly loud snoring, snoring at hi: Particularly loud snoring, snoring at high volume
interictal refers to a period of time be: Interictal refers to a period of time between epileptic seizures. Electroencephalographic (EEG) patterns are important in the differential diagnosis of epilepsy, and the EEG is almost always abnormal during a seizure. Some persons with seizures may show EEG abnormalities between seizures, while others do not. In some cases, multiple interictal EEGs must be recorded before an abnormality is observed. In most cases the electrographic pattern of seizure onset is completely different from the activity recorded during interictal discharge
the presence of two distinct odontoid pr: The presence of two distinct odontoid processes. The odontoid process, also known as the dens of the axis, is a protuberance of the C2 vertebral body around which the first vertebra rotates
os odontoideum is classified into two an: Os odontoideum is classified into two anatomic types (orthotopic and dystopic). Os odontoideum is defined as an ossicle that consists of smooth and separate caudal portions of the odontoid process.With dystopic os odontoideum, the ossicle is located near the basion or is fused with the clivus
an increased concentration of glutamine: An increased concentration of glutamine in the urine
applies to a sign or symptom that is pro: Applies to a sign or symptom that is provoked or brought about by exertion or physical exercise
the presence of autoantibodies (immunogl: The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against thyroid peroxidase
circulating antipituitary antibodies (ap: Circulating antipituitary antibodies (APA) are markers of autoimmune hypophysitis, which may cause deficient pituitary function
reduced fluid intake (drinking) in a cli: Reduced fluid intake (drinking) in a clinical situation where the plasma molarity or sodium concentration normally would induce greater fluid intake
an area of fat accumulation at the back: An area of fat accumulation at the back of the neck in the form of a hump
areas of subcutanous fat tissue that are: Areas of subcutanous fat tissue that are resistant to (do not respond as expected to) diet, life-style alteration, or bariatric surgery
areas of subcutanous fat tissue below th: Areas of subcutanous fat tissue below the waist that are resistant to (do not respond as expected to) diet, life-style alteration, or bariatric surgery
depression of profile in both temporal r: Depression of profile in both temporal regions
a type of resting tremor characterized b: A type of resting tremor characterized by simultaneous rubbing movements of thumb and index fingers against each other
a nodular lesion that develops in the th: A nodular lesion that develops in the thyroid gland. The term "thyroid nodule" refers to any abnormal growth that forms a lump in the thyroid gland
high-resolution computed tomography (hrc: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) can distinguish findings that characterize characterise interstitial lung diseases in a way not possible with other modalities
on pulmonary high-resolution computed to: On pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography, reticular pattern is characterised by innumerable interlacing shadows suggesting a mesh
the so-called crazy paving pattern is ch: The so-called crazy paving pattern is characterised on HRCT by the presence of thickened interlobular septae and intralobular lines superimposed on a background of ground-glass opacity, resembling irregularly shaped paving stones
a nodular pattern is characterised on pu: A nodular pattern is characterised on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography by the presence of numerous rounded opacities that range from 2 mm to 1 cm in diameter, with micronodules defined as smaller than 3 mm in diameter
co-occurrence of reticular and micronodu: Co-occurrence of reticular and micronodular patterns on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography
on pulmonary high-resolution computed to: On pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography, the cystic pattern is composed by well-defined, round and circumscribed air-containing parenchymal spaces with a well-defined wall and interface with normal lung. The wall of the cysts may be uniform or varied in thickness, but usually is thin (less than 2 mm) and occurs without associated emphysema
co-occurrence of the cystic pattern and: Co-occurrence of the cystic pattern and the ground-glass pattern on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography,
areas of low density corresponding to pa: Areas of low density corresponding to parenchymal destruction and reduced perfusion, and attenuation of the pulmonary vasculature, as visualized on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography
a patchwork of intermingled areas of inc: A patchwork of intermingled areas of increased and decreased attenuation visualized on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography
a nodular pattern on pulmonary high-reso: A nodular pattern on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography that has a perilymphatic distribution
A nodular pattern on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography that displays a tree-in-bud pattern, representing centrilobular branching structures that resemble a budding tree
A nodular pattern on pulmonary high-resolution computed tomography that has an apparently random pattern
an abnormality in which the eyes are hel: An abnormality in which the eyes are held permanently wide open
square wave jerks are saccadic eye movem: Square wave jerks are saccadic eye movements which, when recorded with open eyes are considered to be a pathological sign, caused by fixation instability, and pointing to a central neurological lesion
a habitual positioning of the body with: A habitual positioning of the body with the head and upper back bent forward
any anomaly in the process of ocular fix: Any anomaly in the process of ocular fixation, which is the maintaining of the visual gaze on a single location
a deficit in the ability to fixate eye m: A deficit in the ability to fixate eye movements in order to stabilize images on the retina
a state characterized by a feeling of we: A state characterized by a feeling of weakness and loss of strength leading to a generalized weakness of the body
an abnormal connection (fistula) between: An abnormal connection (fistula) between the rectum and the urethra
any anomaly of the structure of the sple: Any anomaly of the structure of the spleen
any anomaly of the function of the splee: Any anomaly of the function of the spleen
joining of the spleen and a gonad during: Joining of the spleen and a gonad during embryological development
a type of urethral stricture affecting t: A type of urethral stricture affecting the fossa navicularis, which is the spongy part of the male urethra located at the glans penis
A type of urethral stricture affecting the pendulous urethra, which is straight and fixed to the corpora cavernosa
A type of urethral stricture affecting the bulbar urethra, which is the part of the urethra that traverses the root of the penis
a narrowing of the vagina owing to scar: A narrowing of the vagina owing to scar formation
urethra more open or expanded than norma: Urethra more open or expanded than normal
death of tissue in the outer part of the: Death of tissue in the outer part of the kidney
an air-filled cystic space within a lung: An air-filled cystic space within a lung
a type of of pulmonary hemorrhage that o: A type of of pulmonary hemorrhage that originates from the pulmonary microcirculation, including the alveolar capillaries, arterioles, and venules. It presents with hemoptysis, anemia, diffuse lung infiltration, and acute respiratory failure. The diagnosis is confirmed by the observation of the accumulation of red blood cells, fibrin, or hemosiderin-laden macrophage in the alveolar space on pathologic biopsy. Hemosiderin, a product of hemoglobin degradation, appears at least 48-72 hours after bleeding and is helpful in distinguishing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage from surgical trauma. Mild interstitial thickening, organizing pneumonia, or diffuse alveolar damage can also be seen
the presence of free air in the mediasti: The presence of free air in the mediastinum
a closed sac-like structure originating: A closed sac-like structure originating from the pleura that contains a liquid, gaseous, or semisolid substance
any anomaly of the structure of the lary: Any anomaly of the structure of the larynx
any anomaly of the function of the laryn: Any anomaly of the function of the larynx
a spasm (involuntary contraction) of the: A spasm (involuntary contraction) of the vocal cords that can make it difficult to speak or breathe
any anomaly of the morphology of the bro: Any anomaly of the morphology of the bronchi
any anomaly of the function of the bronc: Any anomaly of the function of the bronchi
a spasm (sudden, involuntary constrictio: A spasm (sudden, involuntary constriction) of the bronchioles
any anomaly of the vocalizing of an infa: Any anomaly of the vocalizing of an infant's crying, i.e.,the typically loud voice production that is accompanied by tears and agitation
a type of crying in an abnormally high-p: A type of crying in an abnormally high-pitched voice
a type of cry that is abnormal because i: A type of cry that is abnormal because it is consists of unusually shortened and detached vocalizations
a benign epithelial skin tumor manifesti: A benign epithelial skin tumor manifesting as a slightly elevated circular plaque or nodule with a red, pink or brown color and a diameter up to 22 mm
reduced activity of the enzyme lecithin: Reduced activity of the enzyme lecithin cholesterol acyl transferase
a diminished activity of the classical c: A diminished activity of the classical complement pathway as measured by the assay for 50% haemolytic complement (CH50) activity of serum
an elevated activity of the enzyme lacta: An elevated activity of the enzyme lactate dehydrogenase in serum
increased concentration of 11-deoxycorti: Increased concentration of 11-deoxycortisol in the circulation. 11-deoxycorticosterone, which is also known as simply deoxycorticosterone and 21-hydroxyprogesterone, is a steroid hormore that is produces in the adrenals and is a precursor to aldosterone
increased size of the head of sperm: Increased size of the head of sperm
inflammation (due to infection or irrita: Inflammation (due to infection or irritation) of the pharynx
warm reactive autoantibodies are rbc-dir: Warm reactive autoantibodies are RBC-directed immune responses that are maximally reactive at 37 degrees C
ectopic deposition of calcium salts in t: Ectopic deposition of calcium salts in the Achilles tendon
an abnormality of the function of the ca: An abnormality of the function of the cardiac atria
a decrease in the volume (size) of the a: A decrease in the volume (size) of the amygdyla
any structural anomaly of the papillary: Any structural anomaly of the papillary muscles of the left ventricle
a congenital malformation in which one o: A congenital malformation in which one or both of the papillary muscles (posteromedial or anterolateral) insert directly (that is, without interpositioned chordae tendineae) into the anterior mitral leaflet
abnormal location of the insertion of a: Abnormal location of the insertion of a papillary muscle into the left ventricular wall
abnormally anterior location of the papi: Abnormally anterior location of the papillary muscles of the left ventricle
abnormal location of the insertion of th: Abnormal location of the insertion of the anterolateral papillary muscle near to the apex of the left ventricle. This feature may be appreciated by noting that this muscle is usually not seen in the apical level of the parasternal short-axis echocardiographic view,
testicular adrenal rest tumor (tart) isa: Testicular adrenal rest tumor (TART) isa abenign tumor of the testis. TART generally occurs multiply and bilaterally within the rete testis. Histologically, TART resemble adrenocortical tissue, which led to the name. The tumous are not encapsulated and consist of sheets or confluent cords of large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm
a deep skin ulcer with a well defined bo: A deep skin ulcer with a well defined border, which is usually violet or blue. The ulcer edge is often undermined (worn and damaged) and the surrounding skin is erythematous and indurated. The ulcer often starts as a small papule or collection of papules, which break down to form small ulcers with a so called cat's paw appearance. These coalesce and the central area then undergoes necrosis to form a single ulcer
occurence of adrenarche at a later than: Occurence of adrenarche at a later than normal age. Adrenarche normally occurs between six and eight years of age with increased adrenal androgen secretion; its exact biologic role is not well understood. It is accompanied by changes in pilosebaceous units, a transient growth spurt and the appearance of axillary and pubic hair in some children, but no sexual development
any deviation from the normal range of c: Any deviation from the normal range of concentration of a metabolite in the cerebrospinal fluid
csf 5-hiaa (5-hydroxyindolacetic acid) l: CSF 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindolacetic acid) level is below the lower limit of normal
any deviation from the normal range of a: Any deviation from the normal range of a protein concentration in the cerebrospinal fluid
csf total protein level is below the low: CSF total protein level is below the lower limit of normal
an increase above normal limits of the r: An increase above normal limits of the ratio of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) albumin concentration to serum albumin concentration
an elevated level of myoinositol in the: An elevated level of myoinositol in the brain identified by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
an abnormality of the processes that mai: An abnormality of the processes that maintain the redox environment of a cell or compartment within a cell, that is, the balance between reduction and oxidation chemical reactions
an accumulation of free radical groups i: An accumulation of free radical groups in the body inadequately neutralized by antioxidants, which creates a potentially unstable and damaging cellular environment linked to tissue damage
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of beta globulin. The beta globulins are a group of globular (globe-shaped) proteins in blood
increased level of beta 2-microglobulins: Increased level of beta 2-microglobulins in the urine
an abnormal loss of anagen (growth phase: An abnormal loss of anagen (growth phase) hairs
a type of hair loss characterized by an: A type of hair loss characterized by an abnormal increase in dormant, telogen stage hair follicles
a hamartomatous proliferation containing: A hamartomatous proliferation containing malformed hair follicles in various stages of development. Panfolliculomas are well-circumscribed lesions demonstrating all stages of follicular differentiation
a history of repeated fungal infections: A history of repeated fungal infections located on the sole of the foot, usually manifested by scaling, maceration, and itching
a papule (circumscribed, solid elevation: A papule (circumscribed, solid elevation of skin with no visible fluid, varying in size from a pinhead to less than 10mm in diameter at the widest point) that exhibits increased pigmentation (is darker) compared to the surrounding skin
a plaque (a solid, raised, plateau-like: A plaque (a solid, raised, plateau-like (flat-topped) lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter) with a red or reddish color often associated with inflammation or irritation
a macule (flat, distinct, discolored are: A macule (flat, distinct, discolored area of skin less than 1 cm wide that does not involve any change in the thickness or texture of the skin) with a red or reddish color often associated with inflammation or irritation
multiple foci of adipocytes within the t: Multiple foci of adipocytes within the testicular interstitium, usually presenting as multiple bilateral ill-defined hyperechoic intratesticular lesions of different sizes but generally with maximum diameter of 4 mm
calcified deposits in soft tissue struct: Calcified deposits in soft tissue structures outside a joint
atrial standstill or silent atrium is a: Atrial standstill or silent atrium is a rare condition presenting with the absence of electrical and mechanical activity in the atria. It presents with the absence of P waves, bradycardia, and wide QRS complex in the electrocardiogram
neglecting one's own needs and well-bein: Neglecting one's own needs and well-being
a type of spinal dysraphism presenting a: A type of spinal dysraphism presenting as a subcutaneous fatty mass, that is, a spinal defect associated with lipomatous tissue, and covered by skin. The most usual location for lipomyelomeningocele is at the gluteal cleft
absence of one half of the vertebral bod: Absence of one half of the vertebral body in the cervical spine
an abnormal result of the perchlorate di: An abnormal result of the perchlorate discharge test. In this test, first radioactive iodine is administered, sufficinet time is allowed to pass so that the radioactive iodine is captured by the thyroid,and then, perchlorate is administered orally. The perchlorate displaces non-organified iodide from the thyroid. The perchlorate dischanrge test is considered positive (abnormal) if there is an abnormally rapid loss of radioactive iodine from the thyroid
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of thyroglobulin, a protein produced in the thyroid gland that acts as a precursor to thyrroid hormones
an abnormal elevation of the concentrati: An abnormal elevation of the concentration of thyroglobulin, a protein produced in the thyroid gland that acts as a precursor to thyrroid hormones
vaginal adenosis is defined by the prese: Vaginal adenosis is defined by the presence of metaplastic cervical or endometrial epithelium within the vaginal wall, thought to be derived from persistent Müllerian (synonymous with paramesonephric) epithelium islets in postembryonic life
the outer labia are sealed together: The outer labia are sealed together
any structural anomaly of the bladder: Any structural anomaly of the bladder
a urodynamic anomaly characterized by bl: A urodynamic anomaly characterized by bladder outlet obstruction from detrusor muscle contraction with concomitant involuntary urethral sphincter activation
a congenital anomaly characterized by th: A congenital anomaly characterized by the presence of two bladders
a congenital variant of a coronary arter: A congenital variant of a coronary artery in which a portion of an epicardial coronary artery (most frequently the middle segment of the left anterior descending artery) takes an intramuscular course
narrowing of a vein due to intimal hyper: Narrowing of a vein due to intimal hyperplasia and fibrosis
a small pupil (typically diameter less t: A small pupil (typically diameter less than 2 mm) that dilates poorly or not at all in response to topically administered mydriatic drugs
redness of the skin of the palm of the h: Redness of the skin of the palm of the hand and the sole of the foot caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin
regular circumferential periaortic fibro: Regular circumferential periaortic fibrosis involving the whole aorta and leading to a coated aorta appearance on computed tomography scans
significant luminal narrowing of a long: Significant luminal narrowing of a long segment of the descending aorta
any anomaly of the function of a coronar: Any anomaly of the function of a coronary artery
a brief and sudden narrowing of a corona: A brief and sudden narrowing of a coronary artery
absence of ceruloplasmin in the blood: Absence of ceruloplasmin in the blood
obesity with a body mass index of 30 to: Obesity with a body mass index of 30 to 34.9 kg per square meter
obesity with a body mass index of 35 to: Obesity with a body mass index of 35 to 39.9 kg per square meter
obesity with a body mass index of 40 kg: Obesity with a body mass index of 40 kg per square meter or higher
increased body weight with a body mass i: Increased body weight with a body mass index of 25-29.9 kg per square meter
origin of the right coronary artery (rca: Origin of the right coronary artery (RCA) from the left sinus of Valsalva or of the left main (LM) or left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery from the right sinus of Valsalva
the circumflex coronary artery originate: The circumflex coronary artery originates from the right aortic sinus of Valsalva
origin of the right coronary artery (rca: Origin of the right coronary artery (RCA) from the left sinus of Valsalva or of the left main (LM) or left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery from the right sinus of Valsalva, with the additional feature that the artery passes between the two great arteries. This carries a risk of the artery being compressed by these two vessels,
violaceous papules overlying the dorsal: Violaceous papules overlying the dorsal and lateral aspects of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints
flesh-colored or yellowish papules, 2 mm: Flesh-colored or yellowish papules, 2 mm or larger, that are responses to internal mechanical pressure and weakness in the connective tissue in the dermis, appear commonly over the medial aspect of the heel, but in some cases on the wrists. They are thought to represent herniations of adipose tissue through the plantar fascia retinaculum
a tan, regularly bordered patch with dar: A tan, regularly bordered patch with darker macules within the lesion
a solitary yellow-orange slightly raised: A solitary yellow-orange slightly raised plaque typically on scalp or face. The plaque typically thickens and becomes more verrucous or pebbly during childhood
a papule with the same color as the surr: A papule with the same color as the surrounding skin
an abnormality of the optic nerve in whi: An abnormality of the optic nerve in which the optic nerve is large and funneled and displays a conical excavation of the optic disc. The optic disc appears dysplastic
later than normal development of the bre: Later than normal development of the breasts
a congenital malformation with abnormal: A congenital malformation with abnormal connection between one of the coronary arteries and the pulmonary artery
an abnormality of gaze that can be obser: An abnormality of gaze that can be observed following an acute supranuclear cerebral lesion (e.g., stroke) that is characterized by an acute inability to direct gaze contralateral to the side of the lesion and is accompanied by a tendency for tonic deviation of the eyes toward the side of the lesion
multiple biliary hamartomas are a rare c: Multiple biliary hamartomas are a rare clinicopathologic entity, consisting of small (less than 1.5cm), usually multiple and nodular cystic lesions in the liver
deposition of calcium in the skin: Deposition of calcium in the skin
the percentage of fat as a part of total: The percentage of fat as a part of total body weight above the norm, usually defined as 32% for females and 25% for males
abnormal increased in length of the chor: Abnormal increased in length of the chordae tendinae of the mitral valve
a structural anomaly of the chordae tend: A structural anomaly of the chordae tendinae of the mitral valve, whose main function is to transmit the contraction and relaxation of the papillary muscles during the cardiac cycle, thus ensuring the closing of the leaflets of the mitral valve
loss of the outer layer of the epidermis: Loss of the outer layer of the epidermis in large, scale-like flakes localized to the palm of the hand and the sole of the foot
Loss of the outer layer of the epidermis in large, scale-like flakes localized to one or more fingertips
a skin lesions that resembles the lesion: A skin lesions that resembles the lesions observed in psoriasis, viz., an erythematous plaque covered by fine silvery scales. Psoriasiform lesions can be observed in psoriasis as well as in other conditions including allergic contact dermatitis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, Atopic dermatitis, pityriasis rubra, and lichen simplex chronicus
a skin lesion with a snake- or serpent-l: A skin lesion with a snake- or serpent-like distribution
a lesion of the skin with a ring-like di: A lesion of the skin with a ring-like distribution
a nodule of the skin that exhibits an in: A nodule of the skin that exhibits an increased amount of pigmentation
the presence of multiple xanthomas (xant: The presence of multiple xanthomas (xanthomata) in the skin distributed in the creases of the palm of the hand. Xanthomas are yellowish, firm, lipid-laden nodules in the skin
the harlequin phenomenon consists of a s: The Harlequin phenomenon consists of a sudden change in skin colour, resulting in two different body colours, one on each half of the body
with the pathergy test, a small, sterile: With the pathergy test, a small, sterile needle is inserted into the skin of the forearm. The site of injectionis circuled and observed after one and two days. If a small red bump or pustule at the site of needle insertion occurs, the pathergy test is considered to have a positive (abnormal) result
a congenital lesionof the sclera charact: A congenital lesionof the sclera characterized by unilateral patchy but extensive slate-gray or bluish discoloration of the sclera . The conjunctiva are spared
erythematous, poikilodermatous macules d: Erythematous, poikilodermatous macules distributed in a shawl pattern over the shoulders, arms and upper back
Erythematous, poikilodermatous macules distributed in a V-shaped distribution over the anterior neck and chest
an abnormal accumulation of fluid beneat: An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin on sole of the foot
An abnormal accumulation of fluid beneath the skin on the palm of the hand
any abnormality in the proportion t cell: Any abnormality in the proportion T cells subsets relative to the total number of T cells
a deviation from the normal range of the: A deviation from the normal range of the average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell (27 to 31 picograms/cell). A reduced mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) may indicate a hypochromic anemia, but the MCH may be normal if both the total hemoglobin and the red blood cell count are reduced
a reduction from the normal range of the: A reduction from the normal range of the average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell (27 to 31 picograms/cell). A reduced mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) may indicate a hypochromic anemia, but the MCH may be normal if both the total hemoglobin and the red blood cell count are reduced
an elevation over the normal range of th: An elevation over the normal range of the average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell (27 to 31 picograms/cell)
a uniocular condition in which there is: A uniocular condition in which there is fixation of an object by a point other than the fovea. This point adopts the principal visual direction. The degree of the eccentric fixation is defined by its distance from the fovea in degrees
an increase above the normal concentrati: An increase above the normal concentration of ribitol in the blood
abnormal decussation of the visual pathw: Abnormal decussation of the visual pathways, typically identified using visual evoked potentials (VEP) (asymmetrical distribution of the VEP over the posterior scalp)
multiple red/purple spots on the skin th: Multiple red/purple spots on the skin that surrounds the eyes that do not blanch (whiten) upon pressure. Purpura is caused by subcutaneous bleeding
subcutaneous bleeding with a diamter gre: Subcutaneous bleeding with a diamter greater than 1 cm (ecchymosis). The bleeding does not extend into the tarsal plate (the comparatively thick, elongated plates of dense connective tissue within the eyelid) due to an anatomic structure called the orbital septum, which limits extravasation of blood beyond the tarsal plate
a type of skin nodule (a lesions that is: A type of skin nodule (a lesions that is greater than either 10mm in both width and depth, and most frequently centered in the dermis or subcutaneous fat) with a yellowish coloration (that reflects a high lipid content of the lesion)
telangiectasia (small dilated blood vess: Telangiectasia (small dilated blood vessels) located near to the fingernails or toenails
lamellar cataracts with associated linea: Lamellar cataracts with associated linear lens opacities radially extending towards the periphery of the lens
a type of cataract characterised by club: A type of cataract characterised by club-shaped and dot opacities distributed radially in the deep cortex. These lens opacities surround the nucleus in an appearance that is though to resemble a crown
tiny deposits corresponding to cells flo: Tiny deposits corresponding to cells floating in the anterior chamber of the eye. This appearance is typically associated with intraocular inflammation leading to breakdown of the blood-aqueous barrier and resulting in an increase in the number of cells and in the aqueous humor. Grading (SUN Working Group) is performed by estimating the number of cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
anterior chamber cells with 6-15 cells i: Anterior chamber cells with 6-15 cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
anterior chamber cells with 1-5 cells in: Anterior chamber cells with 1-5 cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
anterior chamber cells with less than on: Anterior chamber cells with less than one cell in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
anterior chamber cells with 16-25 cells: Anterior chamber cells with 16-25 cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
anterior chamber cells with 26-50 cells: Anterior chamber cells with 26-50 cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
anterior chamber cells with more than 50: Anterior chamber cells with more than 50 cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit beam field, employing adequate light intensity and magnification on a slit lamp
an anomaly of the retina with serous det: An anomaly of the retina with serous detachment of the neurosensory retina secondary to one or more focal lesions of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), and associated with blurred vision, usually in one eye only and perceived typically by the patient as a dark spot in the centre of the visual field with associated micropsia and metamorphopsia. Normal vision often recurs spontaneously within a few months
the presence of aneurysmal polypoidal le: The presence of aneurysmal polypoidal lesions in the choroidal vasculature. The aneurysmal dilatations, also known as polyps, may be found at subfoveal, juxtafoveal, extrafoveal, peripapillary or even peripheral regions. These polypoidal dilatations may be visible as reddish-orange subretinal nodules during ophthalmoscopic examination. The polypoidal lesions are best detected on indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) and might be associated with a branching vascular network (BVN) of neovascularization
increased tendency of choiroidal blood v: Increased tendency of choiroidal blood vessels to allow fluids to leak characterized by multifocal choroidal hyperfluorescence on indocyanine green angiography (ICGA)
a type of cataract that shows a spectacu: A type of cataract that shows a spectacular display of multiple colours that glitters with the change of incident light like an illuminated Christmas tree
punctal stenosis is a condition in which: Punctal stenosis is a condition in which the external opening of the lacrimal canaliculus is narrowed or occluded
a mild form of myopia with up to -3.00 d: A mild form of myopia with up to -3.00 diopters
any structural anomaly of the principal: Any structural anomaly of the principal vein draining blood from the upper portion of the body and delivering it to the right ventricle of the heart
Any structural anomaly of the principal vein draining blood from the lower portion of the body
aortic valve prolapse can be diagnosed w: Aortic valve prolapse can be diagnosed when either or both of the right or non-coronary aortic valve cusps (seen in the cross sectional echocardiographic long axis view) show backward bowing towards the left ventricle beyond a line joining the points of attachment of the aortic valve leaflets to the annulus
any structural abnormality of the left a: Any structural abnormality of the left atrium
any structural abnormality of the right: Any structural abnormality of the right atrium
bleeding between the neurosensory retina: Bleeding between the neurosensory retina and the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) arising from the choroidal or retinal circulation
golden, scintillating, particulate refle: Golden, scintillating, particulate reflection noted on fundus examination (typically in the macula and sparing the fovea). The term tapetal is used to describe this 'metallic' sheen appearance as it is thought to be similar to the 'tapetal' reflex seen in the eyes of certain animals
a form of manifest strabismus (heterotro: A form of manifest strabismus (heterotropia) in which one eye is deviated downwards when both eyes are open
tendency for the visual ais of one eye t: Tendency for the visual ais of one eye to be higher than that of the other
a type of strabismus characterized by pe: A type of strabismus characterized by permanent upward deviation of the visual axis of one eye
a type of strabismus in which the visual: A type of strabismus in which the visual axis of one eye is higher than that of the other
A type of strabismus in which the visual axis of one eye is lower than that of the other
cyclodeviation is defined as the rotatio: Cyclodeviation is defined as the rotation of an eyeball along the anteroposterior axis and cyclotropia as a misalignment of cyclodeviation between the two eyes
a functional anomaly of the muscles of t: A functional anomaly of the muscles of the eye
a functional anomaly of the superior obl: A functional anomaly of the superior oblique muscle, a fusiform muscle that originates in the upper, medial side of the orbit. The superior oblique muscle abducts, depresses and internally rotates the eye, and is the only extraocular muscle innervated by the fourth cranial nerve
decreased strength of the superior obliq: Decreased strength of the superior oblique muscle
mechanical limitation of the range of mo: Mechanical limitation of the range of movement of the superior oblique muscle
an ocular motility abnormality character: An ocular motility abnormality characterized by an overacting superior oblique muscle resulting to vertical incomitance of the eyes in lateral gaze. On examination, this is commonly seen as a downshoot of the adducting eye occuring when gaze is directed into the field of action of the inferior oblique muscle, producing a greater downward excursion of the adducted eye than of the abducted eye
reduced ocular movement of the superior: Reduced ocular movement of the superior oblique muscle which improves on testing ductions, typically associated with neurogenic palsy
a functional anomaly of the inferior obl: A functional anomaly of the inferior oblique muscle, an extraocular muscle that has its origin on the maxillary bone just posterior to the inferior medial orbital rim and lateral to the nasolacrimal canal and that is innervated by the inferior branch of the oculomotor nerve
mechanical limitation of the range of mo: Mechanical limitation of the range of movement of the inferior oblique muscle
decreased strength of the inferior obliq: Decreased strength of the inferior oblique muscle
a common ocular motility disorder charac: A common ocular motility disorder characterized by vertical incomitance of the eyes in lateral gaze. In primary inferior oblique muscle overaction, an upshoot of the adducting eye occurs when gaze is directed into the field of action of the inferior oblique muscle, producing a greater upward excursion of the adducted eye than of the abducted eye
a functional anomaly of the inferior rec: A functional anomaly of the inferior rectus muscle, which is innervated by the inferior division of oculomotor nerve and functions in the depression, adduction, and lateral rotation (extortion) of the eye
decreased strength of the inferior rectu: Decreased strength of the inferior rectus muscle
mechanical limitation of the range of mo: Mechanical limitation of the range of movement of the inferior rectus muscle
a functional anomaly of the superior rec: A functional anomaly of the superior rectus muscle, an extraocular muscle that is innervated by the superior division of the oculomotor nerve, and whose primary function is the elevation of the globe
a schwannoma (benign, usually encapsulat: A schwannoma (benign, usually encapsulated slow growing tumor composed of Schwann cells) located in the orbit
delayed descent of the upper eyelid on d: Delayed descent of the upper eyelid on downgaze. Also described by some authors as von Graefe sign
a functional anomaly of the medial rectu: A functional anomaly of the medial rectus muscle, an extraocular muscle that is innervated by the inferior division of the oculomotor nerve and whose sole action is the adduction of the eyeball
an inward turning (inversion) of the mar: An inward turning (inversion) of the margin of the upper eyelid
an outward turning (eversion) or rotatio: An outward turning (eversion) or rotation of the eyelid margin (i.e., ectropion) caused by shortening or contraction of the anterior or middle lamellae related to scarring
a type of blepharitis that affects the e: A type of blepharitis that affects the eyelid skin, base of the eyelashes, and the eyelash follicles
a type of blepharitis that affects the m: A type of blepharitis that affects the meibomian glands and meobomian gland orifices. a type of epicanthus in which more exten: A type of epicanthus in which more extensive epicanthal folds with their origins in the eyebrow cover, pass in front of and lateral to the medial canthus (middle corner of the eye)
a type of refractive error related abnor: A type of refractive error related abnormal curvatures on the anterior or posterior surface of the cornea
a type of focal-onset seizure characteri: A type of focal-onset seizure characterized by alterations in mood or emotion, or the appearance of altered emotion without the subjective emotion, at seizure onset. abnormality observed upon electromyograp: Abnormality observed upon electromyography when nerve studied is electrically stimulated six to ten times at 2 or 3 Hertz
a condition in which the eyelids do not: A condition in which the eyelids do not close to cover the eye completely
the inability to close the eyelids durin: The inability to close the eyelids during sleep
a type of lagophthalmos that occurs in a: A type of lagophthalmos that occurs in association with facial nerve palsy
a type of lagophthalmos that occurs foll: A type of lagophthalmos that occurs following trauma or surgery
an acute phenomenon characterized by hyp: An acute phenomenon characterized by hypotension and anasarca due to the loss of plasma volume into peripheral tissues, with evidence of decreased plasma volume (hemoconcentration) and protein loss from the intravascular space (hypoalbuminemia) during acute episodes
abnormality in single fiber emg recordin: Abnormality in single fiber EMG recording, a technique that allows identification of action potentials (APs) from individual muscle fibers
these are spontaneous firing action pote: These are spontaneous firing action potentials stimulated by needle movement of an injured muscle fiber. There is propagation to, but not past, the needle tip. This inhibits the display of the negative deflection of the waveform
a cervix that shows a painless dilation: A cervix that shows a painless dilation and shortening during the second trimester of pregnancy with resultant recurrent pregnancy loss or delivery is considered incompetent
hydrometrocolpos is an accumulation of u: Hydrometrocolpos is an accumulation of uterine and vaginal secretions as well as menstrual blood in the uterus and vagina
a congenital disorder where the hymen (a: A congenital disorder where the hymen (a membrane that surrounds or partially covers the external vaginal opening) does not have an opening and completely obstructs the vagina
the persistent of recurrent difficulty,: The persistent of recurrent difficulty, delay in, or absence of attaining orgasm following sufficient sexual stimulation and arousal
recurrent or persistent genital pain ass: Recurrent or persistent genital pain associated with sexual intercourse
recurrent or persistent involuntary spas: Recurrent or persistent involuntary spasms of the musculature of the outer third of the vagina that interferes with vaginal penetration, and which causes personal distress
cleft between the helix and the lobe: Cleft between the helix and the lobe
small cartilaginous prominence on the po: Small cartilaginous prominence on the posterior concha
variably shaped, cartilage-containing ti: Variably shaped, cartilage-containing tissue anterior to the external auditory meatus
small indentation in the lower part of t: Small indentation in the lower part of the ascending helix, concha, or in the crus helix
flattening instead of curving or rounded: Flattening instead of curving or rounded superior helix, allowing the superior helix to run more horizontally than usual
a morphological anomaly of the nasal car: A morphological anomaly of the nasal cartilage
lack of a palpable nasal cartilage: Lack of a palpable nasal cartilage
divergence of digits along the a/p axis: Divergence of digits along the A/P axis (in the plane of the palm)
the absence of all phalanges of a digit: The absence of all phalanges of a digit and the associated metacarpal /metatarsal
significant reduction in both length and: Significant reduction in both length and girth of the toe compared to the contralateral toe, or alternatively, compared to a typical toe size for an age-matched individual
an incomplete absence of the foot, with: An incomplete absence of the foot, with no bony elements distal to the tarsals, but with preservation of some or all of the tarsals
significant reduction in both length and: Significant reduction in both length and girth of the finger compared to the contralateral finger, or alternatively, compared to a typical finger size for an age-matched individual
presence of abnormal additional layers o: Presence of abnormal additional layers of the basement membrane of the glomerulus
presence of struvite (magnesium ammonium: Presence of struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) containing calculi (kidney stones)
inability of the kidneys to produce eith: Inability of the kidneys to produce either concentrated or dilute urine
a solitary, benign, intramedullary carti: A solitary, benign, intramedullary cartilage tumor that is often found in the short tubular bones of the hands and feet, distal femur, and proximal humerus
a congenital anomaly characterized by a: A congenital anomaly characterized by a joining (fusion) of two or more thoracic vertebral bodies with one another
A congenital anomaly characterized by a joining (fusion) of two or more lumbar vertebral bodies with one another
a schmorl's node is the herniation of nu: A Schmorl's node is the herniation of nucleus pulposus through the cartilaginous and bony end plate into the body of the adjacent vertebra
failure to complete ossification (matura: Failure to complete ossification (maturation and calcification) of the pubic bone
a partial dislocation of the hip joint,: A partial dislocation of the hip joint, whereby the head of the femur is partially displaced from the socket
a bent (flexed) finger or toe joint that: A bent (flexed) finger or toe joint that cannot be straightened actively or passively. It is thus a chronic loss of joint motion due to structural changes in muscle, tendons, ligaments, or skin that prevents normal movement of joints
a reduction in the degree of glycosylati: A reduction in the degree of glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan in muscle tissue
a morphological anomaly of the lateral v: A morphological anomaly of the lateral ventricle
colpocephaly is an anatomic finding in t: Colpocephaly is an anatomic finding in the brain manifested by occipital horns that are disproportionately enlarged in comparison with other parts of the lateral ventricles
a collection of pus, immune cells, and o: A collection of pus, immune cells, and other material in the brain
an abnormal phenomenon characterized by: An abnormal phenomenon characterized by a classic tetrad of excessive daytime sleepiness with irresistible sleep attacks, cataplexy (sudden bilateral loss of muscle tone), hypnagogic hallucination, and sleep paralysis
an abnormal gait pattern characterized b: An abnormal gait pattern characterized by the failue of the heel to contact the floor at the onset of stance during gait
the presence in the inguinal region (gro: The presence in the inguinal region (groin) of an increased number of freckles, small circular spots on the skin that are darker than the surrounding skin because of deposits of melanin
presence of excess fibrous connective ti: Presence of excess fibrous connective tissue surrounding hair follicules
when viewed on end (with the tip of the: When viewed on end (with the tip of the toe pointing toward the examiner's eye) the curve of the toenail forms a tighter curve of convexity
hair that is disorderly, stands out from: Hair that is disorderly, stands out from the scalp, and cannot be combed flat
the presence of an antibody in the blood: The presence of an antibody in the blood circulation that is directed against the organism's own cells or tissues
an irreversible distortion of the morpho: An irreversible distortion of the morphology of an erythrocyte such that the cells are elongated and curved, resembling the blade of a sickle (the hand-held agricultural tool traditionally used to harvest grains)
an abnormal reduction in mitochondrial d: An abnormal reduction in mitochondrial DNA content of cells
a neoplasm derived from nervous tissue (: A neoplasm derived from nervous tissue (not necessarity a neoplasm located in the nervous system)
a neoplasm arising in the neuroectoderm,: A neoplasm arising in the neuroectoderm, the portion of the ectoderm of the early embryo that gives rise to the central and peripheral nervous systems, including some glial cells
a benign pituitary-region neoplasm that: A benign pituitary-region neoplasm that originates from Rathke's pouch. Craniopharyngiomas are benign slow growing tumours that are located within the sellar and para sellar region of the central nervous system
a neoplasm composed of neural epithelium: A neoplasm composed of neural epithelium, not necessarily a neoplasm located in the neural epithelium or neuroepithelium
a benign brain tumor composed of neural: A benign brain tumor composed of neural elements which most often arise from the septum pellucidum and the walls of the lateral ventricles
a tumor that originates in cells from th: A tumor that originates in cells from the primitive neural crest. This group of tumors is characteirzed by the presence of primitive cells with elements of neuronal and/or glial differentiation
a highly malignant embryonal tumor of in: A highly malignant embryonal tumor of infancy and young childhood characterized by neuroectodermal elements organized in distinctive multilayered rosettes. Ependymoblastomas are large lesions that occur in the supratentorial compartment, typically displaying a physical connection to the ventricular system
a primitive neuroectodermal neoplasm tha: A primitive neuroectodermal neoplasm that occurs extracranially in soft tissue and bone
a malignant olfactory neuroblastoma aris: A malignant olfactory neuroblastoma arising from the olfactory epithelium of the superior nasal cavity and cribriform plate
a form of extranodal, high-grade non-hod: A form of extranodal, high-grade non-Hodgkin B-cell neoplasm, usually large cell or immunoblastic type that originates in the brain, leptomeninges, spinal cord, or eyes and typically remains confined to the CNS
a primitive neuroectodermal neoplasm tha: A primitive neuroectodermal neoplasm that occurs in the central nervous system
a primitive neuroectodermal tumor that o: A primitive neuroectodermal tumor that originates from the cells of the embryonic medullary canal
a tumor that originates in the paranasal: A tumor that originates in the paranasal sinus
a usually benign neoplasm originating in: A usually benign neoplasm originating in the chemoreceptor tissue of the cartodi body, glomus jugulare, glomus tympanicum, aortic bodies, or the female genital tract
presence of abnormal cells inside a milk: Presence of abnormal cells inside a milk duct, that is, non-invasive breast cancer. Ductal carcinoma in situ is considered to be a precursor lesion to invasive breast cancer
a tumor originating in a bronchus: A tumor originating in a bronchus
a tumor of the uterine cervix: A tumor of the uterine cervix
a form of undifferentiated malignant lym: A form of undifferentiated malignant lymphoma commonly manifested as a large osteolytic lesion in the jaw or as an abdominal mass
multiple pointlike areas of high t2 sign: Multiple pointlike areas of high T2 signal observed upon magnetic resonance imaging of the periventricular cerebral white matter
abnormal consumption of fluids with exce: Abnormal consumption of fluids with excessive or insufficient consumption of fluid or any other abnormal pattern of fluid consumption
an excessive desire to eat salt (sodium: An excessive desire to eat salt (sodium chloride) or salty foods
an angulation of a digit at an interphal: An angulation of a digit at an interphalangeal joint in the plane of the palm (finger) or sole (toe)
abnormal concentration of lactate in the: Abnormal concentration of lactate in the cerebrospinal fluid
decreased concentration of lactate in th: Decreased concentration of lactate in the cerebrospinal fluid
an anomalous concentration of testostero: An anomalous concentration of testosterone in the blood
an elevated circulating testosterone lev: An elevated circulating testosterone level in the blood
an anomalous amount of protein present i: An anomalous amount of protein present in or on the surface of muscle fibers. This feature may be appreciate upon immunohistochemical investigation of muscle biopsy tissue
an anomalous amount of merosin in muscle: An anomalous amount of merosin in muscle fibers. Merosin is a basement membrane-associated protein found in placenta, striated muscle, and peripheral nerve
lack of merosin protein in the muscle bi: Lack of merosin protein in the muscle biopsy
a reduced amount of merosin in muscle fi: A reduced amount of merosin in muscle fibers. This feature is usually assessed by immunohistochemical examination of muscle biopsy tissue
a deviation from normal of the amount of: A deviation from normal of the amount of laminin beta 1 in muscle fiber tissue. Laminin 2 is a major component of the basal lamina of skeletal muscle cells. It is a heterotrimer composed of 3 chains: merosin (laminin alpha 2 chain), beta 1, and gamma 1
a reduced amount of laminin beta 1 in mu: A reduced amount of laminin beta 1 in muscle fiber tissue. Laminin 2 is a major component of the basal lamina of skeletal muscle cells. It is a heterotrimer composed of 3 chains: merosin (laminin alpha 2 chain), beta 1, and gamma 1
a decreased amount of collagen vi in mus: A decreased amount of collagen VI in muscle tissue. Collagen VI is a primarily associated with the extracellular matrix of skeletal muscle
a deviation from normal in the amount of: A deviation from normal in the amount of dystrophin in muscle fiber tissue. Dystrophin is located at the muscle sarcolemma in a membrane-spanning protein complex that connects the cytoskeleton to the basal lamina
lack of dystrophin in muscle tissue. imm: Lack of dystrophin in muscle tissue. Immunohistochemistry reveals absent dystrophin protein in the muscle biopsy
a decreased amount of dystrophin in musc: A decreased amount of dystrophin in muscle fiber tissue
immunohistochemistry reveals reduced alp: Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced alpha dystroglycan protein in the muscle biopsy. Alpha-dystroglycan is a heavily glycosylated peripheral-membrane component of the dystrophin-associated glycoprotein complex (DAPC), which, in addition to laminin alpha2, binds perlecan and agrin in the extracellular matrix, whereas beta-dystroglycan, derived from the same gene, is a transmembrane protein that links to dystrophin intracellularly
deviation from normal in the amount of a: Deviation from normal in the amount of alpha sarcoglycan in muscle. The alpha, beta, gamma, and delta sarcoglycans are components of the dystrophin-complex. They are all N-glycosylated transmembrane proteins with a short intra-cellular domain, a single transmembrane region and a large extra-cellular domain containing a cluster of conserved cysteines
lack of alpha sarcoglycan in muscle. imm: Lack of alpha sarcoglycan in muscle. Immunohistochemistry reveals absent alpha sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
a decreased amount of alpha sarcoglycan: A decreased amount of alpha sarcoglycan in muscle. Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced alpha sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
deviation from normal in the amount of b: Deviation from normal in the amount of beta sarcoglycan in muscle. The alpha, beta, gamma, and delta sarcoglycans are components of the dystrophin-complex. They are all N-glycosylated transmembrane proteins with a short intra-cellular domain, a single transmembrane region and a large extra-cellular domain containing a cluster of conserved cysteines
deviation from normal in the amount of g: Deviation from normal in the amount of gamma sarcoglycan in muscle. The alpha, beta, gamma, and delta sarcoglycans are components of the dystrophin-complex. They are all N-glycosylated transmembrane proteins with a short intra-cellular domain, a single transmembrane region and a large extra-cellular domain containing a cluster of conserved cysteines
deviation from normal in the amount of d: Deviation from normal in the amount of delta sarcoglycan in muscle. The alpha, beta, gamma, and delta sarcoglycans are components of the dystrophin-complex. They are all N-glycosylated transmembrane proteins with a short intra-cellular domain, a single transmembrane region and a large extra-cellular domain containing a cluster of conserved cysteines
immunohistochemistry shows complete lack: Immunohistochemistry shows complete lack of beta sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
immunohistochemistry reveals reduced bet: Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced beta sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
immunohistochemistry reveals reduced gam: Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced gamma sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
immunohistochemistry shows complete lack: Immunohistochemistry shows complete lack of gamma sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
Immunohistochemistry shows complete lack of delta sarcoglycan protein in the muscle biopsy
a deviation from normal of muscle alpha-: A deviation from normal of muscle alpha-dystroglcan expression. Alpha-dystroglycan is a heavily glycosylated peripheral-membrane component of the dystrophin-associated glycoprotein complex (DAPC), which, in addition to laminin alpha2, binds perlecan and agrin in the extracellular matrix, whereas beta-dystroglycan, derived from the same gene, is a transmembrane protein that links to dystrophin intracellularly
a deviation from normal in the expressio: A deviation from normal in the expression of dysferlin in muscle tissue. Dysferlin is an ubiquitous 230-KDa transmembrane protein involved in calcium-mediated sarcolemma resealing
immunohistochemistry shows complete lack: Immunohistochemistry shows complete lack of dysferlin protein in the muscle biopsy
immunohistochemistry reveals reduced dys: Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced dysferlin protein in the muscle biopsy
a deviation from normal of the amount of: A deviation from normal of the amount of the inner nuclear membrane protein emerin in muscle tissue
immunohistochemistry shows complete lack: Immunohistochemistry shows complete lack of emerin protein in the muscle biopsy
immunohistochemistry reveals reduced eme: Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced emerin protein in the muscle biopsy
a deviation from normal in the amount of: A deviation from normal in the amount of calpain-3 in muscle tissue. Calpains are intracellular nonlysosomal cysteine proteases modulated by calcium ions. A typical calpain is a heterodimer composed of two distinct subunits, one large (over 80 kDa) and the other small (30 kDa). While only one gene encoding the small subunit has been demonstrated, there are many genes for the large one. CAPN3 is similar to ubiquitous Calpain 1 and 2 (m-calpain and micro-calpain), but contains specific insertion sequences (NS, IS1 and IS2). Calpains cleave target proteins to modify their properties, rather than breaking down the substrates
western blot shows complete lack of calp: Western blot shows complete lack of calpain-3 protein in the muscle biopsy tissue
western blot reveals reduced calpain-3 p: Western blot reveals reduced calpain-3 protein in the muscle biopsy tissue
immunohistochemistry reveals reduced per: Immunohistochemistry reveals reduced perlecan protein in the muscle biopsy. Perlecan is a basement membrane-specific heparan sulfate proteoglycan core protein (HSPG) also known as heparan sulfate proteoglycan 2 (HSPG2)
a deviation from the normal amount of la: A deviation from the normal amount of lamin A/C in muscle tissue. The LMNA gene gives rise to at least three splicing isoforms including the two main isoforms, lamin A and lamin C. These are constitutive components of the fibrous nuclear lamina and have different roles, ranging from mechanical nuclear membrane maintenance to gene regulation
a decreased amount of lamin a/c in muscl: A decreased amount of lamin A/C in muscle tissue. This feature can be shown by immunohistochemistry of Western blotting of muscle tissue
a congenital anomaly, in which the trans: A congenital anomaly, in which the transverse process of the last lumbar vertebra (L5) fuses to the sacrum on one side or both, or to ilium, or both
an anomaly of the inner mucous membrane: An anomaly of the inner mucous membrane of the uterus
the growth of endometrial tissue outside: The growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus
abnormal response to ristocetin as manif: Abnormal response to ristocetin as manifested by reduced or lacking aggregation of platelets upon addition of ristocetin to platelet-poor plasma
reduced ability of von willibrand factor: Reduced ability of von Willibrand factor (vWF) to bind collagen. Abnormal response to collagen as manifested by reduced or lacking ability of plasma von WIllebrand Factor to bind collagen. An ELISA-based assay is typically used; the test is sensitive to loss of von Willebrand Factor high molecular weight multimers
deviation from the normal von willebrand: Deviation from the normal von Willebrand factor multimer pattern
absence of large von willebrand factor m: Absence of large von Willebrand Factor multimers on gel electrophoresis
detection of abnormal ultra-large von wi: Detection of abnormal ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers
complete absence of all von willebrand f: Complete absence of all von Willebrand factor multimers
lack of intermediate von willebrand fact: Lack of intermediate von Willebrand Factor multimers on gel electrophoresis
abnormal response to ristocetin as manif: Abnormal response to ristocetin as manifested by increased aggregation of platelets upon addition of low-dose ristocetin to platelet-rich plasma
bleeding that persists for a longer than: Bleeding that persists for a longer than usual time following circumcision
an abnormally increased degree of bleedi: An abnormally increased degree of bleeding following a superfical injury to the surface of the skin
an abnormal high amount of bleeding foll: An abnormal high amount of bleeding following the procedure of taking a blood sample
recurrent or excessive bleeding from the: Recurrent or excessive bleeding from the mouth
an anomaly in the placement or shape of: An anomaly in the placement or shape of the hairline (trichion) on the back of the head (neck), that is, the border between skin on the back of the head that has head hair and that does not
an anomaly of the amount or nature of ab: An anomaly of the amount or nature of abdominal sounds. Abdominal sounds (bowel sounds) are made by the movement of the intestines as they promote passage of abdominal contents by peristalsis
an increased amount of bowel sounds: An increased amount of bowel sounds
an decreased amount of bowel sounds: An decreased amount of bowel sounds
complete lack of abdominal sounds as ass: Complete lack of abdominal sounds as assayed by examination of the abdomen with a stethoscope
a structural anomaly of the liver locate: A structural anomaly of the liver located predominantly in the hepatocytes as opposed to stromal cells
tremor of the trunk in an anterior-poste: Tremor of the trunk in an anterior-posterior plane at 3-4 Hz
an extra or unusual sound heard during a: An extra or unusual sound heard during a heartbeat caused vibrations resulting from the flow of blood through the heart
severely decreased cardiac output with e: Severely decreased cardiac output with evidence of inadequate end-organ perfusion (i.e., tissue hypoxia) in the presence of adequate intravascular volume
an abnormally increased number of plasma: An abnormally increased number of plasma cells in tissues, exudates, or blood
inflammation of the biliary ductal syste: Inflammation of the biliary ductal system, affecting the intrahepatic or extrahepatic portions, or both
cholangiocarcinoma is a primary cancer o: Cholangiocarcinoma is a primary cancer originating in the biliary epithelium i.e., the cholangiocytes, of the extrahepatic and intrahepatic biliary ducts. It is extremely invasive, develops rapidly, often metastasizes, and has a very poor prognosis. They are slow growing tumors which spread longitudinally along the bile ducts with neural, perineural and subepithelial extension
rupture of the wall of the gallbladder: Rupture of the wall of the gallbladder
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the scrotum
the presence of free monoclonal immunogl: The presence of free monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains in the urine
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) and perceived to originate in the flank
eversion of the endocervix with protrusi: Eversion of the endocervix with protrusion of the central (endocervical) columnar epithelium through the external os of the cervis into the vaginal milieu
abnormal growth of tissue projecting fro: Abnormal growth of tissue projecting from a mucous membrane of the endocervix
a sensation of itching in the vagina: A sensation of itching in the vagina
pain in the jaw or ear induced by chewin: Pain in the jaw or ear induced by chewing or otherwise moving the jaw
the presence of an increased number of t: The presence of an increased number of twists and turns of the temporal artery
occurence of excessive sweating during s: Occurence of excessive sweating during sleep
the presence of autoantibodies (immunogl: The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against mitochondria
increase in diameter of the veins locate: Increase in diameter of the veins located underneath the skin of the abdomen
extreme dilation of the submucusoal vein: Extreme dilation of the submucusoal veins in the stomach
presence of a pseudoaneurysm in the arte: Presence of a pseudoaneurysm in the artery that supplies the gallbladder and cystic duct with blood. A pseudoaneurysm, also known as a false aneurysm, forms when blood leaks through a breach of the arterial wall but is contained by the adventitia or surrounding perivascular soft tissue
a collection of clotted blood surroundin: A collection of clotted blood surrounding the kidney
congenital absence of the myelin sheath: Congenital absence of the myelin sheath on a nerve
elevated thickness of the myelin sheath: Elevated thickness of the myelin sheath of peripheral nerves, in a regular and concentric fashion
the presence of multiple sausage-shaped: The presence of multiple sausage-shaped swellings of the myelin sheath (The Latin tomaculum means sausage)
loss of myelin from peripheral nerves in: Loss of myelin from peripheral nerves in a pattern that differs between right and left
an abnormality of the function of the el: An abnormality of the function of the electrical signals with which peripheral nerve cells communicate with each other or with muscles
an anomaly in the magnitude of the actio: An anomaly in the magnitude of the action potential along a peripheral nerve, that is, of the rapid rise and fall of the electrical membrane potential of the nerve
dorsiflexion of the big toe, sometimes a: Dorsiflexion of the big toe, sometimes accompanied by fanning of the other toes, elicited by stroking along the medial side of the tibia (the normal response would be no movement of the big toe)
dorsal extension of the big toe, sometim: Dorsal extension of the big toe, sometimes accompanied by fanning of the other toes, elicited by compressing the calf muscles (a normal response is no movement of the big toe)
loss of strength in all four limbs. tetr: Loss of strength in all four limbs. Tetraplegia refers to a complete loss of strength, whereas Tetraparesis refers to an incomplete loss of strength
the vestibulo-ocular reflex is responsib: The vestibulo-ocular reflex is responsible for the stabilization of the retinal image during movement. The visual vestibular ocular reflex (VVOR) or visual enhanced VOR, maintains ocular stability during head motion by generating compensatory eye movement opposite to head movement, and is a major component of visual vestibular interaction. This feature is an impairment of this reflex, manifested as the combined impairment of the three compensatory eye movement reflexes, namely the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR), smooth pursuit (SP) and optokinetic reflex (OKR)
an isometric tremor occurs with muscle c: An isometric tremor occurs with muscle contraction against a rigid stationary object (e.g., when making a fist)
tremor that occurs during any voluntary: Tremor that occurs during any voluntary movement. It may include visually or non-visually guided movements. Tremor during target directed movement is called intention tremor
nodding movement of the head or body: Nodding movement of the head or body
tremor classified by the affected body p: Tremor classified by the affected body part
Reduced muscle tone of oral musculature. In infants, this feature may be associated with difficulties in breast feeding, and may affetc the latch, jaw motions, tongue placement, lip seal, suck/swallow/breathe pattern and overall feeding behavior
an anomaly in the communication from a n: An anomaly in the communication from a neuron to a target across a synapse in the peripheral nervous system
a type of weakness of the bulbar muscles: A type of weakness of the bulbar muscles (muscles of the mouth and throat responsible for speech and swallowing) that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions
a type of weakness of the muscles involv: A type of weakness of the muscles involved in chewing that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions

A type of weakness of the muscles involved in speech that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions

A type of weakness of the muscles involved in swallowing that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions

A type of weakness of the muscles involved in breathing (respiration) that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions
a type of weakness of skeletal muscle th: A type of weakness of skeletal muscle that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions
a type of weakness of a skeletal muscle: A type of weakness of a skeletal muscle of distal part of a limb that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions

A type of weakness of a skeletal muscle in the neck that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions

A type of weakness of a skeletal muscle of proximal part of a limb that occurs after a muscle group is used and lessens if the muscle group has some rest. That is, there is diminution of strength with repetitive muscle actions
specific drugs interfere selectively wit: Specific drugs interfere selectively with the different cellular mechanisms involved in neuromuscular transmission (synthesis, storage, release, action and inactivation of transmitter).
