The response of a patient to a specific drug can therefore be useful information for the differential diagnosis
improvement of muscle strength in respon: Improvement of muscle strength in response to administration of an acetylcholine esterase inhibitor
lack of improvement of muscle strength i: Lack of improvement of muscle strength in response to administration of an acetylcholine esterase inhibitor
the variation in the time interval betwe: The variation in the time interval between the two action potentials of the same motor unit is called jitter. This term therefore applies to increased variability in the interval between successive action potentials of the same motor unit, which is measured by electromyography (EMG)
a compound muscle action potential (cmap: A compound muscle action potential (CMAP) is a type of electromyography (EMG). CMAP refers to a group of almost simultaneous action potentials from several muscle fibers in the same area evoked by stimulation of the supplying motor nerve and are recorded as one multipeaked summated action potential. This abnormality refers to an abnormal increase in the amplitude during the course of the investigation
breathing movements in which the chest w: Breathing movements in which the chest wall moves in on inspiration and out on expiration, in reverse of the normal movements. It may be seen in children with respiratory distress of any cause, which leads to indrawing of the intercostal spaces during inspiration. Patients with chronic airways obstruction also show indrawing of the lower ribs during inspiration, due to the distorted action of a depressed and flattened diaphragm. Crush injuries of the chest, with fractured ribs and sternum, can lead to a severe degree of paradoxical breathing
the presence of autoantibodies (immunogl: The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against the acetylcholine receptor
The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against voltage-gated calcium channels
The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against muscle specific kinase (anti-MuSK Ab)
reduced velocity and acceleration in the: Reduced velocity and acceleration in the pupillary light response
excessive or pathological tendency to sa: Excessive or pathological tendency to save and collect possessions
lack of emotional reactivity and empathy: Lack of emotional reactivity and empathy for situations or persons, sometime also for family members
pathological persistent sexual disinhibi: Pathological persistent sexual disinhibiting behavior, directed at oneself or to others
uncontrolled episodes of crying, without: Uncontrolled episodes of crying, without apparent motivating stimuli
reduction of goal-directed behaviors lin: Reduction of goal-directed behaviors linked to the impairment in frontal executive functions (planning of an action for example)
difficulty in performing the correct exe: Difficulty in performing the correct execution of limbs movements in absence of motor impairment
punding is a stereotypical motor behavio: Punding is a stereotypical motor behavior characterized by an intense fascination with repetitive, excessive and non-goal oriented handling, and examining of objects
a progressive loss of the ability to rem: A progressive loss of the ability to remember the meaning of words, faces and objects
behavior that is not in line with social: Behavior that is not in line with social norms
excessive desire to eat sweet foods: Excessive desire to eat sweet foods
difficulty in recognizing objects by vis: Difficulty in recognizing objects by visual input in absence of sensorial visual impairment
perseveration can be defined as the cont: Perseveration can be defined as the contextually inappropriate and unintentional repetition of a response or behavioral unit. In other words, the observed repetitiveness does not meet the demands of the situation, is not the product of deliberation, and may even unfold despite counterintention. Perseveration can therefore be differentiated from goal-directed and intentional forms of repetition, such as linguistic redundancies designed to enhance communicative or poetic impact
a deviation from normal in the expressio: A deviation from normal in the expression of desmin in muscle tissue. Desmin is an 53-KDa protein
immunohistochemistry shows accumulation: Immunohistochemistry shows accumulation of desmin protein in the muscle biopsy
a deviation from normal in the expressio: A deviation from normal in the expression of myotilin in muscle tissue. Myotilin is a 57kD cytoskeletal protein
immunohistochemistry shows accumulation: Immunohistochemistry shows accumulation of myotilin protein in the muscle biopsy
a deviation from normal in the expressio: A deviation from normal in the expression of valosin-containing protein in muscle tissue. Valosin-containing protein an ubiquitously expressed multifunctional 100-kD protein that is a member of the AAA+ (ATPase associated with various activities) protein family
immunohistochemistry shows accumulation: Immunohistochemistry shows accumulation of valosin-containing protein in the muscle biopsy
the presence of disorganized areas calle: The presence of disorganized areas called cores in the center of muscle fibers. There is a typical appearance of the biopsy on light microscopy, where the muscle cells have cores that are devoid of mitochondria and specific enzymes. Cores are typically well demarcated and centrally located, but may occasionally be multiple and of eccentric
an increased amount of glycogen in muscl: An increased amount of glycogen in muscle tissue found specifically in lysosomes
elevated glycogen content in the sarcopl: Elevated glycogen content in the sarcoplasm (cytoplasm) of muscle fibers
limitation of wrist and finger extension: Limitation of wrist and finger extension on asking patient to form a prayer sign. This is a result of progressive wrist and finger flexion contractures
an increased cpk level between 4x and 50: An increased CPK level between 4X and 50X above the upper normal level
an increased cpk level more than 50x abo: An increased CPK level more than 50X above the upper normal level
abnormalities of the overall muscle bulk: Abnormalities of the overall muscle bulk based on clinical observation
reduced strength of the musculature of t: Reduced strength of the musculature of the hand
underdevelopment of the musculature of t: Underdevelopment of the musculature of the upper arm, which may include the deltoid, the triceps, the biceps, and the brachioradialis
thrombosis of the portal vein and/or its: Thrombosis of the portal vein and/or its tributaries, which include the splenic vein and the sup-erior and inferior mesenteric veins
an obstruction in the veins of the liver: An obstruction in the veins of the liver caused by a blood clot (thrombosis)
the occurence of an elevated body temper: The occurence of an elevated body temperature of the mother during pregnancy
the occurence of maternal fever during l: The occurence of maternal fever during labor
the occurence of fever in a mother durin: The occurence of fever in a mother during the first trimester of pregnancy
the term splanchnic vein thrombosis enco: The term splanchnic vein thrombosis encompasses Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic vein thrombosis), extrahepatic portal vein obstruction (EHPVO), and mesenteric vein thrombosis; the word splanchnic is used to refer to the visceral organs (of the abdominal cavity)
a clot that obstructs blood flow in a me: A clot that obstructs blood flow in a mesenteric vein (the superior and the inferior mesenteric vein drain blood from the small and large intestine)
a sudden eruption (rash) of the surface: A sudden eruption (rash) of the surface of a mucous membrane of the mouth or pharynx
the presence of multiple granulomata (sm: The presence of multiple granulomata (small nodular inflammatory lesions containing grouped mononuclear phagocytes) in the lung
complete lack of memory b cells, that is: Complete lack of memory B cells, that is, of mature B cell type that is long-lived, readily activated upon re-encounter of its antigenic determinant, and has been selected for expression of higher affinity immunoglobulin
complete lack of mature b cells, that is: Complete lack of mature B cells, that is, of B cells that have left the bone marrow
a reduced ability of a t cell population: A reduced ability of a T cell population to expand by cell division following T cell activation
small areas of bleeding (hemorrhage) und: Small areas of bleeding (hemorrhage) under the fingernail or toenail
the presence of multiple polyps in the l: The presence of multiple polyps in the large intestine
the presence of multiple polyps in the s: The presence of multiple polyps in the small intestine
one or more brown punctate macules on th: One or more brown punctate macules on the skin of the genitalia
localized or generalized increased genit: Localized or generalized increased genital pigmentation
localized or generalized decreased genit: Localized or generalized decreased genital pigmentation
length of penis more than 2 sd below the: Length of penis more than 2 SD below the mean for age accompanied by hypospadias
penile width more than 2 standard deviat: Penile width more than 2 standard deviations (SD) below the mean for age. Alternatively circumference of the flaccid penis more than 2 SD below the mean for age. Alternatively, apparently decreased penile width for age
rotated position of the glans, with or w: Rotated position of the glans, with or without the penile shaft, of 30 degrees or more
ventral skinfold extending from penis to: Ventral skinfold extending from penis to scrotum
distance between left and right side of: Distance between left and right side of the flaccid penis at the attachment to the skin above the pubic symphysis more than 2 standard deviations above the mean for age
deposition of calcium salts in the fibro: Deposition of calcium salts in the fibrous sheet that connects the radius and the ulna
increased growth of callus, the bony and: Increased growth of callus, the bony and cartilaginous material that forms a connecting bridge across a bone fracture during fracture healing
an elevated level of insulin-like growth: An elevated level of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1) in the blood circulation
increase in the activity of adenosine de: Increase in the activity of adenosine deaminase (ADA), an enzyme involved in purine metabolism, within erythrocytes. ADA is involved in the catabolism of adenosine
2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-dpg) control: 2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG) controls the movement of oxygen from red blood cells to tissues. Anemia is usually accompanied by an increased level of 2,3-DPG in order to promote tissue oxygenation. This term refers to an inappropriate low 2,3-DPG concentration in erythrocytes
an altered ability of any enzyme to act: An altered ability of any enzyme to act as catalysts within erythrocytes. This term includes changes due to altered levels of an enzyme
decrease in the activity of adenosine de: Decrease in the activity of adenosine deaminase (ADA), an enzyme involved in purine metabolism, within erythrocytes. ADA is involved in the catabolism of adenosine
additional scrotum, or part of a scrotum: Additional scrotum, or part of a scrotum in an abnormal location
scrotum in a position other than the usu: Scrotum in a position other than the usual position inferior to the base of the penis
abnormal morphology of a vertebral pedic: Abnormal morphology of a vertebral pedical
underdeveloped pedicle of the fifth lumb: Underdeveloped pedicle of the fifth lumbar vertebra
radiolucent focal defect of a rib shaft: Radiolucent focal defect of a rib shaft
fusion of cervical vertebrae at c3 and c: Fusion of cervical vertebrae at C3 and C4, caused by a failure in the normal segmentation or division of the cervical vertebrae during the early weeks of fetal development
radiolucent focal defect of the posterio: Radiolucent focal defect of the posterior portion of a rib shaft. The 'gaps' may lead to flail chest
only part of the septum pellucidum (a th: Only part of the septum pellucidum (a thin, triangular, vertical membrane separating the lateral ventricles of the brain) is present. This feature can be appreciated on magnetic resonance tomography or computed tomography of the brain
a form of macrogloassia (increased size: A form of macrogloassia (increased size of the tongue) characterized by a broad based root of the tongue but a small tongue tip, giving the appearance of a triangle
abnormal splayed configuration (spreadin: Abnormal splayed configuration (spreading out) of the superior cerebellar peduncle
atrophy of the superior cerebellar pedun: Atrophy of the superior cerebellar peduncle
an abnormal flattening of an epiphysis o: An abnormal flattening of an epiphysis of femur
lack of ossification of the sacrum: Lack of ossification of the sacrum
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of one or more metaphyses of a bone of the leg
Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of a metaphysis of a tibia
Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of a metaphysis of a fibula
an anomaly of the metaphysis of the dist: An anomaly of the metaphysis of the distal femur (close to the knee)
presence of only 10 (instead of the usua: Presence of only 10 (instead of the usual 12) pairs of ribs
an anomaly of the anterior commissure, a: An anomaly of the anterior commissure, a bundle of nerve fibers that connect the two cerebral hemispheres across the midline. The anterior commissure plays a role in pain sensation and contains decussating fibers from the olfactory tracts
a deviation from the normal number of ve: A deviation from the normal number of vertebrae in the spinal column
the presence of 11 instead of the normal: The presence of 11 instead of the normal 12 thoracic vertebrae
the presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared): The presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared) metaphyseal segment of one or more long bones of the leg
The presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared) metaphyseal segment of the distal tibia
The presence of a splayed (i.e.,flared) metaphyseal segment of the distal fibula
displacement or malalignment of one or m: Displacement or malalignment of one or more joints in the upper extremity (arm)
Displacement or malalignment of one or more joints in the lower extremity (leg)
absence of the spongy bone structure (or: Absence of the spongy bone structure (or tissue) of the internal part of the skull cap (i.e., of the calvarial diploe)
an anomalous structure of the periosteum: An anomalous structure of the periosteum, i.e., of the membrane that covers the outer surface of bones
a type of inflammation of the lips invol: A type of inflammation of the lips involving one or both of the corners of the mouth
reduced strength of one or more muscles: Reduced strength of one or more muscles innervated by the facial nerve (the seventh cranial nerve)
an increase in the vertical distance bet: An increase in the vertical distance between adjacent vertebral bodies, observed as an increase in the intervertebral disc space
an anomaly of the vertebral artery, the: An anomaly of the vertebral artery, the major artery of the neck that originates from the subclavian artery and merges to form the single midline basilar artery in a complex called the vertebrobasilar system
underdevelopment of the vertebral artery: Underdevelopment of the vertebral artery on one side
Underdevelopment of the vertebral artery on both sides
fissure within the spinal cord of the ne: Fissure within the spinal cord of the neck
an anomaly in the number of macrophages: An anomaly in the number of macrophages
an anomaly in the number of osteoclasts,: An anomaly in the number of osteoclasts, bone-resorbing cells that develop from macrophages
reduced anteroposterior thickness of the: Reduced anteroposterior thickness of the retina. This phenotype can be appreciated by retinal optical coherence tomography (OCT)
the presence of cartilage cells (chondro: The presence of cartilage cells (chondrocytes) that are substantially increased in size and contain more than one nucleus and are located within the resting zone of the epiphyseal cartilage
a reduced ability of the skin of the fin: A reduced ability of the skin of the fingertips to wrinkle when exposed to stimuli such as soaking in water or application of EMLA cream (the fingertip remains smooth)
a structuraly anomaly of t cells that ex: A structuraly anomaly of T cells that express an alpha-beta T cell receptor
a structural anomaly of a cd4-positive,: A structural anomaly of a CD4-positive, CD25-positive, alpha-beta T cell. These cells are regulatory T cells
a deviation from the normal count of cd4: A deviation from the normal count of CD4-positive, CD25-positive, alpha-beta regulatory T cells
lack of cd4+cd25+ t regulatory cells: Lack of CD4+CD25+ T regulatory cells
an increased number of cd4-positive, cd2: An increased number of CD4-positive, CD25-positive regulatory T cells
an anomaly of the circulating level of a: An anomaly of the circulating level of a gonadotropin, that is, of a protein hormone secreted by gonadotrope cells of the anterior pituitary of vertebrates. The primary gonadotropins are luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)
a reduction of the circulating level of: A reduction of the circulating level of a gonadotropin, that is, of a protein hormone secreted by gonadotrope cells of the anterior pituitary of vertebrates. The primary gonadotropins are luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)
A reduction of the circulating level of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)
a reduction in the circulating level of: A reduction in the circulating level of luteinizing hormone (LH)
an anomaly of the circulating level of l: An anomaly of the circulating level of luteinizing hormone (LH)
an anomaly of the circulating level of f: An anomaly of the circulating level of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)
an anomaly in the blood concentration of: An anomaly in the blood concentration of an androgen, that is, of a steroid hormone that controls development and maintenance of masculine characteristics. The androgens include testosterone and Dehydroepiandrosterone
an elevation of the blood concentration: An elevation of the blood concentration of an androgen, that is, of a steroid hormone that controls development and maintenance of masculine characteristics. The androgens include testosterone and Dehydroepiandrosterone
a reduction in the blood concentration o: A reduction in the blood concentration of an androgen, that is, of a steroid hormone that controls development and maintenance of masculine characteristics. The androgens include testosterone and Dehydroepiandrosterone
a circumscribed, solid elevation of skin: A circumscribed, solid elevation of skin with no visible fluid that is reddish (erythematous) in color
a well-circumscribed, intensely pruritic: A well-circumscribed, intensely pruritic, raised wheal (edema of the superficial skin) typically 1 to 2 cm in diameter
an anomalous level of insulin-like growt: An anomalous level of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1) in the blood circulation
a reduced level of insulin-like growth f: A reduced level of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1) in the blood circulation
abnormal levels of interferon in the blo: Abnormal levels of interferon in the blood
abnormal levels of interferon gamma meas: Abnormal levels of interferon gamma measured in the blood circulation
an elevation in the concentration of int: An elevation in the concentration of interferon gamma measured in the blood circulation
small cell lung cancer (sclc) is a type: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is a type of highly malignant lung cancer that is composed of small ovoid cells. In the past, SCLC was called oat cell carcinoma because the microscopic appearance of the cells was felt to resemble oats. SLCLC usually originates near the bronchi and in many cases may grow and metastasize quickly
a type of non-small cell lung carcinoma: A type of non-small cell lung carcinoma that is derived from stratified squamous epithelial cells

A type of non-small cell lung carcinoma that is derived from undifferentiated malignant neoplasms originating from transformed epithelial cells in the lung, and which is differentiate from small-cell lung carcinoma by the larger size of the anaplastic cells, a higher cytoplasmic-to-nuclear size ratio, and a lack of salt-and-pepper appearance of the chromatin
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration in the blood circulation of an icosanoid (also know as eicosanoids). These are signaling molecules derived from oxidation of 20-carbon fatty acids. Most are produced from arachidonic acid, a 20-carbon polyunsaturated fatty acid (5,8,11,14-eicosatetraenoic acid)
a reduction in the level of carnitine in: A reduction in the level of carnitine in muscle tissue
delivery by caesarian section representi: Delivery by Caesarian section representing the first time the mother has delivered by Caesarian section
Delivery by Caesarian section representing where the mother has already had a previous Cesarean delivery, and this is a repeat Cesarean birth
vaginal birth after caesarian (vbac) ref: Vaginal birth after Caesarian (VBAC) refers to the situation where the mother has had a previous Cesarean delivery but has now delivered vaginally
the odon device is an instrument for ass: The Odon device is an instrument for assisted vaginal deliveries that is applied on the head of the baby and used to apply traction to assist the birth process
hyperphalangy is a digit morphology in w: Hyperphalangy is a digit morphology in which increased numbers of phalanges are arranged linearly within a digit. That is, there is an accessory phalanx that is arranged linearly with the other phalanges
an accessory phalanx of the index (secon: An accessory phalanx of the index (second) finger that is arranged linearly with the other phalanges. Hyperphalangy of the index finger results from an accessory ossification center at the metacarpophalangeal joint, resulting in radial deviation of the index finger. Note that this term refers only to this type of hyperphalangy
vaginal delivery following induction of: Vaginal delivery following induction of labor, a procedure used to stimulate uterine contractions during pregnancy before labor begins on its own
a deviation in the normal proportion of: A deviation in the normal proportion of naive B cells (CD19+/CD27-/IgD+/IgM+) relative to the total number of B cells. Naive B cells represent one of the subtypes of B cells in the peripheral blood, and are B cells that have not been exposed to antigen
an elevation above the normal proportion: An elevation above the normal proportion of naive B cells (CD19+/CD27-/IgD+/IgM+) relative to total number of B cells. Naive B cells represent one of the subtypes of B cells in the peripheral blood, and are B cells that have not been exposed to antigen
a reduction below normal proportion of n: A reduction below normal proportion of naive B cells (CD19+/CD27-/IgD+/IgM+) relative to total number of B cells. Naive B cells represent one of the subtypes of B cells in the peripheral blood, and are B cells that have not been exposed to antigen
a deviation of the normal proportion of: A deviation of the normal proportion of memory B cells in circulation relative to total number of B cells. Memory B cells develop from naive B cells. Upon antigen rechallenge, memory B cells rapidly expand and differentiate into plasma cells under the cognate control of memory Th cells (Phase IV)
a reduction in the normal proportion of: A reduction in the normal proportion of memory B cells (CD19+/CD27+) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells. Memory B cells develop from naive B cells. Upon antigen rechallenge, memory B cells rapidly expand and differentiate into plasma cells under the cognate control of memory Th cells (Phase IV)
an elevation in the proportion of memory: An elevation in the proportion of memory B cells (CD19+/CD27+) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells. Memory B cells develop from naive B cells. Upon antigen rechallenge, memory B cells rapidly expand and differentiate into plasma cells under the cognate control of memory Th cells (Phase IV)
a deviation from normal proportion immat: A deviation from normal proportion immature B cells (CD19+/ CD21low) in circulation relative to total number of B cells. Immature B cells (IgM+) are still in final stages of development within the bone marrow. Naive B cells are those which have left the bone marrow, before they bind to the antigen for which they're specific (IgM+/IgD+)
an elevation in the proportion above nor: An elevation in the proportion above normal of immature B cells (CD19+/ CD21low) in circulation relative to total number of B cells. Immature B cells (IgM+) are still in final stages of development within the bone marrow. Naive B cells are those which have left the bone marrow, before they bind to the antigen for which they're specific (IgM+/IgD+)
a reduction in normal proportion of imma: A reduction in normal proportion of immature B cells (CD19+/ CD21low)in circulation relative to total number of B cells. Immature B cells (IgM+) are still in final stages of development within the bone marrow. Naive B cells are those which have left the bone marrow, before they bind to the antigen for which they're specific (IgM+/IgD+)
a deviation in the normal proportion of: A deviation in the normal proportion of transitional B cells (CD19+/CD38high/IgMhigh) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells. B cells originate from precursors in the bone marrow, and the first cells which migrate to the peripheral blood have been classified as transitional B cells
a reduction in the normal proportion of: A reduction in the normal proportion of transitional B cells (CD19+/CD38high/IgMhigh) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells. B cells originate from precursors in the bone marrow, and the first cells which migrate to the peripheral blood have been classified as transitional B cells
an elevation in the normal proportion of: An elevation in the normal proportion of transitional B cells (CD19+/CD38high/IgMhigh) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells. B cells originate from precursors in the bone marrow, and the first cells which migrate to the peripheral blood have been classified as transitional B cells
a deviation of the normal proportion of: A deviation of the normal proportion of marginal zone B cells (CD19+/CD27+/IgM+/IgD+) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells
a reduction in the normal proportion of: A reduction in the normal proportion of marginal zone B cells (CD19+/CD27+/IgM+/IgD+) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells
an elevation in the normal proportion of: An elevation in the normal proportion of marginal zone B cells (CD19+/CD27+/IgM+/IgD+) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells
a deviation of the normal proportion of: A deviation of the normal proportion of class-switched memory B cells (CD19+/CD27+/IgM-/IgD-) in circulation relative to the total number of B cells. Marginal zone B cells undergo limited somatic hypermutation and produce high-affinity IgM and some IgG, whereas class-switched memory B cells synthetize IgG, IgM, and IgA
an increase in the normal proportion of: An increase in the normal proportion of class-switched memory B cells (CD19+/CD27+/IgM+/IgD+) relative to the total number of B cells. Marginal zone B cells undergo limited somatic hypermutation and produce high-affinity IgM and some IgG, whereas class-switched memory B cells synthetize IgG, IgM, and IgA
a reduction in the normal proportion of: A reduction in the normal proportion of class-switched memory B cells (CD19+/CD27+/IgM+/IgD+) relative to the total number of B cells. Marginal zone B cells undergo limited somatic hypermutation and produce high-affinity IgM and some IgG, whereas class-switched memory B cells synthetize IgG, IgM, and IgA
any deivation from the normal concentrat: Any deivation from the normal concentration in the blood circulation of a thromboxane. Thromboxanes are derived from prostaglandin precursors in platelets, and stimulate aggregation of platelets and constriction of blood vessels
an abnormally decreased concentration of: An abnormally decreased concentration of leukotriene C4 in the blood circulation
reduced ability of lexical discriminatio: Reduced ability of lexical discrimination, which refers to the process of distinguishing a stimulus word from other phonologically similar words. Lexical discrimination can be defined as the process of correctly identifying words in the mental lexicon to match the phonological input of a stimulus
intraventricular papillary neoplasm deri: Intraventricular papillary neoplasm derived from choroid plexus epithelium. Plexus tumors are most common in the lateral and fourth ventricles; while 80% of lateral ventricle tumors present in children, fourth ventricle tumors are evenly distributed in all age groups. Clinically, choroid plexus tumors tend to cause hydrocephalus and increased intracranial pressure. Histologically, choroid plexus papillomas correspond to WHO grade I, choroid plexus carcinomas to WHO grade III
a low-grade papillary epithelial neoplas: A low-grade papillary epithelial neoplasm (adenocarcinoma) with a slow growth pattern. The endolymphatic duct emerges from the posterior wall of the saccule (of the inner ear) and ends in a blind pouch, the endolymphatic sac. Endolymphatic sac tumors (ELSTs) are known under different names in the literature (Heffner tumor, aggressive papillary middle ear tumor, and low-grade adenocarcinoma of endolymphatic sac origin)
carcinoma that originates in the fallopi: Carcinoma that originates in the Fallopian tube. It may be located in the wall or within the lumen as a growth attached to the wall by a stalk
platelets are replete with secretory gra: Platelets are replete with secretory granules, which are critical to normal platelet function. Among the three types of platelet secretory granules - alpha-granules, dense granules, and lysosomes - the alpha-granule is the most abundant. Granule contents must be released from their intracellular repository in order to achieve their physiologic function, and this term refers to a functional defect in granule secretion
abnormal release of dense granules from: Abnormal release of dense granules from platelets
abnormal secretion of the platelet dense: Abnormal secretion of the platelet dense-granule content adenosine triphosphate (ATP)
abnormal release of alpha granule conten: Abnormal release of alpha granule contents from platelets
abnormal release of lysosome contents fr: Abnormal release of lysosome contents from platelets
deviation from normal of the ratio of ad: Deviation from normal of the ratio of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) to adenosine diphosphate (ADP) within platelets
an abnormality in the rate and degree to: An abnormality in the rate and degree to which platelets aggregate after the addition of an agonist that stimulates platelet clumping. Platelet aggregation is measured using aggregometer to measure the optical density of platelet-rich plasma, whereby platelet aggregation causes the plasma to become more transparent
clumping together of platelets in the bl: Clumping together of platelets in the blood in a platelet aggregation test without addition of agents normally used to induce aggregation
a type of cancer that originates in the: A type of cancer that originates in the peritoneam. It is to be distinguished from metastatic cancer of the peritoneum. Peritoneal cancer can occur anywhere in the abdominal space, and affects the surface of organs contained inside the peritoneum
a type of pineal parenchymal cell neopla: A type of pineal parenchymal cell neoplasm that is a mature well-differentiated tumour (WHO grade I)
pineoblastoma is a rare primitive neuroe: Pineoblastoma is a rare primitive neuroectodermal tumour (PNET) arising in the pineal gland. Pineoblastomas are classified as a WHO grade IV tumour and comprise one-fourth to one-half of pineal parenchymal tumours. Pineoblastoma is a highly cellular tumor originating in the pineal gland and containing small, poorly differentiated cells
a malignant tumor that arises from the t: A malignant tumor that arises from the transitional (urothelial) epithelial cells lining the urinary tract from the renal calyces to the ureteral orifice
a carcinoma that arises in a sebaseous g: A carcinoma that arises in a sebaseous gland (an exocrine gland of the skin that secretes sebum, a waxy substance
a malignant epithelial tumor with a glan: A malignant epithelial tumor with a glandular organization that originates in the jejunum
A malignant epithelial tumor with a glandular organization that originates in the ileum
a carcinoma derived from a squamous epit: A carcinoma derived from a squamous epithelial cell of the tongue
a low-grade variant of squamous cell car: A low-grade variant of squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue with a warty (verrucous) appearance
sarcomatoid (spindle cell) carcinomas of: Sarcomatoid (spindle cell) carcinomas of the tongue is a variant of squamous carcinoma of tongue that is monoclonal, having evolved from a conventional squamous carcinoma with dedifferentiation associated with sarcomatoid transformation
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the female external genital tract (vulva)
a cancer that originates in the sqamous: A cancer that originates in the sqamous cells that line the surface of the vulva
a type of vulvar cancer that originates: A type of vulvar cancer that originates from melanocytes of the vulva
a cancer arising in a cell of the bartho: A cancer arising in a cell of the Bartholin gland, a racemose gland located slightly posterior to the opening of the vagina
an adenocarcinoma arising in the vulva: An adenocarcinoma arising in the vulva
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the epididymis, an duct that transports spermatozoa from the testis to the vas deferens
a closed sac located in the spleen: A closed sac located in the spleen
a smooth, extratesticular, spherical cys: A smooth, extratesticular, spherical cyst in the head of the epididymis
a cyst of the ovary that exhibits deposi: A cyst of the ovary that exhibits deposition of calcium salts
a benign central bone tumor composed of: A benign central bone tumor composed of fibrous connective tissue within which bone is formed
a benign central bone tumor of the jaw c: A benign central bone tumor of the jaw composed of fibrous connective tissue within which bone is formed
a myxoma originating in the skin: A myxoma originating in the skin
a benign but highly vascular nasopharyng: A benign but highly vascular nasopharyngeal neoplasm. The tumor originates from the sphenopalatine foramen and involves both the pterygopalatine fossa and the posterior nasal cavity
a tumor made up of nerve cells and nerve: A tumor made up of nerve cells and nerve fibers
a cartilage capped bony outgrowth of a l: A cartilage capped bony outgrowth of a long bone. Osteochondroma arises on the external surface of bone containing a marrow cavity that is continuous with that of the underlying bone
a usually benign tumor composed of cells: A usually benign tumor composed of cells which arise from chondroblasts or their precursors and which tend to differentiate into cartilage cells
a bening tumor of bone composed of a cen: A bening tumor of bone composed of a central zone named nidus which is an atypical bone completely enclosed within a well vascularized stroma and a peripheral sclerotic reaction zone
pilomatricoma is an asymptomatic slowly: Pilomatricoma is an asymptomatic slowly growing benign cutaneous tumor, differentiating towards the hair matrix of the hair follicle. It is covered by normal or hyperemic skin, and usually varies in size from 0.5 to 3 cm
fibrofolliculoma is a clinically asympto: Fibrofolliculoma is a clinically asymptomatic, 2-4 mm, skin-colored, dome-shaped smooth papule. It usually arises in the form of multiple lesions in adults in different areas such as the scalp, forehead, face, and neck. According to histology, the lesion is a fibrotic hamartoma characterized by infundibular epithelial proliferation and perifollicular fibrous proliferation
a squamous cell carcinoma that originate: A squamous cell carcinoma that originates in the anal canal
an adenoma carcinoma that originates in: An adenoma carcinoma that originates in the anal canal
a tumor of the anal margin: A tumor of the anal margin
an intraepithelial adenocarcinoma origin: An intraepithelial adenocarcinoma originating in the anal margin and characterized by presence of typical Paget's cells, appearing as large rounded vacuolated cells
a squamous cell carcinoma that originate: A squamous cell carcinoma that originates in the skin of the anal margin
a basal cell carcinoma that originates i: A basal cell carcinoma that originates in the anal margin
a melanoma that originates in the anal m: A melanoma that originates in the anal margin
a malignant cutaneous tumor of the elder: A malignant cutaneous tumor of the elderly that is characterized by an aggressive course with regional nodal involvement, distant metastases and a high rate of recurrence. Most patients present with rapidly growing, painless, firm, non-tender, dome-shaped red, occasionally ulcerated skin nodules, which have a red or bluish color, measuring up to several centimeters, on predominantly sun-exposed areas of the body. The overlying skin is smooth and shiny, sometimes exhibiting ulcerative, acneiform or telangiectatic features
a type of sarcoma (a connective tissue n: A type of sarcoma (A connective tissue neoplasm formed by proliferation of mesodermal cells) that develops from soft tissues like fat, muscle, nerves, fibrous tissues, blood vessels, or deep skin tissues
delivery by means of therapeutic termina: Delivery by means of therapeutic termination of pregnancy. Therapeutic abortion may be done to end a pregnancy if the mother's life is in danger or if the baby has abnormalities involving the major organ systems and is not expected to survive after birth or by choice
a tumor that arises from an element of t: A tumor that arises from an element of the autonomic nervous system
a closed fluid filled sac originating fr: A closed fluid filled sac originating from the mesentary
a type of mesenteric cyst that is lined: A type of mesenteric cyst that is lined with a thin endothelium or mesothelium and filled with chylous and lymphatic fluid
the clinical electro-oculogram (eog) is: The clinical electro-oculogram (EOG) is an electrophysiological test of function of the outer retina and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in which changes in electrical potential across the RPE are recorded during successive periods of dark and light adaptation
no detectable response to the light-adap: No detectable response to the light-adapted 3.0 ERG (single-flash cone response). This type of ERG measures responses of the cone system; a-waves arise from cone photoreceptors and cone off-bipolar cells; the b-wave comes from On- and Off-cone bipolar cells
an anomalous response to a pattern elect: An anomalous response to a pattern electroretinogram (PERG), a particular kind of ERG obtained in response to contrast modulation of patterned visual stimuli at constant mean luminance-typically contrast-reversing gratings or checkerboards-whose characteristics are fundamentally different from those of the traditional ERG in response to diffuse flashes of light
an abnormal reduction in the amplitude o: An abnormal reduction in the amplitude of the a-wave
atrophy of the capillary lamina of choro: Atrophy of the capillary lamina of choroid
an anomaly of the pigmentation in the fo: An anomaly of the pigmentation in the fovea centralis
small, red dots in the superficial retin: Small, red dots in the superficial retinal layers (it is difficult to distinguish between small hemorrhages and microaneurysms)
any structural anomaly of the blood vess: Any structural anomaly of the blood vessels of the macula
yellow-white intraretinal deposits in th: Yellow-white intraretinal deposits in the macula typically associated with damaged outer blood-retina barrier and exudation of serous fluid and lipids from the retinal microvasculature
fluffy white patch on the macula, repres: Fluffy white patch on the macula, representing localized areas of dense white swelling of the retinal nerve fibre layer. They often have a zigzag internal structure, a feathered edge but an otherwise well-delineated form and an approximately 1 mm dimension; they project slightly into the vitreous and sometimes deflect retinal vessels
abnormal increase in retinal thickness i: Abnormal increase in retinal thickness in the macular area observed on fundoscopy or fundus imaging
drusen (singular, 'druse') are tiny yell: Drusen (singular, 'druse') are tiny yellow or white accumulations of extracellular material (lipofuscin) that build up in Bruch's membrane of the eye. This class refers to the presence of Drusen in the macula
splitting of the neuroretinal layers of: Splitting of the neuroretinal layers of the retina
clumped pigmentary changes of nummular a: Clumped pigmentary changes of nummular appearance (i.e., thought to resemble the shape of a coin or multiple coins stuck together) at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium
moderate reduction of the ability to see: Moderate reduction of the ability to see defined as visual acuity less than 6/18 (20/60 in US notation; 0.5 in decimal notation) but at least 6/60 (20/200 in US notation; 0.1 in decimal notation)
a diminution of the peripheral visual fi: A diminution of the peripheral visual field whereby at least 50 degrees of central field are preserved in all meridians
peripheral visual field constriction wit: Peripheral visual field constriction with 20-49 degrees binocular visual field preserved
Peripheral visual field constriction with 10-19 degrees central field preserved
Peripheral visual field constriction with <10 degrees central field preserved
abnormal result of a test designed to te: Abnormal result of a test designed to test an individual's central and peripheral vision by determining the ability of the individual to perceive objects at differing locations of the visual field
fundus autofluorescence (faf) is a non-i: Fundus autofluorescence (FAF) is a non-invasive retinal imaging modality used in clinical practice to provide a density map of lipofuscin, the predominant ocular fluorophore, in the retinal pigment epithelium. Autofluorescent patterns result from the complex interaction of fluorophores such a lipofuscin, which release an autofluorescent signal, and elements such as melanin and rhodopsin, which absorb the excitation beam and attenuate autofluorescence. Other structures such as retinal vessels and the crystalline lens may also influence autofluorescence through blocking and interference
an abnormality observed by retinal fluor: An abnormality observed by retinal fluorescein angiography, which involves the intravenous injection of fluorescein dye followed by fluorescent imaging of the fundus immediately after injection and for up to ten minutes thereafter. It can be used to study various retinal abnormalities including especially anomalies of the choroidal and retinal circulation
loss of the outer nuclear layer (photore: Loss of the outer nuclear layer (photoreceptor layer) as assessed by ocular coherence tomography
increased amount of autofluorescence in: Increased amount of autofluorescence in the macula as ascertained by fundus autofluorescence imaging
decreased amount of autofluorescence in: Decreased amount of autofluorescence in the macula as ascertained by fundus autofluorescence imaging
occult macular dystrophy is a, typically: Occult macular dystrophy is a, typically hereditary, abnormality of the macula associated with progressive foveal cone dysfunction and no apparent fundoscopic, full-field electoretinogram (ERG), or fluorescein angiogram abnormalities
retinal phenotype characterised by cone: Retinal phenotype characterised by cone photoreceptor dysfunction and preserved rod system. The abnormality is typically stationary or very slowly progressive and findings may include reduced central vision, colour vision abnormalities, nystagmus and photophobia
applies to an abnormality that is locate: Applies to an abnormality that is located close to the median plane or midline of the body or of the referenced structure
vitreous haze is the obscuration of fund: Vitreous haze is the obscuration of fundus details by vitreous cells and protein exudation
any cystic lesion associated with the um: Any cystic lesion associated with the umbilical cord
an entwining of a segment of umbilical c: An entwining of a segment of umbilical cord, usually without obstructing fetal circulation and commonly result from fetal slippage through a loop of the cord
focal dilation of the umbilical vein: Focal dilation of the umbilical vein
bleeding from the vessels of the cord wi: Bleeding from the vessels of the cord with extravasation of blood into the Wharton jelly surrounding the umbilical cord vessels
insertion of the umbilical cord within 2: Insertion of the umbilical cord within 2 cm from the placental edge
insertion of the umbilical cord into the: Insertion of the umbilical cord into the chorio-amniotic membranes of the placenta
branching of the umbilical cord before i: Branching of the umbilical cord before its insertion into the placenta
yellow-white inflammatory aggregates in: Yellow-white inflammatory aggregates in the vitreous that are found in the midvitreous and inferior periphery
the presence of inflammatory cells such: The presence of inflammatory cells such as lymphocytes and macrophages in the vitreous
vestigial vitreous gel occupying the imm: Vestigial vitreous gel occupying the immediate retrolental space and minimal to no discernable gel in the central vitreous cavity, giving the appearance of an empty vitreous cavity
weakness of the inferior portion of the: Weakness of the inferior portion of the rectus abdominal muscle, which is ascertained clinically as follows. When a patient sits up or raises the head from a recumbent position, the umbilicus is displaced toward the head. This is the result of paralysis of the inferior portion of the rectus abdominal muscle, so that the upper fibres predominate pulling upwards the umbilicus
rubral tremor is characterized by a slow: Rubral tremor is characterized by a slow coarse tremor at rest that is exacerbated by postural adjustments and by guided voluntary movements
in wound repair, neovascularization (nv): In wound repair, neovascularization (NV) involves the sprouting of new vessels from pre-existent vessels to repair or replace damaged vessels. In the retina, NV is a response to ischemia. The NV adheres to the inner surface of the retina and outer surface of the vitreous. NV are deficient in tight junctions and hence leak plasma into surrounding tissue including the vitreous. Plasma causes the vitreous gel to degenerate, contract, and eventually collapse which pulls on the retina. Since retinal NV is adherent to both retina and vitreous, as the vitreous contracts the NV may be sheared resulting in vitreous hemorrhage or the NV may remain intact and pull the retina with the vitreous resulting in retinal elevation referred to as traction retinal detachment
a type of retinal neovascularization tha: A type of retinal neovascularization that affects the periphery of the retina
a cyst that is localized in the region o: A cyst that is localized in the region of the orbit and exhibits an epithelial lining with a keratin-filled lumen. Hair follicles are one of the adnexal structures that are commonly found in walls of dermoid cysts
a structural anomaly of the adjacent str: A structural anomaly of the adjacent structures (i.e., adnexa) of the eye, defined as the lacrimal apparatus, the extraocular muscles and the eyelids, eyelashes, eyebrows and the conjunctiva
a hamartoma (disordered proliferation of: A hamartoma (disordered proliferation of mature tissues) which can originate from any tissue of the orbital region
any anomaly of the ring of fibrous tissu: Any anomaly of the ring of fibrous tissue that surrounds the optic nerve at its entrance at the apex of the orbit. The common tendinous ring, also know as the annulus of Zinn or annular tendon, is the origin for five of the seven extraocular muscles
the presence of small, white vitreous op: The presence of small, white vitreous opacities consisting of calcium phosphate and complex, layered lipid deposits
a form of vitreoretinopathy characterize: A form of vitreoretinopathy characterized by thinning (erosion) of the retinal pigment epithelium that permits increased visualization of the choroidal vessels
chronic loss of joint motion of the prox: Chronic loss of joint motion of the proximal interphalangeal joint of the 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and 5th fingers due to structural changes in non-bony tissue
sharp pointed superior portion of the ea: Sharp pointed superior portion of the ear, with variable overfolding of the helix
a congenital auricular deformity, which: A congenital auricular deformity, which is mainly characterized by a bulging appearance of the anterosuperior portion of the auricle, a convexly protruded cavum conchae, and a slit-like narrowing of the orifice of the external auditory meatus
a hypopigmented spot in the shape of a l: A hypopigmented spot in the shape of a leaf from the mountain ash tree
any structural anomaly of the heart and: Any structural anomaly of the heart and great vessels
any structural anomaly of the muscular c: Any structural anomaly of the muscular columns which project from the inner surface of the right and left ventricles of the heart (cardiac trabeculae, trabeculae carneae)
left ventricular noncompaction (lvnc) is: Left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC) is defined by 3 markers: prominent left ventricular (LV) trabeculae, deep intertrabecular recesses, and the thin compacted layer
inflammation of the vagina that can resu: Inflammation of the vagina that can result from a spectrum of conditions that cause vaginal and sometimes vulvar symptoms, such as itching, burning, irritation, odor, and vaginal discharge
a deviation from the normal circulating: A deviation from the normal circulating concentration of adiponectin, a 30-kDa complement C1-related protein that is the most abundant secreted protein expressed in adipose tissue, and that plays a crucial role in the regulation of insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism
a reduced circulating concentration of a: A reduced circulating concentration of adiponectin, a 30-kDa complement C1-related protein that is the most abundant secreted protein expressed in adipose tissue
an elevated circulating concentration of: An elevated circulating concentration of adiponectin, a 30-kDa complement C1-related protein that is the most abundant secreted protein expressed in adipose tissue
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of glucagon in the blood circulation
an elevated concentration of glucagon in: An elevated concentration of glucagon in the blood circulation
a reduced concentration of glucagon in t: A reduced concentration of glucagon in the blood circulation
a fissure in the gingiva (gums), i.e., t: A fissure in the gingiva (gums), i.e., the mucosal tissue that lies over the mandible and maxilla
a benign or malignant neoplasm that aris: A benign or malignant neoplasm that arises from or metastasizes to the brain
a benign or malignant neoplasm that occu: A benign or malignant neoplasm that occurs within the intracranial cavity above the tentorium cerebelli
a ranula is a mucocele that occurs in th: A ranula is a mucocele that occurs in the floor of the mouth and usually involve the major salivary glands. Specifically, the ranula originates in the body of the sublingual gland, in the ducts of the sublingual gland, in the Wharton's duct of the submandibular gland or infrequently from the minor salivary glands at this location
lack of development of one lung: Lack of development of one lung
the severe form of a neural tube defect: The severe form of a neural tube defect where the open neural tube appears as a flattened, plate-like mass of nervous tissue with no overlying membrane
myelocystocele is characterized by a lar: Myelocystocele is characterized by a large, ependyma-lined, cystic dilation of the caudal end of the central canal of the spinal cord; it projects dorsally through a lamina defect, with overlying varying amounts of lipomatous subcutaneous tissue. Myelocystoceles are associated with a tethered cord and meningocele, which communicates with the spinal subarachnoid space, but not with the central canal cyst
a form of closed neural tube defect in w: A form of closed neural tube defect in which the spinal tissue lies within the spinal cord having a junction between the spinal cord and the lipoma. Intact skin covers the defect. Neurologic findings first appear during the second year of life
distention of the vagina caused by accum: Distention of the vagina caused by accumulation of fluid due to congenital vaginal obstruction
adhesions or scar tissue that form insid: Adhesions or scar tissue that form inside the cavity of the uterus
gross dilatation of the vein of galen, b: Gross dilatation of the vein of Galen, being fed by large anomalous vessel or vessels arising from the carotid or basilar circulation
a large maternal clot that separates the: A large maternal clot that separates the chorionic plate from the villous chorion
a developmental anomaly characterised by: A developmental anomaly characterised by focal obliteration of the proximal segment of a bronchus. The bronchial pattern is entirely normal distal to the site of stenosis
partial or complete absence of the flat: Partial or complete absence of the flat bones of the cranial vault. The condition is frequently, though not always, associated with anencephaly
peritonitis caused by intrauterine intes: Peritonitis caused by intrauterine intestinal rupture and spillage of fetal meconium into the fetal peritoneal cavity. Intra-peritoneal meconium usually calcifies, sometimes within 24 hours. Ultrasound findings may include intraabdominal calcifications
increase in size of the right atrium: Increase in size of the right atrium
a form of agenesis of the tricuspid valv: A form of agenesis of the tricuspid valve in which (although the normal orifice between the right atrium and right ventricle exists) there is no tricuspid valvular tissue
cyst on the surface of the placenta cons: Cyst on the surface of the placenta consisting of amnion and chorion
ectopic liver is a rare developmental an: Ectopic liver is a rare developmental anomaly in which liver tissue is situated outside the liver. Thus, ectopic liver refers to autonomous islands of normal liver parenchyma located outside the liver. The term ectopic liver is also used, to include liver appendices attached to the native liver by a thin stalk although being fully separated from the latter
dilation and elongation of the penile ur: Dilation and elongation of the penile urethra associated with absence or hypoplasia of the corpora spongiosa and cavernosa
a fluid-filled sac (cyst) located within: A fluid-filled sac (cyst) located within the central nervous system
the neurenteric cyst is a rare lesion co: The neurenteric cyst is a rare lesion composed of heterotopic endodermal tissue. During the third week of human embryogenesis, the neurenteric canal unites the yolk sac and the amniotic cavity as it traverses the primitive notochordal plate. Persistence of the normally transient neurenteric canal prevents appropriate separation of endoderm and notochord. This results in a variable degree of communication between neural and enteric epithelium
a neurenteric cyst located in the spine: A neurenteric cyst located in the spine
a neurenteric cyst located within the sk: A neurenteric cyst located within the skull
partial absence of a free limb (excludin: Partial absence of a free limb (excluding girdle). It can refer to the proximal, middle or distal segment of the upper or lower limb. The deficiency may be transverse or longitudinal. Thus, meromelia is a lack of a part, but not all, of one or more limbs with the presence of a hand or foot
a herniation of meninges through a conge: A herniation of meninges through a congenital bone defect in the skull at the junction of the frontal and ethmoidal bones
A herniation of meninges through a congenital bone defect in the skull in the parietal region
a malignant tumor arising from epithelia: A malignant tumor arising from epithelial cells. Carcinomas that arise from glandular epithelium are called adenocarcinomas, those that arise from squamous epithelium are called squamous cell carcinomas, and those that arise from transitional epithelium are called transitional cell carcinomas (NCI Thesaurus)
a congenital malformation of the tricusp: A congenital malformation of the tricuspid valve characterized by leaflet deformation
an abdominal wall defected related to a: An abdominal wall defected related to a developmental anomaly of the allantois, which is an embryonic structure that develops as a diverticulum off the yolk sac at about 16 days post fertilization. During further development, the allantois becomes incorporated into the body of the embryo, connecting the ventral aspect of the urogenital sinus (which will develop into the upper pole of the urinary bladder) to the external portion of the umbilicus. Upon further development, the lumen of the allantois becomes obliterated and forms a thick fibrous cord called the urachus, which connects the apex of the bladder to the umbilicus. In adults, the urachus is known as the median umbilical ligament. Failure of the allantoic cavity to obliterate can result of one of four conditions: 1) congenital patent urachus (a completely open connection between bladder and umbilicus); 2) vesicourachal diverticulum (a diverticulum off the bladder but not communicating with the umbilicus); umbilical cyst and sinus (not communicating with the bladder); and 4) alternating urachal sinus. An abdominal wall defect can be associated with a urachal cyst
blockage at the level of the bladder and: Blockage at the level of the bladder and the ureter caused by stenosis of the ureteral valves or failure of a narrow juxtavesical ureteral segment to dilate due to segmented fibrosis or localized absence of muscle
a teratoma arising in the sacro-coccygea: A teratoma arising in the sacro-coccygeal region
a type of sacrococcygeal teratoma that i: A type of sacrococcygeal teratoma that is predominantly external and projects from the sacrococcygeal region and presents with distortion of the buttocks
A type of sacrococcygeal teratoma that is predominantly external but has a large intrapelvic component
A type of sacrococcygeal teratoma that is predominantly intrapelvic with a small external, buttock mass
an accessory (not normally present) musc: An accessory (not normally present) muscle bundle in the right ventricle which obstructs the right ventricular outflow tract
a teratoma located within the mediastinu: A teratoma located within the mediastinum (the cavity between the pleural sacs that contains the heart and all of the thoracic viscera except the lungs)
this anomaly, also known as mittendorf d: This anomaly, also known as Mittendorf dot, is a benign, nonprogressive recognizable lesion that does not cause visual impairment. However, it can resemble a pathological congenital or acquired cataract lesion which may enlarge and cause visual impairment. The dot appears as a black speck that ranges in size from the dot made by a sharp pencil point to the size of a poppy seed. It is usually well defined, although occasionally there may be irregular, fine lines radiating outward from the dot
persistanced of a posterior remnant of t: Persistanced of a posterior remnant of the hyaloid artery located at the optic disc
a type of persistance of the hyaloid vas: A type of persistance of the hyaloid vascular system associated with a retrolental mass that may lead to fetal cataract
a saccular or fusiform dilation and elon: A saccular or fusiform dilation and elongation of the ductus arteriosus
bleeding into the ventricles of the brai: Bleeding into the ventricles of the brain
intraventricular hemorrhage that occurs: Intraventricular hemorrhage that occurs in a premature infant
Intraventricular hemorrhage that occurs in a preterm infant and that is restricted to subependymal region/germline matrix which is seen in the caudothalamic groove
Intraventricular hemorrhage that occurs in a preterm infant and that has extension into normal-sized ventricles and typically fills less than 50% of the volume of the ventricle
Intraventricular hemorrhage that occurs in a preterm infant and that has extension into dilated ventricles
Intraventricular hemorrhage that occurs in a preterm infant and that shows parenchymal extension
a nasolacrimal duct obstruction presenti: A nasolacrimal duct obstruction presenting as a grey-blue cystic swelling just below the medial canthus. Believed to be a result of concomitant upper obstruction of the Rosenmuller valve and lower obstruction of the Hasner valve
loss of one twin occurring after midgest: Loss of one twin occurring after midgestation (17 weeks gestation)
a swelling formed at the base of umbilic: A swelling formed at the base of umbilicus associated with a patent urachus which results from an allantoic remnant. The urachus is a fibrous remnant of the allantois which communicates from the apex of the urinary bladder to the umbilicus. Failed obliteration of the urachus can lead to various abnormalities: urachal cyst, urachal diverticulum, sinus or patent urachus - the most common type. Allantoic cysts in infants with patent urachus can be formed due to the drainage of urine into the umbilical cord, or in uncommon situations, after leakage of hypo-osmotic urine into the Wharton's jelly
a teratoma located in the craniofacial r: A teratoma located in the craniofacial region
reddish, brown opalescent discoloration: Reddish, brown opalescent discoloration of teeth in normal light
a pocket of pus located within a region: A pocket of pus located within a region of a tooth
a tooth abscess that occurs at the tip o: A tooth abscess that occurs at the tip of the root (apex) of a tooth
An increase in mean adipocyte cell size. This feature can be measured by determining the average cell diameter of adipocytes microscopically using abdominal subcutaneous adipose tissue obtained by biopsy
renal fibrosis is the consequence of an: Renal fibrosis is the consequence of an excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix that occurs in virtually every type of chronic kidney disease
dissolution or attenuation of mesangial: Dissolution or attenuation of mesangial matrix and degeneration of mesangial cells. In essence, mesangiolysis is an injurious process which affects the glomerular mesangium without causing obvious damage to the capillary basement membranes. The matrix swells, loosens, and eventually dissolves; the mesangial cells may show only edema and vacuolization, or may undergo severe degeneration and necrosis
a sheet like projection that can result: A sheet like projection that can result from uterine synechiae that has been encompassed by the expanding chorion and amnion
brown or blue-gray discoloration of the: Brown or blue-gray discoloration of the skin tha can present on the axillary and inguinal areas, face, palms or soles. In addition, blue-black discoloration can be apparent on skin overlying cartilage in which the pigment is deposited, such as the ears. This is a characteristic manifestation of alkaptonuria, which is an autosomal recessively inherited deficiency of homogentisic acid oxidase that results in accumulation of homogentisic acid in collagenous structures. The sclerae are also typically involved
episodes of intense fear, screaming and: Episodes of intense fear, screaming and flailing although affected individuals are still asleep
pain in the ear can be a consequence of: Pain in the ear can be a consequence of otologic disease (primary or otogenic otalgia), or can arise from pathologic processes and structures other than the ear (secondary or referred otalgia)
epignathus is a teratoma originating fro: Epignathus is a teratoma originating from the upper jaw, usually connected with the sphenoid bone or hard palate
a malformation of the neural tube with a: A malformation of the neural tube with a large amount of protruding brain tissue and absence of calvarium
a neural tube defect in which both the b: A neural tube defect in which both the brain and spinal cord remain open to varying degrees
mallet finger refers to a condition in w: Mallet finger refers to a condition in which the end joint of a finger bends but will not straighten by itself. In this situation, the joint can be pushed straight but will not hold that position on its own
proximal femoral focal deficiency is a d: Proximal femoral focal deficiency is a deformity manifested by hypoplasia of a variable portion of the femur with shortening of the entire limb
an abnormality of conjugate lateral gaze: An abnormality of conjugate lateral gaze in which the affected eye shows impairment of adduction. The pathognomonic clinical sign of internuclear ophthalmoplegia is an impaired adduction while testing horizontal saccades on the side of the lesion in the ipsilateral medial longitudinal fascicule
the mitochondrial matrix refers to the s: The mitochondrial matrix refers to the substance occupying the space enclosed by the inner membrane of a mitochondrion, which contains enzymes, DNA, granules, and inclusions of protein crystals, glycogen, and lipid. Mitochondrial swelling refers to an increase in size of the mitochondrial matrix. This phenomenon is thought to be related to a permeabilized inner membrane that originates a large swelling in the mitochondrial matrix. Mitochondrial swelling may distend the outer membrane until it ruptures
an abnormal magnetic resonance tomograph: An abnormal magnetic resonance tomography signal from a vertebral endplate according to a widely used classification published by Dr. Michael Modic

An abnormal magnetic resonance tomography signal from a vertebral endplate with a low signal on T1-weighted sequences and high signal on T2-weighted sequences. Modic type I changes are thought to represent bone marrow edema and inflammation

An abnormal magnetic resonance tomography signal from a vertebral endplate with a high signal on T1-weighted sequences and high- or isointense signal on T2 sequences. Modic type II signals are thought to indicate fatty replacement in the bone marrow

An abnormal magnetic resonance tomography signal from a vertebral endplate with a low signal on T1 and T2-weighted sequences. Modif type III signals are thought to correspond to subchondral sclerosis seen on plain radiographs
ethmocephaly is the rarest form of holop: Ethmocephaly is the rarest form of holoprosencephaly, which occurs due to an incomplete cleavage of the forebrain. Clinically, the disease presents with a proboscis, hypotelorism, microphthalmos and malformed ears
an anomaly of the protein c anticoagulan: An anomaly of the protein C anticoagulant pathway, which serves as a major system for controlling thrombosis, limiting inflammatory responses, and potentially decreasing endothelial cell apoptosis in response to inflammatory cytokines and ischemia. A natural anticoagulant system denoted the protein C pathway exerts its anticoagulant effect by regulating the activity of FVIIIa and FVa. The vitamin K-dependent protein C is the key component of the pathway. Activated protein C (APC) cleaves and inhibits coagulation cofactors FVIIIa and FVa, which result in downregulation of the activity of the coagulation system. The endothelial protein C receptor stimulates the T-TM-mediated activation of protein C on the endothelial cell surface. The two cofactors, protein S and the intact form of FV, enhance the anticoagulant activity of APC
a higher than normal levels of the fatty: A higher than normal levels of the fatty acids which can occur in plasma as a result of lipolysis in adipose tissue or when plasma triacyglycerols are taken into tissues
an abnormal amount of any of the interle: An abnormal amount of any of the interleukins, a class of cytokines, in the circulation
an increased concentration of interleuki: An increased concentration of interleukin-6 in the circulation
an inability to name people and objects: An inability to name people and objects that are correctly perceived. The individual is able to describe the object in question, but cannot provide the name
a lymphangioma (congenital malformation: A lymphangioma (congenital malformation consisting of focal proliferations of well-differentiated lymphatic tissue in multi cystic or sponge like structures) located within the mediastinum, i.e., the central compartment of the thoracic cavity that is surrounded by loose connective tissue. Mediastinal lymphangioma is a slow growing mass with benign features, and accounts for 1% of all mediastinal tumors
any anomaly of the cerumen (ear wax), th: Any anomaly of the cerumen (ear wax), the yellowish waxy substance secreted in the ear canal
blockage of the external auditory canal: Blockage of the external auditory canal by a buildup of earwax
an anomolous earwax color. earwax (cerum: An anomolous earwax color. Earwax (cerumen) is usually light to dark brown or orange in color
a structural anomaly of the jaw, the bon: A structural anomaly of the jaw, the bony structure of the mouth that consists of the mandible and the maxilla
a tumor originating in the jaw (mandible: A tumor originating in the jaw (mandible or maxilla)
abnormal enlargement in the upper jaw (m: Abnormal enlargement in the upper jaw (maxilla) or in the lower jaw (mandible)
an anomolous circulating concentration o: An anomolous circulating concentration of the connecting (C) peptide, which links the insulin A and B chains in proinsulin, providing thereby a means to promote their efficient folding and assembly in the endoplasmic reticulum during insulin biosynthesis. After cleavage of proinsulin, C-peptide is stored with insulin in the soluble phase of the secretory granules and is subsequently released in equimolar amounts with insulin, providing a useful independent indicator of insulin secretion
a decreased concentration of c-peptide i: A decreased concentration of C-peptide in the circulation. Since C-peptide is secreted in equimolar amounts to insulin, this feature correlates with reduced insulin secretion
an elevated concentration of c-peptide i: An elevated concentration of C-peptide in the circulation. Since C-peptide is secreted in equimolar amounts to insulin, this feature correlates with increased insulin secretion
a diminished volume of the central part: A diminished volume of the central part of the bed nucleus of the stria terminalis
the stria terminalis is a slender, compa: The stria terminalis is a slender, compact fiber bundle that connects the amygdala (amygdaloid body) with the hypothalamus and other basal forebrain regions. The bed nucleus of the stria terminalis is a limbic forebrain structure that receives heavy projections from, among other areas, the basolateral amygdala, and projects in turn to hypothalamic and brainstem target areas that mediate many of the autonomic and behavioral responses to aversive or threatening stimuli. This term refers to an anomaly of the bed nucleus
scaphocephaly is a subtype of dolichocep: Scaphocephaly is a subtype of dolichocephaly where the anterior and posterior aspects of the cranial vault are pointed (boat-shaped). Scaphocephaly is caused by a precocious fusion of sagittal suture without other associated synostosis
an anomaly in the process of visual acco: An anomaly in the process of visual accommodation, which is the process of adjustment of the eye to enable sharp vision of objects at different distances. Accommodation is mediated by contraction of the ciliary muscles, which alter the convexity of the lens and, consequently, its refractive power
a decreased ability of the eye to adjust: A decreased ability of the eye to adjust and thereby enable sharp vision of objects at different distances
inferior malposition of the lower eyelid: Inferior malposition of the lower eyelid margin without eyelid eversion
excessive longitudinal ridging that give: Excessive longitudinal ridging that gives the surface of the nail plate a rough appearance. It results from multiple foci of defective keratinization of the proximal nail matrix
lack of the lunula at the base of a nail: Lack of the lunula at the base of a nail. The lunula is the crescent-shaped whitish area of the bed of a fingernail or toenail
nails whose growth is quicker than norma: Nails whose growth is quicker than normal
nail whose growth pattern or speed devia: Nail whose growth pattern or speed deviates from normal
the cuticle (properly known as the epony: The cuticle (properly known as the eponychium, or the medial nail fold or the proximal nail fold), is the thickened layer of skin surrounding fingernails and toenails. Its function is to protect the area between the nail and epidermis from exposure to bacteria. This term refers to the presence of and irregular edge or outline of the cuticle
any structural anomaly of the tongue: Any structural anomaly of the tongue
any functional anomaly of the tongue: Any functional anomaly of the tongue
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the tongue
increase in size of the tonsils, small c: Increase in size of the tonsils, small collections of lymphoid tissue facing into the aerodigestive tract on either side of the back part of the throat
a phenomenon of orange colored oral tons: A phenomenon of orange colored oral tonsils. This feature is characteristic of Tangier disease and illustrated will by Figure 1 of PMID:19470903
a lipoma localized to the tongue. may pr: A lipoma localized to the tongue. May present as a nontender, soft, spherical mass of the tongue
the loss of gum tissue. the result is th: The loss of gum tissue. The result is that gum tissue is recessed and its position on the tooth is lowered, exposing the roots of the teeth
severe nail curvature, causing the tip o: Severe nail curvature, causing the tip of the nail to point downwards with respect to the axis of the finger. Beaked nails are caused by resorption of the distal digit
the presense of a depressed line (\"cana: The presense of a depressed line ("canal") in the center of the nail
nails that slope upward at the free edge: Nails that slope upward at the free edge
eyelid partly covered by skin when eyes: Eyelid partly covered by skin when eyes are open
lower eyelid partly covered by skin when: Lower eyelid partly covered by skin when eyes are open
upper eyelid partly covered by skin when: Upper eyelid partly covered by skin when eyes are open
increased dimension of the sclera in the: Increased dimension of the sclera in the anterior-posterior axis
change in the color of the fundus from r: Change in the color of the fundus from red in the dark-adapted state to golden immediately or shortly after the onset of the light. The color of the fundus reflex in the light adapted state has also been described as golden-yellow, gray-white, and yellow-white. This reflex can appear either homogeneous or in streaks in the fundus. The retinal vessels appear to be protruding in contrast to the radiant background. Dark adaptation leads to disappearance of the unusual fundus coloration [Digital Journal of Ophthalmology 2008; Volume 14, Number 14]
lack of the foveal reflex, which normall: Lack of the foveal reflex, which normally occurs as a result of the reflection of light from the ophthalmoscope in the foveal pit upon examination. The foveal reflex is a bright pinpoint of light that is observed to move sideways or up and down in response to movement of the opthalmoscope
tiny, repetitive muscle contractions in: Tiny, repetitive muscle contractions in the eyelids, causing the appearance of twitching
a high-pitched whistling sound associate: A high-pitched whistling sound associated with labored breathing
an anomalous (adventitious) sound produc: An anomalous (adventitious) sound produced by the breathing process
crackles are discontinuous, explosive, a: Crackles are discontinuous, explosive, and nonmusical adventitious lung sounds normally heard in inspiration and sometimes during expiration. Crackles are usually classified as fine and coarse crackles based on their duration, loudness, pitch, timing in the respiratory cycle, and relationship to coughing and changing body position
abnormal breath sounds characterized by: Abnormal breath sounds characterized by low-pitched, snoring or rattle-like sounds
fiber- or rope-like opacities located wi: Fiber- or rope-like opacities located within the vitreous humor
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the neck
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the shoulder
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the elbow
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the wrist
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the finger
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the hip
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the knee
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the ankle
An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the toe
incidents in which a piece of food or ot: Incidents in which a piece of food or other objects get stuck in the upper airway and provoke coughing, gagging, inability to talk, and difficulty breathing
extracellular deposition in cardiac tiss: Extracellular deposition in cardiac tissue of a proteinaceous material that, when stained with Congo red, demonstrates apple-green birefringence under polarized light and that has a distinct color when stained with sulfated Alcian blue. Viewed with electron microscopy, the amyloid deposits are seen to be composed of a beta-sheet fibrillar material. These nonbranching fibrils have a diameter of 7.5 to 10 nm and are the result of protein misfolding
absent response to a pattern electroreti: Absent response to a pattern electroretinogram (PERG)
heliotrope rash is a violaceous discolor: Heliotrope rash is a violaceous discoloration of the eyelids associated with periorbital edema
an anomaly of the jugular venous pressur: An anomaly of the jugular venous pressure.
