histoplasmosis infection involving multi: Histoplasmosis infection involving multiple sites of the body. Disseminated histoplasmosis can involve various organs, including reticuloendothelial organs, gastrointestinal tract, adrenal glands, central nervous system, endovascular structures, kidney, and skin. It typically presents with systemic symptoms like fever, generalized fatigue, night sweats, weight loss, and the symptoms related to the specific organ involved. Severe disseminated disease can manifest as septic shock, multi organ failure, and ARDS
infection of the lungs with histoplasma: Infection of the lungs with Histoplasma capsulatum. Symptoms may include fever, headache, weakness, chest pain and dry cough. When imaging is done, chest radiographs may show patchy pneumonia involving one or more lobes with adenopathy of the mediastinum or hilum
the term tinea means fungal infection, w: The term tinea means fungal infection, whereas dermatophyte refers to the fungal organisms that cause tinea. This term refers to a tinea infection that is chronic or recalcitrant to treatment and may be reflective of an immune defect
an infection that is caused by a bacteri: An infection that is caused by a bacterium that would generally not be able to cause an infection in a host with a normal immune system. Such bacteria take advantage of the opportunity, so to speak, that is provided by a weakened immune system
an infection of the lung caused by envir: An infection of the lung caused by environmental mycobacteria. SUch infections can occur in individuals with predisposing lung disease or immune disease
a lung infection by mycobacterium tuberc: A lung infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis a slightly curved non-motile, aerobic, non-capsulated and non-spore forming strains of mycobacteria
abnormal increase in blood pressure. an: Abnormal increase in blood pressure. An individual measurement of increased blood pressure does not necessarily imply hypertension. In practical terms, multiple measurements are recommended to diagnose the presence of hypertension
the presence of autoantibodies (immunogl: The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the serum that react against the NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate)-type glutamate receptor
the presence of an antibody in the cereb: The presence of an antibody in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) that is directed against the organism's own cells or tissues
the presence of autoantibodies (immunogl: The presence of autoantibodies (immunoglobulins) in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) that react against the NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate)-type glutamate receptor
this sign is created by a nonenhancing t: This sign is created by a nonenhancing thrombus in the dural sinus surrounded by triangular enhancing dura as seen on cross-section. The sign, seen on contrast-enhanced CT scan images, suggests dural sinovenous thrombosis. It is best seen on wider window settings. It is a reliable sign of sinus thrombosis but is seen only in 25-30% of these cases
this sign represents thickening and enha: This sign represents thickening and enhancement of the dura mater in continuity with a mass, which on MR images, gives the appearance of a tail arising from the mass. The dural tail is thought to represent reactive change; however, it may also be due to tumor invasion. Three criteria need to be met for a positive dural tail sign: the tail should be seen on two successive images through the tumor, it should taper away from the tumor, and it must enhance more than the tumor
a tram-track sign is composed of two enh: A tram-track sign is composed of two enhancing areas of tumor separated from each other by the negative defect of the optic nerve. It is seen on contrast-enhanced CT scan and MRI images, in optic nerve sheath meningioma. The sign helps distinguish between optic nerve sheath meningioma and optic glioma. Optic glioma arises from glial cells within the optic nerve and there is no clear separation between the nerve and the tumor; hence the tram-track sign is not seen in optic gliomas. Calcification may be seen in optic nerve sheath meningiomas in 20-50% of cases and hence the tram-track sign may be seen on nonenhanced CT scan images as a linear calcification around the nerve, but this is less common
a type of tubercular infection located o: A type of tubercular infection located outside of the lung, which is the most common location of tuberculosis. There are two types of clinical manifestation of tuberculosis (TB) are pulmonary TB (PTB) and extrapulmonary TB (EPTB). The former is most common. EPTB refers to TB involving organs other than the lungs (e.g., pleura, lymph nodes, abdomen, genitourinary tract, skin, joints and bones, or meninges). A patient with both pulmonary and EPTB is classified as a case of PTB
elevated n-acetylaspartic acid (naa) in: Elevated N-acetylaspartic acid (NAA) in urine. This feature can be measured using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry
an abnormally increased concentration of: An abnormally increased concentration of N-Acetylaspartic acid in the blood circulation
An abnormally increased concentration of N-Acetylaspartic acid in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
repeated episodes of a localized, painfu: Repeated episodes of a localized, painful cutaneous eruption related to reactivation of varicella zoster virus (VZV) and characterized by a characteristic rash in one or two adjacent dermatomes
abnormally increased prominence of the f: Abnormally increased prominence of the fat pad underneath the heal. This feature can be appreciated in figure 1 of PMID:26769062
an increase in the level of 2-hydroxyglu: An increase in the level of 2-hydroxyglutaric acid in the urine
deviation from the normal quantity of ba: Deviation from the normal quantity of base excess, defined as the amount of strong acid (in millimoles per liter) that needs to be added in vitro to 1 liter of fully oxygenated whole blood to return a blood sample to standard conditions (pH of 7.40, Pco2 of 40 mm Hg, and temperature of 37 degrees C)
an inflammatory process in skin caused b: An inflammatory process in skin caused by an exogenous agent that directly or indirectly injure the skin. If the offending agent is identified and removed, the eruption will resolve. An unusual or patterned eruption may be a clue to the presence of a contact dermatitis. Patch testing may be helpful in the differential diagnosis
an infection with nontuberculous mycobac: An infection with nontuberculous mycobacteria that affects multiple body sites. Such infections can occur in individuals with immune disease
ultra-low vision but with retained abili: Ultra-low vision but with retained ability to identify a moving object (typically hand motion at distance of 30 cm)

Ultra-low vision but with retained ability to perceive the difference between light and dark. Also when light is projected in each of the four quadrants of the visual field, the individual is able to correctly identify the origin of the light stimulus
Ultra-low vision but with retained ability to perceive the difference between light and dark
lack of skin pigmentation (coloring) of: Lack of skin pigmentation (coloring) of the chest
abnormal keratinization of the epidermal: Abnormal keratinization of the epidermal stratum coreum (horny layer) with increased keratin formation, preservation of the nuclei in the superficial cells, and absence of the stratum granulosum
elevated rate of chromosomal breakage or: Elevated rate of chromosomal breakage or interchanges occurring either spontaneously or following exposure to various DNA-damaging agents. This feature may be assayed by treatment of cultured lymphocytes with agents such as chemical mutagens, irradiation, and alkylating agents
thickening of the retina that takes plac: Thickening of the retina that takes place due to accumulation of fluid in the macula as a nonspecific response to blood-retinal barrier breakdown. Macular edema is a common pathological response to a wide variety of ocular insults, most commonly after intraocular (e.g. cataract) surgery or in association with retinal vascular (e.g. diabetic eye disease, retinal vein occlusion) or inflammatory (e.g. uveitis) disease
a conspicuously happy disposition with f: A conspicuously happy disposition with frequent smiling and laughing that may be context-inappropriate or unrelated to context
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of folate in the blood circulation
any abnormality in the total number of l: Any abnormality in the total number of lymphocytes in the blood
any deviation from the normal overall co: Any deviation from the normal overall count of natural killer (NK) cells in the circulation or a deviation from the normal distribution of NK cell subtypes
an abnormality of the tympanic membrane: An abnormality of the tympanic membrane
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the outer ear
A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the inner ear
A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the ceruminal gland
an abnormality in the reflexive contract: An abnormality in the reflexive contraction of the middle-ear muscles in response to sound stimulation
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of cobalamin (vitamin B12) in the blood. Vitamin B12 is one of the eight B vitamins
an abnormality of the composition of swe: An abnormality of the composition of sweat or the levels of its components
a deviation from the normal circulating: A deviation from the normal circulating concentration of ferritin. Ferritin concentration can be measured in serum or plasma
any abnormality in the serum transferrin: Any abnormality in the serum transferrin saturation, which is calculated by dividing the serum iron level by total iron-binding capacity
a type of acne in which open and closed: A type of acne in which open and closed comedones comprise the majority of the lesions, with substantially fewer papules and pustules
multiple subcutaneous non-fragile and sk: Multiple subcutaneous non-fragile and skin-coloured papules characterized by interstitial infiltrate of spindle and epithelioid histiocytes, and mucin. There are well circumscribed aggregates of epithelioid histiocytes and mucin in the upper and middle dermis,with the histiocytes arranged between collagen bundles and separated from the epidermis by a Grenz zone
yellow nodules of lipoid material are de: Yellow nodules of lipoid material are deposited in the skin and mucosae. This gives rise to granulomatous reactions
decreased activity of the reaction 5-oxo: Decreased activity of the reaction 5-oxo-L-proline + ATP + 2 H(2)O = L-glutamate + ADP + 2 H(+) + phosphate
os odontoideum is classified into two an: Os odontoideum is classified into two anatomic types (orthotopic and dystopic). Os odontoideum is defined as an ossicle that consists of smooth and separate caudal portions of the odontoid process. With orthotopic os odontoideum, the ossicle moves with the anterior arch of the atlas, while the dystopic type consists of an ossicle near the basion, or one that is fused with the clivus
an increase in the level of l-2-hydroxyg: An increase in the level of L-2-hydroxyglutaric acid in the urine
a chronic skin condition involving the i: A chronic skin condition involving the inflammation of the apocrine sweat glands, forming pimple-like bumps known as abscesses
an increased amount of 3-hydroxybutyric: An increased amount of 3-hydroxybutyric acid in the urine
an increased amount of carboxylic acid i: An increased amount of carboxylic acid in the urine
formation of an anuclear keratin layer: Formation of an anuclear keratin layer
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of a phospholipid in the blood circulation
cracking, fissuring, and peeling of the: Cracking, fissuring, and peeling of the skin of the lips
inappropriate sinus tachycardia is a non: Inappropriate sinus tachycardia is a nonparoxysmal tachyarrhythmia characterized by an increased resting heart rate (HR) and/or an exaggerated HR response to minimal exertion or a change in body posture. HR is constantly above the physiological range with no appropriate relation to metabolic or physiological demands
a skin rash that is characterized by dif: A skin rash that is characterized by diffuse cutaneous erythema with areas of skin elevation. It may evolve to vesicles or papules as part of a more severe clinical entity. Different degrees of angioedema with involvement of subcutaneous tissue may also appear
systemic inflammatory response to infect: Systemic inflammatory response to infection in newborn babies
abnormal growth ossification centers in: Abnormal growth ossification centers in children. Initially a degeneration/ necrosis followed by regeneration or recalcification
refers to the loss of the outer layer of: Refers to the loss of the outer layer of the epidermis in large, scale-like flakes
decreased occipito-frontal (head) circum: Decreased occipito-frontal (head) circumference (OFC). For the microcephaly OFC must be between -3 SD and -2 SD compared to appropriate, age matched, normal standards (i.e. -3 SD <= OFC < -2 SD)
encephalomalacia is the softening or los: Encephalomalacia is the softening or loss of brain tissue after cerebral infarction, cerebral ischemia, infection, craniocerebral trauma, or other injury
the inability to maintain postures or po: The inability to maintain postures or positions (such as keeping eyes closed, protruding the tongue, maintaining conjugate gaze steadily in a fixed direction, or making a prolonged 'ah' sound) without repeated prompts
a subset of motor impersistence, defined: A subset of motor impersistence, defined as the inability to perform more than two of the simple voluntary acts simultaneously, such as closing the eyes and protruding the tongue
abnormal concentration of neopterin in t: Abnormal concentration of neopterin in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
increased concentration of neopterin in: Increased concentration of neopterin in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
decreased concentration of neopterin in: Decreased concentration of neopterin in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of neopterin in the blood circulation
abnormal concentration of biopterin in t: Abnormal concentration of biopterin in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
increased concentration of biopterin in: Increased concentration of biopterin in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
decreased concentration of biopterin in: Decreased concentration of biopterin in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
a deviation from the normal concentratio: A deviation from the normal concentration of biopterin in the blood circulation
an abnormality of the skin of the palm,: An abnormality of the skin of the palm, that is, the skin of the front of the hand
fixed sarcastic grimace and anxious expr: Fixed sarcastic grimace and anxious expression. Caused by spasms of the masseter and other facial muscles
hypopnea is referring to breathing that: Hypopnea is referring to breathing that is abnormally shallower and/or slow
an abnormal concentration of insulin in: An abnormal concentration of insulin in the body
An abnormal concentration of insulin in the blood
a decreased concentration of insulin in: A decreased concentration of insulin in the blood
an increased concentration of hemoglobin: An increased concentration of hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c), which is the product of nonenzymatic attachment of a hexose molecule to the N-terminal amino acid of the hemoglobin molecule. This reaction is dependent on blood glucose concentration, and therefore reflects the mean glucose concentration over the previous 8 to 12 weeks. The HbA1c level provides a better indication of long-term glycemic control than one-time blood or urinary glucose measurements
less than normal number of natural kille: Less than normal number of natural killer cells, a type of lymphocyte in the innate immune system with an ability to mediate cytotoxicity and produce cytokines after the ligation of a germline-encoded activation receptor
lack of natural killer cells, a type of: Lack of natural killer cells, a type of lymphocyte in the innate immune system that contains cytoplasmic granzymes, i.e., small granules with perforin and proteases that allow natural killer cells to form pores in the cell membrane of the target cell through which the granzymes and associated molecules can enter, inducing apoptosis
an increased tendency towards thrombosis: An increased tendency towards thrombosis in the mother during a pregnancy
a hemorrhage occurring within the lung: A hemorrhage occurring within the lung
clincial phenotype characterized by dela: Clincial phenotype characterized by delayed bleeding accelerated break down of blood clot (fibrinolysis)
a decrease in high molecular weight von: A decrease in high molecular weight von Willebrand factor multimers
an abnormality of coagulation related to: An abnormality of coagulation related to a decreased concentration of heparin co-factor II
decrease of these levels result in incre: Decrease of these levels result in increased inhibition of fibrinolysis and reduced inhibition of coagulation
thrombomodulin is a cofactor in the thro: Thrombomodulin is a cofactor in the thrombin induced activation of Protein C. In the case of deficiency there will be less Protein C and tendency to clot
tpa catalyzes the conversion of plasimin: tPA catalyzes the conversion of plasiminogen to plasmin, and thus break down of clots. When there is a deficiency there will be an increase of thrombosis
abnormal bleeding related to a procedure: Abnormal bleeding related to a procedure or trauma which does not start at the time of the initial insult, but after delay by at least 24 hours
deficiency of factor xiii subunit a, lea: Deficiency of factor XIII subunit A, leading to a reduced factor XIII activity. Activated Factor XIII cross-links fibrin polymers solidifying the clot
deficiency of factor xiii subunit b, lea: Deficiency of factor XIII subunit B, leading to a reduced factor XIII activity. Activated Factor XIII cross-links fibrin polymers solidifying the clot
the presence of intraceullar inclusion b: The presence of intraceullar inclusion bodies (aggregates of stainable substances, usually proteins) in leukocytes
increased degradation of fibrin, associa: Increased degradation of fibrin, associated with clot instability and bleeding
impaired binding of factor viii to von w: Impaired binding of factor VIII to von Willebrand Factor. This is determined using a modified ELISA assay
an impairment of the migration of neutro: An impairment of the migration of neutrophils towards chemoattractants as part of the innate immune response
increased plasma vitamin k epoxide after: Increased plasma vitamin K epoxide after vitamin K supplementation is present in VKCFD (vitamin K-dependent clotting factor deficiency) type 2, but not in VKCFD type 1
an increased vwf propeptide to vwf antig: An increased VWF propeptide to VWF antigen indicates that deficiency of VWF is not due to impaired synthesis but due to rapid clearance. The VWF propeptide is measured by ELISA
increased platelet agglutination in resp: Increased platelet agglutination in response to low-dose ristocetin
abnormally increased length of time requ: Abnormally increased length of time required for an in vitro clot to dissolve in the absence of the normal plasmin inhibitors. This test is a clinical assay used to measure fibrinolysis. The euglobulin fraction of plasma is precipitated and used to form clot by addition of thrombin; after clot forms the rate of clot breakdown (fibrinolysis) can be monitored
increased time to coagulation in the rus: Increased time to coagulation in the Russell's viper venom assay
Reduced activity of alpha-2-antiplasmin. This protein inactivates the protease plasmin that drives fibrinolysis
reduced antithrombin antigen. a reduced: Reduced antithrombin antigen. A reduced level of antithrombin may lead to an increased risk of thrombus formation
abnormally decreased length of time requ: Abnormally decreased length of time required for an in vitro clot to dissolve in the absence of the normal plasmin inhibitors. This test is a clinical assay used to measure fibrinolysis. The euglobulin fraction of plasma is precipitated and used to form clot by addition of thrombin; after clot forms the rate of clot breakdown (fibrinolysis) can be monitored
reduced activity of plasminogen activato: Reduced activity of plasminogen activator inhibitor 1. This protein down-regulates fibrinolysis in the circulation by inhibiting the two major plasminogen activators: tissue-plasminogen activator and urokinase-plasminogen activator
reduced level of plasminogen activator i: Reduced level of plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 antigen
reduced prothrombin antigen as measured: Reduced prothrombin antigen as measured by ELISA assay. Prothrombin is a vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor that is proteolytically cleaved to form thrombin
a subtype of skin dimples occurring on t: A subtype of skin dimples occurring on the hands
absence or underdevelopment of the nasop: Absence or underdevelopment of the nasopharyngeal adenoids
a deviation in the size of nasopharyngea: A deviation in the size of nasopharyngeal adenoids
absence of the nasopharyngeal adenoids a: Absence of the nasopharyngeal adenoids as a developmental defect
an abnormal decrease in the size of naso: An abnormal decrease in the size of nasopharyngeal adenoids
an abnormal increase in the size of naso: An abnormal increase in the size of nasopharyngeal adenoids
middle ear is filled with glue-like flui: Middle ear is filled with glue-like fluid instead of air
an unpleasant sensation characterized by: An unpleasant sensation characterized by physical discomfort (such as pricking, throbbing, or aching) localized to the jaw
increased susceptibility to middle ear i: Increased susceptibility to middle ear infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of middle ear infections
an abnormal resistance to glucose, i.e.,: An abnormal resistance to glucose, i.e., a reduction in the ability to maintain glucose levels in the blood stream within normal limits following oral or intravenous administration of glucose
a region of high intensity (brightness): A region of high intensity (brightness) observed upon magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans of the spinal cord
a malignant epithelial tumor with a glan: A malignant epithelial tumor with a glandular organization that originates in the intestines
A malignant epithelial tumor with a glandular organization that originates in the small intestine
A malignant epithelial tumor with a glandular organization that originates in the large intestine
a benign tumor (adenoma) of the pituitar: A benign tumor (adenoma) of the pituitary gland
class to represent frequency of phenotyp: Class to represent frequency of phenotypic abnormalities within a patient cohort
always present, i.e. in 100% of the case: Always present, i.e. in 100% of the cases
present in 80% to 99% of the cases: Present in 80% to 99% of the cases
present in 30% to 79% of the cases: Present in 30% to 79% of the cases
present in 5% to 29% of the cases: Present in 5% to 29% of the cases
present in 1% to 4% of the cases: Present in 1% to 4% of the cases
recurrent episodes of abnormally low lev: Recurrent episodes of abnormally low levels of neutrophils in the body (neutropenia)
any deviation from the normal concentrat: Any deviation from the normal concentration of a free fatty acid in the blood circulation
an increased concentration of glycerol i: An increased concentration of glycerol in the urine
increased concentration of glycerol in t: Increased concentration of glycerol in the blood
increased desire for sexual activity on: Increased desire for sexual activity on the part of a male
reduced desire for sexual activity on th: Reduced desire for sexual activity on the part of a male
inability of a male to reach orgasm: Inability of a male to reach orgasm
an inflammatory arthritis characterized: An inflammatory arthritis characterized by purulent synovial fluid with neutrophil accumulation, but with negative cultures
normally, the female reproductive canal: Normally, the female reproductive canal is connected to the internal genitalia. "Blind vagina" denotes cases, where this canal ends in a sac
an abnormal blue color of the urine: An abnormal blue color of the urine
an abnormal red color of the urine: An abnormal red color of the urine
an abnormal dark color of the urine: An abnormal dark color of the urine
an abnormal red-brown color of the urine: An abnormal red-brown color of the urine
an abnormal dark-yellow color of the uri: An abnormal dark-yellow color of the urine
an abnormal purple color of the urine: An abnormal purple color of the urine
redness of the skin of the eyelids, caus: Redness of the skin of the eyelids, caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin
in a heliotrope rash, the color of the s: In a heliotrope rash, the color of the skin turns to violet, which is the color of the heliotrope flower
a cutaneous eruption that consists of mu: A cutaneous eruption that consists of multiple (at least two) concentric erythematous rings
an abnormal morphology of the olfactory: An abnormal morphology of the olfactory bulb (bulbus olfactorius), which is involved in olfaction, i.e. the sense of smell
an abnormal area of increased brightness: An abnormal area of increased brightness (hyperintensity) that is limited to one particular area
An abnormal area of increased brightness (hyperintensity) that occurs in several distinct areas
areas of brighter than expected mri sign: Areas of brighter than expected MRI signal in the white matter of the brain whereby individual patches run together
chronic rhinitis accompanied by pus form: Chronic rhinitis accompanied by pus formation
a neural tube defect arises when the neu: A neural tube defect arises when the neural tube, the embryonic precursor of the brain and spinal cord, fails to close during neurulation. The cranial region (anencephaly) or the low spine (open spina bifida; myelomeningocele) are most commonly affected although, in the severe NTD craniorachischisis, almost the entire neural tube remains open, from midbrain to low spine
defect or defects of the morphogenesis o: Defect or defects of the morphogenesis of the left heart identifiable at birth
reduction in the vertical distance betwe: Reduction in the vertical distance between the upper and lower eyelids
inflammation and thickening (localized f: Inflammation and thickening (localized fibrosis) of the fascia, the tissue under the skin and over the muscle, typically associated with a build up of eosinophils in the muscles and tissues
an increased amount of 3-aminoisobutyric: An increased amount of 3-aminoisobutyric acid in the urine
llymphoma that originates in the stomach: Llymphoma that originates in the stomach itself
a deviation from the normal serum concen: A deviation from the normal serum concentration/activity of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), which catalyzes the reduction of pyruvate to form lactate
a reduced level of the complement compon: A reduced level of the complement component C4 in the circulation
A reduced level of the complement component C4a in circulation
A reduced level of the complement component C4b in circulation
blood concentration of insulin like grow: Blood concentration of insulin like growth factor binding protein acid labile subunit level below normal limits
presence of an abnormal type of hemoglob: Presence of an abnormal type of hemoglobin characterized by the subsitution of a glutamic acid residue at position 7 following the initial methionine residue by a valine (the mutation causative of sickle cell disease). The mutation promotes the polymerization of the HbS under conditions of low oxygen concentration. HbS can be identified by multiple methodologies including hemoglobin electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography
an elevated concentration in the blood o: An elevated concentration in the blood of hemoglobin A2 (HbA2), which is a normal variant of hemoglobin A that consists of two alpha and two delta chains and is normally present at low levels in adults but may be increased in beta thalassemia
an abnormal amount of oxygen passes into: An abnormal amount of oxygen passes into the blood from the lungs and/or an abnormal amount of carbon dioxide passes from the blood into the lungs
any abnormality of the brachial nerve pl: Any abnormality of the brachial nerve plexus
any abnormality of the lumbosacral nerve: Any abnormality of the lumbosacral nerve plexus
a diagnostic alternating light and dark: A diagnostic alternating light and dark banding pattern under polarizing microscopy
an anomaly of the size of the testicle (: An anomaly of the size of the testicle (the male gonad)
a circumscribed, solid elevation of skin: A circumscribed, solid elevation of skin with no visible fluid, varying in size from a pinhead to less than 10mm in diameter at the widest point that is composed of localized hyperkeratosis (the latter may be demonstrated histopathologically)
des-gamma carboxyprothrombin (dcp) or pr: Des-gamma carboxyprothrombin (DCP) or pro-thrombin induced by vitamin K absence-II (PIVKA-II) is an abnormal prothrombin protein that is increased in the serum of patients with HCC. Generation of DCP is thought to be a result of an acquired defect in the post- translational carboxylation of the prothrombin precursor in malignant cells
irregularity of the normally smooth surf: Irregularity of the normally smooth surface of the distal metaphysis of the femur
any abnormality in the proportion of cd3: Any abnormality in the proportion of CD3-positive T cells relative to the total number of T cells
anomaly in the weight-to-height squared: Anomaly in the weight-to-height squared ratio, calculated by dividing the individual's weight in kilograms by the square of the individual's height in meters and used as an indicator of obesity and underweight compared to averages
abnormally decreased weight-to-height sq: Abnormally decreased weight-to-height squared ratio, calculated by dividing the individual's weight in kilograms by the square of the individual's height in meters and used as an indicator of underweight compared to averages
the ability of the knee to move past its: The ability of the knee to move past its normal range of motion, (knee hyperextension is greater than 10 degrees)
an early onset of puberty, in this case: An early onset of puberty, in this case early does not refer to precocious
malignant mesothelioma is a form of canc: Malignant mesothelioma is a form of cancer that originates from the cells of the mesothelium, a thin tissue layer surrounding the body's internal organs. Malignant mesothelioma is almost exclusively caused by asbestos exposure, pleural mesothelioma beeing the most common form, affecting the lining of the lungs called the pleura. Other forms such as perioneal-, percardial- or testicular- mesothelioma are much rarer
a malignant mesothelioma originating fro: A Malignant mesothelioma originating from cells of the pleura (the thin layer of mesothelium lining the lungs). Pleural mesothelioma is the most common form of mesothelioma

A Malignant mesothelioma originating from cells of the peritoneum (the thin layer of mesothelium lining the abdomen). Peritoneal mesothelioma is the second most common form of mesothelioma after pleural mesothelioma
A Malignant mesothelioma originating from cells of the pericardium (the thin layer of mesothelium lining the heart)
a malignant mesothelioma of the testis: A Malignant mesothelioma of the testis
a neoplasm of the central nervous system: A neoplasm of the central nervous system
a benign or malignant neoplasm (tumour): A benign or malignant neoplasm (tumour) of the peripheral nervous system
a benign nerve sheath tumor composed of: A benign nerve sheath tumor composed of Schwann cells
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the eye
A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the breast
an epiretinal membrane is a thin sheet o: An epiretinal membrane is a thin sheet of fibrous tissue that can develop on the surface of the macular area of the retina and cause a disturbance in vision. An epiretinal membrane area can develop on the thin macular area of the retin. An epiretinal membrane is also sometimes called a macular pucker, premacular fibrosis, surface wrinkling retinopathy or cellophane maculopathy
the presence of a supernumerary, i.e. th: The presence of a supernumerary, i.e. third, crus of the helix in the helix, arising at or above the normal bifurcation of the antihelix
folds of membranous tissue (peritoneum,: Folds of membranous tissue (peritoneum, mesothelium) attached to the wall of the abdomen and enclosing viscera. Examples include the mesentery for the small intestine; the transverse mesocolon, which attaches the transverse portion of the colon to the back wall of the abdomen; and the mesosigmoid, which enfolds the sigmoid portion of the colon. Cells of the same embryologic origin also surround the other organs of the body such as the lungs (pleura) or the heart (pericardium)
a cataract that affects the capsule of t: A cataract that affects the capsule of the lens
a nuclear cataract is an opacity or clou: A nuclear cataract is an opacity or clouding that develops in the lens nucleus. That is, a nuclear cataract is one that is located in the center of the lens. The nucleus tends to darken changing from clear to yellow and sometimes brown
a cataract which affects the layer of th: A cataract which affects the layer of the lens surrounding the nucleus, i.e., the lens cortex. It is identified by its unique wedge or spoke appearance
a cataract which is found in the back ou: A cataract which is found in the back outer layer of the lens. This type often develops more rapidly
cerebral palsy describes a group of perm: Cerebral palsy describes a group of permanent disorders of the development of movement and posture, causing activity limitation, that are attributed to nonprogressive disturbances that occurred in the developing fetal or infant brain. The motor disorders of cerebral palsy are often accompanied by disturbances of sensation, perception, cognition, communication, and behaviour, by epilepsy, and by secondary musculoskeletal problems
an abnormality of movement with a neurol: An abnormality of movement with a neurological basis characterized by changes in coordination and speed of voluntary movements
a type of stereotypic behavior in which: A type of stereotypic behavior in which the affected individual repeatedly waves the hands up and down
an unusually happy aspect over time whic: An unusually happy aspect over time which can also may be observed during inappropriate situations that should be causing for example distress, fear or anger
a form of hypersociability that presents: A form of hypersociability that presents as mostly inappropriate people-orientation and friendliness towards others on an inadequate level which might go as far as being dangerous considering for example young children following strangers without restriction
an anomalous configuration of blood vess: An anomalous configuration of blood vessels that shunts arterial blood directly into veins without passing through the capillaries
an aberrant thyroid gland or ectopic thy: An aberrant thyroid gland or Ectopic thyroid located at the base of the tongue, just posterior to the foramen cecum as a result of a failure of the thyroid to descend
accessory ectopic thyroid tissue arising: Accessory ectopic thyroid tissue arising from remnants of the thyroglossal duct anywhere along the path of the thyroglossal duct tract
a tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of t: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the thyroid gland
repeated, individually recognizable, int: Repeated, individually recognizable, intermittent movements or movement fragments that are almost always briefly suppresable and are usually associated with awareness of an urge to perform the movement
movement-based tics affecting discrete m: Movement-based tics affecting discrete muscle groups
involuntary sounds produced by moving ai: Involuntary sounds produced by moving air through the nose, mouth, or throat. The vocal cords are not involved in all tics that produce sound
a band of bone material of decreased den: A band of bone material of decreased density forming alongside the surface of the cortical bone with thickening of the periosteum. Callus formation in the affected area is common and gives the appearance of a false fracture
an abnormality of the hair of head: An abnormality of the hair of head
scalp hair whose growth is slower than n: Scalp hair whose growth is slower than normal
an abnormality of cortical bone leading: An Abnormality of cortical bone leading to an abnormal thickness of the cortex of affected bones
a broad appearance of the second toe: A broad appearance of the second toe
a broad appearance of the third toe: A broad appearance of the third toe
a broad appearance of the fourth toe: A broad appearance of the fourth toe
a broad appearance of the fifth toe: A broad appearance of the fifth toe
epiphyses of the 2nd toe are hard and de: Epiphyses of the 2nd toe are hard and dense like ivory. Such an epiphysis has a uniformly dense appearance on radiographs
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphyses of the 2nd toe
epiphyses of the 3rd toe are hard and de: Epiphyses of the 3rd toe are hard and dense like ivory. Such an epiphysis has a uniformly dense appearance on radiographs
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphyses of the 3rd toe
epiphyses of the 4th toe are hard and de: Epiphyses of the 4th toe are hard and dense like ivory. Such an epiphysis has a uniformly dense appearance on radiographs
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphyses of the 4th toe
epiphyses of the 5th toe are hard and de: Epiphyses of the 5th toe are hard and dense like ivory. Such an epiphysis has a uniformly dense appearance on radiographs
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphyses of the 5th toe

The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphysis of the proximal phalanx of the 2nd toe
abnormality of the growth of the pubic h: Abnormality of the growth of the pubic hair. Pubic hair is part of the secondary sexual hair, which normally ensues during puberty
abnormality of the growth of the axillar: Abnormality of the growth of the axillary hair. Axillary hair is part of the secondary sexual hair, which normally ensues during puberty
the presence of abnormal punctate (speck: The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphyses of the distal phalanx of the 3rd toe

The presence of abnormal punctate (speckled, dot-like) calcifications in the epiphyses of the proximal phalanx of the 5th toe
an abnormal union between bones or parts: An abnormal union between bones or parts of bones of the upper limbs
the abnormal fusion of neighboring bones: The abnormal fusion of neighboring bones across a joint
ectopic respiratory epithelium presentin: Ectopic respiratory epithelium presenting as a superficial lesion in the skin usually localised unilateral in the skin of the forearm and associated with ipsilateral hand malformations
a connective tissue neoplasm formed by p: A connective tissue neoplasm formed by proliferation of mesodermal cells. Bone and soft tissue sarcomas are the main types of sarcoma. Sarcoma is usually highly malignant
a smooth muscle connective tissue tumor,: A smooth muscle connective tissue tumor, which is rare type of cancer that is a malignant neoplasm of smooth muscle. When such a neoplasm is benign, it is called a leiomyoma
a fibroblastic sarcoma is a malignant tu: A fibroblastic sarcoma is a malignant tumor derived from fibrous connective tissue and characterized by immature proliferating fibroblasts or undifferentiated anaplastic spindle cells
benign, slow-growing tumors without any: Benign, slow-growing tumors without any metastatic potential. Despite their benign nature, they can damage nearby structures causing organ dysfunction. Histologically they resemble low-grade fibrosarcomas, but they are very locally aggressive and tend to recur even after complete resection. There is a tendency for recurrence in the setting of prior surgery and the most common localisation of these tumors is intraabdominal from smooth muscle cells of the instestine
osteomas are bony growths found most com: Osteomas are bony growths found most commonly on the skull and mandible; however, they may occur in any bone of the body. Osteomas do not usually cause clinical problems and do not become malignant
a contraction of the diaphragm that repe: A contraction of the diaphragm that repeats several times per minute. In humans, the abrupt rush of air into the lungs causes the epiglottis to close, creating a hic sound. Also known as synchronous diaphragmatic flutter (SDF), or singultus, from the Latin singult, the act of catching one's breath while sobbing. The hiccup is an involuntary action involving a reflex arc
hemiballismus is a rare movement disorde: Hemiballismus is a rare movement disorder that is caused primarily by damage to various areas in the basal ganglia. Hemiballismus is usually characterized by involuntary flinging motions of the extremities. The movements are often violent and have wide amplitudes of motion. They are continuous and random and can involve proximal and/or distal muscles on one side of the body, while some cases even include the facial muscles. The more a patient is active, the more the movements increase. With relaxation comes a decrease in movements
deposition of calcium salts in muscle ti: Deposition of calcium salts in muscle tissue
an abnormality of the medullary cavity (: An abnormality of the medullary cavity (medulla, innermost part), which is the central cavity of bone shafts where red bone marrow and/or yellow bone marrow (adipose tissue) is stored
the presence of dysplastic regions in me: The presence of dysplastic regions in metaphyseal regions
senile plaques are extracellular deposit: Senile plaques are extracellular deposits of amyloid in the gray matter of the brain
a condition in which middle parts of the: A condition in which middle parts of the hands and/or feet (digits and meta-carpals and -tarsals) are missing giving a cleft appearance. The severity is very variable ranging from slightly hypoplastic 3rd toe/fingers over absent 2nd or 3rd toes/fingers as far as oligo- or monodactyl hands and/or feet
a form of polydactyly in which the extra: A form of polydactyly in which the extra digit or digits are localized on the side of the thumb or great toe
A form of polydactyly in which the extra digit or digits are localized on the side of the fifth finger or fifth toe
the presence of a supernumerary finger o: The presence of a supernumerary finger or toe (not a thumb or hallux) involving the third or fourth metacarpal/tarsal with associated osseous syndactyly
an abnormality of the structure or form: An abnormality of the structure or form of the tendons, also often called sinews
the carpus consists of the scaphoid, lun: The carpus consists of the scaphoid, lunate, triquetal, pisiform, captitate, hamate, trapezoid, and trapezium bones. The tarsus consists of the talus, calcaneus, navicular, cuboid, cuneiform, and navicular bones. This term applies if there is any fusion among the bones of the carpus or tarsus
a depression located on a lip: A depression located on a lip
depression located on the vermilion of t: Depression located on the vermilion of the upper lip, usually paramedian
depression located paramedially on the v: Depression located paramedially on the vermilion of a lip
an abnormality resulting from a defect o: An abnormality resulting from a defect or disruption of dorsoventral patterning that normally happens during early development of the limbs. A disruption of the normal development of the dorsoventral axis may lead to a variable spectrum of different phenotypic abnormalities that may affect the nails and or palmar and dorsal side of the hands and/or feet, ultimately changing the normal dorsoventral appearance of the affected limbs
hyponasal speech is when there is an abn: Hyponasal speech is when there is an abnormally reduced nasal airflow during speech often in a setting of nasal obstruction or congestion
a congenital branchial sinus is a remnan: A congenital branchial sinus is a remnant of the embryonic branchial arches and their intervening clefts and pouches that has failed to regress completely. Sinuses typically have their external orifice inferior to the ramus of the mandible. They may traverse the parotid gland, and run in close vicinity to the facial nerve in the external auditory canal
gustatory lacrimation results from an ab: Gustatory lacrimation results from an aberrant innervation of fibres from the seventh cranial nerve to the pterygopalatine ganglion which are destined originally for the submandibular ganglion. This aberrant innervation leads to uncontrollable tearing while eating or in anticipation of a meal
diffuse unlocalised atrophy affecting th: Diffuse unlocalised atrophy affecting the cerebellum
a small, skin-lined tract that leads fro: A small, skin-lined tract that leads from the surface to deep within the tissues
benign congenital lesions of the periaur: Benign congenital lesions of the periauricular soft tissue consisting of a blind-ending narrow tube or pit
a chronic inflammatory bowel disease tha: A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that includes characteristic ulcers, or open sores, in the colon. The main symptom of active disease is usually constant diarrhea mixed with blood, of gradual onset and intermittent periods of exacerbated symptoms contrasting with periods that are relatively symptom-free. In contrast to Crohn's disease this special form of colitis begins in the distal parts of the rectum, spreads continually upwards and affects only mucose and submucose tissue of the colon
a chronic granulomatous inflammatory dis: A chronic granulomatous inflammatory disease of the intestines that may affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract from mouth to anus, causing a wide variety of symptoms. It primarily causes abdominal pain, diarrhea which may be bloody, vomiting, or weight loss, but may also cause complications outside of the gastrointestinal tract such as skin rashes, arthritis, inflammation of the eye, tiredness, and lack of concentration. Crohn's disease is thought to be an autoimmune disease, in which the body's immune system attacks the gastrointestinal tract, causing inflammation
a chronic inflammatory disease of the la: A chronic inflammatory disease of the large intestine (colon, cecum and rectum)
an acute and self-limited inflammatory d: An acute and self-limited inflammatory disease of the large intestine (colon, cecum and rectum)
continuous electromyographic activity of: Continuous electromyographic activity of motor units at rest, i.e., without voluntary movement of the muscles
high frequency discharges in electromyog: High frequency discharges in electromyography (EMG) that vary in amplitude and frequency, waxing and waning continuously with firing frequencies ranging from 150/second down to 20/second and producing a sound that has been referred to as a dive bomber sound
an electromyographic finding associated: An electromyographic finding associated with erratic or absent neuromuscular transmission with erratic, moment-to-moment changes in the shape of the motor unit potential (MUP)
the presence of reduced conduction veloc: The presence of reduced conduction velocity of motor nerves on electromyography
the presence of spontaneous bursts of ra: The presence of spontaneous bursts of rapidly firing potentials that recur at regular intervals of 2-10 per second and are unaffected by voluntary effort. This is an electromyographic (EMG) finding
the presence of amyloid deposition in th: The presence of amyloid deposition in the nerves of the peripheral nervous system
unusual regions of densely packed membra: Unusual regions of densely packed membranous tubules known as tubular aggregates which present as membranous inclusions, derived from membranes of sarcoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria, containing miscellaneous proteins with a variety of enzymatic activities
the presence of inclusion bodies within: The presence of inclusion bodies within the cytoplasm of muscle cells. Inclusion bodies are aggregates (deposits) or stainable material, usually misfolded proteins
The presence of inclusion bodies within the nucleus of muscle cells. Inclusion bodies are aggregates (deposits) or stainable material, usually misfolded proteins
ring fibers are formed by a bundle of pe: Ring fibers are formed by a bundle of peripheral myofibrils which are circumferentially oriented such that they encircle the internal portion of the sarcoplasm which is normal in structure and orientation
atrophy of one side of the brain, charac: Atrophy of one side of the brain, characterized by findings including thinning of the cerebral cortex, reduced volume of the cerebral white matter with abnormal myelination, and enlargement of the ispilateral fourth ventricle
hemorrhage occurring between the dura ma: Hemorrhage occurring between the dura mater and the arachnoid mater
Hemorrhage occurring between the dura mater and the skull
the presence of a germ cell tumor of the: The presence of a germ cell tumor of the cerebrum
cerebral inflammation involving a granul: Cerebral inflammation involving a granulomatous response, i.e., a non-specific inflammatory response involving granulomas, defined as a compact organized collection of mature mononuclear phagocytes including epithelioid and giant cells
nuclear or cytoplasmic aggregates of sta: Nuclear or cytoplasmic aggregates of stainable substances within cells of the brain
intracellular aggregates of actin and ac: Intracellular aggregates of actin and actin-associated proteins within nerve cells
presence of abundant argyrophilic grains: Presence of abundant argyrophilic grains and coiled bodies on microscopic examination of brain tissue
an intraneuronal inclusion body composed: An intraneuronal inclusion body composed of acid mucopolysaccharides
cerebral eosinophilic, discrete, intracy: Cerebral eosinophilic, discrete, intracytoplasmatic inclusions of unknown significance
thick, elongated, worm-like or corkscrew: Thick, elongated, worm-like or corkscrew eosinophilic bundle that are found on HE staining of the brain in the presence of long standing gliosis, occasional tumors, and some metabolic disorders
an abnormality of the dentate nucleus: An abnormality of the dentate nucleus
juvenile aseptic necrosis comprises a gr: Juvenile aseptic necrosis comprises a group of orthopedic diseases characterized by interruption of the blood supply of a bone, followed by localized bony necrosis most often of the epiphyses of bones of children or teenagers
a chronic autoimmune phenomenon characte: A chronic autoimmune phenomenon characterized by fibrosis (or hardening) and vascular alterations of the skin
immunological states where the immune sy: Immunological states where the immune system produces harmful responses upon reexposure to sensitising antigens
hypersensitivity in form of an adverse i: Hypersensitivity in form of an adverse immune reaction against cow milk protein
fusion involving carpal and metacarpal b: Fusion involving carpal and metacarpal bones
a non-midline cleft of the upper lip on: A non-midline cleft of the upper lip on one side only
clefting of the upper lip affecting the: Clefting of the upper lip affecting the lateral portions of the upper lip rather than the midline/median region
a non-midline cleft of the upper lip on: A non-midline cleft of the upper lip on the left and right sides
nonmidline cleft palate on the left and: Nonmidline cleft palate on the left and right sides

The proximal interphalangeal joint of the 3rd toe cannot be straightened actively or passively

an abnormal morphology of the middle pha: An abnormal morphology of the middle phalanx of the third toe, with a short and wide phalanx that tapers distally. Bullet-shaped phalanges lack the normal diaphyseal constriction

un mode de transmission qui concerne les: A mode of inheritance that is observed for traits related to a gene encoded on one of the autosomes (i.e., the human chromosomes 1-22) in which a trait manifests in heterozygotes. In the context of medical genetics, an autosomal dominant disorder is caused when a single copy of the mutant allele is present. Males and females are affected equally, and can both transmit the disorder with a risk of 50% for each child of inheriting the mutant allele

The secretion of hormones from the anterior pituitary is under the strict control of hypothalamic hormones, and the posterior pituitary is essentially an extension of the hypothalamus, so that hypothalamus and pituitary gland may be regarded as a functional unit
